topics.title: Definition and Types of Real-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition and Types of Real-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Real-Time System

A **Real-Time System (RTS)** is a computer system in which the correctness of the system depends not only on the **logical result** of computation, but also on the **time at which the result is produced**. This dual-dependency is the cornerstone of all real-time theory taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition:** *“A real-time system is one whose correctness depends on both the logical correctness of the output and the timeliness of that output. Late answers are considered wrong answers.”*

The temporal bound within which a result must be delivered is called a **Deadline**. Failing to meet a deadline degrades (or annihilates) the system's utility.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are the **head chef of a busy restaurant** during the dinner rush:

- A customer orders a pizza. If the pizza arrives **30 minutes later**, the customer is unhappy, but the meal is still edible — this is a **Soft Real-Time** scenario.
- A customer has a severe nut allergy and orders a nut-free dish. The kitchen must guarantee the dish is **safe and on time**, otherwise the customer goes into anaphylactic shock — this is a **Hard Real-Time** scenario.
- A flight control computer on an aircraft must process sensor inputs within **milliseconds**; missing the deadline means the aircraft crashes.

In each case, the system is "computing" something, but the *when* of the response is just as critical as the *what*. A conventional desktop OS (Windows/Linux desktop) has no such strict obligations — if your browser loads 2 seconds late, nobody dies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exams:** A Real-Time System is **NOT** simply a "fast" or "high-performance" system. A supercomputer that always returns results in 2 minutes is *not* real-time if a deadline of 500 ms is required. Speed alone ≠ Real-Time.

## 1.3 Core Constituent Properties

Every textbook RTS is characterized by the following properties, which KTU examiners love to test in **Part A (3-mark) questions**:

| Property | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Timeliness** | Results must be produced within deadline limits. |
| **Determinism** | Worst-case execution time (WCET) must be bounded and known. |
| **Reliability** | System must operate continuously without failure. |
| **Concurrency** | Multiple tasks/jobs execute simultaneously, often on shared resources. |
| **Predictability** | The system's behaviour at any future time must be anticipatable. |
| **Safety-Criticality** | Many RTS are embedded in life-critical applications. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analysis

## 2.1 Classification Framework — The Three Pillars

KTU syllabus explicitly mandates the study of **three types** of real-time systems, differentiated by the **consequence of missing a deadline**. This classification is rooted in the seminal work of **Krishna and Shin (1997)** and forms the basis of almost every Part B question in this module.

### 2.1.1 Hard Real-Time Systems
- A **Hard deadline** is one whose **miss results in catastrophic system failure** (loss of life, environmental damage, or massive financial loss).
- The deadline is treated as an **absolute, immovable constraint**.
- The system **must guarantee** that every deadline is met under all possible operating conditions (including worst-case load).
- Verification is done through **formal methods, schedulability analysis, and worst-case execution time (WCET) bounds**.

**Examples:** Aircraft flight control (Fly-By-Wire), Nuclear reactor shutdown systems, Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Pacemakers, Missile guidance.

### 2.1.2 Soft Real-Time Systems
- A **Soft deadline** is one whose **miss results in degraded performance** but not catastrophic failure.
- The system attempts to meet deadlines on a *best-effort* basis.
- Missing a few deadlines is **tolerated and statistically acceptable**.
- Performance is measured in terms of **average response time, throughput, and deadline-miss ratio**.

**Examples:** Online video streaming (Netflix), Web servers, Multimedia playback, Airline reservation systems, Audio/video conferencing (Zoom, Skype).

### 2.1.3 Firm Real-Time Systems
- A **Firm deadline** is intermediate: missing the deadline has **no catastrophic consequence**, but the **late result is considered useless** and is **discarded**.
- Unlike soft RTS, there is **no benefit** in delivering a late result — it is simply thrown away.
- Performance metric: **Number of jobs completed by their deadlines** (no credit for tardy jobs).

**Examples:** Real-time stock price feed, Weather forecasting data (after the forecast window), Radar tracking (a missed track is dropped, not re-shown late).

## 2.2 Additional Categorization Dimensions

Beyond the hard/soft/firm trichotomy, KTU also recognizes these orthogonal classifications:

| Classification Axis | Categories | Distinguishing Criterion |
|---|---|---|
| **By Criticality** | Safety-Critical vs. Mission-Critical | Severity of deadline miss (human life vs. mission success) |
| **By Architecture** | Distributed RTS vs. Embedded RTS | Network-coupled vs. self-contained |
| **By Triggering** | Clock-driven vs. Event-driven | Periodic timer vs. asynchronous sensor events |
| **By Size** | Small-scale (single processor) vs. Large-scale (multiprocessor/distributed) | Computational complexity |
| **By Design** | Reactive (respond to environment) vs. Time-sharing (internal scheduling) | Interaction model |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although RTS theory is primarily qualitative, a few quantitative relationships are indispensable for the ESE (End Semester Examination).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Module 1**

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Deadline Relationship** | $D_i \leq P_i$ | Relative deadline of task $i$ must be ≤ its period $P_i$ |
| 2 | **Utilization Bound** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i}$ | Total CPU utilization by $n$ periodic tasks ($C_i$ = WCET, $P_i$ = period) |
| 3 | **Rate Monotonic Bound (Liu & Layland, 1973)** | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Schedulability bound for RMS on $n$ tasks |
| 4 | **Earliest Deadline First (EDF) Bound** | $U \leq 1.0$ | EDF is optimal — works up to 100% utilization |
| 5 | **Limiting Utilization as $n \to \infty$** | $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$ | RMS asymptotic bound is **69.3%** |
| 6 | **Hyperperiod** | $H = \text{lcm}(P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n)$ | Time after which the periodic schedule repeats |
| 7 | **Response Time** | $R_i = C_i + I_i$ | Response time = WCET + Interference from higher-priority tasks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value notations have been replaced with $\vert \cdot \vert$ or \mid to prevent markdown table-breaking. KTU examiners frequently test whether students can *derive* formula 3 from formula 2, so memorize the relationship $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ carefully.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hard/soft/firm classification is the foundation upon which **medical device certification (FDA, IEC 62304)**, **automotive ISO 26262**, and **aerospace DO-178C** standards are built. A KTU student who masters this can directly apply it to embedded systems careers in companies like Bosch, Tata Elxsi, and DRDO labs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 The Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Bound — Full Derivation

This is a **classic 14-mark derivation** in the KTU ESE pattern. The Liu & Layland (1973) result must be derived rigorously, as follows.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider $n$ independent, periodic, preemptible tasks running on a single processor under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**. Under RMS, tasks with **shorter periods get higher priorities**. The question: *what is the maximum total utilization $U$ that guarantees schedulability?*

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define Utilization.**

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i}$$

Each $C_i$ is the worst-case execution time, and $P_i$ is the period of task $i$. **Textual Logic:** Utilization is simply the fraction of CPU time consumed by the task set.

**Step 2 — Consider the Critical Instant.**

The **worst-case response time** for any task occurs at a "critical instant" — a time when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with the task itself. **Textual Logic:** Think of it as the most unfortunate moment for a low-priority task — every rival arrives at once.

**Step 3 — Compute Worst-Case Interference.**

For task $i$, in the interval $[0, P_i]$, the number of times a higher-priority task $j$ (with $P_j < P_i$) is released is bounded by:

$$N_j = \left\lceil \frac{P_i}{P_j} \right\rceil$$

**Textual Logic:** Even if the higher-priority task's period does not perfectly divide $P_i$, an extra release may occur at the boundary, hence the ceiling function.

**Step 4 — Sum the Interference.**

The total CPU time demanded by higher-priority tasks during $[0, P_i]$ is:

$$I_i = \sum_{j : P_j < P_i} \left\lceil \frac{P_i}{P_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

**Textual Logic:** Multiply the number of releases by the WCET of each release, then sum.

**Step 5 — Apply the Critical Instant Theorem (Liu & Layland).**

For a task set to be schedulable at the critical instant, we require:

$$C_i + \sum_{j : P_j < P_i} \left\lceil \frac{P_i}{P_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j \leq P_i$$

**Textual Logic:** The task's own execution plus all interference must fit within one period.

**Step 6 — Bound the Ceiling Function.**

Using the inequality:

$$\left\lceil \frac{P_i}{P_j} \right\rceil \leq \frac{P_i}{P_j} + 1$$

We obtain a sufficient (but not exact) bound. **Textual Logic:** A ceiling is never more than 1 unit larger than the fraction itself.

**Step 7 — Arrive at the Liu & Layland Sufficient Condition.**

After algebraic reduction (see Section 3.2 below for full algebra), the result is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

**Textual Logic:** This is the famous RMS bound. For $n=1$ it gives $U \leq 1.0$ (trivially true). For $n \to \infty$ it gives $U \leq 0.693$.

### 3.1.3 Numerical Instantiation

For $n = 2$ tasks:

$$U \leq 2 \left( 2^{1/2} - 1 \right) = 2 \left( \sqrt{2} - 1 \right) \approx 2(0.4142) \approx 0.8284$$

**Textual Logic:** With 2 tasks, RMS guarantees schedulability if utilization is below **82.84%**. The exact numerical values for $n = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$ are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n=1 &: U \leq 1.0000 \\
n=2 &: U \leq 0.8284 \\
n=3 &: U \leq 0.7798 \\
n=4 &: U \leq 0.7568 \\
n=5 &: U \leq 0.7435 \\
n \to \infty &: U \to 0.6931 \; (\ln 2)
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Detailed Algebraic Reduction (Step 7 Expanded)

Starting from:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i} \leq 1$$

And the ceiling inequality, after the substitutions $x_i = C_i / P_i$ and $P_i = 2^{i-1}$ (a worst-case harmonic construction), the LHS becomes:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{2^{i-1}}$$

Liu & Layland chose $C_i = 2^{i-1} - 1 + \epsilon$ (just enough to cause deadline miss) for the **critical construction**, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i} &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{2^{i-1} - 1 + \epsilon}{2^{i-1}} \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( 1 - \frac{1 - \epsilon}{2^{i-1}} \right) \\
&= n - (1 - \epsilon) \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2^{i-1}} \\
&= n - (1 - \epsilon)(2 - 2^{1-n})
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the limit as $\epsilon \to 0^+$ and $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( 1 - \frac{2 - 2^{1-n}}{n} \right) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

**Textual Logic:** A Taylor series expansion of $2^{1/n} = e^{\ln(2)/n} \approx 1 + \frac{\ln 2}{n} + \cdots$ yields:

$$n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) \approx n \cdot \frac{\ln 2}{n} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

Thus the asymptotic bound is precisely the natural logarithm of 2.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Hard vs. Soft vs. Firm

| Attribute | Hard RTS | Firm RTS | Soft RTS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Consequence of deadline miss** | Catastrophic (system failure) | Useless result (discarded) | Degraded performance |
| **Acceptable miss rate** | **0%** (zero tolerance) | Low, but tolerable | Statistical bound (e.g., < 1%) |
| **Validation method** | Formal proof, exhaustive testing | Statistical testing | Empirical benchmarking |
| **Scheduling algorithm** | Rate Monotonic, Deadline Monotonic | EDF, Proportional Share | FIFO, Priority Queue, Multilevel Feedback |
| **Typical WCET analysis** | Mandatory, static analysis | Often static | Optional, dynamic |
| **Example application** | Pacemaker, ABS, Flight Control | Stock ticker, Weather forecast | Streaming video, VoIP |
| **Response to overload** | Total system failure | Graceful degradation (drop jobs) | Graceful degradation (slow down) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Typical RTS

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    OUT2[DISPLAY PANEL]:::output
    OUT3[LOG FILE]:::output
    SENSOR1 --> KERNEL
    SENSOR2 --> KERNEL
    SENSOR3 --> KERNEL
    TIMER --> KERNEL
    KERNEL --> SCHED
    SCHED --> DISPATCH

    DISPATCH --> TASK2
    DISPATCH --> TASK3
```

## 4.3 Deadline Behaviour Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DEADLINE TYPES]:::root
    A --> B[HARD DEADLINE]:::hard
    A --> C[FIRM DEADLINE]:::firm
    A --> D[SOFT DEADLINE]:::soft
    B --> B1[Miss equals SYSTEM FAILURE]:::crit
    B --> B2[No statistical tolerance]:::crit
    B --> B3[Formal verification required]:::crit
    C --> C1[Miss equals USELESS RESULT]:::warn
    C --> C2[Result is DISCARDED]:::warn
    C --> C3[No penalty for occasional miss]:::warn
    D --> D1[Miss equals DEGRADED PERFORMANCE]:::ok
    D --> D2[Result still USEFUL but late]:::ok
    D --> D3[Statistical bound on misses]:::ok
    classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef hard fill:#c0392b,stroke:#7b241c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    classDef warn fill:#7e5109,stroke:#d68910,color:#ffffff

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a real-time system. Differentiate between hard and soft real-time systems with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** A real-time system is a system in which the correctness of the output depends not only on the **logical result** of computation but also on the **time at which the result is delivered**. Late answers are considered wrong.
- **Hard RTS (1 mark):** A hard real-time system must meet all deadlines without fail. Missing a deadline causes catastrophic failure. *Example:* Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), aircraft autopilot.
- **Soft RTS (1 mark):** A soft real-time system attempts to meet deadlines on a best-effort basis; missing them only degrades performance. *Example:* Online video streaming, VoIP.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a firm real-time system? Give two examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A firm real-time system is one in which a late result has **no usefulness** and is **discarded**, but missing the deadline does not cause catastrophic failure. It is intermediate between hard and soft.
- **Examples (1.5 marks):** (i) Real-time stock price quotation system, (ii) Weather forecasting data (after the forecast window closes), (iii) Radar tracking system where a missed target is dropped, not re-displayed late.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (typically one part descriptive, one part analytical). You are given an internal choice — attempt any **ONE** of the two alternatives.

---

### Part B — Option A: Comprehensive Descriptive + Analytical (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Define a real-time system. With a neat classification, explain the various types of real-time systems based on the consequences of missing a deadline. Provide two real-world examples for each type.**

**Model Solution:**

A real-time system is one whose **correctness depends on both the logical output and the time at which it is produced** (1 mark).

**Classification based on deadline criticality (4 marks):**

1. **Hard Real-Time System:** A missed deadline results in **catastrophic failure** (loss of life, equipment damage, or significant financial loss). The system must guarantee that every deadline is met under worst-case conditions, including peak load and hardware faults. *Examples:* (i) Aircraft fly-by-wire control system, (ii) Cardiac pacemaker, (iii) Nuclear reactor shutdown (SCRAM) system.

2. **Firm Real-Time System:** A missed deadline results in the **late output being discarded** as useless, but does not cause system failure. Late results provide no benefit. *Examples:* (i) Real-time stock ticker feed, (ii) Weather forecasting data ingestion.

3. **Soft Real-Time System:** A missed deadline causes **graceful degradation of performance** but the system continues to operate. Late results retain some value. *Examples:* (i) Online video streaming, (ii) Web server request handling, (iii) VoIP telephony.

**Distinguishing feature (1 mark):** Hard systems require *zero* deadline misses; firm systems tolerate occasional misses with discarded outputs; soft systems tolerate misses with degraded but usable outputs.

**Tabular summary (1 mark):** (Student is expected to draw a comparison table similar to Section 3.3 of these notes.)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Three periodic tasks are to be scheduled under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. The tasks have the following parameters:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &: C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}, \; P_1 = 4 \text{ ms} \\
T_2 &: C_2 = 2 \text{ ms}, \; P_2 = 6 \text{ ms} \\
T_3 &: C_3 = 3 \text{ ms}, \; P_3 = 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Determine whether the task set is schedulable using (i) the Liu & Layland sufficient condition and (ii) the exact critical instant analysis.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Liu & Layland Sufficient Condition (3 marks):**

Compute total utilization:

$$U = \frac{C_1}{P_1} + \frac{C_2}{P_2} + \frac{C_3}{P_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}$$

Converting to common denominator (60):

$$U = \frac{15}{60} + \frac{20}{60} + \frac{18}{60} = \frac{53}{60} \approx 0.8833$$

Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$ tasks:

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(\sqrt[3]{2} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 3(0.2599) \approx 0.7798$$

Since $U = 0.8833 > U_{\text{bound}} = 0.7798$, the sufficient condition **fails**. **However, this does not mean the task set is unschedulable** — it only means the test is inconclusive. The exact test is required.

**(ii) Exact Critical Instant Analysis (4 marks):**

**Textual Logic:** Under RMS, priorities follow periods: $T_1$ (P=4) is highest, $T_2$ (P=6) middle, $T_3$ (P=10) lowest. Examine each task at its critical instant ($t=0$).

**Check $T_1$:** $C_1 = 1 \leq P_1 = 4$. ✓ Schedulable trivially.

**Check $T_2$:** In $[0, 6]$, task $T_1$ is released $\lceil 6/4 \rceil = 2$ times, demanding $2 \times 1 = 2$ ms. Total demand: $C_2 + I_2 = 2 + 2 = 4 \leq 6$. ✓ Schedulable.

**Check $T_3$:** In $[0, 10]$, task $T_1$ is released $\lceil 10/4 \rceil = 3$ times (demanding 3 ms); $T_2$ is released $\lceil 10/6 \rceil = 2$ times (demanding 4 ms). Total demand: $C_3 + I_3 = 3 + 3 + 4 = 10 \leq 10$. ✓ Schedulable (just barely).

**Conclusion:** The task set **is schedulable** under RMS, even though the Liu & Layland sufficient test was inconclusive. The exact test is therefore the more powerful analytical tool.

---

### Part B — Option B: Conceptual + Comparative (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the following classifications of real-time systems with suitable examples: (i) Clock-driven vs Event-driven, (ii) Reactive vs Time-sharing, (iii) Safety-critical vs Mission-critical.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Clock-Driven vs Event-Driven (2.5 marks):**
- **Clock-driven (cyclic executives):** Tasks are triggered by the passage of real time, typically implemented as a periodic tick interrupt. The entire schedule is statically computed offline. Best suited for systems with well-known periodic workloads. *Example:* A digital engine control unit (ECU) that samples crank-shaft position every 10 ms.
- **Event-driven:** Tasks are triggered by asynchronous external events such as sensor interrupts or messages. The system must respond within deadlines defined from the event occurrence. *Example:* A burglar alarm system triggered by a motion sensor.

**(ii) Reactive vs Time-Sharing (2.5 marks):**
- **Reactive (control) systems:** The system maintains a continuous interaction with its physical environment, continuously sensing and actuating. *Example:* A robot arm in an assembly line.
- **Time-sharing systems:** Multiple users/tasks share CPU time, optimizing throughput and average response. *Example:* An airline reservation system accessed by many travel agents.

**(iii) Safety-Critical vs Mission-Critical (2 marks):**
- **Safety-critical:** Failure may cause loss of human life. *Example:* Avionics, railway signalling.
- **Mission-critical:** Failure may cause mission failure (no loss of life, but mission is aborted). *Example:* Satellite payload controller, scientific data acquisition.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast: (i) Earliest Deadline First (EDF) and Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) with respect to optimality, schedulability bound, and runtime overhead. (ii) Asymmetric multiprocessing (AMP) vs Symmetric multiprocessing (SMP) in real-time contexts.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) EDF vs RMS Comparison (4 marks):**

| Property | RMS | EDF |
|---|---|---|
| **Priority assignment** | Fixed, by period (shorter period → higher priority) | Dynamic, by absolute deadline (earlier → higher priority) |
| **Optimality** | Optimal among **fixed-priority** algorithms | Optimal among **all** algorithms (uniprocessor) |
| **Schedulability bound** | $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1) \to 0.693$ | $U \leq 1.0$ |
| **Runtime overhead** | Low (static priorities) | Higher (dynamic priority recalculation at every event) |
| **Implementation complexity** | Simple | Moderate |
| **Transient overload behaviour** | Predictable (low-priority tasks starve) | Domino effect (multiple misses) |

**Key insight (1 mark):** EDF can fully utilize the CPU (up to 100%) but is harder to implement and verify in safety-critical systems. RMS, despite its lower bound, is widely used in avionics and automotive systems because **fixed priorities simplify certification**.

**(ii) AMP vs SMP in Real-Time (3 marks):**

- **AMP (Asymmetric Multiprocessing):** One CPU runs the OS kernel and handles all interrupts; the others run only application tasks. Predictable, deterministic, simple to analyze. *Used in:* Legacy automotive ECUs.
- **SMP (Symmetric Multiprocessing):** All CPUs are equal and share the OS, memory, and interrupts. Higher throughput but harder to bound WCET due to cache coherence traffic and unpredictable interrupt migration. *Used in:* Modern multicore smartphones and ADAS platforms.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "fast" with "real-time":** A supercomputer is not necessarily real-time. KTU examiners deduct **2 marks** if you equate real-time with speed alone.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Late answer = wrong answer" line:** This is the single most-quoted KTU answer line for this topic. Missing it in your definition costs you a full mark.
> 3. **Mixing up firm and soft:** Firm means *discarded*, soft means *degraded but useful*. Examiners test this distinction deliberately. Conflating them loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Insufficient schedulability analysis:** In part (b) numericals, students often stop at the Liu & Layland bound and conclude "not schedulable." Always follow up with the exact critical instant test — the bound is **sufficient, not necessary**.
> 5. **Missing examples:** Every classification question (3 marks) requires at least **two concrete examples** for full credit. Generic examples like "embedded system" are penalized.
> 6. **Formula 3 not memorized:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ must be reproduced verbatim in derivations. Forgetting the term $2^{1/n}$ is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 7. **Ignoring deadlines vs periods:** When $D_i > P_i$, the standard RMS bound does not apply directly. State this assumption explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] A **real-time system** is defined by **temporal correctness** — late output is wrong output.
- [x] Three primary types: **Hard** (catastrophic miss), **Firm** (discarded miss), **Soft** (degraded miss).
- [x] **Hard RTS** examples: ABS, Pacemaker, Aircraft flight control, Nuclear reactor SCRAM.
- [x] **Soft RTS** examples: Video streaming, Web servers, VoIP, online gaming.
- [x] **Firm RTS** examples: Stock tickers, weather forecast feeds, real-time radar tracking.
- [x] **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** fixed priority, shorter period → higher priority.
- [x] **Liu & Layland bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, asymptotic limit **0.6931 ($\ln 2$)**.
- [x] **EDF:** dynamic priority, optimal, bound $U \leq 1.0$.
- [x] **Hyperperiod** $H = \text{lcm}(P_1, \ldots, P_n)$ — the schedule repeats after this interval.
- [x] **Critical Instant** is the worst-case release time for any task.
- [x] Three orthogonal classifications: by **trigger** (clock/event/hybrid), by **architecture** (embedded/distributed/multiprocessor), by **criticality** (safety/mission).
- [x] **SMP** is higher throughput but harder to verify; **AMP** is more deterministic.
- [x] **Real-time ≠ fast** — it means *predictable* and *deadline-bound*.
- [x] KTU favourite line to memorize: *"A late answer is considered a wrong answer."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hard vs. Soft Real-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hard vs. Soft Real-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Real-Time Systems — The Foundation

A **Real-Time System (RTS)** is a computer system in which the correctness of the system depends not only on the **logical result** of a computation, but also on the **time at which the result is produced**. If the timing constraints are not met, the system is considered to have **failed**, even if the computed value is mathematically correct.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Liu & Layland terminology):**
> *“A real-time system is one in which the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical results of computation, but also on the time at which the results are produced.”*

### 1.2 Hard Real-Time Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Real-Time System:** A real-time system in which a missed deadline is treated as a **total system failure** and may result in **catastrophic consequences** (loss of life, environmental damage, or financial disaster). The response time **must** be guaranteed to be within a strict bound under **all** operating conditions, including worst-case load.

**Key Properties of Hard RTS:**
- **Absolute deadline** — task MUST complete before its deadline.
- **Deterministic** — worst-case execution time (WCET) is bounded and known.
- **Zero tolerance** for deadline misses in critical tasks.
- **Pessimistic scheduling** — scheduling decisions are based on the **worst-case** scenario, not the average case.
- Response time is typically in the order of **milliseconds** or **microseconds**.

### 1.3 Soft Real-Time Systems — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soft Real-Time System:** A real-time system in which a missed deadline is **tolerable** and results only in a **degradation of quality of service (QoS)**. The utility of the result decreases gradually as the deadline is missed, but the system continues to operate.

**Key Properties of Soft RTS:**
- **Soft deadline** — missing it reduces usefulness, but does not cause failure.
- **Statistical / probabilistic guarantees** are often acceptable.
- **Average-case performance** is more important than worst-case.
- Useful tasks retain residual value even after the deadline.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 The "Airbag vs. YouTube" Analogy**

| Scenario | Type | What happens if late? |
|---|---|---|
| 🚗 **Airbag deployment sensor** | **Hard** | Crash. The airbag fires 5 ms *after* the impact — useless. Missing the deadline means **death**. |
| 🎮 **Online multiplayer game** | **Soft** | Lag spike. Your character freezes for 200 ms — annoying, but the game continues. You can still play. |
| 🏥 **Pacemaker heartbeat signal** | **Hard** | Irregular heartbeat, organ failure. The pulse must arrive within 40 ms — every single time. |
| 📺 **Netflix video buffer** | **Soft** | Buffering wheel. Picture quality drops, audio stutters — still watchable. |
| ✈️ **Flight control surface actuator** | **Hard** | Plane crashes. Control input must execute within 10 ms. |
| 📧 **Email notification** | **Soft** | Arrives 2 seconds late — no big deal. |

The rule of thumb is simple: **Hard = life-critical / mission-critical**, **Soft = quality-of-experience-critical**.

### 1.5 The Third Category — Firm Real-Time Systems

Many KTU examiners also test the **middle ground**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Firm Real-Time System:** A late result has **zero value** (it is discarded) but missing the deadline does **not** cause catastrophic failure — it simply wastes the computation. A small, bounded number of deadline misses is acceptable.
> **Example:** A stock-ticker that displays the price of a share 500 ms late has no value (the price has changed), but the trading system does not crash.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Classification

Real-time systems are classified based on the **consequence of a deadline miss**, not on speed. A system running on a 1 GHz processor can still be a soft real-time system, and a 10 MHz microcontroller can host a hard real-time system. Classification is governed by **criticality**, not clock speed.

The classification logic follows a 3-step decision tree:

1. **Can a deadline miss cause loss of life, physical damage, or huge financial loss?**
   → If **YES** → **Hard Real-Time**.
2. **Does a late result lose ALL its value (i.e., is it discarded) but the system continues?**
   → If **YES** → **Firm Real-Time**.
3. **Does a late result lose SOME value (graceful degradation) but remains useful?**
   → If **YES** → **Soft Real-Time**.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Sheet

| Parameter | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time | Firm Real-Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Deadline type** | Hard (absolute) | Soft (flexible) | Hard but discardable |
| **Consequence of miss** | **Catastrophic** system failure | QoS degradation | Wasted result, system OK |
| **Guarantee** | Deterministic (worst-case) | Statistical / probabilistic | Bounded number of misses |
| **Scheduling basis** | WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time) | Average execution time | Mostly WCET |
| **Peak load performance** | Must be acceptable | May degrade | Mostly acceptable |
| **Utility function** | Step function at deadline | Linear/gradual decay | Step function at deadline (to zero) |
| **Response time goal** | Predictable under all conditions | Best-effort within reasonable time | Predictable with bounded misses |
| **Typical examples** | Airbag, pacemaker, ABS, fly-by-wire | Video streaming, VoIP, gaming | Stock ticker, weather forecast |
| **Design philosophy** | Pessimistic (handle worst case) | Optimistic (handle average case) | Mixed |
| **Cost & complexity** | Very high | Moderate | High |
| **Operating system** | RTOS (VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS) | GPOS (Linux, Windows) | RTOS with QoS layer |
| **Deadline miss response** | System halt / emergency mode | Continue with degraded service | Discard late task |
| **Validation method** | Formal verification, exhaustive testing | Statistical testing, simulation | Bounded testing |
| **Time scale** | µs – ms | ms – seconds | ms – seconds |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Quantitative Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For any real-time task $\tau_i$, the following timing parameters are essential for KTU numerical and analytical problems.

| Symbol | Metric | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_i$ | **Period** | Time between consecutive activations | **ms** |
| $D_i$ | **Deadline** | Maximum time by which task must complete | **ms** |
| $C_i$ | **WCET** (Worst-Case Execution Time) | Maximum computation time of task | **ms** |
| $R_i$ | **Response Time** | Time from release to completion | **ms** |
| $J_i$ | **Jitter** | Variation in response time between instances | **ms** |
| $U_i$ | **Utilization** | $C_i / T_i$ | dimensionless |
| $U_{total}$ | **CPU Utilization** | $\sum (C_i / T_i)$ | dimensionless |

**The Fundamental Hard RTS Constraint (Liu & Layland, 1973):**

For a set of $n$ periodic hard real-time tasks scheduled by Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), a **sufficient** (but not necessary) schedulability bound is:

$$U_{bound} = n \cdot \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **As $n \to \infty$, $U_{bound} \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693$** — meaning the CPU can never be loaded above ~69.3% under RMS for guaranteed hard real-time performance.

The **hard constraint** for any real-time system is:

$$R_i \le D_i \quad \forall i \in \text{Task Set}$$

For **soft** systems this becomes a **statistical expectation**:

$$P(R_i \le D_i) \ge \alpha$$

where $\alpha$ is the desired probability of meeting the deadline (e.g., 0.95 = 95%).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR)**: Brake-by-wire and engine control are **hard**; infotainment is **soft**.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C)**: Flight control is **hard** at Design Assurance Level (DAL) A.
- **Medical (IEC 62304)**: Ventilator and dialysis machines are **hard** at Class C.
- **Telecommunications (5G URLLC)**: Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication targets **hard** 1 ms latency with 99.999% reliability.
- **Industrial IoT**: PLC scan cycles in a refinery are **hard**; SCADA dashboards are **soft**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deadline Compliance Analysis — Worked Example

> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:**
> Consider a hard real-time task $\tau_1$ with period $T_1 = 20$ ms and WCET $C_1 = 4$ ms. Compute the CPU utilization, the maximum possible utilization under RMS for a single task, and determine if a second task $\tau_2$ with $C_2 = 6$ ms, $T_2 = 30$ ms can be added while remaining schedulable.

#### **Step 1 — Compute Utilization of $\tau_1$**

$$U_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{4 \text{ ms}}{20 \text{ ms}} = 0.20$$

Interpretation: Task $\tau_1$ consumes **20%** of the CPU's time in the worst case.

#### **Step 2 — Compute Utilization of $\tau_2$**

$$U_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{6 \text{ ms}}{30 \text{ ms}} = 0.20$$

#### **Step 3 — Compute Total Utilization**

$$U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 = 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.40$$

#### **Step 4 — Compute RMS Upper Bound for $n = 2$**

$$U_{bound}(n=2) = 2 \cdot \left( 2^{1/2} - 1 \right) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) = 2 \cdot 0.4142 = 0.8284$$

#### **Step 5 — Apply the Schedulability Test**

The necessary and sufficient test (exact test, not the sufficient bound) for RMS is the **Response Time Analysis** (Joseph & Pandya, 1986):

$$R_i^{n+1} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{n}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$

The iteration starts with $R_i^0 = C_i$ and terminates when $R_i^{n+1} = R_i^n$ or $R_i^{n+1} > D_i$.

**For $\tau_1$ (highest priority):**

$$R_1 = C_1 = 4 \text{ ms} \le D_1 = 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**For $\tau_2$ (lower priority):**

Iteration 0:

$$R_2^0 = C_2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

Iteration 1:

$$R_2^1 = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^0}{T_1} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + (0) \cdot 4 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

Iteration 2:

$$R_2^2 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + 0 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

The iteration has converged: $R_2 = 6$ ms.

**Step 6 — Final Verdict**

$$R_1 = 4 \text{ ms} \le D_1 = 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

$$R_2 = 6 \text{ ms} \le D_2 = 30 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result: Both tasks are schedulable under RMS.** The system is **hard real-time** because every deadline is guaranteed to be met under all conditions.

#### **Step 7 — Adding a Third Task to Test the Bound**

Suppose a third task $\tau_3$ is added with $C_3 = 10$ ms, $T_3 = 30$ ms.

$$U_{total} = 0.20 + 0.20 + \frac{10}{30} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.333 = 0.733$$

The Liu & Layland bound for $n=3$:

$$U_{bound}(n=3) = 3 \cdot \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

Since $U_{total} = 0.733 \le 0.7798$, the **sufficient condition** is satisfied. However, to be safe, the **exact response time analysis** must be run, because the bound is sufficient but not necessary.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Utility Function (Value vs. Time)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **utility function** $u(t)$ describes how the value of a result changes as a function of the completion time $t$. This is the most tested concept in KTU exams for this topic.

$$\begin{aligned}
u_{hard}(t) &= \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } t \le D \\ 0 & \text{if } t > D \end{cases} \quad \text{(Step function — total loss)} \\[6pt]
u_{firm}(t) &= \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } t \le D \\ 0 & \text{if } t > D \end{cases} \quad \text{(Same shape, but 0 means "discard", not "crash")} \\[6pt]
u_{soft}(t) &= \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } t \le D \\ \alpha \cdot e^{-\lambda (t - D)} & \text{if } t > D \end{cases} \quad \text{(Exponential decay)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation for the student:**

- **Hard RTS**: $u(t)$ drops from **1 to 0** at deadline $D$ — a vertical cliff. Anything past the deadline is not just useless, it's **catastrophic**.
- **Firm RTS**: $u(t)$ also drops from 1 to 0, but the system is **still alive**. The result is thrown away.
- **Soft RTS**: $u(t)$ **decays smoothly** past the deadline. Late results still have value, just less of it.

### 3.3 Average-Case vs. Worst-Case Scheduling — Decision Logic

$$\text{If system is } \begin{cases} \text{Hard} & \Rightarrow \text{Schedule using } \mathbf{WCET}, \text{ not average time} \\ \text{Soft} & \Rightarrow \text{Can schedule using average time + bounded retries} \\ \text{Firm} & \Rightarrow \text{Schedule using WCET but allow } \epsilon \text{ misses} \end{cases}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A common 7-mark question asks, *"Why can't we use a General Purpose OS (GPOS) like Windows for a hard real-time system?"* The answer is rooted in this analysis: GPOS optimizes for **average throughput**, not **worst-case latency**, so a deadline miss can never be ruled out.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Classification Tree — Real-Time Systems

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Real-Time Systems] --> B[Hard Real-Time]
    A --> C[Firm Real-Time]
    A --> D[Soft Real-Time]
    B --> B1[Airbag System]
    B --> B2[Pacemaker]
    B --> B3[ABS Braking]
    B --> B4[Flight Control]
    B --> B5[Nuclear Reactor Control]
    C --> C1[Stock Ticker]
    C --> C2[Weather Forecast Display]
    C --> C3[Real-Time Bidding Ads]
    D --> D1[Video Streaming]
    D --> D2[VoIP Calls]
    D --> D3[Online Gaming]
    D --> D4[Email Delivery]
    D --> D5[Web Browsing]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Deadline Miss Response Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Task Released] --> exec[Execute Task]
    exec --> check{Deadline Met?}
    check -- Yes, Hard --> okHard[Result Used Successfully]
    check -- No, Hard --> failCatastrophic[System Failure / Catastrophe]
    check -- Yes, Firm --> okFirm[Result Used]
    check -- No, Firm --> discard[Result Discarded System Continues]
    check -- Yes, Soft --> okSoft[Result Used at Full Quality]
    check -- No, Soft --> degrade[Result Used with Degraded Quality]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Response-Time-Analysis Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Task Arrival] --> B[Release at t0]
    B --> C[Pre-empted by Higher Priority Tasks]
    C --> D[Resume Execution]
    D --> E{Deadline Check}
    E -- Within Bound --> F[Success Response Time R]
    E -- Exceeds Bound --> G[Hard RTS Failure]
    F --> H[Next Period T]
    H --> A
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Utility Curve (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Time t equals 0] --> B[Utility equals 1 Full Value]
    B --> C[Time reaches Deadline D]
    C --> D{System Type}
    D -- Hard --> E[Utility drops to 0 Catastrophic]
    D -- Firm --> F[Utility drops to 0 Discarded]
    D -- Soft --> G[Utility decays gradually Exponential Decay]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams for KTU exams:**
> - The **classification tree** is the first thing a 14-mark question expects when it says, *"Classify real-time systems and give two examples of each."*
> - The **deadline-miss flow** demonstrates the *consequence* branch — examiners give full marks only when students show what happens **after** a miss, not just that a miss occurred.
> - The **utility curve** is the visual form of the equations in Section 3.2 and is worth 2–3 marks on its own.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between hard and soft real-time systems. Give two examples of each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hard Real-Time Systems** are those in which a missed deadline is a **total system failure** with potentially catastrophic consequences. The response time must be **deterministically guaranteed** under the worst-case load. *Examples:* (i) Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) in a car, (ii) Pacemaker in a human heart.
>
> **Soft Real-Time Systems** are those in which a missed deadline only **degrades the quality of service** but the system continues to function. Statistical or best-effort guarantees are acceptable. *Examples:* (i) Video streaming application, (ii) Online multiplayer game.
>
> The key distinction is the **consequence** of a deadline miss — not the speed of the system. (3 marks)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is a firm real-time system? How is it different from a hard real-time system? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> A **firm real-time system** is one in which a late result has **zero value** (it is discarded) but the system **does not fail** if the deadline is missed. A small, bounded number of misses is acceptable.
>
> **Difference from hard RTS:**
> - In a **firm RTS**, missing a deadline results in a *wasted computation* — the result is thrown away, but the system continues normally.
> - In a **hard RTS**, missing a deadline results in a *catastrophic system failure* — the system may halt, crash, or cause physical harm.
>
> **Example of firm RTS:** Real-time stock-price ticker that displays a price 500 ms late — the result is useless, but the trading engine is unaffected. (3 marks)

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **📌 Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q.A. (a) Compare hard and soft real-time systems with suitable examples, highlighting at least six points of difference. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Consequence of deadline miss** | Catastrophic system failure | Quality-of-service degradation |
| 2 | **Guarantee type** | Deterministic / worst-case | Statistical / best-effort |
| 3 | **Utility function** | Step function (1 → 0) | Gradual decay (exponential) |
| 4 | **Scheduling basis** | WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time) | Average execution time |
| 5 | **Examples** | Airbag, pacemaker, ABS, fly-by-wire | Video streaming, VoIP, online gaming |
| 6 | **OS platform** | RTOS (VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS) | GPOS (Linux, Windows) |
| 7 | **Cost & design complexity** | Very high | Moderate |

**Additional key points for full marks:**

- A hard RTS **must** meet its deadline under **all** conditions, including peak load, hardware faults, and worst-case interrupt scenarios. A soft RTS only needs to meet its deadline *on average*.
- Hard RTS uses **pessimistic** resource allocation (assumes worst case always), while soft RTS uses **optimistic** allocation.
- In hard RTS, the result past the deadline is **worse than no result** (it may cause harm). In soft RTS, the result past the deadline is **less useful but still valuable**.
- Examples: ABS (Hard) — must fire in < 5 ms; YouTube (Soft) — buffering 1 s late is acceptable. (7 marks)

---

**Q.A. (b) Explain the schedulability test for a set of hard real-time periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. A system has three tasks with the following parameters. Determine if the task set is schedulable using (i) Liu & Layland bound and (ii) Exact Response Time Analysis. (7 Marks)**

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Deadline $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 10 | 2 | 10 |
| $\tau_2$ | 20 | 4 | 20 |
| $\tau_3$ | 40 | 6 | 40 |

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Assign priorities (RMS: shorter period = higher priority):**
$\tau_1$ (priority 1) > $\tau_2$ (priority 2) > $\tau_3$ (priority 3).

**Step 2 — Compute utilizations:**

$$U_1 = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20, \quad U_2 = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20, \quad U_3 = \frac{6}{40} = 0.15$$

$$U_{total} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 0.55$$

**Step 3 — Liu & Layland bound for $n = 3$:**

$$U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

**Step 4 — Verdict (i):** Since $U_{total} = 0.55 \le 0.7798$, the **sufficient condition is satisfied**. The task set is *likely* schedulable. (1 mark)

**Step 5 — Exact Response Time Analysis:**

For $\tau_1$ (highest priority, no pre-emption by higher task):

$$R_1 = C_1 = 2 \text{ ms} \le D_1 = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

For $\tau_2$ (interfered by $\tau_1$):

$$R_2^0 = C_2 = 4 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_2^1 = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + (1) \cdot 2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_2^2 = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + (1) \cdot 2 = 6 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(converged)}$$

$$R_2 = 6 \text{ ms} \le D_2 = 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

For $\tau_3$ (interfered by $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$):

$$R_3^0 = C_3 = 6 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_3^1 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + (1)(2) + (1)(4) = 12 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_3^2 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + (2)(2) + (1)(4) = 14 \text{ ms}$$

$$R_3^3 = 6 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 4 = 6 + (2)(2) + (1)(4) = 14 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(converged)}$$

$$R_3 = 14 \text{ ms} \le D_3 = 40 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Verdict (ii):** All response times are within their deadlines, so the task set is **schedulable under RMS** for a hard real-time system. (1 mark)

**Conclusion:** The system is **hard real-time schedulable** using both the sufficient bound and the exact analysis. (7 marks total)

---

#### **📌 Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q.B. (a) With a neat utility function diagram, explain the difference between hard, firm, and soft real-time systems. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **utility function** $u(t)$ represents the value of a task's output as a function of its completion time $t$.

> **Hard Real-Time System:**

$$u_{hard}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \le D \\ 0, & t > D \end{cases}$$

The utility remains at maximum value (1) until the deadline $D$, after which it **drops vertically to 0**. The result past the deadline is not just useless — its absence or delay causes **catastrophic failure**. *Example:* An airbag controller must fire within 5 ms of crash detection. A 6 ms response means the airbag deploys **after** the occupant's head has hit the steering wheel.

> **Firm Real-Time System:**

$$u_{firm}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \le D \\ 0, & t > D \end{cases}$$

The shape is **identical** to the hard case, but the *interpretation* is different. The late result is **discarded** (utility = 0) but the system itself does not crash. *Example:* A weather-forecast display that arrives 10 minutes late is discarded, but the meteorological system continues running.

> **Soft Real-Time System:**

$$u_{soft}(t) = \begin{cases} 1, & t \le D \\ e^{-\lambda(t - D)}, & t > D \end{cases}$$

The utility **decays gradually** past the deadline. The later the result, the less useful it is, but it still has **positive value**. *Example:* A VoIP packet arriving 50 ms late is still audible (slightly delayed), 200 ms late is choppy, 1000 ms late is unusable. (7 marks)

---

**Q.B. (b) A real-time system must process temperature sensor data at 100 Hz. Each sample requires 2 ms of CPU time. Compute the CPU utilization and state whether the system is schedulable as a hard real-time task. If a second task is added that runs at 50 Hz and requires 4 ms, what is the new utilization? Is it still hard real-time? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Parameters for Task 1:**

$$T_1 = \frac{1}{100 \text{ Hz}} = 10 \text{ ms}, \quad C_1 = 2 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Utilization of Task 1:**

$$U_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 \quad (20\%)$$

**Step 3 — Schedulability for single task:**

For $n = 1$:

$$U_{bound}(1) = 1 \cdot (2^{1/1} - 1) = 1 \cdot 1 = 1.0$$

Since $U_1 = 0.20 \le 1.0$, the task is **schedulable** as hard real-time. (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Add Task 2:**

$$T_2 = \frac{1}{50 \text{ Hz}} = 20 \text{ ms}, \quad C_2 = 4 \text{ ms}$$

$$U_2 = \frac{4}{20} = 0.20 \quad (20\%)$$

**Step 5 — Total Utilization:**

$$U_{total} = 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.40$$

**Step 6 — Liu & Layland bound for $n = 2$:**

$$U_{bound}(2) = 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot 0.4142 = 0.8284$$

**Step 7 — Verdict:** Since $U_{total} = 0.40 \le 0.8284$, the task set **passes the sufficient condition** for hard real-time under RMS. (3 marks)

**Step 8 — Exact Response Time Verification:**

For $\tau_1$ (higher priority, $T_1 = 10$ ms): $R_1 = C_1 = 2$ ms $\le 10$ ms ✓

For $\tau_2$ (lower priority, $T_2 = 20$ ms):

$$R_2^0 = 4, \quad R_2^1 = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 0 = 4 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(converged)}$$

$$R_2 = 4 \text{ ms} \le 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The system is schedulable as **hard real-time** with both tasks. (2 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Mistake 1:** Writing that *"hard real-time means faster than soft real-time."* **WRONG.** A 1 MHz microcontroller can be hard real-time; a 3 GHz desktop running video is soft real-time. The distinction is **consequence**, not speed.
> - **Mistake 2:** Using the Liu & Layland bound as a **necessary** condition. The bound $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is **sufficient but not necessary**. Task sets with $U > U_{bound}$ can still be schedulable — you must run the exact response-time iteration.
> - **Mistake 3:** Confusing **firm** and **hard**. In both, the utility drops to 0 at the deadline, but the *consequence* differs: firm = discarded, hard = catastrophe. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction for 2–3 marks.
> - **Mistake 4:** Forgetting the **ceiling brackets** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ in the response time iteration. Without them, the analysis is mathematically wrong and loses 2 marks.
> - **Mistake 5:** Confusing the **period** $T_i$ with the **deadline** $D_i$. In RMS, $D_i = T_i$ is *implicit*, but in EDF (Earliest Deadline First) and the general case, they are **independent parameters**. KTU questions often deliberately separate them.

---

### **📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points — Last-Minute KTU Revision**

- ✅ **Real-time system** = correctness depends on **both** logical result **and** time of delivery.
- ✅ **Hard RTS** = miss deadline → **catastrophic failure** (life, limb, money).
- ✅ **Soft RTS** = miss deadline → **graceful degradation**, system survives.
- ✅ **Firm RTS** = miss deadline → result **discarded**, system survives.
- ✅ Classification is based on **consequence**, **NOT speed** or clock frequency.
- ✅ **Hard RTS utility** is a **step function** (1 → 0 at $D$).
- ✅ **Soft RTS utility** is a **gradual decay** (often exponential) past $D$.
- ✅ **WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time)** is the basis of hard RTS scheduling, **NOT average time**.
- ✅ **GPOS (Linux/Windows)** cannot guarantee hard real-time because it optimizes for **throughput**, not **worst-case latency**.
- ✅ **RTOS (VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS)** uses **preemptive priority-based** scheduling with bounded interrupt latency.
- ✅ **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**: static priority = inverse of period. **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**: dynamic priority = closest deadline. EDF is optimal for uniprocessor systems.
- ✅ **Liu & Layland bound**: $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1) \to \ln(2) \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$.
- ✅ **Response Time Analysis** (Joseph-Pandya): $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$ — iterate until convergence.
- ✅ **Schedulability condition** for hard RTS: $R_i \le D_i$ for **all** tasks $i$.
- ✅ For **soft RTS**: $P(R_i \le D_i) \ge \alpha$ (probabilistic, not absolute).
- ✅ **Examples to memorize**: Airbag, ABS, Pacemaker, Fly-by-wire = **Hard**; YouTube, Skype, Online Games = **Soft**; Stock ticker, Weather display = **Firm**.
- ✅ **Jitter** = variation in response time between successive task instances — must be **bounded** in hard RTS.
- ✅ **Validation method** for hard RTS = formal verification + exhaustive testing; for soft RTS = statistical testing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Tasks and Their Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Tasks and Their Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-Time Task:** A real-time task is a computational job (program unit) whose correctness depends not only on the **logical result** of computation but also on the **time at which the result is produced**. Every real-time task is associated with a **timing constraint** (typically a deadline) that must be satisfied to deem the system correct.

In the formal model of Real-Time Systems (RTS), a real-time task is characterized as a sequence of **jobs** (also called **task instances**). A single task $T_i$ may generate infinitely many (or finitely many) jobs $J_{i,1}, J_{i,2}, J_{i,3}, \ldots$ depending on whether the task is periodic, aperiodic, or sporadic.

### Formal Task Model Representation

A real-time task is generally abstracted using the following parameter set:

$$T_i = (\phi_i, p_i, e_i, D_i)$$

Where:
- $\phi_i$ = Phase (release time of the very first job)
- $p_i$ = Period (for periodic tasks) or minimum inter-arrival time (for sporadic tasks)
- $e_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)
- $D_i$ = Relative deadline

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 💡

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Ambulance Dispatch System**
> 
> Imagine you call an ambulance. The ambulance must arrive **within a strict time window** (say 8 minutes) to be considered "successful." If it arrives after 15 minutes, the patient may have died — so a "correct but late" response is **catastrophically wrong**. This is exactly what defines a **real-time task**: the value of the response diminishes (or becomes negative) as time progresses.
> 
> - The **ambulance job** = a real-time task instance
> - The **8-minute deadline** = hard real-time constraint
> - **A "How-to" tutorial video buffering on YouTube** = soft real-time task (slight delay is annoying but acceptable)

---

## 1.3 Classification of Real-Time Tasks (High-Yield KTU Overview)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Primary Task Types (Must memorize for KTU 2-mark and 3-mark questions):**

| Category | Trigger Pattern | Typical Application |
|----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Periodic** | Activated regularly at fixed intervals | Sensor sampling, control loops |
| **Aperiodic** | Activated at irregular/unpredictable times | Operator commands, alarms |
| **Sporadic** | Aperiodic + bounded minimum inter-arrival time | Emergency signals, button presses |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Sporadic tasks are aperiodic tasks that come with a **guaranteed lower bound** on the time between consecutive arrivals. This makes them schedulable under Rate Monotonic Analysis.

---

## 1.4 Hard vs. Soft Real-Time — Syllabus Highlight

> [!CAUTION]
> **Hard Real-Time Tasks:** Missing a deadline is a **total system failure** (catastrophic). Example: Airbag deployment, anti-lock braking.
> 
> **Soft Real-Time Tasks:** Missing a deadline degrades performance but is **not catastrophic**. Example: Video frame decoding, audio streaming.
> 
> **Firm Real-Time Tasks:** Late results are **useless** (zero value), but no damage occurs. Example: Stock price feed where old data is meaningless.

The **standard metric** used to quantify timing tolerance in KTU problems is the **Deadline Miss Ratio**, expressed as a percentage:
$$\text{Miss Ratio} = \frac{\text{Number of Deadline Misses}}{\text{Total Activations}} \times 100\%$$
For hard real-time, the acceptable value is **0%**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Real-Time Task — Step-by-Step Breakdown

Every real-time job $J_{i,k}$ (the $k^{th}$ instance of task $T_i$) passes through a sequence of **timing events**. The KTU examiner expects you to label these clearly:

1. **Arrival Time ($a_{i,k}$):** The instant when the job becomes known to the system.
2. **Release Time ($r_{i,k}$):** The instant when the job is ready to execute. Often $r_{i,k} = a_{i,k}$, but they may differ if there is intentional jitter or synchronization.
3. **Start Time ($s_{i,k}$):** The instant when the job actually begins CPU execution.
4. **Completion Time ($c_{i,k}$):** The instant when the job finishes execution.
5. **Absolute Deadline ($d_{i,k}$):** The latest time by which the job **must** complete.
6. **Response Time ($R_{i,k}$):** Total time from release to completion.

---

## 2.2 Core Mathematical Relationships (The "Why" and "How")

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter:** They are the backbone of KTU numerical problems on **schedulability analysis** (RMA, EDF, processor utilization).

The **response time** is fundamentally:

$$R_{i,k} = c_{i,k} - r_{i,k}$$

The **slack time** (or laxity) of a job represents how much "free time" remains:

$$\text{Slack}_{i,k} = d_{i,k} - c_{i,k}$$

The job is **feasible** if and only if:

$$c_{i,k} \leq d_{i,k} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad R_{i,k} \leq D_i$$

For **periodic tasks**, the arrival time of the $k^{th}$ job is:

$$a_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) \cdot p_i$$

And the corresponding absolute deadline is:

$$d_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) \cdot p_i + D_i$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the MOST HIGH-YIELD reference for KTU End Semester Examinations.**

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary |
|---|-----------|--------|----------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Period | $p_i$ | Time between two consecutive releases of $T_i$ | Time units (ms, µs) |
| 2 | Worst-Case Execution Time | $e_i$ | Maximum CPU time required by any job of $T_i$ | $0 < e_i \leq p_i$ |
| 3 | Relative Deadline | $D_i$ | Time allowed from release to completion | $0 < D_i \leq p_i$ |
| 4 | Absolute Deadline | $d_{i,k}$ | $r_{i,k} + D_i$ | Time instant |
| 5 | Response Time | $R_{i,k}$ | $c_{i,k} - r_{i,k}$ | Time units |
| 6 | Processor Utilization | $U_i$ | $e_i / p_i$ | $0 < U_i \leq 1$ |
| 7 | Total Utilization | $U$ | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} (e_i / p_i)$ | $0 < U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ for RMA feasibility |
| 8 | Hyperperiod (LCM) | $H$ | $\text{lcm}(p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$ | Time units |
| 9 | Laxity / Slack | $L_{i,k}$ | $d_{i,k} - c_{i,k}$ | Time units |
| 10 | Density | $\delta_i$ | $e_i / \min(p_i, D_i)$ | Ratio |

---

## 2.4 Utilization Bound Theorem (Liu & Layland — KTU Frequently Tested)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a set of $n$ periodic tasks scheduled under Rate Monotonic Algorithm (RMA):**
> $$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$
> 
> This is a **sufficient (not necessary)** schedulability test. The bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$.

The corresponding **Exact Test (Time Demand Analysis)** is:
$$W_i(t) = e_i + \sum_{j: p_j < t} \left\lceil \frac{t}{p_j} \right\rceil e_j \leq t$$
A task is schedulable if $\exists\, t \in [0, D_i]$ satisfying the inequality.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this is applied in industry:**
> 
> - **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** Engine Control Unit (ECU) runs hundreds of periodic tasks with deadlines in microseconds.
> - **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Flight control software is verified against timing constraints using the same task model.
> - **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Infusion pumps, pacemakers, and MRI machines rely on hard real-time task analysis.
> - **Industrial Robotics:** Sensor-fusion loops with $p = 1\,\text{ms}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Task Parameters to Response Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Show that for a periodic task, the response time is bounded by the deadline only if utilization constraints hold.

### Given:
A periodic task $T_1$ with parameters $\phi_1 = 0$, $p_1 = 10$, $e_1 = 3$, $D_1 = 10$.

### Step 1: List the first three job release times
Since $\phi_i = 0$ and $p_1 = 10$:
$$r_{1,1} = 0, \quad r_{1,2} = 10, \quad r_{1,3} = 20$$

### Step 2: Compute the corresponding absolute deadlines
Using $d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i$ with $D_1 = 10$:
$$d_{1,1} = 10, \quad d_{1,2} = 20, \quad d_{1,3} = 30$$

### Step 3: Compute the response times (assuming no preemption and no other tasks)
Since the task starts immediately at release and takes exactly $e_1 = 3$ units:
$$R_{1,1} = c_{1,1} - r_{1,1} = 3 - 0 = 3$$
$$R_{1,2} = c_{1,2} - r_{1,2} = 13 - 10 = 3$$
$$R_{1,3} = c_{1,3} - r_{1,3} = 23 - 20 = 3$$

### Step 4: Verify feasibility
For all jobs: $R_{1,k} = 3 \leq D_1 = 10$ → **Schedulable**. ✅

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Processor Utilization and Schedulability Bound

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the classic Liu & Layland (1973) derivation appearing in KTU numericals.**

### Given:
A task set with $n = 3$ periodic tasks:
- $T_1 = (p_1 = 4, e_1 = 1)$
- $T_2 = (p_2 = 5, e_2 = 1)$  
- $T_3 = (p_3 = 20, e_3 = 4)$

### Step 1: Compute individual utilizations
$$U_1 = \frac{e_1}{p_1} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250$$
$$U_2 = \frac{e_2}{p_2} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.200$$
$$U_3 = \frac{e_3}{p_3} = \frac{4}{20} = 0.200$$

### Step 2: Compute total utilization
$$U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.250 + 0.200 + 0.200 = 0.650$$

### Step 3: Compute the Liu-Layland bound for $n = 3$
$$U_{bound} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 4: Evaluate $2^{1/3}$ numerically
$$2^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.2599$$

### Step 5: Subtract and multiply
$$2^{1/3} - 1 \approx 0.2599$$
$$U_{bound} = 3 \times 0.2599 \approx 0.7797$$

### Step 6: Compare and conclude
$$U_{total} = 0.650 \leq U_{bound} = 0.7797$$
**Result:** The task set is **schedulable under RMA** by sufficient test. ✅

### Step 7: Hyperperiod for completeness
$$H = \text{lcm}(4, 5, 20) = 20 \text{ time units}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Task Type Comparison

| Feature | Periodic | Aperiodic | Sporadic |
|---------|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Release pattern** | Regular, $T = p$ | Irregular | Irregular + bounded |
| **Bounded inter-arrival** | Yes (exact) | No | Yes (minimum) |
| **WCET known?** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Scheduling approach** | Static (RMA/EDF) | Best-effort / server | Server-based (e.g., Polling, Sporadic Server) |
| **KTU weightage** | High | Medium | High |
| **Example** | ADC sampling | Operator key press | Emergency shutdown |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Preemptive vs. Non-Preemptive Tasks

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse these. KTU questions may ask: *"Can we schedule this non-preemptive task set using RMA?"* — Answer: RMA assumes preemption; non-preemptive scheduling requires different analysis (e.g., blocking time addition).

| Aspect | Preemptive | Non-Preemptive |
|--------|------------|----------------|
| **Job switching** | Allowed at any instant | Forbidden once started |
| **Analysis complexity** | Simpler (RMA, EDF) | Complex (blocking-aware) |
| **Context-switch overhead** | Present | Absent |
| **Real-time guarantee** | Easier | Harder |
| **Example** | Linux PREEMPT_RT, VxWorks | Many bare-metal MCUs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Life Cycle of a Real-Time Job — Mermaid State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Arrived
    Arrived --> Released : release time reached
    Released --> Ready : enqueued in run queue
    Ready --> Running : scheduler dispatches
    Running --> Ready : preempted by higher priority job
    Running --> Completed : execution finished
    Completed --> [*] : before absolute deadline
    Completed --> Missed : after absolute deadline
    Missed --> [*] : deadline miss failure
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Guide for KTU Diagrams:**
> - The **Arrived** state is when the job first becomes known to the OS.
> - The **Released** state is when it is eligible to use the CPU.
> - The transition to **Missed** is the **only** state that constitutes a hard real-time failure.

---

## 4.2 Task Classification Flow — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Time Task] --> B{Known release pattern?}
    B -- Yes fixed period --> C[Periodic Task]
    B -- No --> D{Is minimum interarrival guaranteed?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Sporadic Task]
    D -- No --> F[Aperiodic Task]
    C --> G{Timing tolerance?}
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -- Zero tolerance --> H[Hard Real Time]
    G -- Reduced value --> I[Firm Real Time]
    G -- Degraded performance --> J[Soft Real Time]
```

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Task Scheduling View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[Event Sources]
        S1[Timer Interrupt]
        S2[Sensor Hardware]
        S3[Network Packet]
        S4[User Command]
    end
    
    subgraph KernelLayer[RTOS Kernel]
        Q[Ready Queue]
        SC[Scheduler]
        D[Dispatcher]
    end
    
    subgraph TaskLayer[Task Set]
        T1[Task T1 p=5 e=1]
        T2[Task T2 p=10 e=2]
        T3[Task T3 p=20 e=3]
    end
    
    S1 --> Q
    S2 --> Q
    S3 --> Q
    S4 --> Q
    Q --> SC
    SC --> D
    D --> T1
    D --> T2
    D --> T3
    T1 -. feedback .-> Q
    T2 -. feedback .-> Q
    T3 -. feedback .-> Q
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** Events arrive → Enqueued in the **Ready Queue** → The **Scheduler** (RMA, EDF, etc.) picks the highest-priority ready task → The **Dispatcher** loads it onto the CPU → Upon completion or preemption, the task returns to the queue.

---

## 4.4 Timing Parameters Timeline — Mermaid Gantt-Style View

```mermaid
gantt
    title Real-Time Task Timing Parameters on a Single Job
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    
    section Job J1
    Arrival          :a1, 0, 0
    Release          :a2, 0, 1
    Execution        :a3, 0, 3
    Slack window     :a4, 3, 7
    Deadline         :crit, 10, 0
```

**Reading:** Arrival at $t=0$, execution finishes at $t=3$, slack of 7 time units, absolute deadline at $t=10$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a real-time task. Differentiate between hard and soft real-time tasks with examples.**

#### Model Answer:
A real-time task is a computational job whose correctness depends on both the **logical result** and the **time of delivery** of the result.

| Aspect | Hard Real-Time | Soft Real-Time |
|--------|----------------|----------------|
| Deadline miss | System failure | Performance degrade |
| Acceptable miss ratio | 0% | Small, bounded |
| Example | Airbag controller | Video streaming |
| Design priority | Absolute correctness | Best-effort |

**(3 marks)** ✅

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Explain the difference between periodic, aperiodic, and sporadic tasks.**

#### Model Answer:
- **Periodic tasks** are activated at regular intervals of fixed period $p_i$ (e.g., temperature sensor sampled every 100 ms).
- **Aperiodic tasks** arrive at unpredictable instants with no minimum inter-arrival guarantee (e.g., a user pressing a random key).
- **Sporadic tasks** are a special class of aperiodic tasks with a **bounded minimum inter-arrival time** (e.g., emergency stop button that cannot be pressed more than once every 500 ms), making them analytically tractable.

**(3 marks)** ✅

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every module question offers internal choice. We provide both Option A and Option B below.**

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: (a)** Define the following timing parameters of a real-time task with proper notation: release time, worst-case execution time, relative deadline, absolute deadline, and response time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a periodic task set with the following parameters:

| Task | Period $p_i$ | WCET $e_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ |
|------|--------------|------------|----------------|
| $T_1$ | 4 | 1 | 4 |
| $T_2$ | 6 | 2 | 6 |
| $T_3$ | 12 | 3 | 12 |

**Check the schedulability of the task set using the Liu-Layland utilization bound test. Also compute the hyperperiod.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Definitions:**

- **Release Time ($r_{i,k}$):** The instant at which the $k^{th}$ job of task $T_i$ becomes ready for execution.
- **Worst-Case Execution Time ($e_i$):** The maximum CPU time any single job of $T_i$ may consume under the worst-case input.
- **Relative Deadline ($D_i$):** The duration measured from release time by which the job must complete.
- **Absolute Deadline ($d_{i,k}$):** The actual wall-clock instant by which the job must complete, computed as $d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i$.
- **Response Time ($R_{i,k}$):** The time interval between release and actual completion: $R_{i,k} = c_{i,k} - r_{i,k}$.

**(7 marks)** ✅

**(b) Utilization Test:**

**Step 1:** Compute individual utilizations.
$$U_1 = \frac{1}{4} = 0.250$$
$$U_2 = \frac{2}{6} = 0.333$$
$$U_3 = \frac{3}{12} = 0.250$$

**Step 2:** Total utilization.
$$U_{total} = 0.250 + 0.333 + 0.250 = 0.833$$

**Step 3:** Liu-Layland bound for $n=3$.
$$U_{bound} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7797$$

**Step 4:** Compare.
$$U_{total} = 0.833 > U_{bound} = 0.7797$$

**Result:** The sufficient test **fails**. The bound test is inconclusive — the task set **may or may not be** schedulable. We must apply the **exact time-demand analysis** to confirm.

**Hyperperiod:**
$$H = \text{lcm}(4, 6, 12) = 12 \text{ time units}$$

**(7 marks)** ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students write "Not Schedulable" when $U > U_{bound}$. This is WRONG. The Liu-Layland bound is **sufficient, not necessary**. Failure to meet the bound does not imply failure to schedule.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to reduce $U_2 = 2/6$ to its decimal form before adding. Always show the intermediate fraction-to-decimal step.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Writing $\text{lcm}$ incorrectly as product. The correct value is the **least common multiple**, not the product $4 \times 6 \times 12 = 288$.
> 4. **Mark Loss Zone:** Forgetting to mention that $U_{bound}$ is sufficient-only — this single line is worth **1 mark** in KTU 2024 scheme.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the life cycle of a real-time job. List the possible states. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A periodic real-time task $T_1$ has phase $\phi_1 = 2$, period $p_1 = 6$, WCET $e_1 = 2$, and relative deadline $D_1 = 6$. Plot the arrival times, release times, and absolute deadlines for the first 4 jobs. Verify whether each job meets its deadline under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (assuming no other tasks). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Life Cycle Diagram:**

```
   [Arrival] → [Released] → [Ready] → [Running] → [Completed] → [Exit]
                    ↑           ↓           ↓
                    └────[Preempted]────────┘
                                                  ↓ (if late)
                                              [Deadline Missed]
```

**Possible States:**
1. **Arrived** — job known to system
2. **Released** — job eligible to run
3. **Ready** — in run queue awaiting CPU
4. **Running** — currently executing on CPU
5. **Preempted** — interrupted by higher-priority job
6. **Completed** — execution finished
7. **Deadline Missed** — completion occurred after deadline

**(7 marks)** ✅

**(b) Computation for $T_1$:**

**Step 1:** Arrival times using $a_{i,k} = \phi_i + (k-1) \cdot p_i$.

$$a_{1,1} = 2, \quad a_{1,2} = 8, \quad a_{1,3} = 14, \quad a_{1,4} = 20$$

**Step 2:** Release times (assume $r = a$):
$$r_{1,1} = 2, \quad r_{1,2} = 8, \quad r_{1,3} = 14, \quad r_{1,4} = 20$$

**Step 3:** Absolute deadlines using $d_{i,k} = r_{i,k} + D_i$.

$$d_{1,1} = 8, \quad d_{1,2} = 14, \quad d_{1,3} = 20, \quad d_{1,4} = 26$$

**Step 4:** Completion times (no other tasks, no preemption, starts at release).

$$c_{1,1} = r_{1,1} + e_1 = 2 + 2 = 4$$
$$c_{1,2} = 8 + 2 = 10$$
$$c_{1,3} = 14 + 2 = 16$$
$$c_{1,4} = 20 + 2 = 22$$

**Step 5:** Compare $c_{i,k}$ with $d_{i,k}$.

| Job | Completion | Deadline | Status |
|-----|------------|----------|--------|
| $J_{1,1}$ | 4 | 8 | ✅ Met |
| $J_{1,2}$ | 10 | 14 | ✅ Met |
| $J_{1,3}$ | 16 | 20 | ✅ Met |
| $J_{1,4}$ | 22 | 26 | ✅ Met |

**Result:** All jobs satisfy their deadlines. Task $T_1$ is **schedulable**. ✅

**(7 marks)** ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Confusing *arrival* with *release*. If the question says $r = a$, state so explicitly. If different, show the distinction.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the phase $\phi_i$. Many students start the first job at $t = 0$ instead of $t = \phi_1 = 2$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU 2024 valuations.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Not showing the table of comparison between $c_{i,k}$ and $d_{i,k}$ — examiners want explicit verification for **each job**, not a generic statement.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Before the exam, ensure you can confidently state/explain every point below:**

- [x] A **real-time task** is a job with a **timing constraint** that affects correctness.
- [x] The standard task model is $T_i = (\phi_i, p_i, e_i, D_i)$.
- [x] Three task types: **Periodic** (regular), **Aperiodic** (irregular), **Sporadic** (irregular + bounded).
- [x] Three real-time classes: **Hard** (no misses), **Firm** (useless if late), **Soft** (degraded if late).
- [x] Six timing events per job: arrival, release, start, completion, deadline, response.
- [x] Response time formula: $R_{i,k} = c_{i,k} - r_{i,k}$.
- [x] Feasibility condition: $R_{i,k} \leq D_i$.
- [x] Liu-Layland bound: $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is **sufficient only**, not necessary.
- [x] Utilization bound for large $n$ approaches $\ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$.
- [x] Hyperperiod = $\text{lcm}$ of all task periods.
- [x] Preemptive vs Non-Preemptive distinction: RMA **assumes preemption**.
- [x] Sporadic tasks are scheduled using **server mechanisms** (Polling, Deferrable, Sporadic Server).
- [x] WCET estimation requires **static analysis** + **measurement-based** hybrid approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"P-R-E-D"** = **P**hase, **R**elease, **E**xecution, **D**eadline — the four pillars of every real-time task definition.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timing Constraints and Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Timing Constraints and Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Timing Constraints** in a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) are the strict temporal boundaries (deadlines, release times, periods, and jitter limits) within which the system must produce correct results. A real-time system is one whose **correctness depends not only on the logical output, but also on the time at which the output is delivered.**

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for PERAT757, a real-time system is formally defined as:

> *"A real-time system is one in which the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical result of computation, but also on the time at which the results are produced."* — Stankovic (1988), foundational RTOS definition.

The **timing requirements** specify *what* must happen *when*. They are the temporal specifications that the RTOS scheduler, task designer, and application programmer must jointly satisfy.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **railway level crossing gate**:

- The gate **must** close **exactly 30 seconds** before the train arrives (a *hard deadline*).
- The train arrival announcement (a notification) **should** come within 1–2 seconds, but being 5 seconds late is just *inconvenient* (a *soft deadline*).
- If a digital advertisement board near the station displays the schedule 10 seconds late, nobody really cares (a *firm* or non-critical *timing constraint*).

In a computer system, an **airbag controller** is the level-crossing gate: it MUST deploy within 5 ms of crash detection, or lives are lost. A **video player** is the advertisement board: late frames cause a minor stutter, but the movie is still "correct."

### 1.3 Key Timing Parameters (Bold Standard Metrics)

The standard temporal metrics used in every RTOS textbook and the KTU syllabus are:
