The RTOS-based embedded system is organized into **six logical layers**, from hardware (bottom) to application (top):

| Layer | Name | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| **L0** | **Hardware** | MCU/SoC, sensors, actuators, memory, communication peripherals |
| **L1** | **BSP (Board Support Package)** | Hardware abstraction: boot code, device drivers, pin muxing, clock init |
| **L2** | **RTOS Kernel** | Scheduler, IPC (queues/semaphores/mutexes), memory management, time/tick services |
| **L3** | **API Layer** | POSIX API (for NuttX/QNX) or vendor API (VxWorks API, FreeRTOS API) |
| **L4** | **Middleware** | TCP/IP stack (lwIP, BSD sockets), USB stack, file system (FAT, JFFS2), graphics (LVGL), BLE |
| **L5** | **Application Layer** | Business logic: control loops, state machines, sensor fusion, user interface |

**Task Execution Traversal:**

When an application task (e.g., reading a temperature sensor) executes:

1. **Application (L5)** calls API like `xQueueReceive()` (FreeRTOS) or `read()` (POSIX).
2. **API Layer (L3)** maps the call to RTOS kernel primitives.
3. **RTOS Kernel (L2)** schedules the task, blocks it on a queue/semaphore, or invokes IPC.
4. **BSP (L1)** executes the underlying device driver (I2C read via BSP).
5. **Hardware (L0)** performs the physical I2C transaction to the sensor.
6. Data returns **upward** through the same layers to the application.

The **scheduler** in L2 ensures deterministic task execution using priority-based or round-robin algorithms, while **interrupt service routines (ISRs)** in L1/L2 handle time-critical hardware events.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "RTOS" with "real-time Linux."** Real-time Linux is Linux with the PREEMPT_RT patch; it is still Linux but with reduced latency. A pure Linux kernel is NOT a hard real-time OS.
> 2. **Do NOT say "open source = no cost."** The Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) includes support, training, integration, and certification. Open source shifts cost from *licensing* to *integration effort and risk*.
> 3. **Do NOT use a single example for "all commercial RTOS."** Examiners expect diverse examples across at least two domains (e.g., VxWorks for aerospace, QNX for automotive).
> 4. **Avoid vague claims like "commercial is better" or "open source is better."** Always frame as *"Commercial RTOS is preferred for safety-critical certified domains because of pre-validated certification evidence, whereas open source RTOS is preferred for cost-sensitive high-volume IoT products because of zero licensing cost and full customizability."*
> 5. **Memorize Liu & Layland Utilization Bound** $\left[U = n(2^{1/n} - 1)\right]$ — examiners occasionally ask for the formula and the $\lim_{n \to \infty} U = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ result.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Commercial RTOS** = Proprietary, paid, closed source, certified (e.g., VxWorks, QNX, ThreadX, INTEGRITY, embOS).
- ✅ **Open Source RTOS** = Free, auditable, community-driven, no warranty (e.g., FreeRTOS, Zephyr, eCos, NuttX, RTEMS).
- ✅ **Licensing matters:** Permissive (MIT, BSD, Apache 2.0) vs Copyleft (GPL, LGPL). GPL forces disclosure of derivative works.
- ✅ **Certification** (DO-178C, ISO 26262, IEC 61508, IEC 62304) is the single biggest reason to choose commercial RTOS for safety-critical projects.
- ✅ **TCO** = $C_{license} + C_{support} + C_{training} + C_{certification} + C_{migration} + C_{risk}$ — not just license fee.
- ✅ **Liu & Layland Bound:** $U = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, $\lim_{n \to \infty} U = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- ✅ **Determinism** is measurable via WCET, WCIL, and Jitter — commercial RTOS usually publishes these.
- ✅ **Domain mapping:** Aerospace → VxWorks; Automotive → QNX/AUTOSAR; IoT → FreeRTOS/Zephyr; Drones → NuttX (PX4); Space → VxWorks/RTEMS.
- ✅ **Vendor lock-in** is the main risk of commercial RTOS; **project abandonment** is the main risk of open source RTOS.
- ✅ **Always justify RTOS choice** with three factors: certification need, production volume, and lifetime support requirement.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter-Process Communication (IPC): Shared Memory
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inter-Process Communication (IPC): Shared Memory

# Module 1 — Inter-Process Communication (IPC): Shared Memory

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** is the set of mechanisms provided by the operating system kernel that allows cooperating processes (independent or related) to exchange data and synchronize their execution. IPC mechanisms are broadly classified into **Shared Memory** and **Message Passing**.

**Shared Memory IPC** is a communication paradigm in which two or more processes are given permission to map a common region of physical memory (called a *shared segment* or *shared region*) into each process's own virtual address space. Once mapped, the processes can read from and write to this region as if it were ordinary process-local memory. Since access is mediated by the CPU's normal load/store instructions, shared memory is the **fastest** of all IPC techniques — it bypasses the kernel once the mapping is established.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The shared memory region must be created, attached, and detached using explicit OS primitives (e.g., `shmget`, `shmat`, `shmdt` in System V; `shm_open`, `mmap` in POSIX). Synchronization is **not** provided by the shared memory facility itself — it is the *responsibility of the cooperating processes* (usually via semaphores or mutexes).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> **Analogy — The Office Whiteboard:**
> Imagine two engineers, *Alice* and *Bob*, working in separate rooms. To collaborate, the manager installs a **large whiteboard in the corridor** that both can see and write on. This whiteboard is the *shared memory region*.
> - The **whiteboard** = shared memory segment
> - **Alice & Bob** = cooperating processes
> - **Corridor** = OS kernel / physical RAM
> - The **rule "only one may write at a time"** = the need for synchronization (semaphores)
>
> Without the rule, Alice and Bob may try to write simultaneously, scribbling over each other — this is a *race condition*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Process** | An executing program with its own virtual address space. |
| **Critical Section** | Code segment that accesses shared resources. |
| **Race Condition** | Outcome depends on unpredictable execution ordering of concurrent processes. |
| **Semaphore** | Integer variable accessed only via atomic `wait()` and `signal()`. |
| **Mutex** | Binary semaphore providing mutual exclusion. |
| **Shared Segment** | A region of physical RAM mapped into multiple processes. |
| **Page Table** | Per-process translation table from virtual → physical addresses. |

> [!TIP]
> In **RTOS contexts** (e.g., VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS), shared memory is the *de-facto* IPC mechanism because message passing and context switches add non-deterministic latency, which violates hard real-time deadlines.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Model of Shared Memory IPC

A shared memory IPC system consists of five logical layers, all of which KTU expects you to know:

1. **Shared Segment Creation** — A process (or kernel on its behalf) requests a region of physical memory to be designated as shared. A unique *key* (e.g., `0x1234` in System V) identifies the segment.
2. **Attachment (Mapping)** — Each cooperating process invokes a system call to *attach* the segment, causing the kernel to update the process's **page table** so that the segment's physical pages become accessible via the process's virtual address space.
3. **Access (Read/Write)** — Processes now use ordinary load (`MOV`) and store instructions. No kernel intervention is required per access.
4. **Synchronization** — Before reading or writing, processes use *synchronization primitives* (semaphores, mutexes, spinlocks, or event flags in RTOS) to prevent race conditions.
5. **Detachment & Deletion** — When finished, processes detach the segment, and the creator marks it for deletion. The kernel reclaims physical pages when the reference count reaches zero.

### 2.2 Why Shared Memory is the Fastest IPC

- **Message passing** requires at least *two* kernel crossings (sender → kernel → receiver). Each crossing involves mode switch (user → kernel), copying data into kernel buffers, scheduling, and another mode switch.
- **Shared memory** requires *one-time* kernel involvement to set up the mapping. After that, the data is already in user-accessible memory.
- The theoretical throughput advantage is bounded by the **memory bus bandwidth** and **cache coherence protocol**, not by software overhead.

### 2.3 Why Synchronization Cannot Be Avoided

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often state that "shared memory itself synchronizes access." This is **false**. Shared memory provides *no* built-in mutual exclusion. The operating system guarantees that the memory exists and is mapped — nothing more.

A **race condition** arises when the final state of the shared data depends on the interleaving of read/write operations. Consider two processes both incrementing a shared counter `count`:

| Step | Process P1 | Process P2 | Shared `count` |
|------|------------|------------|----------------|
| 1 | `MOV AX, [count]` (reads 5) | — | 5 |
| 2 | — | `MOV BX, [count]` (reads 5) | 5 |
| 3 | `ADD AX, 1` (AX=6) | — | 5 |
| 4 | — | `ADD BX, 1` (BX=6) | 5 |
| 5 | `MOV [count], AX` | — | 6 |
| 6 | — | `MOV [count], BX` | 6 |

Despite two increments, `count` went from 5 to 6 — **lost update**. This is the classic *TOCTTOU* (time-of-check-to-time-of-use) bug at the hardware level.

### 2.4 Shared Memory Address Translation Mechanics

When a process attaches a shared segment, the kernel inserts entries into the process's **page table** mapping virtual page numbers to the same physical frame numbers used by other attached processes. The following schematic represents one physical page being mapped by two processes:

| Process | Virtual Page # | Physical Frame # | Access Rights |
|---------|----------------|------------------|---------------|
| P1 | VP 12 | PF 581 | R/W |
| P2 | VP 47 | PF 581 | R/W |
| P3 | VP 03 | PF 581 | R/O |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Effective access time via TLB hit | $t_{\text{eff}} = h \cdot t_{TLB} + (1-h)(t_{TLB} + t_{MEM})$ | $h$ = TLB hit ratio, $t_{TLB}$ = TLB lookup, $t_{MEM}$ = memory access |
| 2 | No. of cooperating processes | $n \geq 2$ | At least two processes must map the same region. |
| 3 | Critical section condition (mutual exclusion) | $\forall i,\; CS_i \cap CS_j = \emptyset$ | No two processes can be inside CS simultaneously. |
| 4 | Semaphore invariant | $s \in \mathbb{Z},\; s \leq 1$ for binary | $s$ initialized to 1 for mutex. |
| 5 | Page table entries per process for shared region | $E = \lceil S / P \rceil$ | $S$ = segment size, $P$ = page size. |
| 6 | Shared memory latency advantage | $T_{SM} \ll 2 \cdot T_{\text{mode-switch}} + T_{copy}$ | Bypasses kernel data copying. |
| 7 | Reference count for segment lifetime | $RC = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{attach}_i$ | Segment destroyed only when $RC = 0$. |
| 8 | Effective throughput of shared memory | $\Theta_{\text{SM}} \approx \dfrac{B_{\text{bus}}}{L_{\text{cache-line}}}$ | Limited by hardware bandwidth. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical pipe substitution rule applied:** All absolute-value and conditional notations use `\vert` instead of `$\vert$` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database engines** (Oracle SGA, PostgreSQL shared buffers) use shared memory for inter-process cache sharing.
- **Multimedia frameworks** (GStreamer, DirectX shared surfaces) use shared memory for zero-copy video frame passing between decoder and renderer processes.
- **RTOS automotive stacks** (AUTOSAR, ROS 2 with `iceoryx`) use shared memory for low-latency sensor-data sharing between ADAS modules.
- **High-frequency trading** platforms use shared memory to communicate between matching engine and order gateway with sub-microsecond latency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step: Complete Shared Memory Lifecycle (POSIX Style)

We will walk through every step a process performs to set up and use a shared memory region. The same logical flow applies in System V (with renamed APIs).

**Step 0 — Process A creates the shared object.**

The creator process opens (or creates) a POSIX shared memory object with a name like `/my_region`. The system call returns a *file descriptor* $fd$.

**Step 1 — Process A sets the size of the shared object.**

$$
\text{size} = f(\text{ftruncate}(fd, S)) \;\Rightarrow\; S \text{ bytes reserved by kernel}
$$

The kernel now reserves $S$ bytes of physical memory, which may be paged in lazily on first access.

**Step 2 — Process A maps the object into its address space.**

$$
\text{addr}_A = \text{mmap}(\text{NULL}, S, \text{PROT\_READ} \vert \text{PROT\_WRITE}, \text{MAP\_SHARED}, fd, 0)
$$

The kernel inserts page table entries for Process A such that virtual addresses $[\text{addr}_A,\; \text{addr}_A + S)$ map to the shared physical pages.

**Step 3 — Process A initializes a synchronization primitive.**

A named semaphore `/my_sem` is created with initial value 1 (binary semaphore / mutex).

$$
\text{sem\_init}(\text{sem}, 1, 1)
$$

**Step 4 — Process B opens the same shared object and semaphore.**

Process B (perhaps a child fork or a separate launched binary) calls:

$$
fd_B = \text{shm\_open}("/my\_region", \text{O\_RDWR}, 0)
$$

and maps it:

$$
\text{addr}_B = \text{mmap}(\text{NULL}, S, \text{PROT\_READ} \vert \text{PROT\_WRITE}, \text{MAP\_SHARED}, fd_B, 0)
$$

**Step 5 — Both processes now access the same physical memory.**

A write by Process A at virtual address $\text{addr}_A + k$ updates the same physical page accessed by Process B at $\text{addr}_B + k$. The kernel is *not* involved.

**Step 6 — Process A writes data under semaphore protection.**

The pseudocode operation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{sem\_wait}(\text{sem}) \quad &&\text{// atomic decrement; blocks if sem}=0 \\
&\text{memcpy}(\text{addr}_A, \text{data}, L) \quad &&\text{// critical section} \\
&\text{sem\_post}(\text{sem}) \quad &&\text{// atomic increment}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Process B reads data under semaphore protection.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{sem\_wait}(\text{sem}) \\
&\text{memcpy}(\text{buffer}_B, \text{addr}_B, L) \\
&\text{sem\_post}(\text{sem})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Detachment.**

Each process calls $\text{munmap}(\text{addr}, S)$ to remove the page table entries.

**Step 9 — Deletion.**

The creator calls $\text{shm\_unlink}("/my\_region")$ and $\text{sem\_close} / \text{sem\_unlink}$. The kernel reclaims the physical pages when the reference count drops to zero.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step: Page Table Mapping Calculation

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A shared segment of size $S = 64 \text{ KB}$ is to be mapped into 4 processes on a system with page size $P = 4 \text{ KB}$. Compute the number of page table entries per process and the total physical memory consumed (excluding kernel overhead).

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of pages} &= \left\lceil \frac{S}{P} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil \frac{64 \text{ KB}}{4 \text{ KB}} \right\rceil \\
&= \left\lceil 16 \right\rceil = 16 \text{ pages}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each process requires 16 page table entries pointing to the same 16 physical frames.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Physical memory consumed} &= 16 \text{ frames} \times 4096 \text{ bytes/frame} \\
&= 65\,536 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 64 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total *virtual* memory reserved across all four processes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{total}} &= n \times S \\
&= 4 \times 64 \text{ KB} \\
&= 256 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, shared memory gives an **address-space amplification factor** of $n$, but a **physical-memory saving factor** of $n$ as well (compared to private copies of the same data).

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Solving the Producer–Consumer Race Condition

Consider a shared integer `buffer` and two cooperating processes: a *producer* and a *consumer*.

**Without semaphore (incorrect):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Producer: } R_1 = \text{buffer}; \quad R_1 = R_1 + 1; \quad \text{buffer} = R_1 \\
&\text{Consumer: } R_2 = \text{buffer}; \quad R_2 = R_2 - 1; \quad \text{buffer} = R_2
\end{aligned}
$$

If the producer executes $R_1 = \text{buffer}$ (read 5) and is preempted before writing back, and then the consumer runs $R_2 = \text{buffer}$ (also reads 5), both will write back 6 and 4 respectively. Final value is unpredictable.

**With semaphore (correct):**

Let $S$ be a binary semaphore initialized to 1.

Producer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{wait}(S) \quad &&\text{// acquire lock} \\
&R_1 = \text{buffer} \\
&R_1 = R_1 + 1 \\
&\text{buffer} = R_1 \\
&\text{signal}(S) \quad &&\text{// release lock}
\end{aligned}
$$

Consumer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{wait}(S) \\
&R_2 = \text{buffer} \\
&R_2 = R_2 - 1 \\
&\text{buffer} = R_2 \\
&\text{signal}(S)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, regardless of scheduling, only one process can be in its critical section at a time, guaranteeing atomicity of the read-modify-write.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of IPC Mechanisms

| Criterion | Shared Memory | Message Passing (Pipe) | Message Passing (Socket) | Message Queue |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Speed | **Fastest** | Moderate | Moderate–Slow | Moderate |
| Kernel calls per data unit | 0 (after setup) | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| Synchronization built-in | **No** | Implicit (blocking) | Implicit | Implicit |
| Suitable for RTOS | **Yes** | Limited | No | No |
| Address-space coupling | Coupled | Decoupled | Decoupled | Decoupled |
| Data size | Arbitrary (limited by RAM) | Stream (any size) | Stream/Datagram | Limited |
| Complexity of setup | High | Low | Moderate | Moderate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Shared Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P1[Process P1]
        P1VA[Virtual Address Space P1]
    end
    subgraph P2[Process P2]
        P2VA[Virtual Address Space P2]
    end
    subgraph P3[Process P3]
        P3VA[Virtual Address Space P3]
    end
    subgraph KERN[Kernel Space]
        SHMREG[Shared Memory Region]
        PT1[Page Table P1]
        PT2[Page Table P2]
        PT3[Page Table P3]
        SEM[Semaphore Manager]
    end
    P1VA --> PT1 --> SHMREG
    P2VA --> PT2 --> SHMREG
    P3VA --> PT3 --> SHMREG
    P1VA -. acquire .-> SEM
    P2VA -. release .-> SEM
    P3VA -. query .-> SEM
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Flow of Shared Memory IPC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process A creates shared segment shmget] --> B[Kernel allocates physical pages]
    B --> C[Process A attaches via mmap]
    C --> D[Process A initializes semaphore to 1]
    D --> E[Process B opens same segment]
    E --> F[Process B attaches via mmap]
    F --> G{Process A or B wants access}
    G -->|wait| H[Semaphore decrements atomically]
    H --> I[Process enters critical section]
    I --> J[Read or write shared data]
    J --> K[signal semaphore]
    K --> G
    G -->|cleanup| L[Both processes munmap]
    L --> M[Creator calls shm_unlink]
    M --> N[Reference count RC equals 0]
    N --> O[Kernel reclaims physical memory]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Producer–Consumer with Semaphore Protection

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Prod as Producer Process
    participant Sem as Binary Semaphore S
    participant Shm as Shared Counter C
    participant Cons as Consumer Process

    Note over Sem: S initialized to 1
    Prod->>Sem: wait(S)
    activate Sem
    Sem-->>Prod: S becomes 0, lock acquired
    Prod->>Shm: read C
    Prod->>Shm: write C+1
    Prod->>Sem: signal(S)
    deactivate Sem
    Sem-->>Cons: S becomes 1, lock released
    activate Sem
    Cons->>Sem: wait(S)
    Sem-->>Cons: S becomes 0, lock acquired
    Cons->>Shm: read C
    Cons->>Shm: write C-1
    Cons->>Sem: signal(S)
    deactivate Sem
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Page Table Mapping for Shared Region

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VA1[P1 Virtual Address Space]
        VA1A[VP 12]
        VA1B[VP 13]
        VA1C[VP 14]
    end
    subgraph VA2[P2 Virtual Address Space]
        VA2A[VP 47]
        VA2B[VP 48]
        VA2C[VP 49]
    end
    subgraph PHYS[Physical Memory]
        PF1[Frame 581]
        PF2[Frame 582]
        PF3[Frame 583]
    end
    VA1A --> PF1
    VA1B --> PF2
    VA1C --> PF3
    VA2A --> PF1
    VA2B --> PF2
    VA2C --> PF3
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define Inter-Process Communication (IPC). List any four IPC mechanisms supported by a general-purpose OS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

IPC is a set of operating system facilities that allow processes to communicate and synchronize their actions. Common IPC mechanisms include:

1. **Shared Memory** — multiple processes map a common memory region.
2. **Message Passing** — processes exchange formatted messages via the kernel.
3. **Pipes (named and unnamed)** — byte-stream channels between related processes.
4. **Sockets** — network-capable bidirectional endpoints.
5. **Signals** — asynchronous notifications (also accepted).

> **Mark distribution (KTU typical):** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing four mechanisms.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
**Why is shared memory considered the fastest IPC mechanism? Mention one drawback.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Shared memory is the fastest IPC because, **after the initial kernel setup** (creating and attaching the segment), all subsequent data transfers occur using ordinary CPU load/store instructions — no kernel mode switch or data copying is required per access.

**Drawback:** The cooperating processes must explicitly implement **synchronization** (e.g., using semaphores or mutexes). Failure to do so results in **race conditions** and data corruption. Shared memory also tightly couples the participating processes' address spaces, which complicates porting and security boundaries.

> **Mark distribution:** 2 marks for speed explanation, 1 mark for drawback.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not** describe shared memory as a "synchronized" facility — synchronization is *not* built into shared memory itself.
> 2. **Always** draw a labelled diagram showing virtual-to-physical mapping across multiple processes. Examiners award at least **2–3 marks** for the diagram alone.
> 3. **Do not** confuse the System V API (`shmget`, `shmat`) with POSIX API (`shm_open`, `mmap`). KTU questions may specify one or the other; mixing them is a common error.
> 4. **Always** mention the role of the **reference count** when explaining segment lifetime.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **shared memory model of Inter-Process Communication**. Discuss how processes establish, use, and tear down a shared region. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 mark):** Shared memory IPC is a technique in which two or more processes map a common region of physical memory into their respective virtual address spaces, enabling fast data exchange through ordinary memory read/write operations.

**2. Architecture (2 marks):**

A shared region is a portion of physical RAM. Each participating process has its own page table in which entries are added by the kernel to map the process's chosen virtual addresses onto the shared physical frames. Because the same physical frames are mapped by multiple processes, a write by one is immediately visible to the others (subject to CPU cache coherence).

**3. Lifecycle (3 marks):**

- **Creation:** The creator invokes a kernel primitive (e.g., `shmget` in System V or `shm_open`+`ftruncate` in POSIX) to allocate a new shared segment identified by a key or name.
- **Attachment:** Each process calls `shmat` (System V) or `mmap` (POSIX) to map the segment. The kernel inserts page-table entries.
- **Access:** Processes use `memcpy` or direct pointer access. No kernel involvement per access.
- **Synchronization:** The processes use semaphores (e.g., `sem_wait` / `sem_post`) to protect critical sections.
- **Detachment:** `shmdt` or `munmap` removes the page-table entries from a process.
- **Deletion:** The creator marks the segment for deletion (`shmctl(...,IPC_RMID,...)` or `shm_unlink`). The kernel reclaims physical pages when the reference count reaches zero.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram — three processes' virtual spaces mapping to one shared region via their page tables.]

**(b)** Explain **why synchronization is required** in shared memory IPC. With a suitable example, demonstrate how the **producer–consumer problem** can be solved using **binary semaphores**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Need for synchronization (2 marks):** Although shared memory enables fast access, the underlying CPU instructions (load, store, increment) are not atomic when multiple instructions are required. Two processes can interleave their read-modify-write sequences, producing inconsistent results (race condition).

**2. Semaphore definition (1 mark):** A semaphore is an integer $S$ accessed only through the atomic operations `wait(S)` (also called `P(S)`) and `signal(S)` (also called `V(S)`).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{wait}(S):&\quad \text{while } S \le 0 \text{ do block}; \quad S \leftarrow S - 1 \\
\text{signal}(S):&\quad S \leftarrow S + 1; \quad \text{wakeup one waiting process}
\end{aligned}
$$

**3. Solution (3 marks):**

- Initialize a binary semaphore `mutex = 1`.
- Producer: `wait(mutex); write to shared buffer; signal(mutex);`
- Consumer: `wait(mutex); read from shared buffer; signal(mutex);`
- Mutual exclusion guarantees that the buffer is accessed atomically.

**4. Diagram (1 mark):** [Refer to Section 4.3 sequence diagram — producer and consumer sequences showing semaphore state transitions.]

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Compare **shared memory IPC** and **message passing IPC** under the following criteria: speed, kernel involvement, synchronization, ease of programming, and suitability for real-time systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Shared Memory | Message Passing |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Speed** | Fastest — no kernel per access | Slower — at least 2 kernel calls per message |
| **Kernel Involvement** | Only at create/attach/detach | Every send/receive |
| **Synchronization** | Must be programmed explicitly | Often implicit (blocking) |
| **Ease of Programming** | More complex (sync code) | Simpler (kernel handles) |
| **RTOS Suitability** | Excellent (deterministic) | Poor (variable latency) |

> **Mark split (KTU style):** 1 mark per row × 5 = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for a brief explanatory paragraph.

**(b)** With a step-by-step example, illustrate the use of the **POSIX shared memory API** for inter-process communication. Show the role of `shm_open`, `ftruncate`, `mmap`, and `munmap`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Producer process (writer) (3 marks):**

The producer creates the shared object, sets its size, maps it, writes data, and unmaps.

Conceptual steps:

- Call `shm_open("/myshm", O_CREAT \vert O_RDWR, 0666)` → returns descriptor `fd`.
- Call `ftruncate(fd, 4096)` → reserves 4 KB.
- Call `mmap(NULL, 4096, PROT_READ \vert PROT_WRITE, MAP_SHARED, fd, 0)` → returns `ptr`.
- Write: `sprintf(ptr, "Hello from Producer");`.
- Call `munmap(ptr, 4096)` and `close(fd)`.

**2. Consumer process (reader) (3 marks):**

- Call `shm_open("/myshm", O_RDWR, 0)` → returns `fd`.
- Call `mmap(NULL, 4096, PROT_READ, MAP_SHARED, fd, 0)` → returns `ptr`.
- Read: `printf("Consumer received: %s\n", ptr);`.
- Call `munmap(ptr, 4096)`, `close(fd)`, and `shm_unlink("/myshm")`.

**3. Synchronization mention (1 mark):** A POSIX named semaphore (`sem_open("/mysem", ...)`) is used to coordinate write/read access, since the API itself provides no locking.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (memorize before exam):**

- **Shared memory = fastest IPC** because data transfer avoids kernel crossings after the initial setup.
- **Three mandatory phases:** (1) *Create* the segment, (2) *Attach* via `mmap`/`shmat`, (3) *Synchronize* via semaphores/mutexes.
- **Synchronization is the programmer's responsibility** — shared memory provides *no* built-in locking.
- **Race condition** arises when two or more processes access shared data concurrently and at least one access is a write.
- **Critical section** must satisfy *mutual exclusion, progress, bounded waiting*.
- **System V APIs:** `shmget`, `shmat`, `shmdt`, `shmctl(IPC_RMID)`.
- **POSIX APIs:** `shm_open`, `ftruncate`, `mmap`, `munmap`, `shm_unlink`.
- **Semaphore operations:** `wait` (P) decrements and blocks if ≤ 0; `signal` (V) increments and wakes a waiter.
- **Reference count** governs segment lifetime — physical pages freed only when $RC = 0$.
- **Page table entries per process** for a shared region of size $S$ with page size $P$ is $\lceil S/P \rceil$.
- **RTOS context:** shared memory is preferred for *deterministic* latency; message passing is *non-deterministic* due to scheduler-dependent blocking.
- **Common exam keywords to use:** "critical section," "race condition," "mutual exclusion," "atomic," "kernel crossing," "cache coherence," "deterministic latency."

> **Closing Tip:** When answering a 14-mark question, always structure your answer as **Definition → Diagram → Step-by-Step → Synchronization → Example → Comparison (if asked)**. This template matches KTU's marking scheme precisely.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Passing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Message Passing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Message Passing** is an inter-process communication (IPC) and inter-task synchronization mechanism in a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) in which cooperating tasks exchange information through the explicit transmission and reception of discrete data structures called **messages**, without sharing any mutable memory address space. It is the cornerstone of distributed and loosely-coupled real-time architectures (e.g., VxWorks, FreeRTOS with queue APIs, RTEMS, and QNX message-passing kernels).

In a hard real-time system, message passing is preferred over shared-memory techniques because it **enforces temporal determinism, eliminates race conditions by construction, and provides a clean, verifiable interface for worst-case execution time (WCET) analysis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A *message* is a typed, fixed-or-variable length data envelope containing a payload and a routing identifier, while a *port* (or *mailbox*) is the kernel-managed logical endpoint through which the message is delivered.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **registered postal service** between two office branches:
- **Task A** (Sender) writes a letter, places it inside an envelope, and drops it into the **outgoing mailbox (send port)**.
- The **Postal Department (RTOS Kernel)** sorts and delivers it to the **incoming mailbox (receive port)** of **Task B**.
- **Task B** opens the envelope at its own pace and reads the content.

Crucially, the two branches **never share a desk or drawer** (no shared memory). The post office guarantees **delivery, ordering, and (in synchronous mode) confirmation of receipt**. This is precisely how message passing works in an RTOS — the kernel acts as a *trusted courier* enforcing deterministic hand-off.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Used by KTU Examiners

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Vocabulary You Must Use in Answers:**
> - **Sender Task** — the task invoking the `send()` primitive.
> - **Receiver Task** — the task invoking the `receive()` primitive.
> - **Mailbox / Port / Message Queue** — the kernel object holding messages.
> - **Blocking Send** — sender suspends until the message is accepted/buffered.
> - **Non-Blocking Send** — sender continues regardless of delivery state.
> - **Rendezvous** — synchronous hand-off where send and receive meet in kernel time.
> - **Zero-Copy Messaging** — payload pointer is passed by reference (critical in AUTOSAR, ROS 2, and QNX).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Message Passing (KTU Most-Asked Framework)

Message passing is classified along **two orthogonal axes**:

#### Axis 1 — Naming (Addressing)
1. **Direct Message Passing**
   - Tasks name each other explicitly: `send(P, msg)` and `receive(Q, msg)`.
   - Used in tightly coupled static systems.
   - **Drawback:** High coupling, hard to reconfigure.

2. **Indirect Message Passing**
   - Communication occurs through a **shared mailbox / port** (kernel object).
   - Producer–consumer decoupling; multiple senders/receivers possible.
   - **Industry standard** (e.g., POSIX message queues, VxWorks `msgQLib`).

#### Axis 2 — Synchronization
1. **Synchronous (Blocking / Rendezvous)**
   - `send()` blocks until the receiver executes `receive()`.
   - Provides **strong causal ordering** and tight hand-shaking.
   - Used in **hard real-time control loops** (e.g., flight controller torque request).

2. **Asynchronous (Non-Blocking)**
   - `send()` returns immediately; the kernel buffers the message.
   - Receiver may block on empty queue.
   - Risk: **unbounded buffering → memory exhaustion**.

3. **Asynchronous with Blocking Send on Full Queue**
   - Bounded queue length $N$. If full, sender blocks.
   - Most common in commercial RTOS kernels.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Operational Logic Steps

**Step 1 — Mailbox Creation:** A task (typically the initializer) requests a mailbox via `MBoxCreate()` or `msgQCreate(N, maxsize)`. The kernel allocates a **First-In-First-Out (FIFO) circular buffer** of capacity $N$.

**Step 2 — Send Operation:** The sender copies (or zero-copies) the payload into the mailbox. Kernel checks the count. If < $N$, it enqueues and either wakes a blocked receiver or returns success immediately.

**Step 3 — Receive Operation:** The receiver dequeues the oldest message. If the queue is empty, the receiver is **suspended on a wait list** and is awakened upon the next successful `send()`.

**Step 4 — Timeout / Error Handling:** Real-time primitives must return bounded time. Kernels offer `send(msg, timeout)` and `receive(msg, timeout)` with timeouts $T_{timeout}$ in ticks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Determinism Guarantee:** Each primitive must execute in **O(1) time** for hard real-time. FreeRTOS and VxWorks queue APIs satisfy this; variable-length message copying is the only $O(L)$ component (where $L$ is payload length in bytes).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for all absolute-value expressions in KTU answer scripts to avoid LaTeX table breakage.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---------|----------------------|-----------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | Message Delivery Latency (Blocking Send) | $T_{latency} = T_{send} + T_{sched} + T_{copy} + T_{recv}$ | $T_{send}$: send primitive cost, $T_{sched}$: dispatcher, $T_{copy}$: copy time, $T_{recv}$: receive cost | µs / ticks | Worst-case path analysis |
| 2 | Queue Saturation Bound | $N \ge \lambda_{max} \cdot W_{max}$ | $\lambda_{max}$: peak arrival rate, $W_{max}$: max blocking time of consumer | messages | Buffer sizing |
| 3 | Throughput of Mailbox | $\Theta = \dfrac{1}{T_{send} + T_{recv}}$ | cost per message | msg/s | Channel capacity |
| 4 | Effective Bandwidth | $B = \dfrac{L \cdot \Theta}{8}$ | $L$: payload size in bits | bits/s | Sensor data pipes |
| 5 | Utilization (Hard RT) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1$ | $C_i$: WCET of task $i$, $T_i$: period | dimensionless | Schedulability test |
| 6 | Zero-Copy Savings | $S = 1 - \dfrac{T_{ptr}}{T_{copy}} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert p \vert}{L}$ | $T_{ptr}$: pointer-pass time, $\vert p \vert$: pointer size, $L$: payload | ratio | Embedded optimization |
| 7 | Priority Inversion Bound (PCP) | $B_{inv} \le \sum_{k \in \text{medium}} (T_{max,k})$ | $T_{max,k}$: max critical section of medium-priority task | ticks | Mars Pathfinder style analysis |
| 8 | Blocking Time on Empty Q | $T_{block} = T_{prod} - (q-1)\cdot T_{cons}$ | $q$: current queue length | ticks | Receiver analysis |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Message passing underpins the architecture of:
- **AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive** (automotive ECUs) — COM and SOME/IP layers.
- **ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2)** — DDS publish/subscribe over message passing.
- **ARINC 653 (Avionics)** — Inter-Partition Communication via messages.
- **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** — Mission-critical defense systems.
- **FreeRTOS `xQueueSend` / `xQueueReceive`** — industrial IoT firmware.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Worst-Case Latency of a Synchronous Message Hand-Off

We model the message path as a sequence of four deterministic events executed under a fixed-priority preemptive scheduler.

**Step 1 — Define the time origin.** Let $t=0$ be the instant the sender task issues the `send()` system call.

**Step 2 — Sender Blocking Decision.** Because the primitive is synchronous, the sender immediately suspends. The kernel places it in the *waiting-for-receiver* state. Cost incurred: $T_{send}$ (entry/exit of syscall plus parameter validation).

**Step 3 — Scheduler Dispatch to Receiver.** The kernel dispatcher selects the highest-priority *runnable* task. If the receiver is already runnable, the dispatch cost is $T_{sched}$. If it was previously preempted by a higher-priority task, the worst-case delay becomes $T_{sched} + T_{hp}$, where $T_{hp}$ is the WCET of the intervening task.

**Step 4 — Payload Transfer (Copy Mode).** For a payload of length $L$ bytes transferred across a bus of bandwidth $B_{bus}$ bytes/tick, the copy time is:

$$
T_{copy} = \frac{L}{B_{bus}}
$$

**Step 5 — Receiver Side Hand-shake.** The receiver kernel-level action costs $T_{recv}$.

**Step 6 — Assemble the Latency Equation.** Summing all components yields:

$$
T_{latency} = T_{send} + (T_{sched} + T_{hp}) + \frac{L}{B_{bus}} + T_{recv}
$$

**Step 7 — Hard Real-Time Upper Bound.** To make the bound enforceable, the examiner expects you to identify the *worst-case* $T_{hp}$ across all higher-priority tasks in the system. The final expression used in KTU answers is:

$$
T_{latency}^{WCRT} = T_{send} + T_{sched} + \sum_{j \in HP(i)} C_j + \frac{L}{B_{bus}} + T_{recv}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note the use of `\sum_{j \in HP(i)}` (Roman *HP* set notation) instead of pipes — KTU answer evaluators mark this precision highly.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Mailbox Depth to Avoid Sender Blocking

Consider a producer task $P$ with inter-arrival time $T_P$ and a consumer task $C$ with WCET $C_C$ per message. The arrival rate of messages is $\lambda = 1/T_P$. The service rate is $\mu = 1/C_C$.

**Step 1 — Stability Condition.** A queue is bounded and stable iff $\lambda < \mu$, i.e.:

$$
\frac{1}{T_P} < \frac{1}{C_C} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad T_P > C_C
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Backlog.** Using Little's Law applied to the queue:

$$
\bar{q} = \lambda \cdot \bar{W}
$$

For the **worst-case** backlog over a busy period of length $W_{max}$, the required mailbox capacity is:

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \lambda \cdot W_{max} \right\rceil
$$

Substituting $\lambda = 1/T_P$:

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{W_{max}}{T_P} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 3 — Practical Substitution.** In embedded systems, the busy period is often bounded by the longest non-preemptable region of the consumer, so $W_{max} = C_C$ (one execution). Substituting:

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{C_C}{T_P} \right\rceil
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** If the consumer can process at most one message per period, and the producer issues one message per period, then $N_{min} = 1$, which is the **rendezvous** case.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequent KTU 3-Mark Comparison Question)

| Parameter | Shared Memory | Direct Msg Pass | Indirect Msg Pass (Mailbox) |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|----------------------------|
| Coupling | Tight (data races) | Tight (named tasks) | Loose (decoupled) |
| Determinism | Low (locks needed) | High | High (with bounded Q) |
| Memory Cost | Lowest | Zero overhead | $N \cdot L$ bytes |
| Kernel Cost | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Use Case | DSP cores | Static control | Distributed sensors |
| WCET Verifiable | Hard | Easy | Easy |

| Parameter | Synchronous | Asynchronous Non-Blocking | Asynchronous Blocking-on-Full |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Sender Behavior | Blocks until recv | Always returns | Blocks only if Q full |
| Memory Bound | None (0 buffer) | Unbounded risk | Bounded by $N$ |
| Latency | Highest | Lowest | Medium |
| Predictability | Highest | Lowest | Acceptable |
| Typical Use | Control loops | Logging | Industrial control |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Direct vs Indirect Message Passing — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1[Task P Sender] -- send msg direct --> nodeK1[RTOS Kernel]
    nodeK1 -- deliver by name Q --> nodeT2[Task Q Receiver]
    nodeT3[Task R Other] -. cannot reach .-> nodeT1
    nodeT3 -. cannot reach .-> nodeT2
```

In **direct** passing, the kernel uses the **explicit task name** as the routing key. A third task R cannot intercept the message.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Producer Task P] -- send to MBox --> nodeMB[Kernel Mailbox M]
    nodeS2[Producer Task S] -- send to MBox --> nodeMB
    nodeMB -- deliver FIFO --> nodeR1[Consumer Task C1]
    nodeMB -- deliver FIFO --> nodeR2[Consumer Task C2]
```

In **indirect** passing, the **mailbox** is a shared kernel object. Multiple producers can enqueue; one (or several) consumers dequeue under FIFO discipline.

### 4.2 Synchronous Rendezvous State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state1[Task A Running] --> state2[Task A send called]
    state2 --> state3[Task A Blocked Waiting]
    state3 --> state4[Task B receive called]
    state4 --> state5[Kernel Hand Off msg]
    state5 --> state6[Task A Ready]
    state5 --> state7[Task B Running]
    state6 --> state1
    state7 --> state8[Task B Blocked on Empty Q]
    state8 --> state2
```

This state machine illustrates the classic **CSP-style rendezvous** by C. A. R. Hoare, the theoretical foundation of message passing in real-time kernels such as **QNX (PPS)** and **VxWorks (select-based rendezvous)**.

### 4.3 Mailbox Internal Architecture (Bounded FIFO Buffer)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph KERNEL[RTOS KERNEL ADDRESS SPACE]
        nodeM0[Slot 0 Oldest]
        nodeM1[Slot 1]
        nodeM2[Slot 2]
        nodeMN[Slot N minus 1 Newest]
        nodeM0 --> nodeM1
        nodeM1 --> nodeM2
        nodeM2 --> nodeMN
        nodeHEAD[HEAD Pointer] --> nodeM0
        nodeTAIL[TAIL Pointer] --> nodeMN
        nodeCOUNT[Count and Max N] -. tracks .-> KERNEL
    end
    nodePROD[Producer Task] -- enqueue at TAIL --> nodeMN
    nodeCONS[Consumer Task] -- dequeue at HEAD --> nodeM0
```

The **HEAD** pointer advances on dequeue, **TAIL** on enqueue; both wrap modulo $N$ to form a **circular buffer**. This is the exact data structure used in `xQueueGenericSend()` of FreeRTOS and `msgQSend()` of VxWorks.

### 4.4 Zero-Copy Message Passing (Pointer Hand-Off)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeZ1[Sender Task Allocator] -- pointer p msg --> nodeZP[Kernel passes p only]
    nodeZP -- pointer p only --> nodeZ2[Receiver Task Reads In Place]
    nodeZ3[No byte by byte copy occurs]
```

In **zero-copy** mode (used by QNX *pulse/msg passing* and Linux *kdbus* style), the kernel transfers only a **pointer** and a small header. The payload stays in the sender's address space, mapped read-only into the receiver. This satisfies the **O(1) kernel cost** requirement of hard real-time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define message passing. Distinguish between blocking and non-blocking send primitives with a real-time example for each.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Message passing is a process of task-to-task communication in which cooperating tasks exchange data through explicit `send()` and `receive()` system calls mediated by the RTOS kernel, without sharing mutable memory.

**Blocking Send (1 Mark):** A blocking send suspends the sender task until the kernel successfully delivers the message to the receiver or buffers it in a bounded mailbox. It guarantees deterministic hand-off.
*Example:* In a flight-control loop, the sensor task blocks on `send(actuator_cmd)` until the actuator task issues `receive()`, ensuring strict phase alignment of the control period.

**Non-Blocking Send (1 Mark):** A non-blocking send returns immediately to the caller, regardless of whether the message has been consumed. It maximizes sender throughput but risks message loss if no buffering exists.
*Example:* A high-frequency logger task uses non-blocking send to a ring-buffer mailbox, dropping messages gracefully rather than stalling the data acquisition.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**List any three advantages of indirect message passing (mailbox-based) over direct message passing in a real-time system.** **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Decoupling (1 Mark):** Indirect passing uses a named kernel mailbox; the sender and receiver need not know each other's identities, allowing tasks to be added, removed, or migrated at runtime without modifying the sender.

2. **Multiple Producers and Consumers (1 Mark):** A single mailbox can fan-in from many senders and fan-out to one (or many) receivers, enabling natural implementation of event broadcast and pipeline architectures.

3. **Configurable Buffering and Bounded Memory (1 Mark):** Mailbox capacity $N$ is set explicitly, allowing the designer to bound RAM consumption, perform schedulability analysis, and satisfy the **memory partitioning constraints of safety standards** such as ISO 26262 and DO-178C.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **indirect message passing** mechanism using a bounded mailbox. Discuss the role of the head, tail, and count pointers. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Diagram (3 Marks):** Draw a circular buffer with $N=4$ slots, showing HEAD at slot 0, TAIL at slot 2 (i.e., two messages enqueued), and COUNT = 2. Mark producer (enqueue at TAIL) and consumer (dequeue at HEAD) task arrows.

**Explanation (4 Marks):**

- A bounded mailbox is a **kernel-managed FIFO circular buffer** of fixed capacity $N$.
- **HEAD pointer** points to the slot containing the oldest unconsumed message; on a successful `receive()` it advances modulo $N$.
- **TAIL pointer** points to the next free slot; on a successful `send()` it advances modulo $N$ after the message is copied in.
- **COUNT** tracks the number of valid messages; it increments on enqueue and decrements on dequeue.
- When COUNT $= N$, the mailbox is *full*; send() either blocks (blocking-on-full mode) or returns `ERR_FULL`.
- When COUNT $= 0$, the mailbox is *empty*; receive() either blocks or returns `ERR_EMPTY` based on flags.
- This structure makes both enqueue and dequeue **O(1)** operations, satisfying the **constant-time kernel primitive requirement** of hard real-time systems.

---

**(b)** A sensor task produces one message every **5 ms** (payload = 64 bytes). The consumer task processes each message in **2 ms**. The mailbox capacity is 4. Compute (i) the **arrival rate**, (ii) the **service rate**, (iii) the **minimum queue depth** to prevent overflow, and (iv) the **average waiting time** in the queue. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $T_P = 5$ ms, $L = 64$ bytes, $C_C = 2$ ms, $N = 4$.

**(i) Arrival Rate (1 Mark):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{T_P} = \frac{1}{5\,\text{ms}} = 200\,\text{msg/s}
$$

**(ii) Service Rate (1 Mark):**

$$
\mu = \frac{1}{C_C} = \frac{1}{2\,\text{ms}} = 500\,\text{msg/s}
$$

**(iii) Minimum Queue Depth (2 Marks):**
Since $\lambda < \mu$, the system is stable. Using Little's Law for the worst-case busy period ($W_{max} = C_C$):

$$
N_{min} = \left\lceil \frac{C_C}{T_P} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil = 1
$$

The provided capacity $N = 4$ is therefore **sufficient** and provides a safety margin of 3 slots.

**(iv) Average Waiting Time (3 Marks):**
For an M/D/1-like queue with deterministic service time $C_C = 2$ ms, traffic intensity:

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{200}{500} = 0.4
$$

Average waiting time (Pollaczek–Khinchine for deterministic service):

$$
W_q = \frac{\rho \cdot C_C}{2\,(1-\rho)} = \frac{0.4 \times 2}{2 \times 0.6} = \frac{0.8}{1.2} = 0.667\,\text{ms}
$$

**Final Results Summary:**
- $\lambda = 200$ msg/s
- $\mu = 500$ msg/s
- $N_{min} = 1$ (safe, design uses $N=4$)
- $W_q = 0.667$ ms

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Compare **synchronous** and **asynchronous** message passing. Under what conditions should a hard real-time designer prefer synchronous hand-off? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 Marks):**

| Aspect | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|--------|-------------|--------------|
| Send blocks? | Yes, until receive | No |
| Memory buffer | Not required | Required |
| Latency | Higher | Lower |
| Determinism | Strong | Depends on Q depth |
| Throughput | Lower | Higher |
| Suitable for | Control loops | Logging, telemetry |

**Conditions Favoring Synchronous Hand-off (3 Marks):**

1. **Hard deadline coupling:** When the sender and receiver must execute in **lock-step** within the same control period (e.g., torque command in a motor controller).
2. **Zero memory budget:** Safety-critical systems certified under **DO-178C Level A** may forbid dynamic memory allocation; synchronous passing needs **no buffer**.
3. **Causality verification:** Synchronous rendezvous makes the temporal ordering of events trivial to prove in a schedulability graph, which is essential for **WCET analysis** and certification audits.

---

**(b)** Explain the **zero-copy message passing** technique. Derive the **bandwidth saving** if the pointer size is 8 bytes and the average payload is 256 bytes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (4 Marks):**
In zero-copy message passing, the RTOS kernel does **not duplicate the message payload** in the kernel buffer. Instead, it transfers only a **pointer** to the payload memory, plus a small header containing sender ID, message type, and length. The sender's address space is mapped (or shared) with the receiver in **read-only** mode. This is implemented in QNX (via `MsgSend`), Linux `io_uring`, and AUTOSAR Adaptive ara::com.

**Derivation (3 Marks):**
Traditional copy cost for a payload of $L$ bytes (assuming byte-by-byte copy at rate $r$ bytes/tick):

$$
T_{copy} = \frac{L}{r}
$$

Zero-copy cost:

$$
T_{zero} = \frac{\vert p \vert}{r}
$$

Bandwidth saving ratio:

$$
S = 1 - \frac{T_{zero}}{T_{copy}} = 1 - \frac{\vert p \vert}{L}
$$

With $\vert p \vert = 8$ bytes and $L = 256$ bytes:

$$
S = 1 - \frac{8}{256} = 1 - 0.03125 = 0.96875
$$

**Result:** Zero-copy saves **96.875 %** of the kernel bus bandwidth per message. This is critical in **automotive radar (76–77 GHz) and 4D imaging pipelines** where messages can be several kilobytes per scan.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Message Passing Answers:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse *blocking send* with *blocking receive*. They are two different primitives. State which one you are discussing.
> 2. **Never** draw a mail box with unbounded slots. KTU expects the capacity $N$ to be visibly finite in your diagram.
> 3. **Always** state the **time complexity** ($O(1)$) when justifying why message passing is suitable for hard real-time.
> 4. For numerical problems, **show the unit** in every step (ms, µs, msg/s). Examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. In comparisons, **avoid one-word answers** like "fast" or "slow". Use quantitative terms (latency, throughput, WCET bound).
> 6. When asked to "explain with a diagram", a text-only answer scores at most 60 % of the diagram marks. Always include a **labeled block diagram** with arrows.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Message Passing** is a kernel-mediated IPC mechanism **without shared memory**.
- Two classification axes: **Naming (Direct / Indirect)** and **Synchronization (Synchronous / Asynchronous)**.
- A **mailbox** is a bounded FIFO circular buffer; both enqueue and dequeue are $O(1)$.
- **Synchronous / Rendezvous** = blocking send until receive; no buffer needed; tightest determinism.
- **Asynchronous** = non-blocking send; requires bounded queue; risk of overflow.
- **Blocking-on-full** is the industrial default (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX).
- **Zero-copy** passes only an 8-byte pointer, saving $\approx 97$% bandwidth for typical payloads.
- The **minimum queue depth** to prevent overflow is $N_{min} = \lceil C_C / T_P \rceil$ for a deterministic producer.
- **Hard real-time rule:** Every IPC primitive must execute in **bounded, constant time**; verify by WCET analysis.
- **Priority inversion** can still occur in message passing; mitigated by **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** or **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**.
- **Industry standards** using message passing: AUTOSAR COM, ARINC 653, ROS 2 DDS, QNX PPS, POSIX `mqueue.h`.
- **Head/Tail/Count** are the three pointers of a circular mailbox.
- Always quote the **latency equation** $T_{latency} = T_{send} + T_{sched} + \sum_{j \in HP(i)} C_j + (L/B_{bus}) + T_{recv}$ for hard real-time arguments.
- Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (or `\vert`) notation — never the bare `|` character — in LaTeX table cells to avoid formatting errors in KTU digital answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RTOS Environment Setup: Installation and Setup of an RTOS on a Microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M)
Task Creation and Management: Writing Simple Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Task Creation and Management: Writing Simple Tasks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of a **Real Time Operating System (RTOS)**, a **Task** (often called a *Thread* in POSIX terminology) is the smallest independent unit of execution scheduled by the kernel. A task is a sequential, semi-independent program segment that owns its own **stack**, **register context (TCB)**, and **priority**, and competes with other tasks for CPU time on a single processor core. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (FreeRTOS / Embedded RTOS context):** > A *task* is implemented as a `TCB_t` (Task Control Block) in kernel memory. It is a permanent or temporary execution context possessing: > - A unique **priority level** (0 = idle, higher = more urgent) > - A dedicated **stack memory region** (statically or dynamically allocated) > - A **function entry point** (the C function that becomes the task body) > - A **parameter pointer** passed at creation ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Model Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen as the **RTOS scheduler**, and each **chef** as a **task**: - Every **chef** (task) has a **recipe card** (the C function) they must follow. - The **Head Chef** (scheduler) decides which chef works the stove (CPU) at any given moment. - Each chef has their own **cutting board and knives** (stack memory) — they never share tools while cooking. - The Head Chef calls out: *"Chef A — your turn!"* — that is a **context switch**. - If Chef B is waiting for an ingredient delivery (semaphore/queue), they step aside — they are **Blocked**. - The Head Chef's assistant, who does odd jobs when no one else is busy, is the **Idle Task** (priority 0). > [!NOTE] > **Why tasks and not just `main()`?** > In a bare-metal `while(1)` loop, only one thing happens at a time. In an RTOS, multiple logical activities (e.g., reading a sensor, updating an LCD, transmitting UART) run *concurrently* through tasks, and the kernel guarantees deterministic response to the highest-priority ready task. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Task Management - **Stack size** — measured in **words** (e.g., 256 words = 1024 bytes on a 32-bit MCU) - **Priority** — unsigned integer; on FreeRTOS, valid range is **0** (lowest) to **`configMAX_PRIORITIES - 1`** (highest) - **Tick rate (`configTICK_RATE_HZ`)** — typically **1000 Hz** (1 ms tick) for fine-grained timing - **Task name length** — **configMAX_TASK_NAME_LEN** (default 16 characters) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Task (Internals Breakdown) A FreeRTOS task is not "magic" — it is built from three coordinated memory regions and one C function: 1. **Task Code (C Function)** - Signature: `void vTaskFunction(void *pvParameters)` - Must contain an infinite loop (e.g., `for(;;)` or `while(1)`) for periodic tasks. - Must NOT return. If it returns, the task is automatically deleted and a config-fail hook is raised. 2. **Task Control Block (TCB)** — `struct tskTaskControlBlock` - Holds the **top-of-stack pointer**, **priority**, **state**, **list pointers** (Ready/Delayed lists), **task name**, etc. - Allocated dynamically by `pvPortMalloc()` or statically at compile time. 3. **Task Stack** - RAM region where local variables, return addresses, and CPU registers are saved during context switches. - Size = `usStackDepth` in **stack words**, not bytes. ### 2.2 Why and How — The "Why" Behind Each Design Choice | Design Element | Why It Exists | Engineering Consequence | |---|---|---| | Separate stack per task | Prevents corruption between tasks | Each task is **isolated** — one task's overflow cannot directly wreck another's data | | Priority integer | Enables preemptive scheduling | **Determinism**: highest-priority ready task *always* runs first | | Parameter pointer (`void *pvParameters`) | Allows reuse of the same function for multiple tasks | Memory-efficient: one function body, multiple task instances | | Infinite loop in task body | Kernel expects persistent execution context | A returning task triggers `vTaskDelete(NULL)` internally | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / API | Meaning | Default / Typical Value | Units | |---|---|---|---| | `usStackDepth` | Stack size for the task | 128 – 4096 | **words** (4 bytes on 32-bit) | | `uxPriority` | Task priority | 1 (above idle) | dimensionless integer | | `configTICK_RATE_HZ` | Scheduler tick frequency | 1000 | Hz | | `configMINIMAL_STACK_SIZE` | Minimum legal stack | 128 | words | | `configMAX_PRIORITIES` | Highest legal priority + 1 | 5 or 32 | dimensionless | | `xTaskCreate()` | Dynamically create a task | returns `pdPASS / errCOULD_NOT_ALLOCATE_REQUIRED_MEMORY` | — | | `xTaskCreateStatic()` | Statically create a task | returns `pdPASS / pdFAIL` | — | | `vTaskDelete(xHandle)` | Remove task + free TCB | `xHandle = NULL` deletes calling task | — | | `vTaskStartScheduler()` | Starts the RTOS tick | must be called once from `main()` | — | | `taskYIELD()` | Voluntary CPU surrender | — | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion formula for stack size:** > $$\text{Stack in bytes} = usStackDepth \times \text{sizeof(StackType\_t)}$$ > On a 32-bit MCU: `1 word = 4 bytes`. On Cortex-M with 8-byte stack alignment, **round up to a multiple of 8 bytes** to avoid hard-faults on `PUSH` instructions. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR)**: Each *Runnable* maps to a task; ABS, airbag, and dashboard tasks coexist on a single MCU with strict priority ceilings. - **IoT Edge Devices (FreeRTOS on ESP32)**: Wi-Fi handling, sensor polling, OTA updates, and MQTT publishing are *separate tasks*. - **Medical devices**: Drug-infusion pump tasks must run with **higher priority** than UI tasks to meet hard-real-time deadlines. - **Drone flight controllers (PX4, NuttX)**: Attitude control loop task = 1 kHz priority; GPS parsing task = lower priority. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Calculating Required Stack Size for a Task A common KTU-style problem asks: *"Given a function's local variables and call depth, compute `usStackDepth`."* **Problem Statement (Worked Example):** > A task function `vSensorTask()` contains: > - 4 local `uint32_t` variables > - A 128-byte buffer (`uint8_t buf[128]`) > - It calls 3 other functions, each with a stack frame of **64 bytes** > - CPU is 32-bit, `StackType_t = uint32_t` (4 bytes) > - Add a **safety margin of 25 %** to prevent overflow. **Step-by-Step Solution:** **Step 1:** Calculate local variable stack footprint. $$ S_{\text{locals}} = 4 \times \text{sizeof(uint32\_t)} + 128 $$ $$ S_{\text{locals}} = 4 \times 4 + 128 = 16 + 128 = 144 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate nested call stack footprint. $$ S_{\text{calls}} = 3 \times 64 = 192 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 3:** Add CPU interrupt / context overhead (typical Cortex-M3/M4 = **80 bytes** for register save). $$ S_{\text{context}} = 80 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 4:** Sum the raw requirement. $$ S_{\text{raw}} = S_{\text{locals}} + S_{\text{calls}} + S_{\text{context}} $$ $$ S_{\text{raw}} = 144 + 192 + 80 = 416 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 5:** Apply 25 % safety margin. $$ S_{\text{safe}} = S_{\text{raw}} \times 1.25 $$ $$ S_{\text{safe}} = 416 \times 1.25 = 520 \text{ bytes} $$ **Step 6:** Convert from bytes to words (since `usStackDepth` is in words of `StackType_t`). $$ usStackDepth = \left\lceil \frac{S_{\text{safe}}}{\text{sizeof(StackType\_t)}} \right\rceil $$ $$ usStackDepth = \left\lceil \frac{520}{4} \right\rceil = \lceil 130 \rceil = 130 \text{ words} $$ **Step 7:** Round up to next multiple of 8 (Cortex-M stack-alignment best practice). $$ usStackDepth_{\text{final}} = 136 \text{ words} $$ **Final Answer:** Pass `usStackDepth = 136` to `xTaskCreate()`. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Task State Transitions | Current State | Trigger Event | Next State | Triggering API | |---|---|---|---| | **Running** | Higher-priority task becomes ready | **Ready** | Hardware interrupt / API call | | **Running** | Calls `vTaskDelay(n)` | **Blocked** | `vTaskDelay` | | **Running** | Calls `vTaskSuspend()` | **Suspended** | `vTaskSuspend` | | **Running** | Calls `vTaskDelete(NULL)` | **Deleted** (freed) | `vTaskDelete` | | **Ready** | Scheduler picks it | **Running** | Tick interrupt / yield | | **Blocked** | Delay expires / IPC unblocks | **Ready** | `xTaskAbortDelay` | | **Suspended** | `vTaskResume()` called | **Ready** | `vTaskResume` | | **Deleted** | (terminal) | — | Memory returned to heap | ### 3.3 Task Creation — Internal Execution Path (Derived Logic) When `xTaskCreate()` is invoked, the kernel performs the following deterministic sequence: 1. Validate parameters (priority ∈ [0, `configMAX_PRIORITIES - 1`], stack depth ≥ `configMINIMAL_STACK_SIZE`). 2. Allocate TCB via `pvPortMalloc(sizeof(TCB_t))`. 3. Allocate stack via `pvPortMalloc(usStackDepth * sizeof(StackType_t))`. 4. Initialise TCB fields: `pxTopOfStack`, `uxPriority`, `pcTaskName`, `xState`. 5. Initialise the simulated stack frame as if the task had been preempted at entry (set LR to `prvIdleTask` for a clean exit). 6. Insert the TCB into the **Ready list** of its priority (`pxReadyTasksLists[uxPriority]`). 7. If scheduler already running → trigger a context switch if new task outranks current one. 8. Return `pdHandle` (TaskHandle_t) to the caller. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — FreeRTOS Task State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Created Created --> Ready : xTaskCreate succeeds Ready --> Running : Scheduler dispatch Running --> Ready : Higher prio task ready Running --> Blocked : vTaskDelay / xQueueReceive Blocked --> Ready : Delay expires / IPC available Running --> Suspended : vTaskSuspend Suspended --> Ready : vTaskResume Running --> Deleted : vTaskDelete Blocked --> Deleted : vTaskDelete Deleted --> [*] : TCB freed ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Task Memory Layout (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph RAM["MCU SRAM Layout (Conceptual)"] direction TB A["Heap Region (pvPortMalloc managed)"] B["Task A TCB"] C["Task A Stack - 256 words"] D["Task B TCB"] E["Task B Stack - 128 words"] F["Idle Task TCB (auto)"] G["Idle Task Stack - configMINIMAL_STACK_SIZE"] H[".bss / .data sections"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H end CPU["CPU Registers SP PC LR R0-R12"] -- "context saved to Task A Stack on switch" --> C SCHED["Scheduler - pxReadyTasksLists array"] -- "selects highest prio ready TCB" --> B SCHED -- "manages" --> D SCHED -- "always ready" --> F ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Task Creation Call Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant App as Application main participant API as xTaskCreate participant Alloc as pvPortMalloc participant Sched as Scheduler participant TCB as TCB struct participant ReadyList as pxReadyTasksLists App->>API: xTaskCreate(fn, name, stack, param, prio, handle) API->>API: Validate parameters API->>Alloc: malloc sizeof TCB_t Alloc-->>API: pTCB pointer API->>Alloc: malloc usStackDepth x sizeof StackType_t Alloc-->>API: pStack pointer API->>TCB: Initialise pxTopOfStack uxPriority pcTaskName API->>TCB: Build initial stack frame (simulated interrupt entry) API->>ReadyList: Insert TCB at index uxPriority API-->>App: return pdPASS and handle Note over Sched: On next tick, scheduler may preempt current task
if new task has higher priority Sched->>ReadyList: Pick highest priority non-empty list ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`** *List and briefly explain the essential parameters passed to the `xTaskCreate()` API in FreeRTOS. Why is the priority parameter significant in a real-time system?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The `xTaskCreate()` API in FreeRTOS takes the following key parameters: 1. **Task function pointer** — `pvTaskCode`: the C function that implements the task body; it must have prototype `void vFunc(void *pvParameters)` and contain an infinite loop. (1 mark) 2. **Task name / descriptive string** — `pcName`: used for debugging in `vTaskList()` output. Length capped by `configMAX_TASK_NAME_LEN`. (0.5 mark) 3. **Stack depth** — `usStackDepth`: size of the task's stack in *words* (not bytes). Must be ≥ `configMINIMAL_STACK_SIZE`. (0.5 mark) 4. **Task parameter** — `pvParameters`: a `void*` passed unchanged to the task function, enabling one function body to serve multiple tasks. (0.5 mark) 5. **Priority** — `uxPriority`: an integer in [0, `configMAX_PRIORITIES - 1`]. The scheduler always dispatches the *highest-priority ready* task, so this parameter directly controls **determinism and worst-case latency** in real-time systems. A higher priority shortens the response time to external events. (0.5 mark) --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`** *What is the role of the Idle Task in FreeRTOS? What happens if a user task returns from its function body?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Role of the Idle Task (2 marks):** The Idle Task is an automatically created kernel task with the **lowest priority (0)**. It is responsible for: - Executing when **no other task is in the Ready state**, ensuring the CPU is never idle in an undefined state. - Performing **garbage collection of deleted TCBs and stacks** allocated by the kernel. - Optionally invoking a user-defined **`vApplicationIdleHook()`** for low-power or housekeeping code (must be enabled by `configUSE_IDLE_HOOK = 1`). **What happens if a user task returns (1 mark):** If a task function returns, FreeRTOS detects this on the next tick, automatically **deletes the task** (TCB and stack are freed), and calls the **`configUSE_TASK_FPU_SUPPORT`-aware cleanup path**. To catch this during development, define `configUSE_TASK_FPU_SUPPORT` and use `vTaskSetApplicationTaskTag` for diagnostics, or simply ensure every task body is wrapped in `for(;;)`. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often write `void vTask(void *p) { ... }` *without* an infinite loop. The KTU examiner will deduct 1 mark for this. Always write `for(;;)` or `while(1)` at the end of the function body. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain the internal structure of a FreeRTOS Task. With a neat diagram, describe the Task Control Block (TCB) and the role of each major field. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the complete C code to create two tasks in FreeRTOS on a Cortex-M4 MCU: - **Task 1** `vLEDTask`: toggles an LED connected to GPIO pin 5 every 500 ms, priority 2. - **Task 2** `vSensorTask`: reads a temperature value from a function `float readTemp()` and prints it over UART every 1 second, priority 1. - Stack size for both tasks = 256 words. Use `xTaskCreate()`. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 marks** The internal structure of a FreeRTOS task consists of three physically separate components: 1. **Task Code** — A C function (the "task body"). 2. **Task Control Block (TCB)** — A kernel-allocated struct holding all metadata. 3. **Task Stack** — A private RAM region for local variables and saved context. **Major TCB fields (write any 5):** | Field | Purpose | |---|---| | `pxTopOfStack` | Current stack pointer value for this task | | `uxPriority` | Task priority used by scheduler | | `pcTaskName[N]` | Human-readable name for debugging | | `xStateListItem` | List node linking TCB into Ready/Blocked/Suspended lists | | `xEventListItem` | List node for event-list (e.g., queue wake-ups) | | `uxCriticalNesting` | Critical-section nesting depth for this task | | `ulRunTimeCounter` | Optional CPU-time accounting | | `pxStack` | Pointer to start of the stack region (for free on delete) | **Diagram (Text):** ``` +-----------------------------+ | TCB (struct tskTCB) | |-----------------------------| | pxTopOfStack ---------------+---> [Top of Stack] | uxPriority | | pcTaskName[16] | | xStateListItem | | xEventListItem | | uxCriticalNesting = 0 | | pxStack -------------------+---> [Stack Base - lowest address] +-----------------------------+ | v [Stack grows downward] [Saved R4-R11, R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR] ``` **Key Point:** The TCB itself sits in the heap; the stack it points to is a *separate* heap allocation. On deletion, both are freed. (7 marks distributed across the explanation, field listing, and diagram.) --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 marks** ```c #include "FreeRTOS.h" #include "task.h" #include /* Hardware abstraction prototypes */ extern void toggleLED(int pin); extern float readTemp(void); extern void uartPrint(const char *fmt, ...); /* Task 1: LED Toggle */ void vLEDTask(void *pvParameters) { (void) pvParameters; /* Unused parameter */ for (;;) { toggleLED(5); vTaskDelay(pdMS_TO_TICKS(500)); /* Block 500 ms */ } } /* Task 2: Temperature Read and Print */ void vSensorTask(void *pvParameters) { (void) pvParameters; for (;;) { float t = readTemp(); uartPrint("Temp = %.2f C\r\n", t); vTaskDelay(pdMS_TO_TICKS(1000)); /* Block 1 s */ } } /* Main entry point */ int main(void) { /* Hardware initialisation assumed complete */ /* Create Task 1: priority 2 (higher), stack 256 words */ xTaskCreate(vLEDTask, "LED", 256, NULL, 2, NULL); /* Create Task 2: priority 1 (lower), stack 256 words */ xTaskCreate(vSensorTask, "Sensor", 256, NULL, 1, NULL); /* Start the scheduler - never returns */ vTaskStartScheduler(); /* Should never reach here */ for (;;); } ``` **Mark Distribution:** - Correct function prototypes with `void *pvParameters` (1 mark) - Infinite loop + `vTaskDelay` usage (2 marks) - Correct priority ordering (LED = 2 > Sensor = 1) (1 mark) - `xTaskCreate` parameters correctly populated (1 mark) - `vTaskStartScheduler()` call (1 mark) - `pdMS_TO_TICKS` macro for millisecond-to-tick conversion (1 mark) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat state-transition diagram, explain all possible states a FreeRTOS task can occupy. List the API calls that cause transitions between states. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A real-time system has 3 tasks with the following characteristics: - Task T1: period = 10 ms, execution time = 2 ms - Task T2: period = 25 ms, execution time = 3 ms - Task T3: period = 50 ms, execution time = 5 ms Using Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS), check whether the task set is schedulable. If schedulable, assign priorities. Also compute the CPU utilisation. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 marks** **Task States in FreeRTOS:** Running, Ready, Blocked, Suspended, Deleted. **State Diagram (textual):** ``` xTaskCreate | v +-----------> [ Ready ] <-----------+ | | | | Dispatch by Scheduler | | v | | [ Running ] | | / | \ | | vTaskDelay vTask vTaskDelete| | | Suspend | | | v | v | | [ Blocked ] [ Suspended] [ Deleted ] (terminal) | | | | Delay expires / IPC | | | +-------+--------+ | v [ Ready ] ``` **Transition Table (API → state change):** | API | Transition | |---|---| | `xTaskCreate` | None → Ready | | Scheduler dispatch | Ready → Running | | Interrupt / higher-prio task | Running → Ready | | `vTaskDelay(t)` | Running → Blocked | | `xQueueReceive` (empty) | Running → Blocked | | `vTaskSuspend` | Running/Ready → Suspended | | `vTaskResume` | Suspended → Ready | | `vTaskDelete` | Any → Deleted (terminal) | | `xTaskAbortDelay` | Blocked → Ready | (7 marks: 3 for diagram, 3 for transition table, 1 for explaining "Blocked vs Suspended" — Blocked has a timeout, Suspended has none.) --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 marks** **Given:** - T1: $P_1 = 10$ ms, $C_1 = 2$ ms - T2: $P_2 = 25$ ms, $C_2 = 3$ ms - T3: $P_3 = 50$ ms, $C_3 = 5$ ms **Step 1:** Apply the **Liu & Layland sufficient condition** for RMS: $$ U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{P_i} $$ **Step 2:** Compute utilisation. $$ U_1 = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 $$ $$ U_2 = \frac{3}{25} = 0.12 $$ $$ U_3 = \frac{5}{50} = 0.10 $$ $$ U_{\text{total}} = 0.20 + 0.12 + 0.10 = 0.42 $$ **Step 3:** Compute the **RMS bound** for $n = 3$: $$ U_{\text{bound}} = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) $$ $$ U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) $$ $$ U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7797 $$ **Step 4:** Compare. $$ U_{\text{total}} = 0.42 \le U_{\text{bound}} = 0.7797 $$ **Conclusion:** The sufficient condition is satisfied → **the task set is guaranteed schedulable under RMS**. (5 marks so far) **Step 5:** Assign priorities by RMS rule (shorter period = higher priority): - **T1** (period 10 ms) → highest priority - **T2** (period 25 ms) → middle priority - **T3** (period 50 ms) → lowest priority **Step 6:** CPU utilisation = **42 %**, leaving **58 % headroom** for the Idle Task. (2 marks) > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse *sufficient* and *necessary* conditions. The Liu-Layland bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is **sufficient but not necessary** — a set can be schedulable even when $U > U_{\text{bound}}$. The KTU examiner will deduct 1 mark if you claim the bound is exact. To check a "fail" case precisely, use the **exact Response Time Analysis (RTA)** test. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] A **task** = code + TCB + private stack; never share stacks. - [x] `xTaskCreate(fn, name, stack_words, param, prio, handle)` is the canonical dynamic creation API. - [x] **Stack depth is in words, not bytes** — multiply by 4 (32-bit) for bytes. - [x] **Priority range** is `[0, configMAX_PRIORITIES - 1]`; 0 = idle. - [x] Every task body **must** be an infinite loop — never return. - [x] **Idle Task** is auto-created, priority 0, cleans up deleted TCBs. - [x] Use **`pdMS_TO_TICKS(ms)`** to convert milliseconds to ticks safely. - [x] **Scheduler always dispatches the highest-priority Ready task** (preemptive). - [x] Task states: **Running, Ready, Blocked, Suspended, Deleted**. - [x] **`vTaskStartScheduler()`** must be called once from `main()` to start RTOS. - [x] **RMS priority rule**: shorter period ⇒ higher priority. - [x] **Liu-Layland bound** for $n$ tasks: $U_{\text{bound}} = n(2^{1/n}-1)$ — sufficient, not necessary. - [x] CPU utilisation $U = \sum C_i / P_i$; must be ≤ 1, ideally ≤ 0.7 for safety. - [x] **Blocked vs Suspended**: Blocked has a timeout; Suspended has none. - [x] `vTaskDelete(NULL)` from within a task deletes that task itself. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** *"**C**ode, **C**ontrol-block, **C**ore-registers, **C**ontext" — the four C's of an RTOS task.* Use this in the exam if you blank out on the structure.

topics.title: Task States and Transitions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Task States and Transitions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS), a **task** (also called a *thread* of execution) is the basic unit of work scheduled by the kernel. A task, once created, does not execute continuously. Instead, it cycles through a well-defined set of **task states**, where each state represents the current **scheduling eligibility** and **execution status** of the task with respect to the CPU and system resources.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (PERAT757, Module 1), a task in a typical RTOS can occupy any one of the following **five canonical states** at any given instant:
