| Task | Period $(ms)$ | Execution Time $(ms)$ |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | $8$ | $2$ |
| T2 | $12$ | $3$ |
| T3 | $24$ | $4$ |

If the **context switch time** is $T_{cs} = 0.5 \,ms$ per switch and scheduling is **preemptive priority-based** (T1 > T2 > T3), calculate the **total number of context switches** in one hyperperiod and the **percentage of CPU time lost to context switching**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — TCB Structure and Usage:**

**Diagram: (Refer Section 4.1 Diagram A for the full Mermaid TCB architecture)**

**Explanation:**
The TCB is a critical kernel data structure with the following fields:
- **Task ID**: Unique identifier
- **Task State**: RUNNING, READY, BLOCKED, SUSPENDED
- **Saved Stack Pointer (SP)**: Stores the SP value when task was suspended
- **Stack Base & Size**: Memory region reserved for task's stack
- **Priority**: Used by scheduler
- **Time Slice Counter**: Remaining ticks for current quantum

**Usage in Context Switch:**
1. **Save Phase**: When task A is preempted, its CPU registers are pushed onto its stack; the updated SP value is written into A's TCB field `StackPtr`.
2. **Schedule Phase**: Scheduler reads all TCBs to find the highest-priority READY task (say, Task B).
3. **Restore Phase**: The scheduler reads B's TCB `StackPtr`, sets PSP = that value, and pops B's saved registers from B's stack into the CPU.

**Part (b) — Quantitative Analysis:**

**Step 1: Hyperperiod**

$$
T_{hyper} = lcm(8, 12, 24) = 24 \,ms
$$

**Step 2: Task activations per hyperperiod**

$$
N_{T1} = \dfrac{24}{8} = 3, \quad N_{T2} = \dfrac{24}{12} = 2, \quad N_{T3} = \dfrac{24}{24} = 1
$$

Total task activations: $3 + 2 + 1 = 6$.

**Step 3: Number of Context Switches**

Each task activation requires an entry context switch. Each task completion requires an exit switch. For preemption events, each generates an additional switch pair.

Assuming **one preemptive context switch per task activation** (simplified model):

$$
N_{cs} = 2 \times (\text{Total Activations}) = 2 \times 6 = 12 \text{ context switches}
$$

*(More accurate count requires building the timeline; here we use the standard simplification used in KTU problems.)*

**Step 4: Total Context Switch Overhead**

$$
T_{cs\_total} = N_{cs} \times T_{cs} = 12 \times 0.5 \,ms = 6 \,ms
$$

**Step 5: Percentage CPU Time Lost**

$$
\text{Loss \%} = \dfrac{T_{cs\_total}}{T_{hyper}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{6}{24} \times 100\% = 25\%
$$

**Conclusion:** **25% of CPU time is consumed by context switching overhead**, which is significant and demands optimization (e.g., reducing $T_{cs}$ by using hardware-assisted switches or co-operative sections).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 1. **Forgetting context switch overhead in timeline diagrams** — Many students draw only task execution, omitting the $T_{cs}$ intervals. Always show context switch as a discrete block.
> 2. **Confusing context switch with interrupt handling** — Context switch is a **kernel-level operation** triggered by PendSV/SysTick, not a hardware ISR. The ISR runs to completion; context switch happens at a safe point.
> 3. **Assuming CPU executes two tasks simultaneously** — A single-core CPU runs ONE task at a time; multitasking is an illusion created by rapid context switching.
> 4. **Wrong hyperperiod calculation** — Always verify $T_{hyper} = lcm$ of all periods. Using $gcd$ is a fatal error.
> 5. **Not including $T_{cs}$ in $C_i$** when applying Liu-Layland bound — use $C_i^{eff} = C_i + T_{cs}$ for accurate schedulability.
> 6. **Drawing the diagram but not labeling axes or units** — KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unlabeled diagrams. Always mark time in $ms$ on the X-axis.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU ESE:**

- ✅ **Context Switch** = Save old context + Schedule + Restore new context
- ✅ **Time Components**: $T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{sched} + T_{restore}$
- ✅ **TCB** stores: PC, SP, registers, PSR, priority, state
- ✅ **Three Triggers**: Preemption, Time Slice Expiry, Blocking I/O
- ✅ **Hyperperiod**: $T_{hyper} = lcm(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$
- ✅ **Effective CPU Utilization**: $U^{eff} = (T_{useful} + T_{cs\_total}) / T_{hyper}$
- ✅ **Schedulability test** must include $T_{cs}$: use $C_i^{eff} = C_i + T_{cs}$
- ✅ **Context Switch is atomic** — interrupts are disabled during switching
- ✅ **FreeRTOS uses PendSV interrupt** specifically for context switching on ARM Cortex-M
- ✅ **Single CPU = one task at a time** — multitasking is an illusion
- ✅ **Higher priority = lower priority number** (common convention in KTU problems)
- ✅ **Overhead % = $T_{cs\_total} / T_{hyper} \times 100\%$**
- ✅ **Context switch overhead must be deterministic** in hard RTOS — non-determinism = deadline miss = system failure
- ✅ **Optimizations**: hardware-assisted switching, fewer registers to save, dedicated switch instructions (e.g., ARM `LDMIA`/`STMIA`)
- ✅ **Memory regions**: each task has private stack — no shared stack during context switch
- ✅ **Idle Task** runs when no other task is READY — context switch occurs to/from idle

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed-Priority Scheduling (Rate-Monotonic, Deadline-Monotonic)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fixed-Priority Scheduling (Rate-Monotonic, Deadline-Monotonic)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fixed-Priority Scheduling (FPS) — Foundational Definition

**Fixed-Priority Scheduling (FPS)** is a class of *preemptive, priority-driven scheduling algorithms* used in **hard real-time systems**, where each task is assigned a **static, immutable priority** at design time (offline), and the scheduler always dispatches the **highest-priority runnable task** on the processor. The priorities never change at runtime — they are "frozen" at system design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fixed-priority scheduling is the *most widely deployed* class in commercial real-time operating systems (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS) because it is **simple, predictable, analyzable**, and has bounded overhead. It is the theoretical cornerstone of real-time scheduling theory established by **Liu and Layland (1973)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine an **emergency hospital triage room** with a single doctor:

- Patients keep arriving, each with a fixed "severity tag" assigned at the door (priority).
- The doctor **always treats the sickest patient first** (highest priority wins).
- A low-priority patient currently being examined is **interrupted** the moment a sicker patient arrives (preemption).
- The priority tag is **never reassigned** — once a patient is labeled "yellow," they stay yellow.

**In an RTOS:**
- The "doctor" is the **single CPU core**.
- The "patients" are the **periodic tasks** (sensor reading, motor control, communication).
- The "severity tag" is the **static priority number**.
- The "interruption" is **preemption**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** What *changes* between Rate-Monotonic and Deadline-Monotonic is **how the priority tag is generated** — based on **period (rate)** or **deadline**.

## 1.3 Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — The Classical Optimal Policy

### Formal Definition
**Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is a *static-priority preemptive* algorithm that assigns priorities **inversely proportional to the task period** (i.e., directly proportional to the task **rate** = 1/T).

$$P_i \propto \frac{1}{T_i} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad T_i \downarrow \Rightarrow P_i \uparrow$$

where $P_i$ is the priority of task $\tau_i$ and $T_i$ is its period. The **shorter the period, the higher the priority**.

### Liu & Layland Assumptions
RMS analysis assumes the canonical task model:

1. All tasks are **periodic** with period $T_i$.
2. All tasks are **independent** (no shared resources, no precedence).
3. The relative deadline equals the period: $D_i = T_i$ (implicit deadlines).
4. Worst-case execution time (WCET) $C_i$ is **known and constant**.
5. Zero **release jitter**; all tasks are released simultaneously at $t = 0$.
6. **Preemptive** scheduling on a single processor.
7. No shared resources causing unbounded priority inversion.

## 1.4 Deadline-Monotonic Scheduling (DMS) — The Generalized Optimal Policy

### Formal Definition
**Deadline-Monotonic Scheduling (DMS)** is a *static-priority preemptive* algorithm that assigns priorities **inversely proportional to the task's relative deadline**. The shorter the deadline, the higher the priority.

$$P_i \propto \frac{1}{D_i} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad D_i \downarrow \Rightarrow P_i \uparrow$$

### Why DMS Exists
RMS breaks down when $D_i < T_i$ (constrained deadlines). DMS is the **optimal fixed-priority algorithm** under the assumption $D_i \leq T_i$ for all tasks, and **collapses to RMS** when $D_i = T_i$ for all tasks.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If the question says "deadline ≤ period" → use **DMS**. If "deadline = period" → use **RMS** (they are identical). Many students lose marks by using RMS for constrained deadlines — *do not do this!*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rate-Monotonic Scheduling — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Optimality of RMS (Liu & Layland, 1973)
Among all static-priority algorithms, **no other fixed-priority assignment can schedule a task set that RMS cannot**, provided $D_i = T_i$. This is the **optimality theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof Sketch:** If a task set is schedulable under some priority assignment, the "critical instant" analysis shows that the *highest-priority task first* assignment (RMS for $D_i = T_i$) is at least as feasible. The critical instant for any task is when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with it.

### 2.1.2 Critical Instant & Worst-Case Response Time
The **critical instant** of a task $\tau_i$ is the time at which its **worst-case response time** is maximized. For FPS, this occurs when $\tau_i$ is released *simultaneously* with all higher-priority tasks.

### 2.1.3 Processor Utilization
The **processor utilization factor** $U$ is the fraction of CPU time consumed:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

This is a scalar in $[0, 1]$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Condition / Meaning |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Processor Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$ | Total CPU demand; must be $\leq 1$ |
| 2 | Liu-Layland Sufficient Test | $U \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | Sufficient (not necessary) for RMS schedulability |
| 3 | Asymptotic Bound | $\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{\text{max}} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$ | RMS theoretical limit |
| 4 | Small-$n$ Bounds (RMS) | $n=1{:}\;1.000$ ; $n=2{:}\;0.828$ ; $n=3{:}\;0.779$ ; $n=4{:}\;0.756$ | Sufficient utilization thresholds |
| 5 | Hyperbolic Bound (Necessary + Sufficient) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 + U_i) \leq 2$ | Equivalent to $\sum \ln(1+U_i) \leq \ln 2$ |
| 6 | Time Demand $w_i(t)$ | $w_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Demand of $\tau_i$ in interval $[0,t]$ |
| 7 | Exact Schedulability (TDA) | $R_i = w_i(R_i)$ and $R_i \leq D_i$ | Fixed-point iteration |
| 8 | DMS Optimality | If $D_i \leq T_i \; \forall i$ → DMS is optimal FPS | Generalizes RMS |
| 9 | RMS ≡ DMS condition | $D_i = T_i \; \forall i$ | When deadlines equal periods |
| 10 | Worst-Case Response Time | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil R_i / T_j \right\rceil C_j$ | Iterative computation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used:** $hp(i)$ = set of tasks with **higher priority** than $\tau_i$ ; $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ = **ceiling** function (smallest integer $\geq$ argument).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Fixed-Priority Scheduling

| Domain | Application | Why FPS? |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Avionics** (DO-178C) | Flight control, autopilot | Certifiability — bounded analysis |
| **Automotive** (AUTOSAR-OS) | Engine control, ABS | Predictable jitter, simple runtime |
| **Industrial Robotics** | Servo loops | Shortest period = tightest loop |
| **Medical Devices** | Pacemaker, infusion pump | Hard real-time guarantees |
| **Satellite / Space** | Attitude control | Radiation tolerance + simple scheduler |

## 2.4 Why Fixed Priority is "Industry-Standard"?

- **Offline analysis**: Priorities and feasibility can be verified at design time.
- **Low runtime overhead**: A simple priority queue suffices.
- **Hardware-friendly**: Maps directly to interrupt priority levels.
- **Composable**: Adding a new task only requires re-checking its higher-priority interferers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Liu-Layland Sufficient Utilization Bound

### Step 1 — Define the Critical Instant
The critical instant for task $\tau_k$ is the time when **all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously** with $\tau_k$. At this moment, the **worst-case interference** on $\tau_k$ occurs.

### Step 2 — Formulate the Worst-Case Computation Demand
In any time interval of length $t$ starting at a critical instant, the number of jobs of a higher-priority task $\tau_j$ released is $\left\lceil t / T_j \right\rceil$. The total execution time demanded by all higher-priority tasks plus $\tau_k$ itself is:

$$w_k(t) = C_k + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Step 3 — Critical Condition for Schedulability
A necessary and sufficient condition for $\tau_k$ to meet its deadline is that there exists some $t \in [0, T_k]$ such that:

$$w_k(t) \leq t$$

The smallest such $t$ is the **worst-case response time** $R_k$. We need $R_k \leq T_k$ (since $D_k = T_k$ under RMS).

### Step 4 — Upper-Bound by Replacing Ceilings with Reals
To get a closed-form sufficient test, we use the loose upper bound $\left\lceil t/T_j \right\rceil \leq t/T_j + 1$ only at the *critical instant* of the longest-priority task. The classical proof uses induction on $n$.

### Step 5 — Final Sufficient Condition
After induction:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

### Step 6 — Asymptotic Behavior
Take the limit:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$$

The substitution $2^{1/n} = e^{\ln(2)/n}$ and applying $e^x \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$ gives the limit.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Time Demand Analysis (TDA) Iteration

### Setup
For a given task $\tau_i$, we wish to find the smallest $t$ such that $w_i(t) = t$, where:

$$w_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Iteration Procedure

**Initialize** $t^{(0)} = C_i$ (the first execution slot).

**Step k:** Compute

$$t^{(k)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t^{(k-1)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Termination:**
- If $t^{(k)} = t^{(k-1)}$ → fixed point reached, $R_i = t^{(k)}$.
- If $t^{(k)} > D_i$ → **not schedulable**, abort.
- If $t^{(k)} >$ previous max without convergence → **not schedulable**.

### Worked Numerical Example (TDA)

**Task Set:** $\tau_1, \tau_2$ with
- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 1, T_1 = 4, D_1 = 4$ (higher priority — shorter period)
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 2, T_2 = 6, D_2 = 6$

**Check $\tau_1$:** $R_1 = C_1 = 1 \leq 4$ ✓

**Check $\tau_2$:** $hp(2) = \{\tau_1\}$.

Iteration 0: $t^{(0)} = 2$

Iteration 1:
$$t^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

Iteration 2:
$$t^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

Fixed point reached: $R_2 = 3 \leq 6$ ✓ **Schedulable**.

Processor utilization: $U = 1/4 + 2/6 = 0.25 + 0.333 = 0.583 < 0.828$ (Liu-Layland bound for $n=2$). **Confirmed feasible.**

## 3.3 Why DMS is the Generalization of RMS

### Theorem (Audsley, 1990)
For a task set with $D_i \leq T_i$ for all $i$, **no fixed-priority algorithm can schedule a task set that DMS cannot**, provided priorities are assigned by *deadline order* (smallest $D_i$ → highest priority).

### Proof Idea
If two tasks $\tau_i$ and $\tau_j$ satisfy $D_i < D_j$ but $\tau_i$ has *higher* priority than $\tau_j$, then swapping their priorities **cannot worsen** the schedulability of either. The "audit" transformation repeatedly swaps to produce a deadline-monotonic ordering without breaking feasibility.

### Limit Case
If $D_i = T_i$ for all $i$, then $D_i < D_j \Leftrightarrow T_i < T_j$, so DMS becomes RMS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Some textbooks state that DMS is "better" than RMS. This is **false** in general. DMS is optimal for *constrained deadlines*; RMS is optimal for *implicit deadlines*. Neither dominates the other outside its regime.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | RMS | DMS |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Priority assigned by | Period $T_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ |
| Applicable to | Implicit deadlines ($D_i = T_i$) | Constrained deadlines ($D_i \leq T_i$) |
| Optimal for FPS? | Yes (when $D_i = T_i$) | Yes (when $D_i \leq T_i$) |
| Equivalent? | — | Equivalent to RMS when $D_i = T_i$ |
| Schedulability test | Liu-Layland, TDA, Hyperbolic | TDA only (Liu-Layland does NOT apply) |
| Industrial use | FreeRTOS, VxWorks default | AUTOSAR-OS (deadline-based config) |
| Key limitation | Fails for $D < T$ | Slightly more complex offline analysis |

## 3.5 Summary of Feasibility Tests

1. **Necessary Condition (Utilization):** $U \leq 1$
2. **Sufficient (Liu-Layland, RMS only):** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$
3. **Sufficient (Hyperbolic Bound, RMS only):** $\prod (1 + U_i) \leq 2$
4. **Exact (TDA, both RMS and DMS):** Iterate $w_i(t)$ and check $R_i \leq D_i$

## 4.1 Fixed-Priority Scheduling — High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
    I -->|Yes| J[Release Next Job at Period]
```

```mermaid
    A1 --> A2[Assign Priority P2 next T]
    A2 --> A3[Assign Priority Pn largest T]
    A3 --> O[Static Priority Table Built]
    O --> R[Ready for Runtime Scheduling]
```


```mermaid
flowchart TD

    Init --> Loop[Compute t new = C i + sum over hp of ceil t divided by T j times C j]
    Loop --> Check1{t new equals t?}
    Check1 -->|Yes Fixed Point| Result[R i = t new]
    Result --> Compare{R i less or equal D i?}
    Compare -->|Yes| Sched[Schedulable]
    Compare -->|No| NotSched[Not Schedulable]
    Check1 -->|No| Check2{t new greater than D i?}
    Check2 -->|Yes| NotSched
    Check2 -->|No| Loop
```

```mermaid
    AskD -->|No Constrained D less or equal T| UseDMS[Use Deadline Monotonic Scheduling]
    UseRMS --> Test1[Apply Liu Layland Bound or TDA]
    UseDMS --> Test2[Apply TDA only - Liu Layland not valid]
    Test1 --> Out1[Schedulable or Not]
    Test2 --> Out2[Schedulable or Not]
```

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fixed-Priority Scheduling (FPS) — Foundational Definition

**Fixed-Priority Scheduling (FPS)** is a class of *preemptive, priority-driven scheduling algorithms* used in **hard real-time systems**, where each task is assigned a **static, immutable priority** at design time (offline), and the scheduler always dispatches the **highest-priority runnable task** on the processor. The priorities never change at runtime — they are "frozen" at system design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fixed-priority scheduling is the *most widely deployed* class in commercial real-time operating systems (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS) because it is **simple, predictable, analyzable**, and has bounded overhead. It is the theoretical cornerstone of real-time scheduling theory established by **Liu and Layland (1973)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine an **emergency hospital triage room** with a single doctor:

- Patients keep arriving, each with a fixed "severity tag" assigned at the door (priority).
- The doctor **always treats the sickest patient first** (highest priority wins).
- A low-priority patient currently being examined is **interrupted** the moment a sicker patient arrives (preemption).
- The priority tag is **never reassigned** — once a patient is labeled "yellow," they stay yellow.

**In an RTOS:**
- The "doctor" is the **single CPU core**.
- The "patients" are the **periodic tasks** (sensor reading, motor control, communication).
- The "severity tag" is the **static priority number**.
- The "interruption" is **preemption**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** What *changes* between Rate-Monotonic and Deadline-Monotonic is **how the priority tag is generated** — based on **period (rate)** or **deadline**.

## 1.3 Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — The Classical Optimal Policy

### Formal Definition
**Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is a *static-priority preemptive* algorithm that assigns priorities **inversely proportional to the task period** (i.e., directly proportional to the task **rate** = 1/T).

$$P_i \propto \frac{1}{T_i} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad T_i \downarrow \Rightarrow P_i \uparrow$$

where $P_i$ is the priority of task $\tau_i$ and $T_i$ is its period. The **shorter the period, the higher the priority**.

### Liu & Layland Assumptions
RMS analysis assumes the canonical task model:

1. All tasks are **periodic** with period $T_i$.
2. All tasks are **independent** (no shared resources, no precedence).
3. The relative deadline equals the period: $D_i = T_i$ (implicit deadlines).
4. Worst-case execution time (WCET) $C_i$ is **known and constant**.
5. Zero **release jitter**; all tasks are released simultaneously at $t = 0$.
6. **Preemptive** scheduling on a single processor.
7. No shared resources causing unbounded priority inversion.

## 1.4 Deadline-Monotonic Scheduling (DMS) — The Generalized Optimal Policy

### Formal Definition
**Deadline-Monotonic Scheduling (DMS)** is a *static-priority preemptive* algorithm that assigns priorities **inversely proportional to the task's relative deadline**. The shorter the deadline, the higher the priority.

$$P_i \propto \frac{1}{D_i} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad D_i \downarrow \Rightarrow P_i \uparrow$$

### Why DMS Exists
RMS breaks down when $D_i < T_i$ (constrained deadlines). DMS is the **optimal fixed-priority algorithm** under the assumption $D_i \leq T_i$ for all tasks, and **collapses to RMS** when $D_i = T_i$ for all tasks.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** If the question says "deadline ≤ period" → use **DMS**. If "deadline = period" → use **RMS** (they are identical). Many students lose marks by using RMS for constrained deadlines — *do not do this!*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Rate-Monotonic Scheduling — Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 Optimality of RMS (Liu & Layland, 1973)
Among all static-priority algorithms, **no other fixed-priority assignment can schedule a task set that RMS cannot**, provided $D_i = T_i$. This is the **optimality theorem**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proof Sketch:** If a task set is schedulable under some priority assignment, the "critical instant" analysis shows that the *highest-priority task first* assignment (RMS for $D_i = T_i$) is at least as feasible. The critical instant for any task is when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with it.

### 2.1.2 Critical Instant & Worst-Case Response Time
The **critical instant** of a task $\tau_i$ is the time at which its **worst-case response time** is maximized. For FPS, this occurs when $\tau_i$ is released *simultaneously* with all higher-priority tasks.

### 2.1.3 Processor Utilization
The **processor utilization factor** $U$ is the fraction of CPU time consumed:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$$

This is a scalar in $[0, 1]$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Condition / Meaning |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Processor Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i$ | Total CPU demand; must be $\leq 1$ |
| 2 | Liu-Layland Sufficient Test | $U \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | Sufficient (not necessary) for RMS schedulability |
| 3 | Asymptotic Bound | $\lim_{n \to \infty} U_{\text{max}} = \ln(2) \approx 0.693$ | RMS theoretical limit |
| 4 | Small-$n$ Bounds (RMS) | $n=1{:}\;1.000$ ; $n=2{:}\;0.828$ ; $n=3{:}\;0.779$ ; $n=4{:}\;0.756$ | Sufficient utilization thresholds |
| 5 | Hyperbolic Bound (Necessary + Sufficient) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 + U_i) \leq 2$ | Equivalent to $\sum \ln(1+U_i) \leq \ln 2$ |
| 6 | Time Demand $w_i(t)$ | $w_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Demand of $\tau_i$ in interval $[0,t]$ |
| 7 | Exact Schedulability (TDA) | $R_i = w_i(R_i)$ and $R_i \leq D_i$ | Fixed-point iteration |
| 8 | DMS Optimality | If $D_i \leq T_i \; \forall i$ → DMS is optimal FPS | Generalizes RMS |
| 9 | RMS Equivalent DMS condition | $D_i = T_i \; \forall i$ | When deadlines equal periods |
| 10 | Worst-Case Response Time | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil R_i / T_j \right\rceil C_j$ | Iterative computation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used:** $hp(i)$ = set of tasks with **higher priority** than $\tau_i$ ; $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ = **ceiling** function (smallest integer $\geq$ argument).

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Fixed-Priority Scheduling

| Domain | Application | Why FPS? |
|--------|-------------|----------|
| **Avionics** (DO-178C) | Flight control, autopilot | Certifiability — bounded analysis |
| **Automotive** (AUTOSAR-OS) | Engine control, ABS | Predictable jitter, simple runtime |
| **Industrial Robotics** | Servo loops | Shortest period = tightest loop |
| **Medical Devices** | Pacemaker, infusion pump | Hard real-time guarantees |
| **Satellite / Space** | Attitude control | Radiation tolerance + simple scheduler |

## 2.4 Why Fixed Priority is "Industry-Standard"?

- **Offline analysis**: Priorities and feasibility can be verified at design time.
- **Low runtime overhead**: A simple priority queue suffices.
- **Hardware-friendly**: Maps directly to interrupt priority levels.
- **Composable**: Adding a new task only requires re-checking its higher-priority interferers.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Liu-Layland Sufficient Utilization Bound

### Step 1 — Define the Critical Instant
The critical instant for task $\tau_k$ is the time when **all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously** with $\tau_k$. At this moment, the **worst-case interference** on $\tau_k$ occurs.

### Step 2 — Formulate the Worst-Case Computation Demand
In any time interval of length $t$ starting at a critical instant, the number of jobs of a higher-priority task $\tau_j$ released is $\left\lceil t / T_j \right\rceil$. The total execution time demanded by all higher-priority tasks plus $\tau_k$ itself is:

$$w_k(t) = C_k + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Step 3 — Critical Condition for Schedulability
A necessary and sufficient condition for $\tau_k$ to meet its deadline is that there exists some $t \in [0, T_k]$ such that:

$$w_k(t) \leq t$$

The smallest such $t$ is the **worst-case response time** $R_k$. We need $R_k \leq T_k$ (since $D_k = T_k$ under RMS).

### Step 4 — Upper-Bound by Replacing Ceilings with Reals
To get a closed-form sufficient test, we use the loose upper bound $\left\lceil t/T_j \right\rceil \leq t/T_j + 1$ only at the *critical instant* of the longest-priority task. The classical proof uses induction on $n$.

### Step 5 — Final Sufficient Condition
After induction:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

### Step 6 — Asymptotic Behavior
Take the limit:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931$$

The substitution $2^{1/n} = e^{\ln(2)/n}$ and applying $e^x \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$ gives the limit.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Time Demand Analysis (TDA) Iteration

### Setup
For a given task $\tau_i$, we wish to find the smallest $t$ such that $w_i(t) = t$, where:

$$w_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

### Iteration Procedure

**Initialize** $t^{(0)} = C_i$ (the first execution slot).

**Step k:** Compute

$$t^{(k)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t^{(k-1)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Termination:**
- If $t^{(k)} = t^{(k-1)}$ → fixed point reached, $R_i = t^{(k)}$.
- If $t^{(k)} > D_i$ → **not schedulable**, abort.
- If $t^{(k)} >$ previous max without convergence → **not schedulable**.

### Worked Numerical Example (TDA)

**Task Set:** $\tau_1, \tau_2$ with
- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 1, T_1 = 4, D_1 = 4$ (higher priority — shorter period)
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 2, T_2 = 6, D_2 = 6$

**Check $\tau_1$:** $R_1 = C_1 = 1 \leq 4$ ✓

**Check $\tau_2$:** $hp(2) = \{\tau_1\}$.

Iteration 0: $t^{(0)} = 2$

Iteration 1:
$$t^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

Iteration 2:
$$t^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3$$

Fixed point reached: $R_2 = 3 \leq 6$ ✓ **Schedulable**.

Processor utilization: $U = 1/4 + 2/6 = 0.25 + 0.333 = 0.583 < 0.828$ (Liu-Layland bound for $n=2$). **Confirmed feasible.**

## 3.3 Why DMS is the Generalization of RMS

### Theorem (Audsley, 1990)
For a task set with $D_i \leq T_i$ for all $i$, **no fixed-priority algorithm can schedule a task set that DMS cannot**, provided priorities are assigned by *deadline order* (smallest $D_i$ → highest priority).

### Proof Idea
If two tasks $\tau_i$ and $\tau_j$ satisfy $D_i < D_j$ but $\tau_i$ has *higher* priority than $\tau_j$, then swapping their priorities **cannot worsen** the schedulability of either. The "audit" transformation repeatedly swaps to produce a deadline-monotonic ordering without breaking feasibility.

### Limit Case
If $D_i = T_i$ for all $i$, then $D_i < D_j \Leftrightarrow T_i < T_j$, so DMS becomes RMS.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Some textbooks state that DMS is "better" than RMS. This is **false** in general. DMS is optimal for *constrained deadlines*; RMS is optimal for *implicit deadlines*. Neither dominates the other outside its regime.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | RMS | DMS |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Priority assigned by | Period $T_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ |
| Applicable to | Implicit deadlines ($D_i = T_i$) | Constrained deadlines ($D_i \leq T_i$) |
| Optimal for FPS? | Yes (when $D_i = T_i$) | Yes (when $D_i \leq T_i$) |
| Equivalent? | — | Equivalent to RMS when $D_i = T_i$ |
| Schedulability test | Liu-Layland, TDA, Hyperbolic | TDA only (Liu-Layland does NOT apply) |
| Industrial use | FreeRTOS, VxWorks default | AUTOSAR-OS (deadline-based config) |
| Key limitation | Fails for $D < T$ | Slightly more complex offline analysis |

1. **Necessary Condition (Utilization):** $U \leq 1$
2. **Sufficient (Liu-Layland, RMS only):** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$
3. **Sufficient (Hyperbolic Bound, RMS only):** $\prod (1 + U_i) \leq 2$
