- $C_i^{eff} = C_i + m_i \cdot T_{cs}$

### 🛠️ Key RTOS Terminology
- **PCB / TCB** — Process / Task Control Block
- **ISR** — Interrupt Service Routine
- **PIP** — Priority Inheritance Protocol
- **PCP** — Priority Ceiling Protocol
- **DMS** — Deadline Monotonic Scheduling
- **RMS** — Rate Monotonic Scheduling
- **EDF** — Earliest Deadline First
- **LLF** — Least Laxity First
- **WCET** — Worst-Case Execution Time
- **BCET** — Best-Case Execution Time
- **DMS** — Deadline Monotonic Scheduling

### 🎯 Top 3 KTU 2024 Likely Questions
1. "Explain priority inheritance protocol with a diagram and example." (7 marks, almost certain)
2. "A task set is given. Check schedulability using RMS bound and RTE." (7–14 marks, every year)
3. "Compare fixed-priority and dynamic-priority preemptive scheduling." (7–14 marks, very high probability)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** Always draw a **timeline / Gantt chart** when discussing preemption — it is the single most effective way to communicate preemption logic in a 14-mark answer and earns 2–3 extra marks for *clarity of presentation*, which KTU examiners explicitly reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Round Robin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Round Robin # Round Robin Scheduling — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > **Round Robin (RR) Scheduling** is a **preemptive, time-sharing CPU scheduling algorithm** in which each process in the ready queue is assigned a fixed time interval called a **Time Quantum (TQ)** or **Time Slice**. The CPU scheduler allocates the processor to the process at the head of the circular ready queue, executes it for at most one time quantum, and then either **preempts** it (if its burst time exceeds the quantum) or **dispatches** the next process in the queue. It is fundamentally designed for **fairness, starvation-freedom, and uniform response time** in multi-user, interactive, and real-time embedded systems. The algorithm is also referred to as **cyclic executive** or **time-slicing scheduler** in classical RTOS literature. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] **The Cafeteria Token System Analogy 🍽️** > Imagine a busy college canteen with **5 food counters** (processes) sharing **one common dining hall** (the CPU). A strict rule is imposed: *every counter gets exactly 2 minutes to serve its customers*. If a counter finishes early, it voluntarily yields the dining hall. If a counter exceeds 2 minutes, the hall manager **rings a bell** and forces it to stop, sending it to the back of the queue. This way, no counter starves, and every customer (instruction) is treated fairly. The **2-minute window is the Time Quantum**, the **queue is the Ready Queue**, and the **bell is the timer interrupt** issued by the RTOS kernel. ## 1.3 Core Architectural Components | Component | Role in Round Robin | |---|---| | **Ready Queue** | A **circular FIFO queue** holding all runnable processes | | **Time Quantum (TQ)** | The **fixed duration** (in milliseconds / ticks) allocated per process | | **Dispatch Latency** | Time taken by scheduler to perform context switch | | **Timer Tick / Interrupt** | Hardware trigger that fires after every quantum to preempt CPU | | **Process Control Block (PCB)** | Stores the state, remaining burst time, and context of every process | > [!TIP] **KTU Quick-Recall Mnemonic**: **"F**air **R**otating **Q**ueue" → **F**IFO + **R**ound + **Q**uantum. # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic The Round Robin scheduler follows a deterministic, cyclic decision loop: 1. **Queue Initialization**: On system boot, the kernel populates the **circular ready queue** with all processes whose state is `READY`. 2. **Dispatcher Action**: The process at the **head of the queue** is loaded into the CPU (context restore). 3. **Timer Arming**: A **programmable interval timer (PIT)** is set to expire after exactly **one Time Quantum (TQ)**. 4. **Execution Phase**: The process executes until any one of the following three terminating conditions occurs: - **(a) Quantum Expiry**: The timer interrupt fires → the process is **preempted**, its PCB updated with remaining burst time, and re-appended to the **tail** of the ready queue. - **(b) Voluntary Yield**: The process issues a system call like `I/O wait`, `sleep()`, or `sem_wait()` → moves to `BLOCKED` queue. - **(c) Completion**: Burst time reaches zero → process transitions to `TERMINATED` state and is removed from the system. 5. **Repeat**: Steps 2–4 are repeated indefinitely until the ready queue is empty. > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Design Insight** > Round Robin is essentially **FCFS with preemption**. When TQ → ∞, RR degenerates to FCFS. When TQ → 0, the system performs only context switches (thrashing). Hence, **TQ selection is the most critical design decision** in RR. ## 2.2 Influence of Time Quantum on System Performance | Time Quantum (TQ) | Behaviour | Drawback | |---|---|---| | **Very Large (TQ >> max burst)** | Behaves like **FCFS** | Poor response time for short jobs | | **Very Small (TQ ≈ 0)** | **Excessive context switches** | CPU thrashing, throughput collapses | | **Optimal (TQ ≈ 80% of CPU bursts finish within 1 quantum)** | Balanced throughput + response | Requires profiling burst-time distribution | The empirical rule (from Tanenbaum): choose TQ such that **~80% of CPU bursts are shorter than the quantum**. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Performance Metrics | Metric | Mathematical Definition | Units | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | Turnaround Time (TAT) | $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$ | ms | Time from arrival to completion | | Waiting Time (WT) | $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$ | ms | Time spent in ready queue | | Response Time (RT) | $RT_i = \text{First CPU Allocation}_i - AT_i$ | ms | Critical for real-time systems | | Normalized TAT | $NTAT_i = TAT_i / BT_i$ | dimensionless | Indicates relative delay | | Average TAT | $\overline{TAT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} TAT_i$ | ms | KTU-favourite metric | | Average WT | $\overline{WT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} WT_i$ | ms | KTU-favourite metric | | Throughput | $\Theta = \frac{n}{\text{Total Simulation Time}}$ | processes/ms | Higher is better | | CPU Utilization | $U = \frac{\sum BT_i}{\text{Total Time} + n_{cs} \cdot t_{cs}}$ | % | $t_{cs}$ = context switch time | | Context Switch Overhead | $\text{OH} = \frac{n_{cs} \cdot t_{cs}}{T_{total}}$ | % | Penalizes very small TQ | > [!TIP] **KTU Pitfall**: When calculating **Waiting Time in RR**, you MUST subtract the *actual execution* of the process from its TAT — do NOT simply use TAT − original BT if remaining BT was modified. Always re-derive from Gantt chart. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Linux Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS)** uses a variant of weighted Round Robin with virtual runtime. - **VxWorks** (RTOS used in NASA Mars rovers, Boeing 787) implements RR as the default task scheduler. - **FreeRTOS** supports `configUSE_TIME_SLICING = 1` enabling RR between equal-priority tasks. - **Network routers** use RR to service packets from multiple QoS queues. - **Embedded automotive ECUs** (AUTOSAR OS) use RR in mixed-criticality scheduling. # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical ## 3.1 Solved KTU-Style Numerical (Full Step-by-Step) > [!NOTE] **Problem Statement** > Consider the following 4 processes arriving at time 0, with CPU burst times given below. Apply **Round Robin Scheduling** with **Time Quantum = 2 ms**. Compute the **Average Waiting Time** and **Average Turnaround Time**. | Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) | |---|---|---| | P1 | 0 | 5 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | | P3 | 2 | 2 | | P4 | 3 | 1 | ### Step 1: Build Initial Ready Queue and Execute First Quantum At **t = 0**, only **P1** has arrived (AT = 0). It occupies the CPU. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P1 executes from } t &= 0 \text{ to } t = 2 \\ \text{Remaining BT of P1} &= 5 - 2 = 3 \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** Quantum = 2 ms. P1 consumed 2 ms; remainder = original − consumed = 5 − 2 = 3 ms. ### Step 2: Account for New Arrivals at t = 2 By t = 2, both **P2** (AT = 1) and **P3** (AT = 2) have arrived. **P4** has not (AT = 3). Updated Ready Queue (FIFO order): **[P2, P3, P1]** ### Step 3: Execute P2 for One Quantum $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P2 executes from } t &= 2 \text{ to } t = 4 \\ \text{Remaining BT of P2} &= 4 - 2 = 2 \end{aligned} $$ At t = 4, P4 (AT = 3) has now arrived. Updated Queue: **[P3, P1, P4, P2]** ### Step 4: Execute P3 — Completes Within Quantum $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P3 executes from } t &= 4 \text{ to } t = 5 \\ \text{Remaining BT of P3} &= 2 - 1 = 1 \\ \text{Next quantum boundary } (t=6) \text{ is not reached} \Rightarrow \text{P3 COMPLETES at } t = 5 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** P3 needed only 1 more ms, so it voluntarily finishes. Updated Queue: **[P1, P4, P2]** ### Step 5: Execute P1 — Completes Within Quantum $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P1 executes from } t &= 5 \text{ to } t = 6 \\ \text{Remaining BT of P1} &= 3 - 1 = 2 \\ \text{Original quantum is } 2 \text{ ms, P1 needs exactly } 2 \Rightarrow \text{P1 COMPLETES at } t = 6 \end{aligned} $$ Updated Queue: **[P4, P2]** ### Step 6: Execute P4 — Completes Within Quantum $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P4 executes from } t &= 6 \text{ to } t = 7 \\ \text{Remaining BT of P4} &= 1 - 1 = 0 \Rightarrow \text{P4 COMPLETES at } t = 7 \end{aligned} $$ Updated Queue: **[P2]** ### Step 7: Execute P2 to Completion $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P2 executes from } t &= 7 \text{ to } t = 9 \\ \text{Remaining BT of P2} &= 2 - 2 = 0 \Rightarrow \text{P2 COMPLETES at } t = 9 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 8: Final Gantt Chart Summary | Time | 0 → 2 | 2 → 4 | 4 → 5 | 5 → 6 | 6 → 7 | 7 → 9 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Process | P1 | P2 | P3 | P1 | P4 | P2 | ### Step 9: Compute Completion, Turnaround, and Waiting Times | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT = CT − AT | WT = TAT − BT | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | P1 | 0 | 5 | 6 | 6 | 1 | | P2 | 1 | 4 | 9 | 8 | 4 | | P3 | 2 | 2 | 5 | 3 | 1 | | P4 | 3 | 1 | 7 | 4 | 3 | ### Step 10: Compute Averages $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{6 + 8 + 3 + 4}{4} = \frac{21}{4} = 5.25 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{1 + 4 + 1 + 3}{4} = \frac{9}{4} = 2.25 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Final Answer**: Average Turnaround Time = **5.25 ms**, Average Waiting Time = **2.25 ms**. ### Step 11: Count Context Switches A context switch occurs at every quantum boundary (excluding the first dispatch and final completion). Here: t = 2, 4, 5, 6, 7 → **5 context switches**. ## 3.2 Comparison Matrix — RR vs Other Scheduling Algorithms | Criterion | FCFS | SJF (Non-Preemptive) | Priority | **Round Robin** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Preemption | No | No | Optional | **Yes (Timer-driven)** | | Starvation Possible | No | Yes (long jobs) | Yes (low priority) | **No** | | Fairness | Low | Low | Low | **High** | | Best for | Batch | Batch | Real-time | **Time-sharing / RTOS** | | Complexity | $O(1)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ dispatch | | Response Time | Poor | Excellent for short | Variable | **Predictable** | | Overhead | Very Low | Medium | Medium | **High (context switches)** | # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Gantt Chart — Visualizing the Above Numerical ```mermaid gantt title Round Robin Scheduling Gantt Chart (TQ = 2 ms) dateFormat X axisFormat %S section P1 P1 first slice :p1a, 0, 2 P1 second slice :p1b, 5, 1 section P2 P2 first slice :p2a, 2, 2 P2 second slice :p2b, 7, 2 section P3 P3 completes :p3, 4, 1 section P4 P4 completes :p4, 6, 1 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of RR Scheduler ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hardware Timer
Programmable Interval Timer PIT] -->|Tick Interrupt| B[Interrupt Service Routine ISR] B --> C[Scheduler Dispatcher] C --> D{Ready Queue
Empty?} D -- Yes --> E[CPU Idle
HALT State] D -- No --> F[Dequeue Head Process] F --> G[Context Restore
Load PCB Registers] G --> H[CPU Execution
of Process] H --> I{Process Event} I -->|Quantum Expired| J[Save Context to PCB] I -->|I/O or Wait| K[Move to Blocked Queue] I -->|Completed| L[Move to Terminated List] J --> M[Re-enqueue at Tail of Ready Queue] M --> D K --> D L --> D ``` ## 4.3 Circular Ready Queue Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR RQHead((Head
Pointer)) --> P2Proc[P2
Rem BT: 2] P2Proc --> P3Proc[P3
Rem BT: 0] P3Proc --> P1Proc[P1
Rem BT: 3] P1Proc --> P4Proc[P4
Rem BT: 1] P4Proc --> RQHead ``` ## 4.4 State Transition Diagram for a Process Under RR ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> New New --> Ready: Admitted Ready --> Running: Dispatched by Scheduler Running --> Ready: Quantum Expired or Preempted Running --> Blocked: I/O or Event Wait Blocked --> Ready: I/O Completion or Event Signal Running --> Exit: Burst Time Complete Exit --> [*] ``` # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define Round Robin Scheduling. What is a Time Quantum? How does its size affect system performance? **Model Answer (10 lines):** Round Robin Scheduling is a **preemptive CPU scheduling algorithm** designed for time-sharing systems where each process is assigned a fixed time slice called the **Time Quantum (TQ)**. The process at the head of the circular ready queue executes for at most one TQ. If it does not finish, it is preempted and re-queued at the tail. The **size of TQ critically affects performance**: a **very large TQ** degenerates behaviour to FCFS (poor response), while a **very small TQ** causes excessive context switches, reducing throughput (CPU thrashing). An **optimal TQ** is one in which approximately 80% of CPU bursts are shorter than the quantum, giving a balance between fairness and throughput. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Why is Round Robin preferred over FCFS for time-sharing and interactive RTOS environments? **Model Answer (8 lines):** Round Robin overcomes the major drawback of FCFS — the **convoy effect** — where a single long process monopolizes the CPU and starves short interactive jobs. In RR, every process gets **equal CPU share** at regular intervals, ensuring **predictable and bounded response time**. This is essential for interactive applications (e.g., GUI responsiveness) and real-time systems where deadline adherence requires guaranteed CPU access. FCFS, being non-preemptive, cannot guarantee such responsiveness, making RR the de facto choice for modern time-sharing kernels. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` > Consider the following set of processes with their arrival and burst times. Apply **Round Robin Scheduling** with Time Quantum = **3 ms**. | Process | Arrival Time | Burst Time | |---|---|---| | P1 | 0 | 7 | | P2 | 1 | 5 | | P3 | 2 | 3 | | P4 | 3 | 1 | | P5 | 4 | 2 | **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the Gantt chart and compute the **waiting time** and **turnaround time** for each process. **(b) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **average waiting time**, **average turnaround time**, and the **number of context switches**. Discuss how the choice of time quantum influences these metrics. #### Model Solution for (a): **Step 1 — Gantt Chart Construction (TQ = 3):** - t = 0 → 3: P1 runs (AT=0 only). P1 rem = 7 − 3 = 4. Queue: [P2, P3, P1] - t = 3 → 6: P2 runs (3 arrived). P2 rem = 5 − 3 = 2. Queue: [P3, P1, P4, P2] - t = 6 → 9: P3 runs (P3 rem=0, P4=1, P5=2). P3 COMPLETES at t=6 (wait — burst is 3, so runs 3 ms to t=6). Queue: [P1, P4, P5, P2] - t = 6 → 9: P1 runs. P1 rem = 4 − 3 = 1. Queue: [P4, P5, P2, P1] - t = 9 → 10: P4 runs (needs 1 ms only). P4 COMPLETES at t=10. Queue: [P5, P2, P1] - t = 10 → 12: P5 runs (needs 2 ms). P5 COMPLETES at t=12. Queue: [P2, P1] - t = 12 → 14: P2 runs (rem=2). P2 COMPLETES at t=14. Queue: [P1] - t = 14 → 15: P1 runs (rem=1). P1 COMPLETES at t=15. Queue: [ ] **Final Gantt Chart:** | Slot | 0–3 | 3–6 | 6–9 | 9–10 | 10–12 | 12–14 | 14–15 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Process | P1 | P2 | P1 | P4 | P5 | P2 | P1 | **Step 2 — Computation Table:** | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT = CT − AT | WT = TAT − BT | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | P1 | 0 | 7 | 15 | 15 | 8 | | P2 | 1 | 5 | 14 | 13 | 8 | | P3 | 2 | 3 | 6 | 4 | 1 | | P4 | 3 | 1 | 10 | 7 | 6 | | P5 | 4 | 2 | 12 | 8 | 6 | #### Model Solution for (b): $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{TAT} &= \frac{15 + 13 + 4 + 7 + 8}{5} = \frac{47}{5} = 9.4 \text{ ms} \\ \overline{WT} &= \frac{8 + 8 + 1 + 6 + 6}{5} = \frac{29}{5} = 5.8 \text{ ms} \\ \text{Context Switches} &= 6 \text{ (at } t = 3, 6, 9, 10, 12, 14\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Discussion on Time Quantum Influence:** - **Smaller TQ** (e.g., 1 ms) → higher context switch count, lower throughput, better response time. - **Larger TQ** (e.g., 10 ms) → fewer context switches, higher throughput, but degraded fairness (acts like FCFS). - The optimal TQ is empirically chosen such that **~80% of CPU bursts are shorter than the quantum**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Round Robin Scheduling algorithm** with the help of a neat diagram. Discuss its **advantages and disadvantages** in real-time operating systems. > **(b) [7 Marks]** Consider 4 processes P1, P2, P3, P4 with arrival times 0, 2, 4, 5 and burst times 6, 3, 1, 4 respectively. Apply RR with TQ = 2 and compute the **average waiting time** and **context switch count**. #### Model Solution for (a): **Algorithm Explanation:** (refer to Section 1.1 and 2.1 above) **Neat Diagram:** ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Process P1
AT=0 BT=6] --> Q((Ready Queue)) B[Process P2
AT=2 BT=3] --> Q C[Process P3
AT=4 BT=1] --> Q D[Process P4
AT=5 BT=4] --> Q Q -->|Dispatch| CPU[CPU Core] CPU -->|TQ Expired| Q ``` **Advantages:** 1. **Starvation-free** — every process gets CPU access. 2. **Fair CPU share** — suitable for multi-user systems. 3. **Predictable response time** — critical for soft real-time tasks. 4. **Simple implementation** — only a FIFO queue and timer are needed. 5. **No priority inversion issues** inherent to priority scheduling. **Disadvantages:** 1. **Higher context-switch overhead** with very small quantum. 2. **Not optimal for batch systems** with predominantly long jobs. 3. **Performance depends heavily on quantum size selection.** 4. **No priority differentiation** — all processes treated equally (unless modified). 5. **Larger turnaround time for long jobs** compared to SJF. #### Model Solution for (b): **Given:** TQ = 2 ms. AT: P1=0, P2=2, P3=4, P4=5. BT: P1=6, P2=3, P3=1, P4=4. **Gantt Chart Construction:** - t=0 → 2: P1 runs (rem=4). Queue: [P1] (no one else arrived) - t=2 → 4: P1 runs (P2 arrived at 2). P1 rem=2. Queue: [P2, P1] - t=4 → 5: P2 runs (P3 arrived at 4). P2 rem=1. P2 finishes at t=5 (needs only 1). Queue: [P1, P3] - t=5 → 7: P1 runs (P4 arrived at 5). P1 rem=0, COMPLETES at t=7 (needs 2). Queue: [P3, P4] - t=7 → 8: P3 runs (rem=0). COMPLETES at t=8. Queue: [P4] - t=8 → 10: P4 runs (rem=2). COMPLETES at t=10. **Final Gantt Chart:** | Slot | 0–2 | 2–4 | 4–5 | 5–7 | 7–8 | 8–10 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Process | P1 | P1 | P2 | P1 | P3 | P4 | **Computation Table:** | Process | AT | BT | CT | TAT | WT | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | P1 | 0 | 6 | 7 | 7 | 1 | | P2 | 2 | 3 | 5 | 3 | 0 | | P3 | 4 | 1 | 8 | 4 | 3 | | P4 | 5 | 4 | 10 | 5 | 1 | $$ \begin{aligned} \overline{WT} &= \frac{1 + 0 + 3 + 1}{4} = \frac{5}{4} = 1.25 \text{ ms} \\ \text{Context Switches} &= 5 \text{ (at } t = 2, 4, 5, 7, 8\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Wrong Queue Order at t=0**: Many students include all 4 processes in the queue from t=0. The correct approach is to **only enqueue processes that have actually arrived** by the current time. > 2. **Forgetting the "completion-within-quantum" rule**: A process must NOT be re-queued if it finishes mid-quantum. Skipping this changes CT values and hence TAT/WT drastically. > 3. **Counting wrong context switches**: The first dispatch is NOT a context switch (system initialization). Only subsequent preemptions count. Also, completion is a switch. > 4. **Wrong WT formula**: In RR, WT ≠ TAT − original BT for processes whose execution was split across multiple quanta. Always recompute from Gantt chart segments. > 5. **Unit mismatch**: KTU expects all times in **ms** (or whatever unit the question specifies). Writing "1.25" without units loses 0.5 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀 > [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day** - ✅ **RR is preemptive FCFS** — always remember the relationship between TQ size and FCFS behaviour. - ✅ **Time Quantum (TQ)** is the soul of RR — choose wisely (80% rule). - ✅ **Ready Queue is circular FIFO** — head gets dispatched, tail gets re-queued. - ✅ **Three termination triggers**: Quantum expiry (preempt), I/O wait (block), Burst completion (terminate). - ✅ **WT = TAT − BT** is the master formula — but verify via Gantt chart. - ✅ **TAT = CT − AT** — always start with completion time extraction. - ✅ **Response Time** = first CPU allocation − AT (often asked in Part A). - ✅ **Context Switch count** = number of preemptions + completions (excluding first dispatch). - ✅ **Starvation-free** is the biggest advantage — remember to state it explicitly. - ✅ **Convoy effect** is FCFS's weakness that RR solves. - ✅ **Very small TQ → thrashing**; **very large TQ → FCFS behaviour**. - ✅ **Linux CFS, FreeRTOS, VxWorks** are real-world RR implementers — use them as RTOS application examples. - ✅ Always draw the **Gantt chart** in Part B answers — it carries partial marks even if calculations are slightly off. - ✅ Mention **dispatcher, timer interrupt, and PCB** in algorithm explanation — these are KTU-favourite keywords.

topics.title: Priority Inversion and Inheritance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Priority Inversion and Inheritance # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Priority Inversion (PI)** is a scheduling anomaly in real-time and multitasking systems in which a **higher-priority task** is **indirectly preempted** by a **lower-priority task** — effectively "inverting" the relative priorities of the two tasks, and thereby violating the priority-ordering assumption of any fixed/ dynamic priority preemptive scheduler. In the KTU 2024 scheme context (PERAT757 – Real Time Operating Systems), Priority Inversion is classified under **Synchronization Protocols** because it arises specifically when tasks share **mutually exclusive resources** (semaphores, mutexes, shared memory, I/O registers) accessed under the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** or **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** threat model. The phenomenon was formally characterized by **Lui Sha, Ragunathan Rajkumar, and John Lehoczky** in their seminal 1990 IEEE Transactions on Computers paper *"Priority Inheritance Protocols: An Approach to Real-Time Synchronization"*. > [!NOTE] > **Key Metric (KTU Examiner's Term):** The duration for which priority inversion persists is called the **Blocking Time (B)** of the higher-priority task, and the maximum value across all tasks directly impacts **schedulability** via the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** equations. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Three-Traffic-Lane Problem" 🚗🚗🚗 Imagine three cars at a traffic signal: | Car | Driver (Priority) | Lane | |-----|-------------------|------| | A | Ambulance Driver (HIGH) | Lane 1 | | B | VIP Minister's Car (MEDIUM) | Lane 2 | | C | Truck Driver (LOW) | Lane 3 | **Scenario:** 1. Truck C enters a narrow one-lane tunnel first (locks the resource). 2. Ambulance A arrives — but it cannot enter because C is blocking the tunnel (resource held). 3. VIP B arrives — by the rules of the road, B *can overtake* the truck, so B enters the tunnel and blocks A even further. 4. **Result:** A HIGH-priority ambulance (A) is waiting behind a MEDIUM-priority VIP car (B), which is waiting behind a LOW-priority truck (C). The priorities are **INVERTED**. This is exactly what happens in an RTOS when: - **Task L (low)** locks a mutex, - **Task H (high)** tries to lock the same mutex and blocks, - **Task M (medium)** preempts Task L (because M has higher priority than L), thus preventing L from releasing the mutex and unblocking H. **The medium-priority task — which has nothing to do with the shared resource — delays the high-priority task.** This is the textbook Priority Inversion problem. --- ## 1.3 Bounded vs Unbounded Priority Inversion > [!NOTE] > **Two KTU-Recognized Variants:** | Variant | Definition | Bounded? | |---------|-----------|----------| | **Bounded Priority Inversion** | Occurs naturally when a high-priority task blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task that is actively executing. | ✅ Yes — duration = execution time of the lower-priority critical section. | | **Unbounded Priority Inversion** | Occurs when the lower-priority task is preempted by *any number* of medium-priority tasks during its critical section. | ❌ No — duration is **unbounded** because the number of medium tasks is theoretically infinite. | The **unbounded** variant caused the famous **Mars Pathfinder spacecraft anomaly (July 4, 1997)** — a real KTU viva-favorite case study. --- ## 1.4 Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) — Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** A synchronization protocol in which, when a high-priority task blocks on a resource held by a lower-priority task, the lower-priority task **temporarily inherits** the priority of the highest-priority task waiting on that resource. This inheritance persists until the resource is released, after which the task reverts to its original priority. The protocol was designed by **Sha, Rajkumar, and Lehoczky (1990)** to **bound** the priority inversion duration to a known, schedulable quantity. --- ## 1.5 Standard KTU Notation Table | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |--------|---------|--------------| | $T_i$ | i-th task in the task set | — | | $P_i$ | Original (assigned) priority of task $T_i$ | Integer (1 = highest) | | $H_i$ | Effective (current) priority of task $T_i$ under PIP | Integer | | $R_i$ | A shared resource | — | | $C_i$ | Worst-case execution time of $T_i$ | Time units | | $B_i$ | Worst-case blocking time of $T_i$ | Time units | | $J_i$ | Release jitter of $T_i$ | Time units | | $D_i$ | Relative deadline of $T_i$ | Time units | > Bold items (e.g., **blocking time**, **priority inversion**) are KTU examiner's favorite scoring keywords. # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Conditions — When Does Priority Inversion Occur? Priority Inversion occurs when **ALL** of the following three conditions are satisfied simultaneously: 1. **Shared Resource (Mutual Exclusion):** Two or more tasks share at least one resource protected by a mutex/semaphore. 2. **Preemptive Scheduler:** The OS uses a **preemptive priority-based scheduler** (the de facto KTU standard for RTOS). 3. **Heterogeneous Priorities:** At least one task of *intermediate* priority exists between the priorities of the locking task and the highest-priority task attempting to lock the same resource. --- ## 2.2 The Three-Step Operational Trace (How Inversion Happens) Let the task set be $\tau = \{T_H, T_M, T_L\}$ with priorities $P_H > P_M > P_L$ (i.e., $T_H$ is highest priority). **Step 1 — Initial Lock:** $T_L$ starts execution, enters critical section, locks mutex on shared resource $R_x$. **Step 2 — Preemption by High-Priority Task:** $T_H$ becomes ready, preempts $T_L$, attempts to lock the same mutex $R_x$. The mutex is already locked by $T_L$, so $T_H$ **blocks** and is placed on the mutex's wait queue. **Step 3 — Preemption by Medium-Priority Task (THE INVERSION):** $T_M$ becomes ready. Since $T_L$ is currently the only *running* task (because $T_H$ is blocked), and $T_M$ has higher priority than $T_L$, the scheduler preempts $T_L$ and dispatches $T_M$. Now $T_H$ is forced to wait for $T_M$ to finish, even though $T_M$ has **no relationship** with resource $R_x$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Core "Why":** Inversion duration is unbounded because $T_M$ (and any number of medium tasks) can repeatedly preempt $T_L$ — extending $T_H$'s blocking time indefinitely until $T_L$ finally re-acquires the CPU and releases the mutex. --- ## 2.3 Priority Inheritance Protocol — Operational Rules (KTU Board Exam Favorites) PIP operates under **four canonical rules**. Memorize these for full marks. > [!NOTE] > **Rule 1 — Scheduling Rule:** > Always dispatch the **ready task with the highest effective (current) priority** $H_i$. > [!NOTE] > **Rule 2 — Inheritance Rule (The Heart of PIP):** > When task $T_i$ **blocks** task $T_k$ on resource $R_x$, the OS raises the effective priority of $T_i$ to $\max(P_i, P_k)$. This inheritance is **transitive** along the wait-for chain. > [!NOTE] > **Rule 3 — Exit Rule (Critical Section Completion):** > When $T_i$ exits the critical section, it releases $R_x$. If there are tasks waiting on $R_x$, the highest-priority waiter inherits its original priority back. If there is a chain of inherited priorities, $T_i$ reverts to the **highest priority among the remaining tasks it blocks** (or its original priority if no one is left). > [!NOTE] > **Rule 4 — Protocol Rule (Resource Acquisition):** > A task $T_i$ may lock resource $R_x$ **only if** it is not currently held by any other task. (This is just normal mutex behavior — restated for completeness.) --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions with ~80% probability.** | # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | KTU Use Case | |---|--------------------|---------|--------------| | 1 | $B_i \leq \sum_{k=1}^{m_i} \text{CS}_{i,k}^{\max}$ | Blocking time is bounded by the sum of the *longest* critical sections of any lower-priority task per shared resource. | Schedulability test | | 2 | $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$ | Response time = computation + blocking + interference. | Response Time Analysis | | 3 | $R_i^{n+1} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^n}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Iterative RTA equation for fixed-priority PIP systems. | RTA iterative test | | 4 | $\forall i: \ R_i \leq D_i$ | Schedulability condition (Response Time ≤ Deadline). | Pass/Fail check | | 5 | $B_i^{\text{max}} = \max_{k: P_k < P_i} \left( \sum_{r \in R_i \cap R_k} \text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max} \right)$ | Maximum blocking of $T_i$ = sum of longest CS of every lower-priority task that shares at least one resource with $T_i$. | Direct KTU question | | 6 | Transitivity: $H_{T_L} = \max(P_L, P_M, P_H)$ | Effective priority of a task that has inherited through a chain equals the max along the chain. | PIP chain problem | | 7 | Number of priority inversions (PIP) $\leq n(n-1)/2$ for $n$ tasks | Worst-case PIP blocking bounded by quadratic function of task count. | Bound estimation | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Equation 1 uses the `max` of critical sections, NOT the sum. Many students incorrectly sum all critical sections and lose 2 marks. --- ## 2.5 Properties of the Priority Inheritance Protocol | Property | Statement | Significance | |----------|-----------|--------------| | **Deadlock Freedom** | PIP cannot deadlock if resources are nested only via semaphores (no cycles). | KTU theory question | | **Transitivity** | If $T_A \to T_B \to T_C$ (A blocked by B, B blocked by C), then C inherits A's priority. | Chain problems | | **Bounded Blocking** | A high-priority task can be blocked by **at most one critical section per lower-priority task** that shares a resource. | Schedulability | | **No Prevention of Deadlocks** | PIP only bounds inversion — it does NOT prevent deadlocks from circular wait cycles. | Important distinction vs PCP | | **Chained Blocking** | A task may block on multiple resources in a chain (transitive inheritance applies). | Common 14-mark KTU scenario | --- ## 2.6 Engineering Utility — Where Priority Inversion Matters | Domain | Real-World Consequence | |--------|------------------------| | **Aerospace (NASA)** | Mars Pathfinder (1997) — system resets due to unbounded PI. | | **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | Engine control unit (ECU) timing violations in real-time CAN bus arbitration. | | **Robotics (ROS 2)** | Inverse-kinematics solver starved by motion planner → dropped control loops. | | **Medical Devices (FDA)** | Pacemaker missed beats due to display refresh blocking arrhythmia detector. | | **Industrial Control (PLC)** | Emergency stop latency in SCADA systems. | | **5G Base Station (O-RAN)** | L1 (PHY) processing starved by L3 (RRC) handshake task. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Viva Tip:** Be ready to explain *why* `vxWorks` (used in Mars Pathfinder) originally lacked PIP, and how NASA **uploaded a runtime patch** to enable PIP remotely — this saved the $327M mission. # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Three-Task Priority Inversion Scenario **Given:** - $T_H$: High-priority task, period $T_H = 4$, WCET $C_H = 1$, uses resource $R_1$ (critical section length $cs_H = 0.5$). - $T_M$: Medium-priority task, $T_M = 6$, $C_M = 2$, uses **no shared resource**. - $T_L$: Low-priority task, $T_L = 10$, $C_L = 3$, uses resource $R_1$ ($cs_L = 2$). All tasks arrive at $t = 0$. Deadline = Period (implicit deadline). ### Step 1 — Construct the Gantt chart WITHOUT PIP ``` Time: 0 ----- 2 ----- 4 ----- 6 ----- 10 | T_L | T_M | T_M | T_L | | (CS) | (preempted by T_M)| | [---locks R_1---][T_M preempts][--T_L finishes CS, releases R_1--] T_H: blocked ------------------ waiting -- can only run at t=6 if R_1 free ``` > **Blocking Time of $T_H$:** $B_H = 4$ time units (from $t=0$ to $t=4$). > **Without PIP, $B_H$ is unbounded** because additional medium tasks could arrive. ### Step 2 — Construct the Gantt chart WITH PIP Under PIP, when $T_H$ blocks on $R_1$ held by $T_L$, task $T_L$ inherits $P_H$. ``` Time: 0 ----- 2 ----- 3 ----- 4 | T_L (CS, prio=P_H inherited) | T_H (CS) | T_M (rest of slice) | [---T_L runs at priority P_H, immune to T_M preemption---] T_L exits CS at t=2 → releases R_1 → T_H preempts T_L → T_H runs CS (0.5) → T_M runs. ``` > **Blocking Time of $T_H$ under PIP:** $B_H^{\text{PIP}} = 2$ time units (only the CS of $T_L$). This is the **exact bound** specified by Equation 5 in Section 2.4. --- ## 3.2 Formal Derivations — Blocking Time Under PIP ### Derivation 1: Maximum Blocking Bound for Task $T_i$ We derive the worst-case blocking time experienced by a task $T_i$ under PIP. **Given:** A task set $\tau = \{T_1, T_2, ..., T_n\}$ with priorities $P_1 > P_2 > ... > P_n$. A set of resources $\mathcal{R} = \{R_1, R_2, ..., R_m\}$. **Let:** - $\mathcal{R}_i = $ set of resources accessed by $T_i$. - $\mathcal{L}_i = \{T_k \mid P_k < P_i\}$ = set of lower-priority tasks than $T_i$. - $\text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max} = $ maximum critical section length of $T_k$ on resource $r$. **Claim:** Under PIP, the worst-case blocking time of $T_i$ is given by: $$ B_i^{\max} = \max_{k \in \mathcal{L}_i} \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}_i \cap \mathcal{R}_k} \text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max} $$ **Proof (Step-by-Step Derivation):** **Step (i) — When is $T_i$ blocked?** $T_i$ is blocked **iff** there exists a lower-priority task $T_k$ ($P_k < P_i$) that currently holds a resource $r \in \mathcal{R}_i$. **Step (ii) — Maximum hold time of $T_k$ on $r$:** The maximum time $T_k$ can hold $r$ while $T_i$ waits is $\text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max}$ (because PIP forces $T_k$ to execute its critical section at $T_i$'s priority, preventing preemption). **Step (iii) — Sum over shared resources:** If $T_k$ shares multiple resources with $T_i$, $T_i$ may be blocked by $T_k$ on each resource. Under PIP, the blocking from $T_k$ is the **sum** of the longest CS of $T_k$ on each shared resource: $$ B_{i,k} = \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}_i \cap \mathcal{R}_k} \text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max} $$ **Step (iv) — Take the maximum over all lower-priority tasks:** Since $T_i$ can be blocked by at most one lower-priority task at any instant (PIP serializes access), the worst-case total blocking is the maximum over all such tasks: $$ B_i^{\max} = \max_{k \in \mathcal{L}_i} B_{i,k} = \max_{k \in \mathcal{L}_i} \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}_i \cap \mathcal{R}_k} \text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max} $$ This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$ --- ### Derivation 2: Iterative Response Time Analysis Under PIP **Setup:** For a fixed-priority system using PIP, the worst-case response time $R_i$ of task $T_i$ is computed by the iterative equation: $$ R_i^{n+1} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^n}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$ Where: - $C_i$ = WCET of $T_i$. - $B_i$ = worst-case blocking (from Equation above). - $hp(i)$ = set of tasks with **higher** priority than $T_i$. - $\lceil R_i^n / T_j \rceil$ = maximum number of jobs of $T_j$ that can interfere with $T_i$'s response window. **Derivation (Recursive Reasoning):** **Step (i) — Initialize:** $$ R_i^0 = C_i + B_i $$ **Step (ii) — Compute interference from higher-priority tasks:** In a window of length $R_i$, the maximum number of jobs of $T_j$ is $\lceil R_i / T_j \rceil$. Each job consumes $C_j$ time. So total interference from $T_j$ is: $$ I_{j \to i} = \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$ **Step (iii) — Sum interference over all higher-priority tasks:** $$ I_i = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$ **Step (iv) — Combine:** $$ R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i $$ Since $I_i$ depends on $R_i$ itself, the equation is **fixed-point** and solved iteratively: $$ R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(n)}}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j $$ **Convergence Test:** Iteration stops when $R_i^{(n+1)} = R_i^{(n)}$ (fixed point) OR when $R_i^{(n+1)} > D_i$ (failed, system not schedulable). **Example Calculation:** Suppose $T_1$ (highest priority), $T_2$, $T_3$ with: - $C_1 = 1$, $T_1 = 4$, $D_1 = 4$ - $C_2 = 2$, $T_2 = 6$, $D_2 = 6$ - $C_3 = 3$, $T_3 = 10$, $D_3 = 10$ - $B_2 = 1$ (blocked by $T_3$ on shared resource) Compute $R_2$: - $R_2^0 = C_2 + B_2 = 2 + 1 = 3$ - $R_2^1 = 3 + \lceil 3/4 \rceil \cdot 1 = 3 + 1 \cdot 1 = 4$ - $R_2^2 = 3 + \lceil 4/4 \rceil \cdot 1 = 3 + 1 = 4$ - **Fixed point:** $R_2 = 4 \le D_2 = 6$ ✅ **Schedulable**. --- ### Derivation 3: Upper Bound on Number of Priority Inversions (PIP) For a system of $n$ tasks, the **maximum number of priority inversion events** that can occur under PIP is bounded by: $$ N_{\text{inversion}}^{\max} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} $$ **Reasoning:** - Each pair of tasks $(T_i, T_j)$ with $i \ne j$ can contribute **at most one** inversion event under PIP. - The number of unordered pairs of $n$ tasks is $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$. - This bound is **tight** — it is achieved by carefully constructed adversarial task sets (Sha et al. 1990). **Engineering Implication:** Even with PIP, the **total CPU time lost to priority inversion** scales quadratically with $n$, which is why PIP is supplemented by **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** in safety-critical systems. # 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Priority Inversion Without PIP (Sequence Flow) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant TL as T_L (Low Priority) participant TM as T_M (Medium Priority) participant TH as T_H (High Priority) participant R1 as Resource R_1 (Mutex) participant SCH as Scheduler Note over TL,R1: t = 0 TL->>R1: Lock R_1 (Enter CS) TL->>SCH: Running at P_L SCH-->>TL: Dispatch T_L Note over TH,R1: t = 1 TH->>SCH: Becomes ready SCH->>TL: Preempt T_L SCH->>TH: Dispatch T_H TH->>R1: Lock R_1 (BLOCKED) Note over TH: T_H is BLOCKED — waits for T_L Note over TM,TH: t = 2 TM->>SCH: Becomes ready SCH->>TH: T_H is blocked (not ready) SCH->>TL: Resume T_L Note right of TL: T_L still holds R_1 SCH->>TL: T_L has lower priority than T_M Note over TM: ⚠️ INVERSION BEGINS SCH->>TM: Dispatch T_M (preempts T_L) TM->>TM: Executes entire WCET (2 units) Note over TL,TM: t = 4 SCH->>TL: Resume T_L (T_M done) TL->>R1: Exit CS — Unlock R_1 Note over TH,R1: t = 6 SCH->>TH: T_H finally wakes up TH->>R1: Lock R_1 (succeeds) TH->>TH: Execute CS Note over TH: ✅ T_H Completes — Total Block = 5 units Note right of TM: 🚨 Unbounded Inversion
Caused by T_M preemption ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Priority Inversion Resolved WITH PIP ```mermaid sequenceDiagram autonumber participant TL as T_L (Original P_L) participant TM as T_M (Medium Priority) participant TH as T_H (High Priority) participant R1 as Resource R_1 participant SCH as Scheduler Note over TL,R1: t = 0 TL->>R1: Lock R_1 TL->>SCH: Running at P_L SCH-->>TL: Dispatch T_L Note over TH,TL: t = 1 TH->>SCH: Becomes ready SCH->>TL: Preempt T_L SCH->>TH: Dispatch T_H TH->>R1: Lock R_1 (BLOCKED) Note over TH: T_H blocks on R_1 held by T_L Note over SCH,TL: ⚙️ PIP TRIGGERED SCH->>TL: ⚡ Boost T_L effective priority to P_H Note right of TL: T_L now runs at INHERITED priority P_H Note over TM,TL: t = 2 TM->>SCH: Becomes ready SCH->>TM: T_M priority = P_M SCH->>TL: T_L effective = P_H > P_M Note right of TL: 🛡️ T_L is PROTECTED from T_M preemption SCH->>TL: Dispatch T_L (priority wins) Note over TL,TH: t = 3 TL->>R1: Exit CS — Unlock R_1 SCH->>TL: Restore original priority P_L SCH->>TH: Wake T_H TH->>R1: Lock R_1 (success) TH->>TH: Execute CS Note over TM,TH: t = 3.5 TH->>R1: Unlock R_1 SCH->>TL: Preempt T_L SCH->>TM: Dispatch T_M Note over TH: ✅ T_H Total Block = 2 units
BOUNDED by CS length ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Transitive Priority Inheritance (Chained Blocking) ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph chain["Transitive Inheritance Chain"] direction TB nodeTA["Task T_A (Priority P_A = HIGHEST = 7)"] nodeTB["Task T_B (Original P_B = 4)"] nodeTC["Task T_C (Original P_C = 1 = LOWEST)"] end resR1["Resource R_1"] -. "Held by T_C" .-> nodeTC resR2["Resource R_2"] -. "Held by T_B" .-> nodeTB nodeTA -->|"Attempts Lock R_2
BLOCKED by T_B"| nodeTB nodeTB -->|"Attempts Lock R_1
BLOCKED by T_C"| nodeTC note1["Step 1: T_C inherits P_A = 7
(because T_C blocks T_B who blocks T_A)"] note2["Step 2: T_B inherits P_A = 7
(because T_B blocks T_A)"] note3["Step 3: Effective priority of T_C = 7
(transitive)"] nodeTC -.-> note1 nodeTB -.-> note2 note1 -.-> note3 style nodeTA fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c92a2a,color:#ffffff style nodeTB fill:#ffd43b,stroke:#f59f00,color:#000000 style nodeTC fill:#74c0fc,stroke:#1971c2,color:#ffffff style note1 fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000000 style note2 fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000000 style note3 fill:#d3f9d8,stroke:#2f9e44,color:#000000 ``` --- ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Comparison Matrix of Protocols (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT["Input Task Set"] T1["T_1 (P_1 = 7)"] T2["T_2 (P_2 = 4)"] T3["T_3 (P_3 = 1)"] end subgraph SHARED["Shared Resources"] R_A["Resource R_A (Mutex)"] R_B["Resource R_B (Mutex)"] end subgraph UNPROT["Unprotected / No Protocol"] UP["Unbounded Inversion
Blocking = INFINITE in worst case"] end subgraph PIP["Priority Inheritance Protocol PIP"] PIPBL["Bounded Blocking
B_i = max over lower-priority tasks
of sum of shared CS lengths"] end subgraph PCP["Priority Ceiling Protocol PCP"] PCPBL["Single-Level Blocking
B_i = longest CS of any lower-priority task
PCP also prevents deadlocks"] end subgraph ICPP["Immediate Priority Ceiling IPCP"] ICPPBL["One Blocking per task
B_i = longest CS of any lower-priority task
Cheapest runtime check"] end T1 --> UP T2 --> UP T3 --> UP R_A --> UP R_B --> UP T1 --> PIPBL T2 --> PIPBL T3 --> PIPBL T1 --> PCPBL T2 --> PCPBL T3 --> PCPBL T1 --> ICPPBL T2 --> ICPPBL T3 --> ICPPBL style UP fill:#ffa8a8,stroke:#c92a2a style PIPBL fill:#ffd8a8,stroke:#d9480f style PCPBL fill:#b2f2bb,stroke:#2f9e44 style ICPPBL fill:#a5d8ff,stroke:#1971c2 ``` --- ## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Mars Pathfinder Anomaly Timeline (Case Study) ```mermaid gantt title Mars Pathfinder (1997) — Priority Inversion Timeline dateFormat X axisFormat %s section Spacecraft Landing & Boot :done, land, 0, 1 Normal Operation :active, norm, 1, 3 System Resets Detected :crit, reset1, 4, 1 Reset #2 :crit, reset2, 6, 1 Reset #3 :crit, reset3, 8, 1 section Diagnosis Logs Replayed :diag, 9, 3 PI Confirmed :milestone, m1, 12, 0 section Fix PIP Patch Uploaded :fix, 13, 2 Mission Resumed :done, resume, 15, 2 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Each system reset corresponds to a watch-dog timeout caused by **unbounded priority inversion** between the bus-management task (low priority) and the meteorology data task (high priority). The medium-priority communication task repeatedly preempted the bus manager. # 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** **Define Priority Inversion. How does it differ from Unbounded Priority Inversion? (3 Marks)** > **Model Answer (Word-for-word KTU scoring template):** **Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly in real-time systems in which a higher-priority task is **indirectly preempted** by a lower-priority task due to the presence of one or more medium-priority tasks that preempt the lower-priority task while it holds a shared resource needed by the higher-priority task. **Difference:** | Aspect | Bounded PI | Unbounded PI | |--------|------------|--------------| | **Cause** | Only the lower-priority task's own critical section time | Repeated preemption by medium-priority tasks | | **Duration** | Bounded by CS length | **Unbounded** — depends on number of medium tasks | | **Predictability** | Predictable | Not predictable / unsafe for hard real-time | (3 marks — Definition 1, Difference table 2) --- ### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`** **Write the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) rules. Mention any two properties. (3 Marks)** > **Model Answer:** **Rules of PIP:** 1. **Scheduling Rule:** Schedule the ready task with the **highest current (effective) priority**. 2. **Inheritance Rule:** When task $T_i$ blocks higher-priority task $T_k$ on resource $R$, boost $T_i$'s priority to $\max(P_i, P_k)$. 3. **Exit Rule:** On exiting the critical section, $T_i$ reverts to its previous priority (or the next highest among remaining blocked tasks). **Two Properties:** - **Deadlock-Free:** PIP prevents deadlocks in nested-locking scenarios provided no circular wait exists. - **Transitive Inheritance:** If $T_A \to T_B \to T_C$ chain, then $T_C$ inherits $T_A$'s priority through $T_B$. (3 marks — 3 rules = 2 marks, 2 properties = 1 mark) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice) — 14 Marks Each > **Choose ONE of the following:** --- ### **📌 QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`** #### **(a)** Explain the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) with a suitable example. Discuss how it bounds priority inversion. **(7 Marks)** > **Model Answer:** **(i) Definition (2 Marks):** The Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP), proposed by Sha, Rajkumar, and Lehoczky (1990), is a synchronization mechanism that **temporarily raises** the priority of a task holding a shared resource to the priority of the highest-priority task waiting for that resource. The boost lasts until the resource is released. **(ii) Operational Example (3 Marks):** Consider three tasks $T_H, T_M, T_L$ sharing resource $R$: ``` t=0: T_L starts, locks R. t=1: T_H arrives, preempts T_L, attempts lock R → BLOCKED. → PIP triggers: T_L inherits priority of T_H. t=2: T_M arrives. Without PIP, T_M would preempt T_L. → With PIP: T_L runs at P_H > P_M, so T_M CANNOT preempt. t=4: T_L exits critical section, releases R. → T_H acquires R and finishes its CS. t=4.5: T_L reverts to P_L. T_M now runs (only after T_H done). ``` **Blocking Time of $T_H$ = 3 units (length of $T_L$'s CS).** **(iii) Bounding Property (2 Marks):** Under PIP, $T_H$ can be blocked by **at most one critical section per lower-priority task** that shares a resource with $T_H$. The total blocking time is: $$ B_H = \sum_{k \in \mathcal{L}_H} \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}_H \cap \mathcal{R}_k} \text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max} $$ This bound is **finite, computable a priori**, and integrable into schedulability tests (RTA, utilization-based). --- #### **(b)** A task set has 3 tasks with the following parameters. Compute worst-case blocking time for each task under PIP. **(7 Marks)** | Task | Priority | Period (T) | WCET (C) | Critical Sections | |------|----------|------------|----------|-------------------| | $T_1$ | 3 (Highest) | 5 | 1 | $R_1$: 0.5 | | $T_2$ | 2 (Medium) | 10 | 2 | $R_1$: 0.8, $R_2$: 1.0 | | $T_3$ | 1 (Lowest) | 20 | 3 | $R_2$: 1.5 | **Shared resources:** $R_1$ between $T_1$ & $T_2$; $R_2$ between $T_2$ & $T_3$. > **Model Answer:** **Identify shared resources for each task:** - $T_1$: shares $R_1$ only → shared with $T_2$. - $T_2$: shares $R_1$ (with $T_1$) and $R_2$ (with $T_3$). - $T_3$: shares $R_2$ only → shared with $T_2$. **Identify lower-priority tasks:** - For $T_1$: lower-priority = $\{T_2, T_3\}$. - For $T_2$: lower-priority = $\{T_3\}$. - For $T_3$: lower-priority = $\{\}$ (none). **Compute $B_i$ using $B_i = \max_{k: P_k < P_i} \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}_i \cap \mathcal{R}_k} \text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max}$:** **For $T_1$:** - From $T_2$: shares $R_1$, $\text{cs}_{T_2, R_1} = 0.8$ - From $T_3$: shares **nothing** with $T_1$ (since $T_3$ only uses $R_2$, and $T_1$ only uses $R_1$). $$ B_{T_2 \to T_1} = 0.8 $$ $$ B_{T_3 \to T_1} = 0 $$ $$ B_1 = \max(0.8, 0) = \mathbf{0.8 \text{ time units}} $$ **For $T_2$:** - From $T_3$: shares $R_2$, $\text{cs}_{T_3, R_2} = 1.5$. $$ B_2 = \mathbf{1.5 \text{ time units}} $$ **For $T_3$:** No lower-priority tasks. $$ B_3 = \mathbf{0 \text{ time units}} $$ **Final Answer Table:** | Task | $B_i$ (PIP) | |------|-------------| | $T_1$ | **0.8** | | $T_2$ | **1.5** | | $T_3$ | **0.0** | (7 marks — 2 for setup, 3 for calculation, 2 for final table) --- ### **📌 QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`** #### **(a)** What is unbounded priority inversion? Explain the Mars Pathfinder case study. **(7 Marks)** > **Model Answer:** **(i) Unbounded Priority Inversion (2 Marks):** Unbounded priority inversion occurs when the duration of inversion is **not bounded** by any task parameter. It happens when the medium-priority task (which has no relation to the shared resource) preempts the low-priority task holding the resource, and additional medium-priority tasks can continue to arrive, indefinitely extending the high-priority task's wait. Formally, the blocking time of $T_H$ under unbounded inversion is: $$ B_H^{\text{unbounded}} \to \infty \quad \text{(no finite upper bound)} $$ **(ii) Mars Pathfinder Case Study (5 Marks):** **Background:** NASA's Mars Pathfinder spacecraft landed on Mars on **July 4, 1997**. Within a few days, the lander began experiencing **spontaneous system resets**, threatening the $327 million mission. **Root Cause Analysis (by David Wilner & Glenn Reeves of JPL):** The onboard RTOS (**VxWorks** by Wind River) was running with PIP **disabled by default**. Three critical tasks existed: | Task | Priority | Function | |------|----------|----------| | **ASI/MET** (High) | 98 | Read meteorological data | | **BCR** (Medium) | ~70 | Bus communication | | **Bus Manager** (Low) | ~50 | Manage shared data bus | The **Bus Manager** task held a mutex on the shared information bus (resource). The **ASI/MET** (high-priority) task attempted to access this bus and **blocked**. The **BCR** (medium-priority) task, having nothing to do with the bus, preempted the low-priority Bus Manager — **creating unbounded priority inversion**. **Symptom:** ASI/MET's critical data was not delivered in time → the **watchdog timer expired** → system reset. **Fix:** JPL engineers **re-enabled PIP** by changing a single global Boolean (`#define INCLUDE_PRIORITY_INHERITANCE 1`) in the VxWorks configuration and uploaded the patch to the spacecraft **from Earth**. No further resets occurred, and the mission completed successfully. **Significance to RTOS Theory:** Mars Pathfinder became the **canonical KTU board-exam example** of why PIP is essential in safety-critical real-time systems. --- #### **(b)** Compare Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) and Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) on at least four parameters. **(7 Marks)** > **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) | Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) | |-----------|--------------------------------------|----------------------------------| | **1. Deadlock Prevention** | ❌ Does NOT prevent deadlocks (only bounds inversion). | ✅ Prevents deadlocks by pre-assigning ceiling priorities. | | **2. Blocking Bound** | A task can be blocked by **multiple critical sections** (chained blocking). | A task is blocked by **at most one CS** of any lower-priority task (single-level blocking). | | **3. Implementation Complexity** | **Low** — only requires dynamic priority boost at block time. | **Higher** — requires global ceiling priority computation per resource. | | **4. Runtime Overhead** | Moderate (priority updates only on block/exit). | Low (constant-time ceiling check on lock attempt). | | **5. Transitive Inheritance** | Yes (chains propagate). | No (ceiling is fixed at compile time). | | **6. Use Case** | General-purpose RTOS where deadlocks are avoided by design. | Safety-critical hard real-time (aerospace, medical). | | **7. Example System** | VxWorks (configurable), FreeRTOS (mutex with INHERIT flag). | AUTOSAR OS, ARINC 653. | **Blocking Time Comparison:** $$ B_i^{\text{PIP}} = \max_{k \in \mathcal{L}_i} \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}_i \cap \mathcal{R}_k} \text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max} $$ $$ B_i^{\text{PCP}} = \max_{k \in \mathcal{L}_i} \max_{r \in \mathcal{R}_k} \text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max} $$ In general, $B_i^{\text{PCP}} \leq B_i^{\text{PIP}}$, making PCP **more predictable** for schedulability. (7 marks — 4 parameters = 4 marks, equations = 2 marks, conclusion = 1 mark) --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Priority Inversion Questions:** 1. **Confusing PIP with PCP** — Students often write "PIP prevents deadlocks." It does NOT. Use PCP for deadlock prevention. 2. **Using SUM instead of MAX for blocking time** — Equation 1 uses `max` over lower-priority tasks, not `sum`. The two lowest tasks cannot simultaneously block one high task on the same resource. 3. **Forgetting Transitivity** — When asked about chained inheritance ($T_A \to T_B \to T_C$), students forget that $T_C$ inherits $T_A$'s priority through $T_B$. Marks lost: 2. 4. **Mixing "Original" and "Effective" priority** — Always clarify: $P_i$ is **assigned**, $H_i$ is **current (running)**. PIP modifies $H_i$, not $P_i$. 5. **Wrong Blocking for Chained Resources** — If $T_i$ accesses $R_1$ and $R_2$, and $T_k$ (lower pri) accesses $R_1$ and $R_3$, then the SHARED set is $\{R_1\}$ only. Students mistakenly include $R_3$. Marks lost: 1. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Before walking into the KTU exam hall, ensure you can confidently write/explain each of these:** - [ ] **Definition** of Priority Inversion (bounded vs unbounded) — know the **3-task canonical scenario** ($T_H, T_M, T_L$). - [ ] **4 Rules of PIP:** Scheduling, Inheritance, Exit, Protocol. - [ ] **Blocking time formula** under PIP: $B_i = \max_{k: P_k < P_i} \sum_{r \in \mathcal{R}_i \cap \mathcal{R}_k} \text{cs}_{k,r}^{\max}$. - [ ] **PIP does NOT prevent deadlock**; **PCP does**. (High-frequency 2-mark question.) - [ ] **Transitive inheritance** property and how to draw the chain. - [ ] **Iterative RTA equation** under PIP: $R_i^{(n+1)} = C_i + B_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(n)} / T_j \rceil C_j$. - [ ] **Mars Pathfinder case study** — the 3 tasks, the bug, the fix, the mission cost ($327M). - [ ] **PIP vs PCP comparison** on ≥ 4 axes (deadlock prevention, blocking bound, runtime overhead, complexity). - [ ] **Maximum PI count** bound: $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$. - [ ] The phrase **"effective (current) priority"** vs **"assigned (original) priority"** — and that PIP modifies the former. - [ ] **Notation:** $T_i$, $P_i$, $H_i$, $R_i$, $C_i$, $B_i$, $J_i$, $D_i$ — know what each symbol stands for. - [ ] **Immediate Priority Ceiling Protocol (IPCP)** as a faster variant of PCP — bonus marks if mentioned. > ✅ **END OF NOTES — Priority Inversion and Inheritance (Module 2, PERAT757, KTU 2024 Scheme)** > *All content aligned with KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus, Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (L2–L5), and standard Board valuation key patterns.*

topics.title: Event Flags
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Event Flags

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS), an **Event Flag** (also called an event flag bit) is a single bit of synchronization information that signals the **occurrence (or non-occurrence) of a particular event** within the system. A collection of such bits, grouped together as a kernel-managed object called an **Event Flag Group**, allows tasks to synchronize their execution based on the state of one or many events simultaneously.

Formally, an event flag group is a kernel data structure of width $W$ bits (commonly $W \in \{8, 16, 32\}$), where each bit position represents a distinct, named event. Tasks may **set** (post), **clear**, or **wait on** (pend) individual or combinations of these bits, making event flags a powerful **multi-event synchronization primitive** in real-time systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT757 – Module 2):**
> Event Flags are studied as part of the broader topic of *Inter-Process / Inter-Task Communication and Synchronization*. They are often grouped with semaphores, mailboxes, and message queues as core kernel services of commercial RTOS kernels such as **μC/OS-II, μC/OS-III, VxWorks, and FreeRTOS event groups.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Notice Board" Model

Imagine a public notice board in a college office with 8 colored LED lights lined up in a row. Each LED represents a different event:

- LED 0 (Red) = "Temperature sensor reading ready"
- LED 1 (Blue) = "Network packet received"
- LED 2 (Green) = "User button pressed"
- LED 3 (Yellow) = "Timer tick expired"
- ... and so on.

Now imagine **multiple staff members (tasks)** standing in front of this board:

- The **"Sensor Reader"** task turns ON the Red LED whenever it finishes a sensor read.
- The **"Controller"** task is waiting at the board and says: *"I will only leave my chair and start processing ONCE BOTH the Red AND the Green LED are on."*
- The **"Logger"** task is also at the board and says: *"I just need ANY ONE of the Blue or Yellow LED to be ON, and I will start logging."*

This notice board is the **Event Flag Group**. The act of switching ON a light is a **Set/Post** operation, and the act of waiting is a **Pend/Wait** operation. After the controller reads the board, it may either **leave the lights ON (non-consume)** or **switch them OFF (consume)**.

## 1.3 Why Event Flags are Needed in RTOS

A semaphore can only handle **one resource/event** at a time. But real systems often need a task to wait for **multiple, independent events to occur in any combination** before proceeding. Without event flags, a developer would need a chain of semaphores or flags, which is cumbersome, error-prone, and inefficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of Event Flags in RTOS:**
> 1. **Multi-Event Synchronization** — A single primitive can synchronize a task against many events.
> 2. **Atomic Check-and-Wait** — A task checks all required bits in one atomic operation; no race conditions.
> 3. **Flexible Logic** — Supports **AND** (wait for ALL) and **OR** (wait for ANY) conditions.
> 4. **Broadcast Capable** — One task can signal many waiting tasks simultaneously.
> 5. **Low Overhead** — Operates on bitwise operations, extremely fast in hardware.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

The following are standard values used across most commercial RTOS implementations:

- **Flag Group Width $W$** = **8**, **16**, or **32** bits.
- **Maximum simultaneous waiters** = usually **unlimited (or kernel-configured)**.
- **Wait resolution time** = dependent on system tick rate, typically **10 μs to 1 ms**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an Event Flag Group

An event flag group is a kernel-managed data structure with the following logical components:

1. **Flag Bits Register ($F$)** — A $W$-bit unsigned integer holding the current state of all flags. Each bit $f_i$ ($i = 0$ to $W-1$) represents one event.
2. **Wait List ($L$)** — A doubly-linked list of Task Control Blocks (TCBs) currently blocked (waiting) on the flag group.
3. **Wait Type & Mask** — Stored per task in its TCB, indicating the bits the task is interested in and whether it uses AND or OR logic.

## 2.2 The Two Fundamental Operations

### 2.2.1 POST (Set / Signal) Operation

A task (or ISR) calls the kernel's POST service to set one or more bits in the flag group. After setting the bits, the kernel traverses the wait list and wakes up any task whose wait condition is now satisfied.

- **Single-flag post**: Sets exactly one bit (e.g., bit 3).
- **Multi-flag post**: Sets a mask of bits atomically using a logical OR.

### 2.2.2 PEND (Wait / Read) Operation

A task calls the kernel's PEND service to wait until specific bits meet a logical condition. The task is **blocked** (placed in the wait list) until the condition is satisfied, or until an optional timeout expires.

- **AND Wait (OS_FLAG_WAIT_SET_ALL)**: Task wakes up only when **ALL** bits in its mask are set.
- **OR Wait (OS_FLAG_WAIT_SET_ANY)**: Task wakes up when **AT LEAST ONE** bit in its mask is set.
- **Consume Option (OS_FLAG_CONSUME)**: After waking, the matched bits are automatically cleared.
- **No-Consume Option (OS_FLAG_NO_CONSUME)**: After waking, the bits remain set for other waiters.

## 2.3 Why AND vs OR Logic Matters — The 'How' Explained

- **AND (ALL)** is used when the task requires **all** prerequisites to be true before it can proceed. Example: *"Begin motor startup only when both the 'Start Button Pressed' AND 'Safety Door Closed' flags are set."*
- **OR (ANY)** is used when the task can react to **any one of several possible events**. Example: *"Sound the alarm if ANY of 'Smoke Detected' OR 'High Temperature' OR 'CO2 High' is set."*

This dual-logic capability is what makes event flags far more expressive than a simple binary semaphore.

## 2.4 The 'Consume' Mechanism — Why it Exists

Consider two tasks waiting on the same flag group. If the first waiting task does **not** consume the flag, the second task will see it as already set and exit its wait immediately — a behavior that may be incorrect. The **consume** option ensures the flag is cleared atomically after the **first** waking task reads it, preventing double-triggering of the same event across multiple waiters.

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:**
> - Use **consume** when each event should be processed exactly **once** (single-writer, single-reader-style semantics).
> - Use **no-consume** when the flag represents a **persistent state** (e.g., "system armed") that all tasks should observe.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical / Symbolic Form** | **Description / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Flag Group Width | $W \in \{8, 16, 32\}$ | Total bits available in one flag group |
| Number of Distinct Events | $N_{events} = W$ | Each bit = 1 event |
| Flag Group State | $F = \sum_{i=0}^{W-1} f_i \cdot 2^{i}$ | Integer value of current flag bits |
| AND Wait Satisfaction | $R_{AND} = (F \ \& \ M_{wait}) \equiv M_{wait}$ | TRUE iff all bits in mask are set |
| OR Wait Satisfaction | $R_{OR} = (F \ \& \ M_{wait}) \neq 0$ | TRUE iff at least one mask bit is set |
| POST (Set) Operation | $F_{new} = F_{old} \ \vert\ M_{post}$ | Sets specified bits using bitwise OR |
| PEND-Consume (Clear) | $F_{new} = F_{old} \ \& \ \sim M_{consumed}$ | Clears matched bits using bitwise AND with inverted mask |
| Timeout Behavior | $T_{wait} \leq T_{timeout}$ | Pend returns error if not satisfied within timeout |
| Max Concurrent Waiters | $N_{waiters}$ | Configurable per kernel (often unlimited) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, the symbol `\&` denotes the bitwise AND operator, and `\vert` denotes the bitwise OR operator. These are essential for understanding kernel-level flag manipulation in C/C++ based RTOS implementations.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Event flags are heavily used in:

- **Automotive ECUs** (e.g., waiting for ignition ON + brake pressed + gear in Park before enabling drive).
- **Industrial PLCs** for safety interlocks (e.g., emergency stop + guard door closed + motor stopped).
- **Aerospace flight controllers** (e.g., wait for IMU ready + GPS lock + Air Data valid before launch).
- **IoT sensor hubs** (e.g., wait for at least 3 of 5 sensors to report temperature before averaging).

The deterministic check time (single bitwise compare) makes event flags ideal for **hard real-time** applications where bounded latency is critical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Since Event Flags are a synchronization primitive rather than a physics or signal-processing concept, the "derivations" here are **operational state-machine derivations** — i.e., we mathematically prove the correctness of the AND/OR wait conditions and trace the full lifecycle of a flag group through a worked numerical example.

## 3.1 Derivation of the AND Wait Condition

We want to formally derive the condition under which an **AND-waiting task** should be released.

**Step 1 — Define Variables.**

Let $F$ be the current value of the flag group register. Let $M_{wait}$ be the bitmask the waiting task is interested in (a $W$-bit integer with 1s in the positions the task cares about).

**Step 2 — Apply Bitwise AND.**

The kernel computes $(F \ \& \ M_{wait})$. This produces a $W$-bit integer where the bit at position $i$ is 1 **only if** both $F_i = 1$ AND $M_{wait,i} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Compare to Mask.**

If the result equals the mask $M_{wait}$, then **every bit that the task was waiting on is set** in the current flag group. If any bit is missing, the result will have a 0 in that position, and the comparison will fail.

$$
R_{AND} = \begin{cases} 1 \ (\text{TRUE}) & \text{if } (F \ \& \ M_{wait}) \equiv M_{wait} \\ 0 \ (\text{FALSE}) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with Example.**

Suppose $W = 8$, $F = 0b11010110$ (218 decimal), and $M_{wait} = 0b10010000$ (144 decimal). The task is waiting for bits 4 and 7 to be set.

$$
F \ \& \ M_{wait} = 0b11010110 \ \& \ 0b10010000 = 0b10010000 = M_{wait}
$$

Since the result equals $M_{wait}$, the task is released. Verified.

## 3.2 Derivation of the OR Wait Condition

For the OR condition, the task should be released as soon as **any** one of the bits in its mask is set.

**Step 1 — Apply Bitwise AND.**

The kernel computes $(F \ \& \ M_{wait})$. If **at least one** position has both $F_i = 1$ and $M_{wait,i} = 1$, the result will be a non-zero integer.

**Step 2 — Test for Non-Zero.**

The condition is satisfied if and only if the result is not equal to zero.

$$
R_{OR} = \begin{cases} 1 \ (\text{TRUE}) & \text{if } (F \ \& \ M_{wait}) \neq 0 \\ 0 \ (\text{FALSE}) & \text{if } (F \ \& \ M_{wait}) = 0 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Verification with Example.**

Let $F = 0b00000101$ and $M_{wait} = 0b00001010$ (the task wants bits 1 or 3).

$$
F \ \& \ M_{wait} = 0b00000101 \ \& \ 0b00001010 = 0b00000000 = 0
$$

The result is zero, so the task is **not** released. The task waits until some task posts a flag that sets either bit 1 or bit 3.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Walk-Through — A 3-Task Scenario

Let us trace a full scenario with $W = 8$ and three tasks.

**Initial State:** $F = 0b00000000$ (no flags set).

**Task A** calls `Pend(Mask = 0b00000101, Mode = AND)`.  
   Task A is blocked. ($F \ \& \ M_A = 0 \neq M_A$.)

**Task B** calls `Pend(Mask = 0b00001000, Mode = OR)`.  
   Task B is blocked. ($F \ \& \ M_B = 0$.)

**Task C** calls `Post(Mask = 0b00000101)`.  
   New $F = 0b00000000 \ \vert\ 0b00000101 = 0b00000101$.

**Kernel checks wait list:**

   *For Task A:* $F \ \& \ M_A = 0b00000101 \ \& \ 0b00000101 = 0b00000101 = M_A$. AND satisfied → **Task A released.** If consume mode, $F$ becomes $0b00000000$.
   *For Task B:* $F \ \& \ M_B = 0b00000000 \ \& \ 0b00001000 = 0$. Not satisfied → **Task B remains blocked.**

**Task D** calls `Post(Mask = 0b00001000)`.  
   New $F = 0b00000000 \ \vert\ 0b00001000 = 0b00001000$.

**Kernel checks wait list again:**

   *For Task B:* $F \ \& \ M_B = 0b00001000 \ \& \ 0b00001000 = 0b00001000 = M_B$. OR satisfied → **Task B released.**

This end-to-end trace shows the deterministic, atomic nature of event flag operations in an RTOS.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Truth Table for Wait Conditions

| **Bit Position $i$** | **$F_i$ (Flag Set?)** | **$M_{wait,i}$ (Task Wants?)** | **AND Check ($F_i$ AND $M_i$)** | **OR Check ($F_i$ OR $M_i$) Effect** | **AND Satisfied Position?** | **OR Triggers on This Bit?** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | irrelevant | ❌ | ❌ (need other bits) |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | triggers | ✅ at this pos | ✅ YES |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | irrelevant | ❌ | ❌ |
| 3 | 1 | 0 | 0 (masked out) | irrelevant | — (not in mask) | — (not in mask) |
| 4 | 1 | 1 | 1 | triggers | ✅ at this pos | ✅ YES |

**Key Insight:** The AND wait condition requires **all** "YES" entries in column "AND Satisfied Position" to be TRUE simultaneously. The OR wait condition is satisfied as soon as **any one** "YES" entry in column "OR Triggers on This Bit" is TRUE.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Event Flag Group Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Task 1 Producer]:::producer
    T2[Task 2 Producer]:::producer
    T3[Task 3 Consumer AND Wait]:::consumerA
    T4[Task 4 Consumer OR Wait]:::consumerB
    ISR1[ISR Service Routine]:::isr

    FGRP{{Event Flag Group Width 8 to 32 bits}}:::flaggroup
    REG[Flag Bits Register F]:::register
    WLIST[Wait List Doubly Linked List]:::waitlist
    KERN[RTOS Kernel Scheduler]:::kernel

    T1 -->|OSFlagPost Mask A| FGRP
    T2 -->|OSFlagPost Mask B| FGRP
    ISR1 -->|OSFlagPost from ISR Mask C| FGRP

    FGRP --> REG
    FGRP --> WLIST
    FGRP --> KERN

    T3 -->|OSFlagPend Mask X AND| FGRP
    T4 -->|OSFlagPend Mask Y OR| FGRP

    KERN -->|Wake Task 3 on ALL match| T3
    KERN -->|Wake Task 4 on ANY match| T4
    KERN -->|Optionally clear matched bits| REG

    classDef producer fill:#E0F2FF,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef consumerA fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef consumerB fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef isr fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flaggroup fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef register fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef waitlist fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef kernel fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 State Diagram — Lifecycle of an Event Flag Bit

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Cleared
    Cleared --> Set : POST by Task or ISR
    Set --> Cleared : CONSUME after Pend
    Set --> Set : NO_CONSUME Pend continues to see bit as set
    Set --> Set : Additional POST on same bit remains set
    Cleared --> [*] : Optional deletion of flag group on kernel exit
```

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Multi-Task Synchronization Using Event Flags

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SensorTask as Sensor Task
    participant CommTask as Communication Task
    participant CtrlTask as Controller Task AND wait
    participant LoggerTask as Logger Task OR wait
    participant FlagGrp as Event Flag Group
    participant Kernel as RTOS Kernel

    Note over SensorTask,LoggerTask: Initial state: all flags = 0

    SensorTask->>Kernel: OSFlagPost(bit 0 = DATA_READY)
    Kernel->>FlagGrp: Set bit 0
    Kernel->>CtrlTask: Check AND condition not satisfied
    Kernel->>LoggerTask: Check OR condition satisfied
    Kernel-->>LoggerTask: Wake LoggerTask (no consume)

    CommTask->>Kernel: OSFlagPost(bit 1 = COMM_COMPLETE)
    Kernel->>FlagGrp: Set bit 1
    Kernel->>CtrlTask: Check AND condition satisfied
    Kernel-->>CtrlTask: Wake CtrlTask (consume clears bits 0 and 1)
    Kernel->>FlagGrp: Clear bits 0 and 1
```

## 4.4 Memory Architecture of the Event Flag Group

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Flag Group Control Block]:::struct
    B[Flag Value: 32-bit F]:::field
    C[Wait List Head Pointer]:::field
    D[Wait List Tail Pointer]:::field
    E[Number of Waiters N]:::field
    F[Group Name String]:::field

    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F

    C -->|next| W1[TCB of Waiting Task 1]:::tcb
    C -->|next| W2[TCB of Waiting Task 2]:::tcb
    C -->|next| W3[TCB of Waiting Task 3]:::tcb

    classDef struct fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef field fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef tcb fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define an event flag. How does it differ from a binary semaphore in an RTOS?**

**Model Answer:**

An event flag is a single bit within an event flag group that signals the occurrence of a particular event in the system. A binary semaphore is a single synchronization token that can only be in one of two states (available/unavailable) and can only signal **one event**.

The key differences are:

1. **Granularity:** A semaphore represents exactly **one** event/resource. A flag group can represent up to **$W$** events ($W \in \{8, 16, 32\}$).
2. **Wait Logic:** A semaphore supports only "wait for one" semantics. Event flags support both **AND (all)** and **OR (any)** wait conditions.
3. **Multi-Event Sync:** Multiple events can be checked atomically in a single flag group; semaphores require multiple instances for multi-event sync.
4. **Consume Behavior:** Semaphores always consume on take; event flags can optionally retain their state (no-consume mode).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Explain the difference between AND-wait and OR-wait on an event flag group with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

- **AND-wait (OS_FLAG_WAIT_SET_ALL):** A task is unblocked only when **all** the bits in its wait mask are set in the flag group. The check is $R_{AND} = (F \ \& \ M_{wait}) \equiv M_{wait}$.
- **OR-wait (OS_FLAG_WAIT_SET_ANY):** A task is unblocked when **at least one** of the bits in its wait mask is set. The check is $R_{OR} = (F \ \& \ M_{wait}) \neq 0$.

**Example (Automatic Washing Machine):**
- The **Motor Start task** uses **AND-wait** on flags "Lid Closed" AND "Water Level Full" — it must wait for **both** to be set.
- The **Alarm task** uses **OR-wait** on flags "Over-Temperature" OR "Imbalance Detected" OR "Lid Opened Mid-Cycle" — it wakes on **any** of these.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 2 questions offer an internal choice. Both alternatives below are from the Event Flags / Inter-Task Communication module. Each contains sub-parts (a) and (b) for 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of event flags in a Real-Time Operating System. Discuss the various operations supported on event flags. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, illustrate the use of AND-wait and OR-wait on event flag groups. Show how the flag group state changes during the operations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Event flags are kernel-managed bits used to synchronize tasks based on the occurrence of events. A collection of bits forms an **Event Flag Group** (typically 8, 16, or 32 bits wide).

**Operations supported on event flags:**

1. **Create / Delete Flag Group:** `OSFlagCreate()`, `OSFlagDel()` — manages the kernel object.
2. **POST (Set Bits):** `OSFlagPost()` — atomically sets one or more bits. Wakes any waiting task whose condition is satisfied. May also be called from an ISR (e.g., `OSFlagPostFromISR()`).
3. **PEND (Wait on Bits):** `OSFlagPend()` — blocks the calling task until the AND/OR condition is met on the specified mask. Accepts an optional timeout.
4. **Query:** `OSFlagQuery()` — non-blocking read of the current flag value.
5. **Accept / Non-blocking Pend:** `OSFlagAccept()` — checks flags without blocking; useful for polling.
6. **Consume / No-Consume Option:** When a task wakes, it may optionally clear the matched bits (consume) or leave them set (no-consume).

**Key advantages:**
- Atomic multi-bit check (no race condition).
- Supports both AND and OR synchronization semantics.
- Efficient (single bitwise compare for wait check).

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Definition of event flag + flag group: 2 marks
- Operations list with brief explanation: 3 marks
- Advantages / kernel use case: 2 marks

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Scenario:** A robotic arm system has 4 flags in a flag group:
- Bit 0 = `OBJECT_DETECTED`
- Bit 1 = `GRIPPER_OPEN`
- Bit 2 = `SAFETY_ZONE_CLEAR`
- Bit 3 = `HOME_POSITION_REACHED`

**Task X — "Pick and Place"** uses **AND-wait** with mask = 0b0111 (bits 0, 1, 2):

- Initially $F = 0$. Task X is blocked.
- ISR sets bit 0 (camera detected object): $F = 0b0001$. Not yet satisfied.
- Task Y posts bit 1 (gripper opened): $F = 0b0011$. Still not satisfied.
- Task Z posts bit 2 (safety confirmed): $F = 0b0111$. AND satisfied → Task X released.

**Task W — "Safety Logger"** uses **OR-wait** with mask = 0b1100 (bits 2, 3):

- Initially $F = 0$. Task W is blocked.
- As soon as **any** of bits 2 or 3 is set (e.g., safety zone cleared: $F = 0b0100$), the check $R_{OR} = 0b0100 \neq 0$ is true → Task W released immediately.

**State Change Trace (combined):**

| **Step** | **Action** | **$F$ (after)** | **Task X (AND)** | **Task W (OR)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Initial state | `0b0000` | Blocked | Blocked |
| 1 | Post bit 0 | `0b0001` | Blocked | Blocked |
| 2 | Post bit 1 | `0b0011` | Blocked | Blocked |
| 3 | Post bit 2 | `0b0111` | **Released (AND satisfied)** | **Released (OR satisfied)** |
| 4 | Post bit 3 (later) | `0b1111` | — | — |

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Clear example with 3+ flags: 2 marks
- AND-wait explanation + state trace: 2.5 marks
- OR-wait explanation + state trace: 2.5 marks

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
