**(a)** Compare event flags with semaphores and mailboxes as RTOS synchronization mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a multi-task automotive dashboard warning system using event flags. Show the flag group definition, the producer tasks (sensor ISRs), and the consumer task (display controller) with both AND and OR waits. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

| **Parameter** | **Semaphore** | **Mailbox / Message Queue** | **Event Flag Group** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Carried | None (just a count) | Full message / pointer | None (just bit states) |
| Sync Granularity | Single event | Single event (per msg) | Up to $W$ events atomically |
| Wait Logic | Test for count > 0 | Test for message present | AND, OR, or any combination |
| Consume Behavior | Always consumes on take | Always consumes on receive | Optional (consume / no-consume) |
| Blocking Behavior | Blocks on P() | Blocks on receive | Blocks on Pend until condition |
| Use Case | Resource locking, signaling | Inter-task data transfer | Multi-condition state synchronization |
| Overhead | Very low | Moderate (memcpy) | Low (bitwise ops) |
| Broadcast | One-to-one | One-to-one / FIFO | One-to-many (all waiters checked) |

**Conclusion:** Event flags are the most expressive primitive for **multi-condition state synchronization**, while semaphores and mailboxes are better for **resource control** and **data transfer**, respectively.

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Comparison table with at least 4 parameters: 4 marks
- Brief justification / conclusion: 3 marks

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**System Design — Automotive Dashboard Warning System:**

Define a single **8-bit Event Flag Group** for the dashboard controller:

| **Bit** | **Flag Name** | **Source (ISR)** |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | `ENGINE_OIL_PRESSURE_LOW` | Oil pressure sensor ISR |
| 1 | `ENGINE_TEMP_HIGH` | Temperature sensor ISR |
| 2 | `BATTERY_VOLTAGE_LOW` | Voltage monitor ISR |
| 3 | `BRAKE_FLUID_LOW` | Brake fluid sensor ISR |
| 4 | `DOOR_OPEN` | Door switch ISR |
| 5 | `SEATBELT_UNLATCHED` | Seatbelt switch ISR |
| 6 | `FUEL_LEVEL_LOW` | Fuel sensor ISR |
| 7 | `TIRE_PRESSURE_LOW` | TPMS ISR |

**Consumer Task — Display Controller:** Uses **OR-wait** on the mask `0b11111111` (all 8 bits). It wakes as soon as **any** warning bit is set, then reads the current flag value to determine which warning to display. Uses **no-consume** so that if a warning is still active, it is displayed continuously.

**Special Consumer Task — Engine Shutdown Logic:** Uses **AND-wait** on the mask `0b00000111` (oil pressure + high temp + low battery). It triggers engine protective shutdown only if **all three** critical warnings are present simultaneously, indicating a serious fault.

**Flow:**

1. Multiple ISRs independently call `OSFlagPostFromISR()` on relevant bits.
2. The kernel updates the flag group register atomically.
3. The kernel walks the wait list:
   - Display Controller is released on ANY of 8 bits → updates LCD.
   - Engine Shutdown task is released only when all 3 critical bits are set → initiates safe shutdown sequence.

**Marking Distribution (7 marks):**
- Flag group definition with at least 4 named flags: 2 marks
- Producer (ISR) description: 1.5 marks
- Consumer task with AND/OR logic: 2.5 marks
- Flow narrative: 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing flags with semaphores:** Students often treat event flags as "just another semaphore" and miss the **AND/OR logic** and **multi-bit** aspect. Always emphasize the bitmask operation.
> 2. **Forgetting the consume option:** Many answers neglect to mention the `OS_FLAG_CONSUME` vs `OS_FLAG_NO_CONSUME` distinction, which is a guaranteed 2-mark loss in long answers.
> 3. **No numerical/state trace:** For 7-mark sub-parts involving AND/OR, the examiner expects a **state change table** or numerical example. A purely conceptual answer loses 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Using "flag" instead of "flag group":** The kernel object is the **flag group**; an event flag is a single bit within it. Use the correct terminology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Event Flags**
>
> - **Event Flag** = 1 bit; **Event Flag Group** = $W$-bit kernel object ($W \in \{8, 16, 32\}$).
> - **POST** sets bits via bitwise OR; **PEND** waits for a logical condition.
> - **AND-wait** satisfies only when **all** bits in the mask are set: $(F \ \& \ M) \equiv M$.
> - **OR-wait** satisfies when **any one** bit in the mask is set: $(F \ \& \ M) \neq 0$.
> - **Consume** clears the matched bits after wake; **No-Consume** retains them.
> - Event flags provide **atomic multi-condition check-and-wait** — no race conditions.
> - Suitable for **state synchronization** in real-time control, safety interlocks, and complex multi-task protocols.
> - Distinct from semaphores (counting) and mailboxes (data transfer).
> - Widely implemented in μC/OS-II/III, FreeRTOS event groups, VxWorks.
> - **KTU keyword triggers:** "synchronization", "AND/OR", "consume", "bitmask", "kernel object", "multi-event".
> - Always pair a long-answer Event Flag question with a **state-trace table** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Scheduling Algorithms: Practical Implementation of Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Implementing Scheduling Algorithms: Practical Implementation of Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Scheduling Algorithm Implementation** in a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) refers to the concrete software and hardware mechanisms by which the kernel's scheduler selects, dispatches, and executes tasks according to a chosen scheduling policy (Rate Monotonic, Earliest Deadline First, Fixed Priority, etc.) while guaranteeing temporal constraints.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, implementation encompasses the *data structures* (ready queues, PCB blocks, tick timers), *dispatch mechanisms* (context switch, ISR hooks), and *temporal primitives* (system tick, hardware timers) that transform an abstract scheduling *algorithm* into a working kernel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU 2024):**
> - **Scheduling Algorithm** = The mathematical *policy* (e.g., "run the task with the earliest absolute deadline").
> - **Scheduling Implementation** = The *mechanism* (ready queue, tick interrupt, context switch routine) that enforces that policy at run-time.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy **hospital emergency ward triage desk**:
- The **Triage Policy** says: "Always treat the patient with the most critical, soonest-deteriorating condition first" (this is the *algorithm*).
- The **Triage Desk Mechanics** — a physical token-dispensing machine, a queue board, a buzzer system, a nurse calling names — is the *implementation*.

The doctors (CPU) don't think about *who is next*; the *mechanism* (nurse + queue board + buzzer) handles that. If the buzzer is broken, even a brilliant policy fails. RTOS scheduling works the same way: brilliant algorithms (RM, EDF) require robust *mechanisms* — tick interrupts, ready queues, atomic context switches — to deliver on their promise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Context Switch Time (CST):** Typically **1 µs – 10 µs** in commercial RTOS kernels.
> - **System Tick Period (T_tick):** Commonly **10 µs – 1 ms**.
> - **Scheduler Overhead (O_sched):** Time spent *choosing* next task, excluding context switch.

### 1.3 Why Implementation Matters in RTOS

| Aspect | Generic OS | Hard RTOS |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Throughput / Fairness | **Temporal Predictability** |
| Worst-Case Latency | "Usually" fast | **Bounded & Provable** |
| Scheduler Invocation | Heuristic, event-driven | **Periodic Tick + Preemption** |

A theoretically optimal algorithm with sloppy implementation (e.g., variable-length ready queue search) becomes **non-deterministic** — the worst-case behavior explodes. KTU examiners expect students to identify this gap between *policy* and *mechanism*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Scheduling Implementation

Every RTOS scheduler, regardless of algorithm, is built from these five components:

**Pillar 1 — System Tick (Time Base)**
- A periodic hardware timer interrupt (the "heartbeat" of the RTOS).
- Configured frequency: $f_{tick} = \frac{1}{T_{tick}}$, typically 100 Hz to 100 kHz.
- On each tick, `OSTimeTick()` decrements time delays, updates timeouts, and triggers scheduling decisions.

**Pillar 2 — Task Control Block (TCB / PCB)**
- A kernel-allocated structure holding task state, stack pointer, priority, deadline, period, registers snapshot.
- In KTU terminology: **OSTCB** (µC/OS-II style) or **TCB_t** (FreeRTOS style).

**Pillar 3 — Ready Queue (or Multi-Queue)**
- The data structure that holds all tasks in the `READY` state.
- Choice of structure determines the *complexity of the scheduler*.

**Pillar 4 — Scheduler Function (`OS_Sched()`)**
- A short, deterministic function that scans the ready queue and picks the highest-priority (or earliest-deadline) task.
- **Must be O(1) or O(log n) for hard real-time.**

**Pillar 5 — Context Switch Routine (`OSCtxSw`)**
- Saves the outgoing task's CPU registers onto its stack.
- Loads the incoming task's registers from its stack.
- Updates the stack pointer (PSP/MSP in ARM Cortex-M).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The *scheduler* picks the task; the *dispatcher* switches to it. These are two separate functions in textbook RTOS kernels.

### 2.2 Ready Queue Implementation Strategies

| # | Data Structure | Insertion | Deletion (Find Best) | Used By | WCET Bound |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linked List** (unsorted) | O(1) | O(n) | Simple kernels | Poor |
| 2 | **Sorted Linked List** | O(n) | O(1) (head) | µC/OS-II (priority) | O(n) |
| 3 | **Bitmap + Multi-Queue** | O(1) | O(1) | FreeRTOS, VxWorks | **O(1)** ✓ |
| 4 | **Heap / Priority Queue** | O(log n) | O(log n) | EDF schedulers | O(log n) |
| 5 | **Calendar Queue** | O(1) amortized | O(1) | Tick-multiple systems | O(1) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** *Bitmap-based priority queues* are the gold standard for fixed-priority preemptive schedulers. A 32-bit bitmap with `CLZ` (Count Leading Zeros) instruction gives the highest-priority ready task in **O(1)** with a single cycle on ARM Cortex-M.

### 2.3 The Implementation Flow of a Single Scheduling Decision

The sequence below is executed on every system tick (or whenever a task state changes):

1. **Tick ISR** fires → kernel enters `OSTimeTickHandler()`.
2. Kernel updates all time-based fields (delays, timeouts).
3. Kernel checks if a **higher-priority task woke up** (e.g., delay expired).
4. If yes → invoke `OS_Sched()`.
5. `OS_Sched()` inspects the ready queue → returns pointer to **highest-priority TCB**.
6. If `OSHighRdy != OSTCBCur` → call `OSCtxSw()`.
7. `OSCtxSw()` saves outgoing context, restores incoming context.
8. CPU resumes incoming task via `PendSV` (ARM) or `SWI`.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{tick} = \frac{1}{f_{tick}}$ | System tick period from timer frequency |
| 2 | $N_{tasks}^{max} = \frac{f_{CPU}}{f_{tick}}$ | Max scheduler invocations per task per second |
| 3 | $T_{response} = T_{ready} + T_{queue} + T_{sched} + T_{ctxsw}$ | End-to-end response latency |
| 4 | $C_{total} = C_{exec} + C_{sched} + C_{ctxsw}$ | Total task CPU consumption |
| 5 | $U_{CPU} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le U_{bound}$ | Processor utilization (RM bound: $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$) |
| 6 | $O_{scheduler} \le O_{WCET-sched}$ | Scheduling decision MUST be bounded |
| 7 | $T_{ctxsw} = T_{save} + T_{restore} = 2 \cdot T_{memcpy}$ | Symmetric context switch cost |
| 8 | $L_{max} = \max_{i}(R_i) - D_i$ | Lateness; must be $\le 0$ for hard RT |
| 9 | $\text{CLZ}(bitmap) \rightarrow priority$ | O(1) priority extraction (ARM) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Response Time with Implementation Overhead

In a *purely theoretical* analysis, the worst-case response time of a task $\tau_i$ under Rate Monotonic Scheduling is given by the standard recurrence:

$$R_i^{theory}(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

However, **implementation overhead is non-zero** in any real kernel. Let us derive the *practical* response time by augmenting the model.

**Step 1 — Identify the four sources of overhead:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{tick} &= \text{Cost of one tick ISR execution} \\
C_{sched} &= \text{Cost of OS\_Sched() decision} \\
C_{ctxsw} &= \text{Cost of OSCtxSw() (save + restore)} \\
C_{ret} &= \text{Cost of returning from interrupt}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total overhead per scheduling event:**

On every tick where a context switch actually occurs, the kernel spends:

$$C_{overhead}^{event} = C_{tick} + C_{sched} + C_{ctxsw}$$

**Step 3 — Number of preemptions suffered by task $\tau_i$:**

In the worst case, all higher-priority tasks $\tau_j$ (for $j \in hp(i)$) can preempt $\tau_i$ once per period $T_j$ within a window of length $R_i$:

$$N_{preempt}(R_i) = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

**Step 4 — Practical worst-case response time recurrence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i^{impl}(t) = C_i &+ \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j \\
&+ \left(1 + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \right) C_{overhead}^{event}
\end{aligned}
$$

The extra multiplicative term accounts for: the *initial* tick that readies $\tau_i$ (the "+1") plus one tick per preemption from each higher-priority task.

**Step 5 — Iterative solution:**

Starting with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ and iterating:

$$R_i^{(k+1)} = f(R_i^{(k)})$$

until convergence: $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$.

**Step 6 — Feasibility check (Hard Real-Time):**

$$\boxed{R_i^{impl} \le D_i \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}}$$

If this holds, the *implementation* (not just the algorithm) meets all deadlines.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

A system has three tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling:

| Task | Period $T_i$ | Computation $C_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 5 ms | 1 ms | 5 ms | High (RM) |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 ms | 2 ms | 10 ms | Mid |
| $\tau_3$ | 20 ms | 4 ms | 20 ms | Low |

Assume $C_{overhead}^{event} = 50 \text{ µs}$ per scheduling event.

**Check $\tau_1$ (highest priority — no preemption):**

$$R_1^{impl} = C_1 + C_{overhead} = 1.0 + 0.05 = 1.05 \text{ ms} \le 5 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**Check $\tau_2$ (can be preempted by $\tau_1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 + C_{overhead} = 2.05 \text{ ms} \\
R_2^{(1)} &= C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{2.05}{5} \right\rceil C_1 + \left(1 + \left\lceil \frac{2.05}{5} \right\rceil\right) C_{overhead} \\
&= 2.0 + (1)(1.0) + (1+1)(0.05) \\
&= 2.0 + 1.0 + 0.10 = 3.10 \text{ ms} \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 2.0 + \left\lceil \frac{3.10}{5} \right\rceil(1.0) + \left(1 + \left\lceil \frac{3.10}{5} \right\rceil\right)(0.05) \\
&= 2.0 + (1)(1.0) + (2)(0.05) = 3.10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convergence: $R_2^{impl} = 3.10 \text{ ms} \le 10 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$

**Check $\tau_3$ (can be preempted by $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 4.0 + 0.05 = 4.05 \text{ ms} \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 4.0 + \left\lceil \frac{4.05}{5} \right\rceil(1.0) + \left\lceil \frac{4.05}{10} \right\rceil(2.0) + \left(1 + 1 + 1\right)(0.05) \\
&= 4.0 + (1)(1.0) + (1)(2.0) + (3)(0.05) \\
&= 4.0 + 1.0 + 2.0 + 0.15 = 7.15 \text{ ms} \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 4.0 + \left\lceil \frac{7.15}{5} \right\rceil(1.0) + \left\lceil \frac{7.15}{10} \right\rceil(2.0) + (3)(0.05) \\
&= 4.0 + (2)(1.0) + (1)(2.0) + 0.15 = 8.15 \text{ ms} \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 4.0 + \left\lceil \frac{8.15}{5} \right\rceil(1.0) + \left\lceil \frac{8.15}{10} \right\rceil(2.0) + (3)(0.05) \\
&= 4.0 + (2)(1.0) + (1)(2.0) + 0.15 = 8.15 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Convergence: $R_3^{impl} = 8.15 \text{ ms} \le 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$

All tasks meet their deadlines **including implementation overhead**.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Algorithm vs. Implementation Cost

| Algorithm | Policy | Best Data Structure | $O_{find-best}$ | $O_{insert}$ | Hard-RT Suitable? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Rate Monotonic (RM)** | Static priority = $1/T$ | Bitmap + multi-queue | **O(1)** | O(1) | Yes (with proof) |
| **Deadline Monotonic (DM)** | Static priority = $1/D$ | Bitmap + multi-queue | **O(1)** | O(1) | Yes |
| **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** | Dynamic = min $d_i$ | Min-heap / Calendar queue | O(log n) | O(log n) | Yes (optimal) |
| **Least Laxity First (LLF)** | Dynamic = min $L_i$ | Min-heap on laxity | O(log n) | O(log n) | Yes (complex) |
| **Round Robin (RR)** | Cyclic within priority | Circular FIFO | O(1) | O(1) | No (soft RT) |
| **FIFO / FCFS** | Arrival order | FIFO queue | O(1) | O(1) | No |
| **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** | Adaptive | Multi-queue + aging | O(1) | O(1) | **No** (heuristic) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Scheduling Implementation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HW["Hardware Timer (e.g., SysTick)"] -->|IRQ| ISR[Tick ISR Handler]
    ISR --> TICK[OSTimeTick Handler]
    TICK -->|update delays| DLY[Delay List / Delta List]
    TICK -->|timeout| WAKER[Wake Blocked Tasks]
    WAKER -->|insert READY| RQ[Ready Queue]
    RQ --> SCHED[OS Scheduler Function]
    SCHED -->|OSHighRdy chosen| SEL{HighRdy != Curr?}
    SEL -->|Yes| CTX[Context Switch OSCtxSw]
    SEL -->|No| RTN[Return to ISR]
    CTX -->|PendSV trap| PSV[ARM PendSV Handler]
    PSV -->|save outgoing| STK1[Push to Outgoing TCB Stack]
    PSV -->|restore incoming| STK2[Pop from Incoming TCB Stack]
    STK2 --> EXC[Exception Return]
    EXC --> TASK[Incoming Task Executes]
    RTN --> TASK
```

### 4.2 Ready Queue as a Bitmap + Multi-Queue Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BM["Priority Bitmap (32-bit)"] -->|CLZ instruction| P0["Priority 0 (highest)"]
    BM --> P1["Priority 1"]
    BM --> P2["Priority 2"]
    BM --> Pn["Priority n (lowest)"]
    P0 --> Q0["FIFO Queue of TCBs at Prio 0"]
    P1 --> Q1["FIFO Queue of TCBs at Prio 1"]
    P2 --> Q2["FIFO Queue of TCBs at Prio 2"]
    Pn --> Qn["FIFO Queue of TCBs at Prio n"]
    Q0 --> HEAD[Scheduler always picks HEAD of selected queue]
    Q1 --> HEAD
    Q2 --> HEAD
    Qn --> HEAD
```

### 4.3 Task State Machine in the Implementation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Dormant
    Dormant --> Ready: TaskCreate / makeRdy
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Preempted by higher priority / time slice
    Running --> Blocked: OSPend on semaphore / mailbox / delay
    Running --> Dormant: TaskDelete
    Blocked --> Ready: Event signaled / delay expired
    Blocked --> Dormant: TaskDelete
    Running --> ISR: Interrupt
    ISR --> Ready: ISR returns and triggers scheduler
```

### 4.4 EDF Scheduler Implementation using a Min-Heap

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    INSERT["Insert Task with Absolute Deadline d_i"] --> HEAPIFY[Heapify Up]
    HEAPIFY --> HEAP[Binary Min-Heap of TCB Pointers]
    HEAP --> ROOT[Root holds task with minimum deadline]
    ROOT --> PICK[Scheduler extracts root in O log n]
    PICK --> EXEC[Dispatch selected task]
    EXEC -->|job completes| REM[Remove from heap]
    EXEC -->|new job arrival| INSERT
    REM --> REHEAP[Heapify Down to restore invariant]
```

### 4.5 Tickless Scheduling Implementation (Modern RTOS)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IDLE["Idle Task Active: CPU in low-power mode"] --> NEXT[Compute time to next event]
    NEXT -->|next = task delay expiry| T1[next event = t1]
    NEXT -->|next = task periodic release| T2[next event = t2]
    NEXT -->|next = timeout| T3[next event = t3]
    T1 --> MIN[min t1, t2, t3]
    T2 --> MIN
    T3 --> MIN
    MIN --> PROG[Program hardware timer for min - safety margin]
    PROG --> SLEEP[Enter WFI / WFE low-power state]
    SLEEP --> WAKE[Hardware timer fires]
    WAKE --> TICK[Resume normal tick scheduling]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between the scheduling algorithm and scheduling implementation in an RTOS. Why is implementation critical in hard real-time systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Scheduling Algorithm** is the *policy* that decides which task should run next based on parameters like priority, deadline, or period (e.g., Rate Monotonic, EDF). **(1 mark)**
2. **Scheduling Implementation** is the *mechanism* — the data structures (ready queue, bitmap), routines (OS_Sched, OSCtxSw), and hardware support (tick timer, PendSV) — that *enforces* the policy in software/hardware. **(1 mark)**
3. In hard real-time systems, the *worst-case* behavior must be bounded. A theoretically optimal algorithm becomes useless if its implementation has unbounded search time, non-atomic context switches, or variable tick jitter. Hence implementation overhead must be analyzed and bounded. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is a context switch? List any four registers/states that must be saved and restored during a context switch in a typical ARM Cortex-M RTOS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A context switch is the kernel procedure of saving the state of the currently running task and loading the state of the next scheduled task, such that execution can resume seamlessly.

Four typical saved/restored items: **(0.5 each = 2 marks)**
1. **Program Counter (PC) / Link Register (LR)**
2. **Stack Pointer (SP) — either PSP or MSP**
3. **General-purpose registers (R0–R12)**
4. **Program Status Register (xPSR — including APSR, IPSR, EPSR)**
5. *(Bonus: CONTROL, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI)* **(1 mark for assembly & completeness)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, describe the **five-pillar implementation architecture** of a typical preemptive priority-based RTOS scheduler. Explain the role of the system tick, ready queue, scheduler, dispatcher, and context switch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider three periodic tasks in a Rate Monotonic system: $\tau_1(T_1=4, C_1=1)$, $\tau_2(T_2=6, C_2=2)$, $\tau_3(T_3=12, C_3=3)$. Assume implementation overhead per scheduling event $C_{overhead} = 0.1$ ms. Determine the **practical worst-case response time** of $\tau_3$ and verify if it meets its deadline $D_3 = 12$ ms. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Solution:**

**Pillar 1 — System Tick:** A hardware timer (e.g., ARM Cortex-M SysTick) generates a periodic interrupt at frequency $f_{tick}$. The ISR calls `OSTimeTick()` which decrements task delays and triggers `OS_Sched()`. **(1.5 marks)**

**Pillar 2 — Task Control Block (TCB):** A statically/dynamically allocated structure storing task stack pointer, priority, state, period, deadline, and register-save area. Each task has exactly one TCB. **(1 mark)**

**Pillar 3 — Ready Queue:** Holds pointers to all TCBs in the READY state. A bitmap+multi-queue gives O(1) priority extraction. A heap is used for EDF. **(1.5 marks)**

**Pillar 4 — Scheduler (`OS_Sched`):** A short kernel function that finds the highest-priority (or earliest-deadline) ready task in bounded time. Returns pointer to that TCB. **(1.5 marks)**

**Pillar 5 — Dispatcher / Context Switch:** On a PendSV trap, the outgoing task's registers are saved onto its stack, the incoming TCB's stack is loaded, and CPU execution resumes in the new task via exception return. **(1.5 marks)**

*(Use the diagrams from SECTION_4 of these notes for full marks.)*

**(b) Solution:**

Given: $\tau_1(T_1=4, C_1=1)$, $\tau_2(T_2=6, C_2=2)$, $\tau_3(T_3=12, C_3=3)$, $C_{overhead} = 0.1$ ms, $D_3 = 12$ ms.

Under RM: $\tau_1$ has highest priority (smallest period). Preemption set $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$.

**Iteration 0:** $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 + C_{overhead} = 3.1$ ms

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(1)} &= C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{3.1}{4} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{3.1}{6} \right\rceil C_2 + (1 + 1 + 1) C_{overhead} \\
&= 3 + (1)(1) + (1)(2) + 3(0.1) \\
&= 3 + 1 + 2 + 0.3 = 6.3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6.3}{4} \right\rceil(1) + \left\lceil \frac{6.3}{6} \right\rceil(2) + 3(0.1) \\
&= 3 + (2)(1) + (2)(2) + 0.3 \\
&= 3 + 2 + 4 + 0.3 = 9.3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 3:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(3)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9.3}{4} \right\rceil(1) + \left\lceil \frac{9.3}{6} \right\rceil(2) + 3(0.1) \\
&= 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) + 0.3 \\
&= 3 + 3 + 4 + 0.3 = 10.3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(4)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10.3}{4} \right\rceil(1) + \left\lceil \frac{10.3}{6} \right\rceil(2) + 3(0.1) \\
&= 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) + 0.3 \\
&= 3 + 3 + 4 + 0.3 = 10.3 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converged: $\boxed{R_3^{impl} = 10.3 \text{ ms} \le D_3 = 12 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark}$

**Conclusion:** Task $\tau_3$ meets its deadline **even with implementation overhead**. The system is feasible under the practical analysis.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare the implementation of **Rate Monotonic (RM)** and **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** schedulers in terms of (i) ready queue data structure, (ii) complexity of scheduler decision, (iii) handling of dynamic priorities, and (iv) feasibility test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain **tickless scheduling** with a block diagram. Why is it preferred in modern low-power IoT RTOS? What is the trade-off involved? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Solution:**

| Aspect | Rate Monotonic (RM) | Earliest Deadline First (EDF) |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Ready Queue | Bitmap + multi-queue (O(1) ops) | Min-heap on absolute deadlines (O(log n) ops) |
| (ii) Scheduler Complexity | O(1) — CLZ on bitmap gives highest prio | O(log n) — heapify on insert/extract |
| (iii) Dynamic Priorities | **Static** — assigned at task creation, never changes | **Dynamic** — recomputed each instance from current deadline |
| (iv) Feasibility Test | Sufficient: $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; Exact: response time analysis | Necessary & sufficient: $U \le 1.0$ |

**(b) Solution:**

**Tickless Scheduling (Concept):**
In traditional RTOS, the system tick fires at a fixed rate (e.g., 1 kHz) even when no task is ready. This wastes CPU cycles and prevents the CPU from entering deep low-power states (Sleep/Stop/Standby). **(2 marks)**

**Block Diagram:** (See SECTION 4.5 — Tickless Scheduling Implementation flow)

**Working:**
1. When all tasks are blocked and only the Idle task runs, the kernel computes the *time to the next scheduled event* (delay expiry, periodic release, or timeout). **(2 marks)**
2. The kernel programs a hardware timer (e.g., RTC or LPTIM) to wake the CPU at `t_next_event − safety_margin`. **(1 mark)**
3. CPU enters `WFI` (Wait For Interrupt) — clocks/peripherals gated off, current draw drops to µA range. **(1 mark)**
4. Hardware timer fires → ISR resumes normal tick-driven scheduling. **(1 mark)**

**Trade-off (0.5 + 0.5):**
- ✅ **Benefit:** Power consumption drops by 10×–1000×; longer battery life.
- ⚠️ **Cost:** Added complexity; harder worst-case timing analysis because the wake-up latency of the low-power mode must be accounted for in $R_i^{impl}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 in the overhead term** — students often write $C_{overhead}$ once per preemption, but the *initial* tick that readies the task also incurs overhead; that's the "+1" multiplier inside the parentheses. **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing scheduler vs. dispatcher** — they are NOT the same. The scheduler *chooses*; the dispatcher *swaps*. Examiners explicitly test this distinction. **−1.5 marks.**
> 3. **Omitting convergence check** in the iteration — you must show at least two iterations where $R^{(k)} = R^{(k-1)}$ to prove convergence. **−1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting to mention that bitmap+CLZ gives O(1) priority extraction** — this is a favorite KTU sub-question in Part A. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Drawing Mermaid with `end` as a node name** — system will refuse to render. Use `nodeEnd` or `taskEnd`.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Algorithm ≠ Implementation.** Policy vs. Mechanism — KTU's #1 test point.
- ✅ The **5 pillars** of scheduling implementation: Tick, TCB, Ready Queue, Scheduler, Context Switch.
- ✅ **Bitmap + multi-queue** is the gold standard for fixed-priority O(1) schedulers (FreeRTOS, VxWorks).
- ✅ **Min-heap** is the typical structure for EDF (dynamic priority).
- ✅ **Context switch = save outgoing state + restore incoming state**; on ARM Cortex-M this happens in the **PendSV** handler.
- ✅ **Scheduler complexity MUST be bounded** — O(1) or O(log n); never O(n) in critical paths.
- ✅ The **practical worst-case response time** recurrence includes implementation overhead:

$$R_i^{impl} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j + \left(1 + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \right) C_{overhead}$$

- ✅ **Tickless scheduling** is essential for low-power IoT — programs a one-shot timer to next event before entering `WFI`.
- ✅ **Convergence** of the response-time recurrence is the proof of feasibility under a *given* implementation.
- ✅ Standard **tick rates**: 100 Hz (Linux PREEMPT_RT) to 1 MHz (high-end DSPs).
- ✅ **Hard real-time ≠ fast** — it means **bounded and predictable** worst-case latency.
- ✅ **Calendar queues** give O(1) amortized for tick-multiple schedulers; advanced topic.
- ✅ **RM feasibility bound**: $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — a *sufficient* (not necessary) test.
- ✅ **EDF optimality**: any feasible task set on one processor is schedulable by EDF (Liu & Layland, 1973).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronization Mechanisms: Practical Implementation of Semaphores and Mutexes in Task Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Synchronization Mechanisms: Practical Implementation of Semaphores and Mutexes in Task Synchronization ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)**, **Synchronization Mechanisms** are kernel-level services used to coordinate the execution of multiple concurrent tasks (threads/processes) so that shared resources are accessed in a mutually exclusive, deterministic, and time-bounded manner. The two primary synchronization primitives covered in KTU Module 2 are: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Semaphore** — A kernel object, originally proposed by **Edsger W. Dijkstra** in 1965, consisting of an **integer counter** and a **wait queue**. It supports two atomic operations: **P() / wait() / take()** (decrement/pend) and **V() / signal() / give()** (increment/post). A semaphore is classified as **Binary Semaphore (0/1)** or **Counting Semaphore (0…N)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mutex (Mutual Exclusion Lock)** — A specialized **binary semaphore** that, in addition to mutual exclusion, enforces **ownership** (only the locking task may unlock) and implements **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** to mitigate unbounded **priority inversion**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restroom Key" & "Parking Lot Token" | Primitive | Real-World Analogy | Why it Works | |-----------|--------------------|--------------| | **Binary Semaphore** | A single restroom key hanging on a hook | Whoever takes the key enters; only one person at a time. The key is *ownerless* — anyone can put it back. | | **Mutex** | A personal locker key assigned to *you* on entry | You must be the locker owner to return the key — prevents accidental cross-release by other tasks. | | **Counting Semaphore** | A parking lot with **N** tokens at the gate | Up to **N** cars may park; the 11th car waits until a token is returned. | ### 1.3 The "Why" — Race Conditions in Embedded Systems In an RTOS such as **FreeRTOS**, **VxWorks**, or **RTX**, multiple tasks (e.g., a sensor-acquisition task, a motor-control task, and a communication task) may try to read/write the *same global variable* or peripheral register (e.g., a UART data register, an SPI bus). Without synchronization: $$\text{Without Synchronization: } \text{Task}_1 \rightarrow \text{UART\_DR} \rightarrow \text{Task}_2 \rightarrow \text{Corrupted Data}$$ This is the classic **Race Condition**, which is *fatal* in hard real-time systems where response must be deterministic. ### 1.4 Standard KTU Metric Callouts > [!NOTE] > - **Jitter** in a real-time system must be ≤ the task's deadline minus WCET. > - **Atomicity** of `wait()` and `signal()` is guaranteed by disabling interrupts or via hardware-supported **Test-and-Set (TAS)** / **Compare-And-Swap (CAS)** instructions. > - A typical RTOS semaphore API call executes in **O(1)** time and is interrupt-safe. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Semaphore Primitives A semaphore `S` is a kernel data structure holding: - `S.count` — an integer - `S.wait_queue` — a list of blocked TCBs (Task Control Blocks) **The two atomic primitives (Dijkstra's notation):** 1. **`P(S)` — Proberen (to test/decrement)** 1. Atomically **decrement** `S.count` by 1. 2. If `S.count < 0`, **block** the calling task and append its TCB to `S.wait_queue`. 3. Else, the task continues execution into the Critical Section. 2. **`V(S)` — Verhogen (to increment)** 1. Atomically **increment** `S.count` by 1. 2. If `S.count ≤ 0`, **wake** one task from `S.wait_queue` (ready state). 3. Else, no task is waiting; the count simply increases. > [!NOTE] > In the KTU syllabus, the modern API names **wait()/signal()** (POSIX) or **pend()/post()** (FreeRTOS-style) are accepted equivalents. ### 2.2 Operational Breakdown — Mutex Primitives A **Mutex** `M` extends the binary semaphore with three extra fields: - `M.owner` — TCB pointer of the task currently holding the lock. - `M.lock_count` — supports **recursive locking** (nested `mutex_lock()`). - `M.priority` — supports **Priority Inheritance Protocol**. **Operations:** - `mutex_lock(M)` → if `M.locked == 0`, set `M.locked = 1`, `M.owner = current_TCB`; else block & set `M.owner → current_TCB->priority`. - `mutex_unlock(M)` → only permitted if `M.owner == current_TCB`; otherwise error. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Semaphore Invariant:} \quad & S.\text{count} \geq 0 \;\; \text{(when implemented as a *counting* semaphore with a positive ceiling)} \\ \text{Atomicity Condition:} \quad & \text{Critical Section execution interval} \;\; \Delta t \;\; \leq \;\; \text{Deadline} - \text{WCET} \\ \text{Resource Utilization:} \quad & U = \frac{C_1 + C_2 + \dots + C_n}{T_1 + T_2 + \dots + T_n} \quad (\text{Rate Monotonic bound}) \\ \text{Priority Inversion Time Bound (PIP):} \quad & T_{\text{inversion}} \leq \sum_{k \in \mathcal{M}} T_k \end{aligned} $$ | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Typical RTOS Value** | **Engineering Meaning** | |---------------|-----------|------------------------|--------------------------| | Semaphore ceiling | $N$ | 1 (Binary), 1…255 (Counting) | Maximum concurrent holders | | Critical section length | $C_i$ | 1 µs – 100 µs | Time the lock is held | | Priority inheritance level | $P_{\text{boost}}$ | $\max(P_{\text{holder}}, P_{\text{waiter}})$ | Temporary priority of mutex owner | | Context-switch cost | $T_{\text{cs}}$ | 1 µs – 10 µs | Time to swap TCBs | | Blocking timeout | $T_{\text{block}}$ | configurable | Maximum time a task may wait | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL — LaTeX Pipe Rule**: Inside every markdown table, the absolute-value bar has been replaced with `\vert` so as not to break the table renderer. ### 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR)**: Mutexes protect shared CAN bus mailboxes; a 5 ms violation can stall airbag deployment. - **Industrial Robotics**: Semaphores coordinate robot-arm joints so two axes never write to the same trajectory buffer simultaneously. - **IoT/Embedded Sensor Hubs**: A counting semaphore with $N = 5$ limits in-flight DMA descriptors, preventing memory exhaustion under burst traffic. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Atomicity of the `wait()` Operation The KTU examiner expects you to demonstrate that **`wait()` and `signal()` are uninterruptible atomic operations**. We model this by combining the *decrement* and *block* steps into a single critical section of the *kernel* itself. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Step 1: Mask interrupts} \quad & \text{IRQ\_MASK} \leftarrow 0 \\ \text{Step 2: Read-modify-write} \quad & S.\text{count} \leftarrow S.\text{count} - 1 \\ \text{Step 3: Branch} \quad & \text{if } S.\text{count} < 0 \text{ then } \text{TCB.state} \leftarrow \text{BLOCKED} \\ \text{Step 4: Yield} \quad & \text{scheduler\_invoke()} \\ \text{Step 5: Unmask interrupts} \quad & \text{IRQ\_MASK} \leftarrow 1 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic row by row:** The interrupt mask prevents any ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) or higher-priority task from observing an intermediate state. Steps 2–4 are the indivisible kernel action. Once the kernel has either let the task proceed or blocked it, interrupts are re-enabled — yielding the canonical **Test-and-Set** atomicity guarantee. ### 3.2 Derivation: Proof of Mutual Exclusion for Binary Semaphore We must show that **at most one task** can be inside the Critical Section (CS) at any instant $t$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Hypothesis:} \quad & \text{Let } T_1 \in CS(t) \text{ and } T_2 \in CS(t) \text{ with } T_1 \neq T_2. \\ \text{Entry condition:} \quad & \text{Both tasks must have completed } P(S) \text{ successfully.} \\ \text{Since } S \in \{0, 1\}, \text{ the first } P(S) \text{ sets } S = 0. \quad & \text{The second } P(S) \text{ attempts } S \leftarrow -1. \\ \text{Block rule:} \quad & \text{If } S < 0, \text{ task is appended to } S.\text{wait\_queue}. \\ \text{Contradiction:} \quad & \text{Only when a } V(S) \text{ occurs does one task leave the queue.} \\ \therefore \;\; |\{T \mid T \in CS(t)\}| \leq 1 \quad & \blacksquare \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Semaphore vs. Mutex vs. Event Flag | **Property** | **Binary Semaphore** | **Mutex** | **Event Flag (Event Group)** | |--------------|----------------------|-----------|-------------------------------| | Ownership | ❌ None | ✅ Single owner | ❌ None | | Priority Inheritance | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (in FreeRTOS) | ❌ No | | Recursive Lock | ✅ Allowed (in some RTOS) | ✅ Allowed | N/A | | Suitable for ISR ↔ Task sync | ✅ Yes | ❌ Not ISR-safe | ✅ Yes | | Suitable for mutual exclusion | ⚠️ Possible but unsafe | ✅ Recommended | ❌ Not for CS | | Typical API | `xSemaphoreTake/Give` | `xSemaphoreTakeRecursive` | `xEventGroupWaitBits` | | Blocking on multiple conditions | ❌ One semaphore only | ❌ One mutex only | ✅ `AND` / `OR` of bits | | O(1) overhead | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ### 3.4 Worked Example — Priority Inversion & Inheritance Consider three tasks at priorities **High (H)**, **Medium (M)**, and **Low (L)**. All share a mutex `Mtx` protecting a peripheral. $$ \begin{aligned} t_0: \quad & L \text{ acquires } Mtx, \text{ enters CS.} \\ t_1: \quad & H \text{ preempts } L \text{ (higher priority), requests } Mtx \Rightarrow \text{blocked on } Mtx. \\ t_2: \quad & H \text{ would have been blocked indefinitely — UNBOUNDED priority inversion.} \end{aligned} $$ **With Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{At } t_1: \quad & Mtx.\text{owner} = T_L, \quad T_H.\text{prio} > T_L.\text{prio} \Rightarrow \text{boost } T_L.\text{prio} \to P_H. \\ \text{At } t_2: \quad & T_M \text{ tries to run but } T_L \text{ now runs at } P_H, \text{ so } T_M \text{ cannot preempt.} \\ t_3: \quad & T_L \text{ releases } Mtx \Rightarrow T_L.\text{prio} \text{ restored}, \; T_H \text{ unblocks immediately.} \end{aligned} $$ > **Bounding inversion:** Inversion time is bounded by the **CS duration** of $T_L$, eliminating unbounded blocking. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — Semaphore Transitions ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Available : init(S, N) Available --> Taken : wait()/P() if count>0 Taken --> Available : signal()/V() when no waiter Taken --> Blocked : wait()/P() if count<=0 Blocked --> Ready : signal()/V() wakes head Ready --> Taken : scheduler dispatch Available --> [*] : delete(S) ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Mutex Lock/Unlock with Priority Inheritance ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Task calls mutex_lock] --> B{Mtx.locked ?} B -- No --> C[locked = true
owner = current_TCB
lock_count++] B -- Yes --> D{owner == current_TCB ?} D -- Yes, recursive --> E[lock_count++
no priority change] D -- No --> F[block task
if waiter prio higher than owner
boost owner prio] F --> G[Append to Mtx.wait_queue] G --> H[Schedule next ready task] H --> I[When owner releases mutex] I --> J[Restore original priority] J --> K[Wake highest-prio waiter] K --> L[Transfer ownership] style A fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style C fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style F fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style K fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Task Synchronization ```mermaid block-beta columns 4 block:groupA["APPLICATION TASKS"]:4 T1["Task A
Priority 5"] T2["Task B
Priority 3"] T3["Task C
Priority 1"] T4["ISR
UART Rx"] end block:groupB["RTOS KERNEL SYNCHRONIZATION LAYER"]:4 SEM["Counting Semaphore
count = N"] MTX["Mutex
owner tracking
PIP enabled"] EVT["Event Group
flags = 8 bits"] Q["Message Queue
depth = 10"] end block:groupC["SHARED RESOURCES"]:4 R1["SPI Bus"] R2["Global Buffer
sensor_data"] R3["EEPROM"] R4["GPIO Port"] end T1 --> SEM T2 --> MTX T3 --> EVT T4 --> Q SEM --> R1 MTX --> R2 EVT --> R3 Q --> R4 ``` ### 4.4 Sequence Diagram — Producer–Consumer with Counting Semaphore ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant P as Producer Task participant S as Counting Semaphore (empty_slots) participant B as Shared Buffer (size N) participant S2 as Counting Semaphore (full_slots) participant C as Consumer Task Note over P,C: Initialization: empty_slots = N, full_slots = 0 P->>S: wait(empty_slots) // P(S) S-->>P: count-- (>=0) P->>B: write(item) P->>S2: signal(full_slots) // V(S2) C->>S2: wait(full_slots) S2-->>C: count-- (>=0) C->>B: read(item) C->>S: signal(empty_slots) ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Differentiate between a binary semaphore and a mutex. State any two scenarios where a mutex is preferred over a binary semaphore.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Binary Semaphore | Mutex | |--------|------------------|-------| | Ownership | None — any task can `signal()` | Only the owner task can `unlock()` | | Priority Inheritance | Not supported | Supported (PIP) | | Use case | Signaling / ISR ↔ Task sync | Mutual exclusion of shared resource | **Scenarios where Mutex is preferred (any two):** 1. Protecting a shared data structure where accidental cross-release could corrupt state (e.g., a linked-list head pointer). 2. When tasks of differing priorities access the same resource and **priority inversion must be bounded** (e.g., motor control + user interface). *(1 mark for correct differentiation table; 2 marks for two valid scenarios.)* --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **What is the **Priority Inversion Problem**? How does the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) mitigate it?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Priority inversion occurs when a higher-priority task is *indirectly* preempted by a lower-priority task — not by direct preemption, but because the low-priority task holds a resource that the high-priority task needs. The inversion period is unbounded if a medium-priority task preempts the low-priority holder. - **PIP mitigation (1.5 marks):** When a high-priority task blocks on a mutex held by a lower-priority task, the kernel **temporarily boosts** the holder's priority to that of the highest-priority waiter. The lower task now runs at the elevated priority, finishes its CS quickly, releases the mutex, and then its priority is restored. The inversion is *bounded* by the CS duration. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. Students frequently *omit the atomicity proof* when describing `wait()`/`signal()`. Marks are deducted if the word **"atomic"** or equivalent (interrupt-mask, TAS) is missing. > 2. In PIP questions, failing to mention the **priority restoration** step after the mutex is released results in a 2-mark loss. > 3. Avoid writing *"semaphore and mutex are the same"* — this is a guaranteed 0 in any mutual-exclusion question. > 4. Always draw at least one state-transition or sequence diagram — diagrams carry 2–3 marks and students often skip them. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Producer–Consumer problem** using **two counting semaphores** `empty` and `full`. Show the algorithm for both processes and prove that it satisfies mutual exclusion and progress. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** with a suitable example involving three tasks (High, Medium, Low priority) sharing a mutex. Show how unbounded priority inversion is bounded. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — (a):** **Initialisation:** - `sem_init(&empty, N);` — counts empty slots in buffer - `sem_init(&full, 0);` — counts full slots **Producer Algorithm:** ```c while (1) { wait(&empty); // P(empty) - decrement empty count wait(&mutex); // protect buffer produce_item(); signal(&mutex); signal(&full); // V(full) - increment full count } ``` **Consumer Algorithm:** ```c while (1) { wait(&full); // P(full) wait(&mutex); consume_item(); signal(&mutex); signal(&empty); // V(empty) } ``` **Proof of Correctness (3 sub-points for full marks):** 1. **Mutual exclusion:** A binary `mutex` ensures only one process modifies the buffer index at a time. 2. **Progress:** If buffer is non-empty, `full > 0`, so consumer proceeds. If buffer is non-full, `empty > 0`, so producer proceeds. 3. **Bounded waiting:** Both semaphores use FIFO queues; no starvation. **Diagram:** Refer to the **Sequence Diagram in Section 4.4** above — replicate this in your answer script for full credit. --- **Model Solution — (b):** Refer to the **Worked Example in Section 3.4**. In your exam script, redraw the *time-line* showing: - $T_L$ acquires mutex at $t_0$. - $T_H$ preempts and blocks at $t_1$. - $T_L$'s priority is **boosted** to $P_H$. - $T_L$ finishes CS, releases mutex at $t_3$. - $T_L$'s priority is **restored** to original. **Conclusion (must write):** PIP bounds the blocking time of the highest-priority task to the **worst-case execution time of the critical section**, not to the execution time of intermediate-priority tasks. *(Marks awarded for explicit bound statement.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`** **(a)** Describe the **internal data structure of a semaphore** in an RTOS kernel. Explain the role of the **wait queue** and the **atomicity** of `pend()` and `post()` operations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **Mutex, Binary Semaphore, Counting Semaphore, and Event Flags** using a parameter table. Provide a real-world scenario for each primitive. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution — (a):** **Internal Data Structure (write as a C-struct in your answer):** ```c typedef struct { volatile uint32_t count; // current count (0..N) list_t wait_queue; // sorted by priority uint8_t type; // BINARY | COUNTING | MUTEX void *owner; // TCB* for mutex, NULL for semaphore uint32_t ceil; // max value for counting semaphore } sem_t; ``` **Wait Queue Role (2 marks):** - The wait queue stores TCBs of tasks blocked on this primitive. - On `post()`, the head of the queue is moved to the **Ready Queue** of the scheduler. - In RTOS, the queue is typically **priority-ordered** to satisfy the highest-priority waiter first (preventing priority inversion at the primitive level itself). **Atomicity (3 marks):** - Achieved by **disabling interrupts** around the read-modify-write of `count`. - Alternative: hardware `LDREX/STREX` (ARM) or `CMPXCHG` (x86) instruction. - Without atomicity, two tasks could simultaneously read `count = 1`, both decrement to `0`, both pass the `< 0` test, and both enter the CS — destroying mutual exclusion. **Kernel Time Complexity:** $O(1)$ for `pend()`/`post()` when wait list is doubly-linked. --- **Model Solution — (b):** Replicate the **Analytical Matrix from Section 3.3** above in your answer. Augment each row with a **scenario** (write each scenario as a 1-sentence justification, ~0.5 marks each): | Primitive | Real-World Scenario | |-----------|---------------------| | Mutex | A medical infusion pump: motor-control task and alarm task both writing to the same dose-register; PIP needed. | | Binary Semaphore | UART ISR signals a "data received" event to a parser task — ownerless handoff. | | Counting Semaphore | DMA engine with 4 channels; allows up to 4 concurrent transfers. | | Event Flags | Wi-Fi task waits for `(WIFI_CONNECTED_BIT \| IP_ASSIGNED_BIT)` — synchronous on multiple sources. | --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **semaphore** is a *counter + wait queue*; a **mutex** is a *binary semaphore + owner + priority inheritance*. - ✅ All `wait()` / `signal()` (or `pend()` / `post()`) calls must be **atomic** — interrupt-mask or hardware TAS. - ✅ **Ownership rule**: only the mutex owner may unlock it; violating this is a hard kernel fault. - ✅ **Priority inversion** is *unbounded by default*; mitigated by **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** — implemented in FreeRTOS via `configUSE_MUTEXES = 1`. - ✅ **Recursive locking**: a task may `mutex_lock` multiple times; tracked via `lock_count`; `mutex_unlock` must be called equal times. - ✅ **Binary Semaphore vs. Mutex in ISR**: Only **semaphores** are ISR-safe (`xSemaphoreGiveFromISR`); never call mutex APIs from ISR. - ✅ **Counting semaphore** $N$ allows up to $N$ holders; effective for *resource pooling* (DMA channels, memory pools). - ✅ **Event Groups/Flags** are the KTU-preferred primitive when a task must synchronize on **multiple simultaneous conditions** (`AND` / `OR` of bits). - ✅ Deadlock-prone patterns to avoid: **circular wait**, **hold-and-wait**, **no preemption**, **mutual exclusion** — break any one to prevent deadlock. - ✅ KTU expected answer components: (i) diagram, (ii) algorithm/code snippet, (iii) atomicity proof, (iv) correctness proof. - ✅ Exam-relevant RTOS names: **FreeRTOS, VxWorks, μC/OS-II, RTEMS, QNX** — always cite API names. - ✅ PIP bounding formula: **Inversion time ≤ Critical Section length of lowest-priority holder** (not sum of all medium-priority tasks).

topics.title: Demonstrating Priority Inversion and Its Mitigation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstrating Priority Inversion and Its Mitigation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly in real-time and multitasking systems where a **higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task**, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities of the two tasks and defeating the purpose of a priority-based scheduler.

Mathematically, if a higher-priority task $T_H$ must wait for a lower-priority task $T_L$ to release a shared resource, and during this waiting period a medium-priority task $T_M$ preempts $T_L$, then the effective execution ordering violates the priority order:

$$P(T_H) > P(T_M) > P(T_L) \quad \text{but} \quad T_M \text{ executes before } T_H$$

where $P(\cdot)$ denotes the static priority assignment. This phenomenon is termed **priority inversion** because the higher-priority task is forced to wait for the lower-priority task to complete — exactly the opposite of what priority-based scheduling guarantees.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — The Hospital Emergency Analogy:**
> Imagine a hospital emergency room with three patients: a **critical patient** (high priority), a **serious patient** (medium priority), and a **minor patient** (low priority). Suppose the minor patient is currently using the only MRI machine (the *shared resource*). The critical patient arrives and needs the MRI urgently, but must wait. While waiting, the serious patient arrives and gets attended to by another doctor (preempting the minor patient). The minor patient cannot finish using the MRI until the serious patient is done. As a result, the **critical patient waits far longer than expected** — this is priority inversion.

The fundamental problem is that **bounded waiting time becomes unbounded** — a high-priority task can be delayed by an arbitrary number of medium-priority tasks, violating real-time guarantees.

## 1.3 Why It Matters in Real-Time Systems

> [!WARNING]
> **Historical Note — The Mars Pathfinder Bug (1997):**
> The Mars Pathfinder spacecraft famously suffered from **unbounded priority inversion** that caused repeated system resets. A low-priority meteorological task held a shared bus semaphore, a high-priority bus management task blocked on it, and medium-priority tasks ran in between, causing watchdog timeouts. The fix was enabling the **Priority Inheritance Protocol** in the VxWorks RTOS.

This makes the study of priority inversion and its mitigation **mandatory** for any RTOS designer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Classic Three-Task Scenario

Consider three periodic tasks sharing a single shared resource $R$ protected by a mutex:

| Task | Static Priority | Period $T_i$ | Execution $C_i$ | Uses $R$? |
|:----:|:---------------:|:------------:|:---------------:|:---------:|
| $T_H$ | High (3) | 50 ms | 20 ms | Yes |
| $T_M$ | Medium (2) | 100 ms | 30 ms | No |
| $T_L$ | Low (1) | 200 ms | 40 ms | Yes |

> [!NOTE]
> Higher number = higher priority (priority value notation). Tasks $T_H$ and $T_L$ both access the shared resource $R$.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Occurrence of Unbounded Priority Inversion

The phenomenon unfolds in distinct phases. Let $t_0$ denote the time the scenario begins.

- **Phase 1 ($t_0 \rightarrow t_1$):** Task $T_L$ starts execution and acquires mutex $R$ at $t_0$.
- **Phase 2 ($t_1$):** Task $T_H$ arrives (its release time) and preempts $T_L$ because $P(T_H) > P(T_L)$.
- **Phase 3 ($t_2$):** Task $T_H$ attempts to lock $R$ but finds it held by $T_L$. $T_H$ **blocks** on $R$ and is moved to the blocked state.
- **Phase 4 ($t_3$):** Task $T_L$ resumes execution (now holding $R$ at the priority of the highest waiter effectively, but naively NOT raised).
- **Phase 5 ($t_4$):** Task $T_M$ arrives and preempts $T_L$ since $P(T_M) > P(T_L)$ and $T_H$ is blocked.
- **Phase 6 ($t_5$):** $T_M$ runs to completion (no resource conflict).
- **Phase 7 ($t_6$):** $T_L$ resumes, finishes critical section, releases $R$.
- **Phase 8 ($t_7$):** $T_H$ unblocks and acquires $R$, completes its work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Observation:** During the interval $[t_2, t_6]$, task $T_H$ is blocked not only by the critical section of $T_L$ but also by the **entire execution of $T_M$**. If multiple medium-priority tasks exist, $T_H$'s blocking time becomes **unbounded**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for solving KTU numerical and analytical questions on priority inversion and blocking time analysis.

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|:--------|:---------------------|:-------------|
| Worst-Case Blocking Time of $T_i$ | $B_i = \max_{j} \text{(critical section length of lower-prio task using shared resource)}$ | Time units (ms / $\mu$s) |
| Effective Worst-Case Response Time | $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i$ | $I_i$ = interference from higher-priority tasks |
| Priority Inheritance Trigger | If $T_H$ blocks on resource held by $T_L$, then $P(T_L) \leftarrow P(T_H)$ temporarily | Dynamic priority boost |
| Priority Ceiling of Resource $R_k$ | $\Pi(R_k) = \max\{P(T_j) \mid T_j \text{ accesses } R_k\}$ | Highest priority of any task using $R_k$ |
| Effective Priority under PCP | $P_{\text{eff}}(T_i) = \max(P_{\text{static}}(T_i), \Pi(R_{\text{held}}))$ | Always at least ceiling |
| Unbounded Inversion Duration | $t_{\text{inv}} = \sum_{m \in \text{Medium}} C_m + C_{\text{cs}}(T_L)$ | Sum of all medium-prio executions between block and unblock |
| Bounded Inversion (with PIP) | $t_{\text{inv}}^{\text{PIP}} = C_{\text{cs}}(T_L)$ | Equals only the critical section length |
| Stack Resource Policy Ceiling | $\hat{\Pi}_s = \max\{\Pi(R_j) \mid R_j \text{ accessed by any task}\}$ | System-wide ceiling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Blocking time $B_i$ appears directly in the **response time analysis** equation, which is the cornerstone of schedulability tests for fixed-priority preemptive systems under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aerospace:** NASA missions (Mars Pathfinder, Deep Space 1) rely on PIP/PCP to guarantee deterministic behavior.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** OSEK/VDX RTOS standards mandate priority ceiling protocol for ECU communication.
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers and infusion pumps require bounded blocking to meet safety integrity levels (IEC 62304, ISO 14971).
- **Industrial Robotics:** Real-time control loops need guaranteed access to sensor data structures.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Blocking Time Under Unbounded Inversion

> [!NOTE]
> We derive an explicit expression for how long a high-priority task $T_H$ can be delayed due to priority inversion in the **unmitigated** case.

Let:
- $C_{\text{cs}}(T_L)$ = length of $T_L$'s critical section accessing resource $R$
- $C_k$ = execution time of any medium-priority task $T_k$ with $P(T_L) < P(T_k) < P(T_H)$
- $N_M$ = number of medium-priority tasks that can release during the inversion window

**Step 1:** Identify the blocking window.
When $T_H$ blocks on $R$, the inversion window opens. It closes only when $T_L$ releases $R$.

**Step 2:** Within this window, any released medium-priority task preempts $T_L$ (since $T_L$'s priority remains low in the unmitigated case).

$$
t_{\text{inv}}^{\text{unbounded}} = C_{\text{cs}}(T_L) + \sum_{k=1}^{N_M} C_k
$$

**Step 3:** Convert this to a worst-case bound.
Under Rate Monotonic scheduling, the worst-case scenario occurs when all medium-priority tasks release just after $T_H$ blocks:

$$
B_H^{\text{worst}} = C_{\text{cs}}(T_L) + \sum_{k: P(T_L) < P(T_k) < P(T_H)} \left\lceil \frac{T_H}{T_k} \right\rceil \cdot C_k
$$

where $\lceil T_H / T_k \rceil$ counts the maximum number of releases of $T_k$ within one period of $T_H$.

**Step 4:** Interpretation.
This shows that **without mitigation, $B_H$ grows linearly with the number of medium-priority tasks** — hence "unbounded" in the limit of system complexity.

## 3.2 Derivation: Bounded Blocking Time Under Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) — Rules:**
> 1. When a task $T_H$ blocks on resource $R$ held by $T_L$, **temporarily raise** $P(T_L) \leftarrow P(T_H)$.
> 2. $T_L$ continues execution at the inherited priority until it releases $R$.
> 3. If $T_L$ blocks on another resource $R_2$ held by $T_{LL}$, propagate the inheritance transitively.
> 4. On releasing $R$, restore $T_L$'s original priority.

**Step 1:** With PIP active, $T_L$ now runs at $P(T_H)$.
Therefore, no medium-priority task $T_M$ can preempt $T_L$.

**Step 2:** The blocking window now equals exactly $T_L$'s remaining critical section time.

$$
B_H^{\text{PIP}} = C_{\text{cs}}(T_L)
$$

**Step 3:** Verify the schedulability condition.
For $T_H$ to meet its deadline $D_H$, we need:

$$
R_H = C_H + B_H^{\text{PIP}} + I_H \le D_H
$$

$$
C_H + C_{\text{cs}}(T_L) + \sum_{k: P(T_k) > P(T_H)} \left\lceil \frac{D_H}{T_k} \right\rceil \cdot C_k \le D_H
$$

This inequality is **strictly tighter** than the unmitigated case.

## 3.3 Derivation: Blocking Time Under Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)

> [!NOTE]
> **Immediate Priority Ceiling Protocol (IPCP):**
> Each mutex $R_k$ has a predefined ceiling $\Pi(R_k) = \max\{P(T_j) \mid T_j \text{ accesses } R_k\}$.
> When a task acquires $R_k$, its effective priority is immediately raised to $\Pi(R_k)$.

**Step 1:** Compute ceiling for resource $R$ shared by $T_H$ and $T_L$.

$$
\Pi(R) = \max\{P(T_H), P(T_L)\} = P(T_H)
$$

**Step 2:** When $T_L$ acquires $R$, its effective priority becomes $\Pi(R) = P(T_H)$.

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(T_L) = P(T_H)
$$

**Step 3:** Key invariant — no task can block on more than one mutex (deadlock-free guarantee).

$$
B_i^{\text{PCP}} = \max_{R_k \text{ accessed by } T_i} C_{\text{cs}}(\text{lower-prio task on } R_k)
$$

**Step 4:** Blocking time per task is bounded by at most **one critical section**.

$$
B_i^{\text{PCP}} \le \max\{C_{\text{cs}}(T_j) \mid P(T_j) < P(T_i)\}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Reference Table — Frequently Asked in KTU:**

| Property | Unbounded (No Protocol) | PIP | IPCP | SRP |
|:---------|:------------------------|:----|:-----|:----|
| Blocking Time Bound | Unbounded | $\le$ sum of CS lengths | $\le$ one CS length | $\le$ one CS length |
| Deadlock-Free | No | No (chained blocking possible) | Yes | Yes |
| Implementation Cost | None | Low | Medium | High |
| Runtime Overhead | None | Inheritance on block | Immediate boost | Precompute stack |
| Transitive Inheritance | N/A | Yes | N/A (prevented) | N/A (prevented) |
| Suitable for Hard RT | No | Limited | Yes | Yes |
| Adopted in POSIX | N/A | `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT)` | `PTHREAD_PRIO_PROTECT` | Not standard |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sequence Diagram: Priority Inversion Timeline

The following Mermaid sequence diagram illustrates the temporal interaction between the three tasks $T_H$, $T_M$, and $T_L$ without any mitigation protocol. The diagram visually captures the blocking chain and the resulting unbounded delay.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant tL as T_LOW
    participant tM as T_MED
    participant tH as T_HIGH
    participant mutex as SHARED_RESOURCE_R
    participant scheduler as SCHEDULER

    Note over tL,scheduler: t0 - System starts
    scheduler ->> tL: DISPATCH T_LOW
    tL ->> mutex: LOCK R (success)
    activate mutex
    Note over tL,mutex: t1 - T_HIGH arrives
    scheduler ->> tH: DISPATCH T_HIGH
    activate tH
    tH ->> mutex: LOCK R (blocked)
    deactivate tH
    activate tH
    Note over tH,mutex: T_HIGH enters BLOCKED state
    scheduler ->> tL: RESUME T_LOW
    Note over tL,scheduler: t2 - T_MED arrives and preempts T_LOW
    scheduler ->> tM: DISPATCH T_MED
    activate tM
    Note over tM,scheduler: T_MED runs uninterrupted
    tM -->> scheduler: COMPLETE
    deactivate tM
    Note over tL,scheduler: t3 - T_LOW resumes
    scheduler ->> tL: RESUME T_LOW
    tL ->> mutex: UNLOCK R
    deactivate mutex
    scheduler ->> tH: UNBLOCK T_HIGH
    tH ->> mutex: LOCK R (success)
    activate mutex
    tH -->> scheduler: COMPLETE
    deactivate tH
    deactivate mutex
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: PIP Mitigation Mechanism

The following block diagram depicts how Priority Inheritance Protocol dynamically elevates the priority of the resource-holding task to eliminate medium-priority preemption.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[System Initialize] --> taskSet[Three Tasks: T_HIGH, T_MED, T_LOW]
    taskSet --> sharedRes[Shared Resource R with Mutex]
    sharedRes --> scenario1[Scenario without Mitigation]
    sharedRes --> scenario2[Scenario with PIP]

    scenario1 --> phase1[Phase 1: T_LOW acquires R]
    phase1 --> phase2[Phase 2: T_HIGH arrives and preempts T_LOW]
    phase2 --> phase3[Phase 3: T_HIGH blocks on R]
    phase3 --> phase4[Phase 4: T_MED preempts T_LOW]
    phase4 --> phase5[Phase 5: Unbounded Delay for T_HIGH]
    phase5 --> outcome1[Result: Deadline Miss Possible]

    scenario2 --> prot1[Step 1: T_LOW acquires R at native priority]
    prot1 --> prot2[Step 2: T_HIGH blocks on R]
    prot2 --> prot3[Step 3: T_LOW inherits P_HIGH]
    prot3 --> prot4[Step 4: T_MED cannot preempt T_LOW]
    prot4 --> prot5[Step 5: T_LOW finishes CS quickly]
    prot5 --> prot6[Step 6: T_LOW releases R and restores priority]
    prot6 --> prot7[Step 7: T_HIGH acquires R and completes]
    prot7 --> outcome2[Result: Bounded Delay, Deadline Met]
```

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Choosing a Mitigation Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    decision1{Does system share resources among tasks?}
    decision1 --> noChoice1[Use basic fixed-priority scheduler]
    decision1 --> yesChoice1{Is hard real-time guarantee required?}

    yesChoice1 --> noHard[Choose Priority Inheritance Protocol]
    yesChoice1 --> yesHard{Is deadlock prevention mandatory?}

    yesHard --> yesDL[Choose Priority Ceiling Protocol]
    yesHard --> noDL[Choose Stack Resource Policy]

    noChoice1 --> endNode1[No inversion protection needed]
    noHard --> endNode2[Use PIP for bounded blocking]
    yesDL --> endNode3[Use IPCP for full deadlock prevention]
    noDL --> endNode4[Use SRP for stack sharing efficiency]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Definition `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define priority inversion in real-time systems. Why is it considered a scheduling anomaly?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority task, effectively inverting the assigned priorities. It occurs when a high-priority task must wait for a shared resource held by a low-priority task, and during this waiting period medium-priority tasks preempt the low-priority task, causing unbounded delay.
>
> **Why an anomaly (1 mark):** It violates the fundamental guarantee of fixed-priority preemptive scheduling — that higher-priority tasks always execute before lower-priority ones. It can cause **deadline misses** in hard real-time systems, leading to catastrophic failures (e.g., Mars Pathfinder reset).

### Question 2: Comparison `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Compare Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) and Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) with respect to deadlock handling and blocking bound.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | PIP | PCP |
> |:-------|:----|:----|
> | Deadlock Prevention | No — chained blocking can occur | Yes — by ceiling priority enforcement |
> | Blocking Bound | Sum of critical section lengths | At most one critical section |
> | Inheritance Type | On block (lazy) | On acquisition (immediate) |
>
> Hence PCP provides **stronger guarantees** and is preferred for hard real-time systems.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the phenomenon of priority inversion with a suitable three-task example. Illustrate the timeline and show why it is called **unbounded priority inversion**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** in detail. Show how it mitigates priority inversion and derive the worst-case blocking time for the high-priority task. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
> Consider three periodic tasks sharing a single resource $R$:
> - $T_H$ (high priority) — accesses $R$
> - $T_M$ (medium priority) — does not access $R$
> - $T_L$ (low priority) — accesses $R$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Timeline (3 marks):**
> - At $t_0$: $T_L$ starts and acquires $R$.
> - At $t_1$: $T_H$ arrives, preempts $T_L$, attempts to lock $R$, **blocks** because $R$ is held by $T_L$.
> - At $t_2$: $T_M$ arrives and preempts $T_L$ (since $T_H$ is blocked and $T_M$ has higher priority than $T_L$).
> - At $t_3$: $T_M$ runs to completion.
> - At $t_4$: $T_L$ resumes, completes its critical section, releases $R$.
> - At $t_5$: $T_H$ acquires $R$, runs, and completes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Why "Unbounded" (3 marks):**
> The blocking duration of $T_H$ equals $C_{\text{cs}}(T_L) + \sum_{k} C_k$, where $C_k$ are executions of all medium-priority tasks released in the interval. If $N$ medium-priority tasks exist, the bound grows with $N$. Hence the inversion is **unbounded** — there is no finite a priori upper limit without protocol enforcement.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — PIP Rules (2 marks):**
> - When $T_H$ blocks on $R$ held by $T_L$, set $P(T_L) \leftarrow P(T_H)$.
> - $T_L$ continues at the inherited priority until $R$ is released.
> - Transitive inheritance applies if $T_L$ itself blocks on another resource.
> - On release, restore original priority.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Mitigation Analysis (3 marks):**
> With PIP active, once $T_L$ inherits $P(T_H)$, no medium-priority task $T_M$ can preempt $T_L$ (since $P(T_L)_{\text{inherited}} = P(T_H) > P(T_M)$). Therefore $T_L$ runs uninterrupted through its critical section.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Blocking Time Derivation (2 marks):**
>
> $$
> B_H^{\text{PIP}} = C_{\text{cs}}(T_L)
> $$
>
> The response time of $T_H$ becomes:
>
> $$
> R_H = C_H + B_H^{\text{PIP}} + I_H = C_H + C_{\text{cs}}(T_L) + \sum_{k: P(T_k) > P(T_H)} \left\lceil \frac{D_H}{T_k} \right\rceil \cdot C_k
> $$
>
> This is **bounded** and independent of medium-priority task executions.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** with its formal rules. Explain how it prevents both priority inversion and deadlock. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare PIP and PCP using a 3-task scenario. Compute the worst-case blocking time under both protocols. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Definition of Ceiling (2 marks):**
> Each resource $R_k$ has a ceiling priority $\Pi(R_k) = \max\{P(T_j) \mid T_j \text{ accesses } R_k\}$. A system ceiling $\Pi_s$ is the maximum of all active resource ceilings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — IPCP Rules (3 marks):**
> - When task $T_i$ locks $R_k$, its effective priority becomes $P_{\text{eff}}(T_i) = \max(P_{\text{static}}(T_i), \Pi(R_k))$.
> - A task $T_i$ may lock $R_k$ only if $P(T_i) > \Pi_s$ (where $\Pi_s$ is the current system ceiling) or if $T_i$ already holds the resource at the system ceiling.
> - On unlocking, recompute the system ceiling and restore priorities if needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Deadlock Prevention (2 marks):**
> Because a task can only enter a critical section if its priority strictly exceeds the system ceiling, and since nested locking is restricted, **circular wait conditions cannot form**. Therefore deadlock is structurally impossible under IPCP.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Scenario (2 marks):**
> Same three tasks $T_H$, $T_M$, $T_L$ with shared resource $R$. Ceiling $\Pi(R) = P(T_H) = 3$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — PIP Analysis (2 marks):**
> Under PIP: $B_H = C_{\text{cs}}(T_L)$. Blocking can extend across multiple critical sections if transitive inheritance occurs (chained blocking risk).

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — PCP Analysis (3 marks):**
> Under PCP: $B_H = C_{\text{cs}}(T_L)$ (same as PIP for this case). However, if there were multiple resources, PCP gives:
>
> $$
> B_H^{\text{PCP}} = \max_{R_k \text{ used by } T_H} C_{\text{cs}}(\text{any lower-priority task on } R_k)
> $$
>
> which is strictly tighter than PIP's chained-blocking bound.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PIP with PCP** — PIP raises priority *on block* (lazy); PCP raises priority *on acquisition* (immediate). Examiners deduct marks if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Forgetting transitive inheritance** — PIP can propagate across multiple tasks; this is often a 2-mark sub-question.
> 3. **Incorrect blocking time formula** — Under PIP, $B_i$ is the *sum* of nested critical sections, not just the outer one.
> 4. **Missing deadline verification** — Always substitute $B_i$ into the response time equation and verify $R_i \le D_i$.
> 5. **Not mentioning deadlock prevention** — PIP does *not* prevent deadlock; only PCP/SRP do. Many students write this incorrectly.
> 6. **Skipping the Mars Pathfinder example** — Examiners love this real-world case; mentioning it scores an easy 1–2 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> - **Priority Inversion** = higher-priority task waits for lower-priority task due to shared resource.
> - **Unbounded Inversion** = high-priority task delayed by medium-priority tasks indefinitely.
> - **PIP** = on block, raise holding task's priority; transitive; bounded but deadlock-prone.
> - **PCP (IPCP)** = on acquisition, raise to ceiling; **deadlock-free**; strict single-CS blocking.
> - **SRP** = stack resource policy; optimized for stack sharing; ceiling-based.
> - **Blocking time under PIP** = sum of nested CS lengths.
> - **Blocking time under PCP** = at most one CS length.
> - **Schedulability test** = verify $R_i = C_i + B_i + I_i \le D_i$ for every task $T_i$.
> - **POSIX mapping** = `PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT` (PIP), `PTHREAD_PRIO_PROTECT` (PCP).
> - **Real-world case** = Mars Pathfinder 1997 bug — caused by unbounded inversion, fixed by enabling PIP in VxWorks.
> - **Standard exam keywords** = *inheritance*, *ceiling*, *deadlock*, *bounded*, *transitive*, *blocking*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Task Communication: Implementing Inter-Task Communication Using Queues and Mailboxes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Task Communication: Implementing Inter-Task Communication Using Queues and Mailboxes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Real-Time Task Communication: Implementing Inter-Task Communication Using Queues and Mailboxes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)**, tasks are concurrent, independently executing units that must synchronize and exchange data in a **deterministic, bounded time**. **Inter-Task Communication (ITC)** is the mechanism by which tasks transfer information or control signals while preserving real-time guarantees.

Two of the most fundamental ITC primitives in classical RTOS theory (as taught under the Liu & Layland extended model and used in µC/OS-II, FreeRTOS, VxWorks, and RT-Linux) are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Queue:** A **FIFO (First-In-First-Out)** kernel object that allows a producer task to send a fixed-size message (or pointer to a message buffer) to one or more consumer tasks. Queues are **non-lossy** when properly sized and operate under atomic copy semantics to prevent race conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mailbox:** A lightweight kernel object that holds a **single message slot** (typically one pointer or one fixed-size data word). It is essentially a queue of depth **one**, used for short signalling between exactly two tasks.

Both objects are managed by the RTOS kernel and accessed only through well-defined API calls (e.g., `OSQCreate()`, `OSQPend()`, `OSQPost()` in µC/OS-II; `xQueueCreate()`, `xQueueSend()`, `xQueueReceive()` in FreeRTOS).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **conveyor belt with labelled boxes** in a factory:

- **Queue = Multi-Belt Conveyor (Depth > 1):** Workers place finished items on one end, and an inspector picks them up from the other end in the **exact order** they arrived. If the inspector is busy, items queue up to a maximum length. This is a **Queue**.
- **Mailbox = A Single Pigeonhole (Depth = 1):** A clerk drops one urgent memo into a slot, and the manager reads it when free. If a second memo arrives before the first is read, behaviour depends on policy — it may **overwrite** the previous one (overwrite-mode mailbox) or the sender may be **blocked** (blocking-mode mailbox).

### 1.3 Why Queues and Mailboxes are Critical in Real-Time Systems

| Requirement | How Queues/Mailboxes Satisfy It |
|---|---|
| **Determinism** | Bounded blocking time (worst-case execution time is analyzable) |
| **Decoupling** | Producer and consumer tasks need not know each other's addresses |
| **Atomicity** | Send/receive operations are non-interruptible kernel calls |
| **Synchronization** | Implicit blocking acts as a rendezvous point |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric used in KTU valuation:** Mailbox/Queue operations are characterized by their **worst-case latency** $W_{\text{IPC}}$ (in microseconds), which must be less than the smallest task deadline in the system.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Model of a Queue

A queue $Q$ is formally defined as a 5-tuple:

$$Q = (B,\ N,\ H,\ T,\ \Pi)$$

where:

- $B$ = size of each message buffer (bytes)
- $N$ = maximum number of messages (queue depth)
- $H$ = head pointer (read index)
- $T$ = tail pointer (write index)
- $\Pi$ = waiting policy (FIFO priority, LIFO, or prioritized)

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Queue Operation Logic

**Step 1 — Initialization (OSQCreate):**
The kernel allocates an **OS_Q** control block, a message array of size $N$, and initializes $H = T = 0$.

**Step 2 — Post/Send (OSQPost):**
1. Disable interrupts / acquire a mutex.
2. Check if queue is **full**: $\text{if } (T + 1) \bmod N = H$ then task blocks (or returns error).
3. Copy message into $\text{buffer}[T]$.
4. Update $T \leftarrow (T + 1) \bmod N$.
5. Increment message counter.
6. If any task is blocked on `Pend`, wake the **highest-priority** waiting task.
7. Enable interrupts / release mutex.

**Step 3 — Pend/Receive (OSQPend):**
1. Check if queue has messages ($H \neq T$).
2. If yes, copy message from $\text{buffer}[H]$ to caller's buffer, update $H \leftarrow (H + 1) \bmod N$.
3. If no, block the task with a timeout $\tau_{\text{wait}}$ and yield CPU.
