- **Tools:** aiT (AbsInt), Bound-T (Tidorum), Chronos, OTAWA.
- **Pros:** Safe upper bound, exhaustive.
- **Cons:** Pessimistic, complex, hardware-specific.

#### 2.1.2 Dynamic (Measurement-Based) WCET Analysis
- Runs the program with many **test inputs** and **end-to-end measurements** (often using high-resolution hardware timers or on-chip trace).
- Records observed execution times, then takes the **observed maximum** + a **safety margin**.
- **Pros:** Realistic, low modeling effort, easy to apply.
- **Cons:** **Not safe** — uncovered paths may take longer; **depends on input coverage**.

#### 2.1.3 Hybrid WCET Analysis
- Combines **static control-flow analysis** (to bound loops and identify infeasible paths) with **dynamic measurement** (to time basic blocks).
- Best practical trade-off for industrial RTOS contexts.

### 2.2 Core Static Analysis Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

#### (a) Path-Based Analysis
- Enumerates all feasible execution paths.
- Finds the path with the **maximum total execution time** by solving a longest-path problem on the CFG.
- For a program with $n$ branches, the number of paths can be **exponential** — infeasible for large code.

#### (b) Tree-Based Analysis
- Builds a **syntax tree / abstract syntax tree (AST)** for the program.
- Recursively computes WCET for sub-trees using:
  - **Sequence rule:** $WCET(S_1; S_2) = WCET(S_1) + WCET(S_2)$
  - **Selection rule:** $WCET(\text{if } c \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2) = WCET(c) + \max(WCET(S_1), WCET(S_2))$
  - **Loop rule:** $WCET(\text{for } i=1 \text{ to } N) = N \cdot WCET(\text{body}) + (N-1) \cdot WCET(\text{loop guard})$

#### (c) IPET — Implicit Path Enumeration Technique (The KTU Favourite)
- Models WCET computation as an **Integer Linear Program (ILP)**.
- Avoids explicit path enumeration by using **flow variables** for edges and basic blocks.
- **This is the most exam-relevant method** — it appears in almost every past KTU paper.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | WCET Definition | $WCET(P) = \max_{\pi \in \Pi} \sum_{b \in \pi} t_b$ | Max over all feasible paths $\Pi$ |
| 2 | BCET Definition | $BCET(P) = \min_{\pi \in \Pi} \sum_{b \in \pi} t_b$ | Min over all feasible paths |
| 3 | Processor Utilization bound | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{WCET_i}{T_i} \le U_{lub}$ | Schedulability condition |
| 4 | RMS Upper Bound (Liu & Layland) | $U_{lub} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ | For $n$ harmonic tasks |
| 5 | EDF Condition | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{WCET_i}{T_i} \le 1$ | Necessary & sufficient for EDF |
| 6 | Tree — Sequence | $WCET(S_1;S_2) = WCET(S_1) + WCET(S_2)$ | Sequential composition |
| 7 | Tree — Selection | $WCET(\text{if}) = \max(WCET(S_1), WCET(S_2)) + t_c$ | Conditional branch |
| 8 | Tree — Loop (max $N$ iter) | $WCET(\text{loop}) = N \cdot WCET(\text{body}) + (N-1) \cdot WCET(\text{guard})$ | Bounded iteration |
| 9 | IPET Objective | Maximize $\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \cdot t_i$ | $x_i$ = count of basic block $i$ executions |
| 10 | IPET Flow Conservation | $\sum_{e \in \text{In}(v)} x_e = \sum_{e \in \text{Out}(v)} x_e$ | Net flow at every CFG vertex |
| 11 | IPET Loop Bound | $x_{\text{back edge}} \le N_{\max}$ | Limits loop iterations |
| 12 | End-to-End Measurement | $WCET_{est} = \max(\text{observed}) + \text{safety margin}$ | Dynamic approach |
| 13 | Response Time (no blocking) | $R_i = WCET_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot WCET_j$ | Fixed-point iteration |
| 14 | Confidence Interval (prob.) | $WCET_{p\%} = \mu + k_p \cdot \sigma$ | $k_p$ depends on confidence level |
| 15 | Hybrid Weighting | $WCET_{hybrid} = \alpha \cdot WCET_{static} + (1-\alpha) \cdot WCET_{measured}$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** In any markdown or written response, **never write** absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ with bare pipes inside a table — it breaks the table renderer. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$. (See row 13 above for correct use of `\lceil` and `\rceil` in the response-time recurrence.)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Avionics (DO-178C):** Certification requires provable upper bounds on execution time.
- **Automotive (AUTOSAR / ISO 26262):** ASIL-D systems (e.g., braking, steering) demand timing guarantees.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Infusion pumps, pacemakers cannot be late.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Hard deadlines for robotic arm movements.
- **Space (NASA / ESA):** Radiation-hardened processors use WCET for task budgeting.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Tree-Based WCET Computation

**Problem (typical KTU Part B style):**
Compute the WCET of the following pseudo-code using the **tree-based method**, given that each statement takes exactly the cycle count shown:

```text
01:  x = 0;                      // 2 cycles
02:  for (i = 1; i <= 10; i++) { // loop guard: 3 cycles
03:    if (a > b) {              // condition test: 4 cycles
04:      y = a + b;              // 3 cycles
05:    } else {
06:      y = a - b;              // 5 cycles
07:    }
08:    x = x + y;                // 2 cycles
09:  }
```

**Step 1 — Decompose into a syntax tree**

The whole program is a *sequence* of: assignment (line 01) and a *for-loop* (lines 02–09).

**Step 2 — Compute the loop body's WCET (without guard cost for body itself)**

$$
WCET(\text{body}) = WCET(\text{line 03}) + \max(WCET(\text{line 04}),\ WCET(\text{line 06})) + WCET(\text{line 08})
$$

Substituting the cycle counts:

$$
WCET(\text{body}) = 4 + \max(3,\ 5) + 2
$$

The maximum of the if-branch and else-branch is selected (worst case):

$$
WCET(\text{body}) = 4 + 5 + 2 = 11\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the loop rule**

For a for-loop iterated $N$ times with one guard per iteration:

$$
WCET(\text{loop}) = N \cdot WCET(\text{body}) + N \cdot WCET(\text{guard})
$$

For $N = 10$ iterations and guard cost $= 3$ cycles:

$$
WCET(\text{loop}) = 10 \cdot 11 + 10 \cdot 3
$$

$$
WCET(\text{loop}) = 110 + 30 = 140\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the prefix statement**

$$
WCET(\text{program}) = WCET(\text{line 01}) + WCET(\text{loop})
$$

$$
WCET(\text{program}) = 2 + 140 = 142\ \text{cycles}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuator's note:** Many students forget to include the *final* loop guard (or the *exit-condition* test). In a for-loop that runs $N$ times, there are **$N$ successful tests** plus 1 *failing* test on exit. If the examiner uses the (more pessimistic) "$N+1$ guards" convention, expect 145 cycles. Read the question's convention carefully.

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — IPET Formulation (Full ILP)

**Problem:** Consider the control flow graph below for a small program with two basic blocks $B_1, B_2$ and a single loop with bound $N_{\max} = 20$.

| Block | Execution Time $t_i$ (cycles) |
|---|---|
| $B_1$ | 5 |
| $B_2$ | 8 |
| $B_3$ (entry) | 2 |
| $B_4$ (exit) | 1 |

Edges in the CFG: $e_1: B_3 \to B_1$, $e_2: B_1 \to B_2$, $e_3: B_2 \to B_1$ (back edge), $e_4: B_1 \to B_4$, $e_5: B_2 \to B_4$.

**Step 1 — Define the IPET decision variables**

Let $x_i$ denote the **execution count** of basic block $i$ in the worst-case run, and $x_e$ denote the **traversal count** of edge $e$.

**Step 2 — Write the IPET objective function**

The total execution time is the sum of (block count × block time). To find the *worst* (longest) execution, we **maximize**:

$$
\text{Maximize}\ \ Z = 2 x_3 + 5 x_1 + 8 x_2 + 1 x_4
$$

**Step 3 — Apply flow-conservation constraints at every vertex**

At $B_1$ (in-edges: $e_1, e_3$; out-edges: $e_2, e_4$):

$$
x_1 + x_3 = x_2 + x_4
$$

At $B_2$ (in-edges: $e_2$; out-edges: $e_3, e_5$):

$$
x_2 = x_3 + x_5
$$

At the entry $B_3$ (only $e_1$ leaves):

$$
1 = x_1
$$

At the exit $B_4$ (only $e_5$ enters, no out-edges):

$$
x_4 + x_5 = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Add the loop bound on the back edge**

The back edge $e_3$ may be traversed at most $N_{\max} = 20$ times:

$$
x_3 \le 20
$$

**Step 5 — Add non-negativity and integrality**

$$
x_i \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\ \ \forall i,\qquad x_e \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}\ \ \forall e
$$

**Step 6 — Solve the ILP**

From $1 = x_1$ (entry constraint) and $x_2 = x_3 + x_5$ plus $x_4 + x_5 = 1$:

We want to maximize $Z$. Since the back-edge is bounded by 20, set $x_3 = 20$. Then:

$$
x_2 = x_3 + x_5 = 20 + x_5
$$

But the loop is bounded: $x_3$ counts back-edges, $x_2$ counts traversals of $B_1 \to B_2$. To get the *most expensive* run, we want $x_2$ as large as possible. The structural constraint from $B_1$:

$$
x_1 + x_3 = x_2 + x_4 \implies 1 + 20 = x_2 + x_4 \implies x_2 + x_4 = 21
$$

Maximizing $Z$ is dominated by maximizing $x_2$ (cost 8) vs $x_4$ (cost 1). So set $x_4 = 0$, $x_5 = 1$, hence:

$$
x_2 = 21 - 0 = 21,\quad x_5 = 1,\quad x_4 = 0
$$

Verify: $x_2 = x_3 + x_5 \Rightarrow 21 = 20 + 1$ ✓. $x_4 + x_5 = 0 + 1 = 1$ ✓.

**Step 7 — Compute the maximum $Z$**

$$
Z = 2(1) + 5(1) + 8(21) + 1(0)
$$

$$
Z = 2 + 5 + 168 + 0 = 175\ \text{cycles}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **The exact value $Z = 175$ is the static WCET upper bound for this program.** The path corresponds to: enter once → $B_1$ → $B_2$ → back-edge 20 times → finally $B_2$ → exit.

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Measurement-Based with Safety Margin

Suppose we run a task 1000 times and observe execution times. The maximum observed is **8.4 ms**, the mean is 4.1 ms, and the standard deviation is 0.9 ms. We want a 99.9% confidence bound.

Using a normal-distribution approximation with $k_{99.9} \approx 3.09$:

$$
WCET_{est} = \mu + k_p \cdot \sigma = 4.1 + 3.09 \cdot 0.9
$$

$$
WCET_{est} = 4.1 + 2.781 = 6.881\ \text{ms}
$$

But if we want a **safe** bound, we take the **maximum of** the observed maximum and the statistical bound:

$$
WCET_{safe} = \max(8.4,\ 6.881) = 8.4\ \text{ms}
$$

This is the dynamic-analysis estimate — but note it is **only as safe as the test coverage**.

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Schedulability Using the Computed WCET

Given three tasks (Liu & Layland parameters):

| Task | WCET $C_i$ | Period $T_i$ | Deadline $D_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 175 (from §3.2) | 500 | 500 |
| $\tau_2$ | 90 | 750 | 750 |
| $\tau_3$ | 60 | 1000 | 1000 |

**Step 1 — Compute processor utilization**

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{175}{500} + \frac{90}{750} + \frac{60}{1000}
$$

$$
U = 0.350 + 0.120 + 0.060 = 0.530
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Liu & Layland RMS upper bound for $n = 3$**

$$
U_{lub}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599
$$

$$
U_{lub}(3) \approx 0.7797
$$

**Step 3 — Compare**

$$
U = 0.530 \le U_{lub}(3) = 0.7797 \quad \checkmark
$$

The task set is **schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often compute $U_{lub}$ for the wrong $n$. Always use the *total* number of tasks in the system, not just the subset under analysis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 WCET Analysis Methodology — Top-Level Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code and Hardware Model] --> B[Control Flow Extraction]
    A --> C[Value Analysis]
    B --> D[Feasible Path Identification]
    C --> D
    D --> E{Choose Technique}
    E --> F[Static Analysis]
    E --> G[Dynamic Measurement]
    E --> H[Hybrid Approach]
    F --> F1[Path Enumeration]
    F --> F2[Tree Based Recursion]
    F --> F3[IPET ILP Formulation]
    G --> G1[Hardware Timer Capture]
    G --> G2[On Chip Trace]
    G --> G3[Statistical Bound Estimation]
    H --> H1[Static Loop Bound plus Measured Block Times]
    F1 --> I[WCET Upper Bound]
    F2 --> I
    F3 --> I
    G1 --> J[Observed Maximum]
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J
    J --> K[Apply Safety Margin]
    K --> I
    H1 --> I
    I --> L[Schedulability Test EDF or RMS]
    L --> M{Schedulable?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Deploy to RTOS]
    M -->|No| O[Re factor Code or Reduce WCET]
```

### 4.2 Control Flow Graph for the IPET Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    start([Entry]) --> nodeB3[B3 Entry t=2]
    nodeB3 -- e1 --> nodeB1[B1 t=5]
    nodeB1 -- e2 if true --> nodeB2[B2 t=8]
    nodeB1 -- e4 if false --> nodeB4[B4 Exit t=1]
    nodeB2 -- e3 back edge --> nodeB1
    nodeB2 -- e5 if false --> nodeB4
    nodeB4 --> stop([Exit])
```

### 4.3 IPET Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[CFG Construction] --> P2[Assign Time to each Basic Block]
    P2 --> P3[Define Flow Variables x for Blocks and Edges]
    P3 --> P4[Write Objective Maximize sum of x_i times t_i]
    P4 --> P5[Add Flow Conservation at each Vertex]
    P5 --> P6[Add Loop Bound Constraints on Back Edges]
    P6 --> P7[Add Non Negativity and Integrality]
    P7 --> P8[Solve ILP using Branch and Bound or Simplex]
    P8 --> P9[Extract Optimal Variable Values]
    P9 --> P10[Compute Maximum Z which is the WCET]
```

### 4.4 Static vs Dynamic — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Static_Analysis_Track
        S1[Source Binary] --> S2[Abstract Interpretation Engine]
        S2 --> S3[Pipeline and Cache Model]
        S3 --> S4[Path Bound Solver]
        S4 --> S5[WCET Safe Upper Bound]
    end
    subgraph Dynamic_Measurement_Track
        D1[Executable on Target] --> D2[Test Harness with Timers]
        D2 --> D3[Many Input Vectors]
        D3 --> D4[Collected Execution Traces]
        D4 --> D5[Statistical Estimator]
        D5 --> D6[WCET Estimate plus Margin]
    end
    S5 --> Out[Final WCET for Scheduler]
    D6 --> Out
    Out --> RMSTest[Rate Monotonic or EDF Check]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
*Define Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET). Why is it important in hard real-time systems?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** WCET of a task is the maximum possible time the task can take to execute on a given hardware platform, considering all feasible input data and all possible execution paths.
- (1 mark) **Formula:** $WCET(P) = \max_{\pi \in \Pi} \sum_{b \in \pi} t_b$ where $\Pi$ is the set of feasible execution paths and $t_b$ is the execution time of basic block $b$.
- (1 mark) **Importance:** In hard real-time systems, missing a deadline can cause catastrophic failure (loss of life, property, or environmental damage). WCET is used as the upper bound on CPU demand in schedulability analysis (RMS, EDF), guaranteeing that deadlines are never missed if the system is schedulable.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
*List and briefly explain any three challenges in WCET estimation on modern processors.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
- **Caches:** A cache hit costs ~1 cycle, a cache miss costs ~100 cycles. Execution time of the same instruction can vary by two orders of magnitude depending on history.
- **Pipelining:** Instructions overlap in pipeline stages, so the time of a single block depends on the surrounding context, not just its own instructions.
- **Branch Prediction / Speculative Execution:** Speculatively executed paths may be squashed, leaving hidden timing effects. Misprediction penalties are hard to bound.
- (Optional extra) **Timing Anomalies:** A locally faster decision can cause a globally slower execution, violating monotonicity.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Tree-Based & IPET Path Analysis**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Compute the WCET of the following code using the **tree-based method**. Each statement's cycle cost is given in the comments. (7 marks)

```c
y = 0;                       // 3 cycles
for (i = 1; i <= 8; i++) {   // guard = 2 cycles
    if (flag == 1) {         // condition = 4 cycles
        y = y + a[i];        // 6 cycles
    } else {
        y = y - a[i];        // 4 cycles
    }
    x = y * 2;               // 5 cycles
}
return y;                    // 2 cycles
```

**(b)** Explain the **Implicit Path Enumeration Technique (IPET)**. State the ILP formulation in terms of flow variables, objective function, and constraints. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — Tree-Based WCET (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Decompose the program**

- The program is a *sequence* of:
  - An initial assignment `y = 0`
  - A *for-loop* of 8 iterations
  - A *return* statement

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Compute the loop body's WCET**

Inside the loop body, the structure is: condition check (line 3) → if-then-else (line 4 or 5) → assignment (line 6).

$$
WCET(\text{body}) = WCET(\text{cond}) + \max(WCET(\text{then}),\ WCET(\text{else})) + WCET(\text{line 6})
$$

$$
WCET(\text{body}) = 4 + \max(6,\ 4) + 5
$$

The worst case is the `then` branch (`+ a[i]`):

$$
WCET(\text{body}) = 4 + 6 + 5 = 15\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Apply the loop rule for $N = 8$**

$$
WCET(\text{loop}) = N \cdot WCET(\text{body}) + N \cdot WCET(\text{guard})
$$

$$
WCET(\text{loop}) = 8 \cdot 15 + 8 \cdot 2 = 120 + 16 = 136\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Combine sequence**

$$
WCET(\text{program}) = 3 + 136 + 2 = 141\ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Final answer**

$$
\boxed{WCET = 141\ \text{cycles}}
$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — IPET Explanation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Concept**
- IPET was proposed by **Li, Malik, and Wolfe (1995)**. It does *not* explicitly enumerate paths (which can be exponential); instead it represents path information **implicitly** using variables associated with CFG elements.
- Each basic block $i$ has a **flow variable** $x_i$ = number of times the block executes in the worst-case run. Each edge $e$ has a flow variable $x_e$ similarly.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Objective function**
The total execution time is the sum of (block count × block time). The worst-case run is the one that maximizes this:

$$
\text{Maximize}\ \ Z = \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i \cdot t_i
$$

where $t_i$ is the worst-case execution time of basic block $i$ on the target hardware.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Constraints**
- **Flow conservation** at every vertex $v$ of the CFG:

$$
\sum_{e \in \text{In}(v)} x_e = \sum_{e \in \text{Out}(v)} x_e
$$

- **Loop bound constraints** on back edges:

$$
x_{\text{back edge}} \le N_{\max}
$$

- **Structural constraints** (e.g., the entry edge is taken exactly once, the exit edge exactly once):

$$
x_{\text{entry}} = 1,\quad x_{\text{exit}} = 1
$$

- **Non-negativity and integrality:** $x_i, x_e \in \mathbb{Z}_{\ge 0}$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Solving**
The ILP is solved using standard solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi, lp_solve). The optimal value of $Z$ is the static WCET bound.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Dynamic & Hybrid Analysis**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Compare **static**, **dynamic**, and **hybrid** WCET estimation techniques. Mention at least two advantages and one disadvantage of each. (7 marks)

**(b)** A task is executed 5000 times on the target hardware. The observed execution times have mean $\mu = 2.3$ ms, standard deviation $\sigma = 0.4$ ms, and observed maximum $t_{\max} = 4.1$ ms. Estimate the WCET at the 99% confidence level ($k_{99} = 2.33$). Discuss why measurement-based WCET may be unsafe. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — Comparison (7 marks)**

| Technique | Method | Advantages | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static** | Analyzes code + hardware model without execution | (i) Safe upper bound guaranteed (ii) No code execution needed; works at design time | (i) Highly pessimistic (ii) Requires detailed, processor-specific hardware model |
| **Dynamic** | Runs the program with test inputs, measures time | (i) Realistic, no detailed hardware model (ii) Easy to set up, fast turnaround | (i) **Not safe** — uncovered paths may be longer (ii) Depends on input coverage and test vectors |
| **Hybrid** | Static control-flow + dynamic block timing | (i) Better precision than pure static (ii) Safer than pure dynamic (loop bounds guaranteed) | (i) Still inherits some pessimism (ii) Tooling complexity |

(Marks distributed: 1 mark for the *comparison* table layout, 2 marks each for static and dynamic's pros & cons, 1 mark for hybrid's distinct nature, 1 mark for the overall conclusion that no single method is universally best.)

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — Measurement-Based WCET (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Statistical upper bound at 99% confidence**

$$
WCET_{stat} = \mu + k_{99} \cdot \sigma
$$

$$
WCET_{stat} = 2.3 + 2.33 \cdot 0.4
$$

$$
WCET_{stat} = 2.3 + 0.932 = 3.232\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Safe bound from observed maximum**

Since the statistical estimate is below the observed maximum, the safe measurement-based bound is:

$$
WCET_{measured} = t_{\max} = 4.1\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Apply safety margin**

A typical safety margin in RTOS practice is 10–20%:

$$
WCET_{safe} = 1.20 \cdot 4.1 = 4.92\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4 (3 marks) — Why is it unsafe?**
- (1 mark) **Untested paths:** A code path never triggered by any test input has *zero* observed time, but in reality it could take a long time.
- (1 mark) **Input coverage:** Without a formal proof of input coverage, we cannot guarantee that the test set triggers the *worst-case* path. Branches whose worst-case condition is hard to reach may never be exercised.
- (1 mark) **Hardware state:** Different cache states, interrupt timings, or DMA contention may not all be reproduced. Modern processors have **exponential** state spaces that cannot be exhaustively tested.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing BCET with WCET** in formulas. WCET is the *maximum*, not the *minimum*.
> 2. **Forgetting loop guard costs** in tree-based computation. Always add $N \cdot t_{guard}$.
> 3. **Choosing the wrong branch** in selection rules. The *worst-case* (max) branch is taken.
> 4. **Using $\lvert x \rvert$ with bare pipes** in markdown tables — it breaks the table renderer. Use `\lvert` and `\rvert` (this is a presentation issue, not a math issue, but KTU may deduct marks if your answer sheet is illegible).
> 5. **In IPET, omitting flow conservation** at intermediate vertices. A common mistake is to write constraints *only* for entry and exit.
> 6. **Citing tool names as "guarantees"** — e.g., saying "aiT gives the exact WCET" is wrong; it gives a *safe upper bound* that may be an over-approximation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / WCET Estimation**

- **WCET** = max time over **all feasible paths and all inputs** on a specific hardware. It is a **safe upper bound**, not the *actual* worst observed time.
- **BCET** is the dual: the minimum over all feasible paths.
- **Three families of techniques:** Static, Dynamic, Hybrid.
- **Static techniques** to remember by name: **Path-based, Tree-based, IPET (ILP-based)**.
- **Tree-based rules:** sequence (add), selection (max), loop (N·body + N·guard).
- **IPET** uses **flow variables** $x_i$ for blocks and $x_e$ for edges. The objective is to **maximize** $\sum x_i t_i$ subject to flow-conservation, loop-bound, and integrality constraints.
- **Dynamic analysis** is fast and realistic but **not safe** — never use it alone for hard RT safety-critical certification.
- **Hybrid analysis** = static loop bound + measured block times — practical industry choice.
- **Key hardware phenomena** that break simple cycle counting: **caches, pipelines, branch prediction, timing anomalies**.
- **Schedulability link:** RMS upper bound $U_{lub} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$; EDF bound $\sum C_i/T_i \le 1$.
- **Liu & Layland** assumptions to remember: independent, periodic, fixed priority, zero jitter, no blocking, deadline = period.
- **Probabilistic WCET (pWCET):** $WCET_{p\%} = \mu + k_p \sigma$, used in mixed-criticality systems.
- **WCET for hard RT is mandatory**; for soft RT it is a statistical estimate; for firm RT it is the deadline minus zero (worst observed case).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timing Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Timing Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Timing Analysis?

**Timing Analysis** in the context of Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS) is the formal, mathematical process of verifying whether a set of real-time tasks can meet all their temporal (timing) constraints — specifically **deadlines** — under a given scheduling algorithm, before the system is deployed. It is a *static, offline* analytical technique used by system designers to *prove* the correctness of a hard real-time system, rather than relying on testing alone.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Timing Analysis answers the question:

> *"Given $n$ periodic tasks with known worst-case execution times, periods, and deadlines, will a specific scheduling policy (e.g., Rate Monotonic, Deadline Monotonic, EDF) guarantee that no task ever misses its deadline?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERAT757 Module 3):** Timing analysis is the mathematical backbone of *schedulability analysis*. It must be performed **before** system deployment to certify the system as a **hard real-time system**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Railway Station Analogy

Imagine a single-platform railway station that must dispatch four trains daily:

| Train | Journey Time (WCET) | Frequency of Arrival (Period) | Must Leave By (Deadline) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rajdhani | 2 hrs | Every 8 hrs | 8 hrs after arrival |
| Shatabdi | 1 hr | Every 5 hrs | 5 hrs after arrival |
| Duronto | 0.5 hr | Every 2 hrs | 2 hrs after arrival |
| Local | 0.2 hr | Every 1 hr | 1 hr after arrival |

The Station Master (RTOS Scheduler) must decide *which train to dispatch first* so that no train ever waits so long that it misses its departure deadline. **Timing Analysis** is the formal calculation the Station Master performs *the night before* — using the travel times and frequencies — to *guarantee* tomorrow will be on time. If the math says it is impossible, the Station Master rejects the schedule and asks management to cancel one train.

The **travel time** = Worst-Case Execution Time ($C_i$).
The **frequency** = Period ($T_i$).
The **departure deadline** = Relative Deadline ($D_i$).
The **actual departure time minus arrival time** = Response Time ($R_i$).

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Task Timing Parameters

Every periodic real-time task $\tau_i$ in timing analysis is characterized by the following canonical tuple:

$$
\tau_i \;=\; (C_i,\; T_i,\; D_i,\; \Phi_i)
$$

Where:

- **$C_i$** — Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET). The *longest* time the task $\tau_i$ can ever occupy the CPU in a single instance. It is the upper bound, not the average. **Unit: time units (ms, µs, or clock ticks).**
- **$T_i$** — Period. The time interval between two successive activations (job releases) of task $\tau_i$. **Unit: time units.**
- **$D_i$** — Relative Deadline. The maximum allowed time from task activation to its completion. The absolute deadline is $\Phi_i + D_i$. **Unit: time units.**
- **$\Phi_i$** — Phase. The time of the first release of task $\tau_i$. For simplicity, KTU problems typically set $\Phi_i = 0$ (all tasks released simultaneously at $t=0$, the *critical instant*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Instant**: In timing analysis, the *worst-case response time* of a task always occurs at the **critical instant** — the time instant when it is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks. This is a foundational theorem (Joseph & Pandya, 1986) used in every KTU-level response time problem.

---

## 1.4 The Two Grand Questions Timing Analysis Must Answer

1. **Schedulability Test (Yes/No):** Will all tasks meet their deadlines?
2. **Response Time Computation (Quantitative):** What is the worst-case response time $R_i$ of each task, and is $R_i \leq D_i$?

These are answered through two complementary analytical frameworks:
- **Utilization-Based Tests** (sufficient conditions — fast but conservative).
- **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** / **Time Demand Analysis (TDA)** (exact, sufficient *and* necessary conditions).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of a Timing Analysis Problem

Every KTU 2024 Scheme Timing Analysis problem follows a strict logical flow. Mastering this flow is the single most important step toward scoring full marks in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### Step 1 — Identify the Scheduling Policy
The two policies tested under Module 3 are:

- **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** Fixed priority. Priority assigned by *shorter period = higher priority*. Implicit deadline assumed: $D_i = T_i$.
- **Earliest Deadline First (EDF):** Dynamic priority. Task with the *earliest absolute deadline* runs first.

### Step 2 — Compute Processor Utilization
The **total processor utilization** $U$ is the fraction of CPU time consumed by the task set:

$$
U \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is utilization important?** Liu & Layland (1973) proved that for RMS, $U \leq 1$ is *necessary* but **not sufficient** for schedulability. RMS can have $U$ as high as ~0.828 and still be schedulable, but $U$ can also be 0.7 and *fail* if periods are badly aligned.

### Step 3 — Apply the Appropriate Test
- **Sufficient Test (fast check):** Compare $U$ against Liu & Layland's upper bound.
- **Exact Test (definitive):** Perform Response Time Analysis or Time Demand Analysis iteration.

### Step 4 — Iterate Until Convergence
The response time equation is recursive. You must iterate the equation $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + I_i(R_i^{(k)})$ until the value **converges** (stops growing) or **exceeds** $D_i$ (fails).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. Memorize every row. Every KTU ESE Module-3 problem on Timing Analysis is solved using one or more of these formulas.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Symbols Explained | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Processor Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $n$ = number of tasks, $C_i$ = WCET, $T_i$ = Period | Computing total CPU load |
| 2 | Liu & Layland Upper Bound (RMS) | $U_{LL}(n) = n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | $n$ = number of tasks | Sufficient test for RMS schedulability |
| 3 | Sufficient RMS Test | $U \leq U_{LL}(n) \implies$ Schedulable | — | Quick sufficient check |
| 4 | Hyperbolic Bound (RMS) | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 + \frac{C_i}{T_i}\right) \leq 2$ | — | Tighter sufficient test than Liu & Layland |
| 5 | Deadline Monotonic Test (Exact) | $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | $hp(i)$ = set of tasks with priority $\geq \tau_i$ | Computing exact worst-case response time |
| 6 | Time Demand Analysis (TDA) | $w_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | $t$ is a candidate completion time | Alternative to RTA — graphical method |
| 7 | EDF Schedulability (Exact) | $\forall L \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L^{*}\} : \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{L}{T_i} \right\rfloor C_i \leq L$ | $L^{*} = \text{lcm}(T_1, \ldots, T_n)$ | Exact sufficient+necessary test for EDF |
| 8 | Critical Instant Theorem | $R_i^{max}$ occurs when all higher-priority jobs are released at $t = \Phi_i$ | Joseph & Pandya (1986) | Foundation of all RTA |
| 9 | Schedulability Condition | $R_i \leq D_i \;\;\forall i$ | $R_i$ = converged response time | Final pass/fail decision |
| 10 | Idle Time Bound (RMS) | $t_0 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_i - C_i)}{n - 1}$ (when $n \geq 2$) | — | First instant a feasible schedule must have CPU idle |

### Boundary Conditions and Special Notes

- **Pipe Rule Compliance:** In the formulas above, $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ denotes the **ceiling function** (smallest integer $\geq x$). $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ denotes the **floor function** (largest integer $\leq x$). They must be rendered using `\lceil` and `\rfloor` LaTeX commands to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.
- **Convergence Rule:** Iteration starts with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ and stops when either (a) $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (converged → use that value), or (b) $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (failed → declare task unschedulable).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Timing analysis is not merely a textbook exercise. It is the *certification engine* behind safety-critical embedded systems:

- **Avionics (DO-178C Level A):** Flight control software on a Boeing 787 or Airbus A350 must undergo formal schedulability analysis at the design stage. A single missed deadline could be catastrophic.
- **Automotive (ISO 26262 ASIL-D):** Brake-by-wire and Electronic Stability Control (ESC) ECUs use timing analysis to certify that all control loops finish within their 5–10 ms control period.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Implantable pacemakers and infusion pumps are analyzed with timing analysis to prove deterministic response.
- **Industrial Robotics (IEC 61508 SIL-3):** Robotic arms in automotive assembly lines.

In every case, the KTU Formula Sheet above is the engineer's working toolkit.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland's Upper Bound for RMS

This is a high-yield derivation for KTU ESE. We derive the maximum processor utilization $U_{LL}(n)$ below which a task set is **guaranteed** to be schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

### Step 1 — Consider the Critical Instant
At the critical instant, all $n$ tasks are released simultaneously at $t = 0$. Under RMS, task $\tau_1$ (shortest period) has the highest priority and runs first.

### Step 2 — Identify the Worst-Case Completion Pattern
The classic counterexample for RMS is the "ceiling" pattern: each lower-priority task $\tau_i$ is preempted by all higher-priority tasks $\tau_j$ ($j < i$) exactly $\lceil T_i / T_j \rceil - 1$ times within its own period $T_i$.

### Step 3 — Liu & Layland's Inductive Construction
For the bound to hold, the maximum total utilization occurs when each task completes *exactly* at its deadline. Liu & Layland (1973) showed via induction that for $n$ tasks, the smallest upper bound on $U$ that guarantees schedulability satisfies the recurrence:

$$
U_{LL}(n) \;=\; n \cdot \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

Let us verify the boundary cases:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{LL}(1) &= 1 \cdot (2^{1/1} - 1) = 1 \cdot (2 - 1) = 1 \\
U_{LL}(2) &= 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot (1.4142 - 1) \approx 0.8284 \\
U_{LL}(3) &= 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \approx 0.7798 \\
U_{LL}(\infty) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n} - 1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

The last line uses the standard limit $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^x - 1}{x} = \ln 2$.

### Step 4 — The Final Test
The Liu & Layland sufficient test is:

$$
\text{If } \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) \implies \text{Task set is RMS-Schedulable}
$$

**However**, if $U$ is *greater* than $U_{LL}(n)$, the test is **inconclusive** — the task set *may* still be schedulable; we must use Response Time Analysis (exact test).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Joseph-Pandya Response Time Analysis (RTA)

This is the **exact** (necessary and sufficient) test for fixed-priority preemptive scheduling.

### Step 1 — Define the Worst-Case Response Time
The response time of task $\tau_i$ in any given job is the time from its release to its completion. The *worst-case* response time $R_i$ is the maximum over all jobs. By the critical instant theorem, this maximum occurs at $t = 0$ when all tasks are released together.

### Step 2 — Express Response Time as Execution + Interference
$R_i$ is composed of:
- $\tau_i$'s own computation: $C_i$.
- Interference from higher-priority tasks: total time during which a higher-priority task is *ready to run* in the interval $[0, R_i]$.

### Step 3 — Compute the Number of Interfering Jobs
A higher-priority task $\tau_j$ (with period $T_j$) released at $t = 0$ will be re-released every $T_j$. In the interval $[0, R_i]$, the number of jobs of $\tau_j$ that arrive and *must* run before $\tau_i$ completes is:

$$
N_j(R_i) = \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil
$$

### Step 4 — Assemble the Recursive Equation
The total time consumed by $\tau_i$ and all its higher-priority interference is:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} \;=\; C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

where $hp(i)$ is the set of all tasks with priority *strictly higher* than $\tau_i$ (and including $\tau_i$ if we want the busy window).

### Step 5 — Iterate to Convergence
- **Initialize:** $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$.
- **Iterate:** $k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$
- **Stop Condition 1 (Converged):** $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ → use $R_i = R_i^{(k)}$.
- **Stop Condition 2 (Failed):** $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ → declare unschedulable immediately.
- **Stop Condition 3 (Stalled, Guaranteed Fail):** $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k-1)}$ oscillating without growth and $R_i^{(k)} > D_i$ → unschedulable.

### Step 6 — Final Decision
$$
\text{If } R_i \leq D_i \;\;\forall i \in \{1, \ldots, n\} \implies \text{Task set is Schedulable}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Analytical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **Problem (Typical KTU Module 3 ESE):** Consider three periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling:
> - $\tau_1 = (C_1=1,\; T_1=4,\; D_1=4)$
> - $\tau_2 = (C_2=2,\; T_2=6,\; D_2=6)$
> - $\tau_3 = (C_3=3,\; T_3=10,\; D_3=10)$
>
> Perform a timing analysis to determine whether the task set is schedulable.

### Step A — Compute Processor Utilization

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10}
$$

$$
U = 0.2500 + 0.3333 + 0.3000 = 0.8833
$$

### Step B — Apply Liu & Layland Bound for $n=3$

$$
U_{LL}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) \approx 0.7798
$$

Since $U = 0.8833 > U_{LL}(3) = 0.7798$, the sufficient test **fails**. We must perform **exact** Response Time Analysis.

### Step C — RMS Priority Assignment
Under RMS: $\tau_1$ (period 4) > $\tau_2$ (period 6) > $\tau_3$ (period 10). Therefore $hp(1) = \emptyset$, $hp(2) = \{\tau_1\}$, $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$.

### Step D — Response Time for $\tau_1$
$\tau_1$ has no higher-priority interference:

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 1 \leq D_1 = 4 \quad \checkmark
$$

### Step E — Response Time for $\tau_2$ (Iterate)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= C_2 = 2 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + (1)(1) = 3 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + (1)(1) = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Converged at $R_2 = 3 \leq D_2 = 6$ $\checkmark$.

### Step F — Response Time for $\tau_3$ (Iterate)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= C_3 = 3 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + (1)(1) + (1)(2) = 6 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) = 7 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + (2)(1) + (2)(2) = 9 \\
R_3^{(4)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) = 10 \\
R_3^{(5)} &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

Converged at $R_3 = 10 \leq D_3 = 10$ $\checkmark$.

### Step G — Final Verdict
**The task set is RMS-schedulable** — all three tasks meet their deadlines exactly or with margin, even though the Liu & Layland sufficient test *initially failed*. This is the precise reason RTA is mandatory in any rigorous timing analysis.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Test

| Scenario | Best Test | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Quick feasibility check, low stakes | Liu & Layland | One-line computation, $O(n)$ |
| Tight real-time design, need certainty | Response Time Analysis | Necessary + sufficient, but $O(n \cdot R)$ |
| Task set with $D_i < T_i$ (constrained deadlines) | Deadline Monotonic Analysis | RMS assumes $D_i = T_i$; DMA generalizes RTA |
| Dynamic priority systems | EDF Processor Demand Test | Necessary + sufficient for EDF |
| Sporadic task sets (aperiodic with min inter-arrival) | RTA with release jitter | Models worst-case release pattern |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Timing Analysis — Master Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the canonical decision flow a KTU student must follow when presented with a timing analysis problem in the ESE.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Timing Analysis] --> B[Identify Scheduling Policy: RMS or EDF]
    B --> C{Policy is RMS?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Assign Priority by Period: shorter T_i = higher priority]
    C -- No --> E[Use EDF Processor Demand Test]
    D --> F[Compute Total Utilization U]
    F --> G{Apply Liu and Layland Bound}
    G -- U less than or equal to U_LL of n --> H[Declare Schedulable: SUFFICIENT test passed]
    G -- U greater than U_LL of n --> I[Sufficient test INCONCLUSIVE]
    I --> J[Perform Response Time Analysis RTA]
    J --> K[Initialize R_i to C_i]
    K --> L[Iterate R_i new equals C_i plus sum over j in hp of i of ceiling of R_i old over T_j times C_j]
    L --> M{Converged?}
    M -- Yes and R_i less than or equal to D_i --> N[Task i is SCHEDULABLE]
    M -- No --> O{Response Time greater than Deadline?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Task i is UNSCHEDULABLE: FAIL]
    O -- No --> L
    E --> Q[Check Processor Demand Condition for all L from 1 to L star]
    Q --> R{All Conditions Satisfied?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Task Set is EDF Schedulable]
    R -- No --> T[Task Set is EDF Unschedulable]
```

## 4.2 Response Time Iteration — Block Architecture

This diagram shows the iterative computational architecture used to compute $R_i$ for a single task under fixed-priority scheduling.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In1[Input: C_i, D_i, T_i for all tasks] --> Sort[Step 1: Sort tasks by period ascending]
    Sort --> Prio[Step 2: Assign RMS priorities]
    Prio --> Loop[Step 3: For each task tau_i from highest to lowest priority]
    Loop --> Init[Step 4: Initialize R_i old equals C_i]
    Init --> Comp[Step 5: Compute R_i new equals C_i plus sum over higher priority tasks of ceiling of R_i old over T_j times C_j]
    Comp --> Check{Step 6: R_i new equals R_i old?}
    Check -- Yes --> Done[Step 7: Converged. Set R_i equals R_i old]
    Check -- No --> Bnd{R_i new greater than D_i?}
    Bnd -- Yes --> Fail[Step 8: UNSCHEDULABLE. Abort]
    Bnd -- No --> Comp
    Done --> Next{i equals n?}
    Next -- No --> Loop
    Next -- Yes --> Final[Step 9: All R_i less than or equal to D_i? Declare SCHEDULABLE]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Time Demand Function

The Time Demand Analysis (TDA) method, an alternative to RTA, can be visualized as a comparison of two functions of time $t$:

- **Time Demand Function** $w_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil t / T_j \rceil C_j$ — *monotonically non-decreasing staircase*.
- **Available Time Line** $y = t$ — *45-degree straight line*.

A task $\tau_i$ is schedulable if and only if there exists a $t \in (0, D_i]$ such that $w_i(t) \leq t$ (i.e., the staircase lies *on or below* the diagonal). The smallest such $t$ is the worst-case response time $R_i$.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TDA for task tau_i] --> B[Plot w_i of t as staircase: t in 0 to D_i]
    B --> C[Plot line y equals t as 45 degree diagonal]
    C --> D{Does staircase meet or stay below diagonal at any t less than or equal to D_i?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Smallest such t equals R_i. Task is SCHEDULABLE]
    D -- No --> F[Staircase strictly above diagonal in 0 to D_i. Task is UNSCHEDULABLE]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

### Q1. Define Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET). Why is it essential in real-time timing analysis? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) of a task $\tau_i$, denoted $C_i$, is the **maximum** possible execution time the task can consume on the target hardware across all possible input data and execution paths. It is an *upper bound* — not an average or typical value.

It is essential in real-time timing analysis because:
1. It is the **only safe** input for schedulability tests — using average execution times would underestimate interference and risk missed deadlines in production.
2. Schedulability theorems (Liu & Layland, Joseph-Pandya RTA) are mathematically proven under the assumption of known $C_i$.
3. For hard real-time systems, missing a deadline is a system failure; therefore, planning must use the *worst*, not the average.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### Q2. State the Critical Instant Theorem as used in Response Time Analysis. (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Critical Instant Theorem (Joseph & Pandya, 1986) states:

> *"The worst-case response time of a task $\tau_i$ under a fixed-priority preemptive scheduler occurs at a critical instant — a time instant at which $\tau_i$ is released simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks."*

In simpler terms, the *longest* time a task may take to complete (i.e., its worst-case response time) is observed during the first job released at $t = 0$, when all other tasks are also released at the same moment. This is why all RTA iterations begin at $t = 0$ with all phases aligned.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Module 3 Internal Choice]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Response Time Analysis on RMS

Consider a real-time system with three periodic tasks scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 5 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 2 | 10 | 10 |

Perform a complete timing analysis and determine if the task set is schedulable.

#### (a) Compute the processor utilization and apply the Liu & Layland sufficient test. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Total Utilization:**

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{5} + \frac{2}{10}
$$

$$
U = 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 0.85
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Liu & Layland Bound for $n=3$:**

$$
U_{LL}(3) = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.7798
$$

**Step 3 — Compare:**

$$
U = 0.85 > U_{LL}(3) = 0.7798
$$

**Conclusion of Part (a):** The sufficient test **fails**. The task set *may still* be schedulable, but we must perform the exact test (Response Time Analysis) to confirm.

#### (b) Perform Response Time Analysis for all three tasks and determine schedulability. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — RMS Priority Assignment:**
By period (shorter = higher): $\tau_1$ (T=4) > $\tau_2$ (T=5) > $\tau_3$ (T=10).

**Step 2 — Response Time of $\tau_1$ (no interference):**

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 1 \leq D_1 = 4 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Response Time of $\tau_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= 2 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Converged at $R_2 = 3 \leq D_2 = 5$ $\checkmark$.

**Step 4 — Response Time of $\tau_3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 2 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 + 2 = 5 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 2 = 6 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 4 = 8 \\
R_3^{(4)} &= 2 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 4 = 8
\end{aligned}
$$

Converged at $R_3 = 8 \leq D_3 = 10$ $\checkmark$.

**Final Verdict:** Task set is **schedulable under RMS**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — EDF and Processor Demand Analysis

Consider the same three tasks from Question A, but the system uses **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** scheduling instead of RMS.

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 5 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 2 | 10 | 10 |

#### (a) State the exact schedulability condition for EDF. Compute the total utilization and apply the simple necessary condition. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Exact EDF Condition (Horn's Theorem):**

A task set scheduled by EDF is schedulable if and only if for all $L \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L^*\}$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{L}{T_i} \right\rfloor C_i \leq L
$$

where $L^* = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n)$ is the least common multiple of all task periods.

**Step 2 — Compute Utilization:**

$$
U = 0.25 + 0.40 + 0.20 = 0.85
$$

**Step 3 — Necessary Condition for EDF:**

$$
U \leq 1.0
$$

Since $0.85 \leq 1.0$ $\checkmark$, the necessary condition holds. The exact test must now be applied.

#### (b) Apply the exact Processor Demand test to determine schedulability. (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute $L^*$:**

$$
L^* = \text{lcm}(4, 5, 10) = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Check Processor Demand for selected $L$ values:**

For $L = 4$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{3} \left\lfloor \frac{4}{T_i} \right\rfloor C_i = \left\lfloor \frac{4}{4} \right\rfloor \cdot 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{4}{5} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 + \left\lfloor \frac{4}{10} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 = 1 + 0 + 0 = 1 \leq 4 \quad \checkmark
$$

For $L = 5$:

$$
\left\lfloor \frac{5}{4} \right\rfloor \cdot 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{5}{5} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 + \left\lfloor \frac{5}{10} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 = 1 + 2 + 0 = 3 \leq 5 \quad \checkmark
$$

For $L = 8$:

$$
\left\lfloor \frac{8}{4} \right\rfloor \cdot 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{8}{5} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 + \left\lfloor \frac{8}{10} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 = 2 + 2 + 0 = 4 \leq 8 \quad \checkmark
$$

For $L = 10$:

$$
\left\lfloor \frac{10}{4} \right\rfloor \cdot 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{10}{5} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 + \left\lfloor \frac{10}{10} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 = 2 + 4 + 2 = 8 \leq 10 \quad \checkmark
$$

For $L = 20$:

$$
\left\lfloor \frac{20}{4} \right\rfloor \cdot 1 + \left\lfloor \frac{20}{5} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 + \left\lfloor \frac{20}{10} \right\rfloor \cdot 2 = 5 + 8 + 4 = 17 \leq 20 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Final Verdict:** The processor demand condition is satisfied for all checked $L$ values. **The task set is EDF-schedulable.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Floor vs. Ceiling Confusion:** In the RTA equation, students *must* use **ceiling** $\lceil \cdot \rceil$, not floor. The number of *full* jobs of a higher-priority task in an interval of length $R_i$ is $\lceil R_i / T_j \rceil$.
> 2. **Initial Value of $R_i$:** Always start with $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$, never with $R_i^{(0)} = 0$ or $R_i^{(0)} = T_i$. Using $0$ would never increment; using $T_i$ would already include unnecessary interference.
> 3. **Convergence Vigilance:** Many students stop iterating after 2-3 steps. If $R_i$ keeps growing, **keep iterating** — sometimes convergence happens at step 5, 6, or 7.
> 4. **Mark Loss on $L^*$ for EDF:** Students often forget that the EDF processor demand check must be done at *every* multiple of periods up to $L^* = \text{lcm}$. Doing it at only $L = T_i$ loses 2-3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Timing Analysis (KTU PERAT757 Module 3):**

- **Task Tuple:** Every periodic task is $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i, \Phi_i)$.
- **WCET ($C_i$):** Always the *worst case*, never the average.
- **Critical Instant:** Worst-case response occurs at $t=0$ with all tasks released together.
- **Utilization:** $U = \sum C_i / T_i$. Necessary condition for any scheduler: $U \leq 1$.
- **Liu & Layland Bound (RMS sufficient):** $U_{LL}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. Memorize: $n=1 \to 1.0$, $n=2 \to 0.828$, $n=3 \to 0.780$, $n \to \infty \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **Hyperbolic Bound (RMS, tighter):** $\prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 + C_i/T_i) \leq 2$.
- **RTA Iteration Formula (Deadline Monotonic / RMS):** $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil C_j$.
- **TDA Equation:** $w_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil t / T_j \rceil C_j \leq t$ for some $t \leq D_i$.
- **Convergence Rule:** Stop when $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (converged) or $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (failed immediately).
- **EDF Exact Test:** $\forall L \in [1, L^*]$: $\sum \lfloor L / T_i \rfloor C_i \leq L$, where $L^* = \text{lcm}(T_1, \ldots, T_n)$.
- **Pipeline Order for ESE:** (1) Identify scheduler → (2) Compute $U$ → (3) Apply sufficient test → (4) If inconclusive, apply RTA/TDA → (5) Compare $R_i$ vs $D_i$ for all tasks.
- **Pipe Symbol in Markdown Tables:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value; never raw `|` in tables.
- **Common Mistake:** Stopping RTA iteration prematurely. Always iterate until true convergence.
- **Always use $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ (ceiling) in RTA, $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ (floor) in EDF Processor Demand.**
- **Implicit vs. Constrained Deadlines:** RMS assumes $D_i = T_i$ (implicit). For $D_i < T_i$, use Deadline Monotonic Analysis with priority by $D_i$.
- **Why RTA beats Liu & Layland:** RTA is necessary *and* sufficient. Liu & Layland is only sufficient.
- **Significance of $L^*$:** Beyond $L^*$, the pattern repeats due to periodicity — checking beyond it is redundant.
- **Earliest Deadline First (EDF) is optimal** among all scheduling algorithms on a single processor: if any scheduler can meet all deadlines, EDF can.
- **Rate Monotonic (RM) is optimal** among all *fixed-priority* schedulers: no other fixed-priority assignment beats RM.
- **Trade-off:** EDF has higher runtime overhead (dynamic priority) but full utilization; RMS is cheaper to implement but caps utilization at $\ln 2$ in the limit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability and Fault Tolerance: Redundancy
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability and Fault Tolerance: Redundancy

# Reliability and Fault Tolerance: Redundancy

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability (R(t))** is the conditional probability that a system performs its intended function correctly over a specified interval of time $[0, t]$, given that it was operating correctly at time $t = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fault Tolerance** is the ability of a system (often a Real-Time Operating System or embedded system) to continue delivering correct, timely service even in the presence of hardware or software faults. A fault-tolerant system **masks**, **detects**, and **recovers** from faults without disrupting critical operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Redundancy** is the deliberate provision of additional (duplicate) resources — beyond what is strictly required for normal operation — so that the system can tolerate component failures. Redundancy is the **engineering mechanism** through which fault tolerance is achieved.

The relationship can be expressed as:

$$
\text{Fault Tolerance} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Achieved via} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{Redundancy}
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a pilot flying a commercial passenger aircraft (e.g., a Boeing 747 or Airbus A380) on a long-haul international flight.

- **Single Engine Analogy (No Redundancy):** A small Cessna aircraft has only **one engine**. If that engine fails mid-flight, the aircraft crashes. This system has **low reliability** and **zero fault tolerance**.

- **Twin-Engine Analogy (Passive Redundancy):** A commercial airliner typically has **two, three, or four engines**. It can safely continue flying and even land even if **one engine completely fails**. The extra engines are *redundant* — they are not strictly required for *normal* flight, but they guarantee operation during *abnormal* (fault) conditions.

- **Triple Modular Redundancy (Active Redundancy):** In critical flight-control computers, **three identical processors** run the same computations in parallel, and a **voter circuit** selects the majority result. Even if one processor malfunctions and outputs garbage, the other two "outvote" the faulty one, and the aircraft continues operating correctly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** In real-time embedded systems (RTOS, automotive ECUs, pacemakers, railway signaling), a single fault can be catastrophic. Redundancy is therefore not a luxury — it is a **design necessity** mandated by safety standards such as **IEC 61508 (SIL levels)**, **ISO 26262 (ASIL levels)**, and **DO-178C (avionics)**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations

The following metrics are universally expected in any KTU question on this topic and must be memorized:

- **MTTF** — Mean Time To Failure (for non-repairable systems)
- **MTTR** — Mean Time To Repair (for repairable systems)
- **MTBF** — Mean Time Between Failures (where $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$)
- **Availability $A$** — Fraction of time the system is operational
- **Failure Rate $\lambda$** — Failures per unit time (assumed constant in exponential reliability model)
- **Reliability $R(t)$** — Probability of survival up to time $t$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Fault Tolerance

Fault tolerance is engineered through three orthogonal layers. KTU questions frequently test the student's ability to *classify* and *contrast* these layers.

#### Pillar 1: Fault Detection
Before a fault can be tolerated, it must be *detected*. Common detection mechanisms:
- **Watchdog Timers** — Hardware counter that resets the CPU if not periodically serviced by healthy software.
- **Parity Codes / Checksums** — Detect single-bit or burst errors in memory/data transmission.
- **Built-In Self-Test (BIST)** — Periodic diagnostic routines run at idle time.
- **Sanity Checks / Assertions** — Software-level invariant verification.

#### Pillar 2: Fault Isolation
Once detected, the fault's blast radius must be contained.
- **Memory Protection Units (MPU)** — Prevent one task from corrupting another's memory.
- **Spatial Isolation** — Separate power domains, separate processors, separate communication buses.
- **Temporal Isolation** — Time-triggered scheduling ensures faults in one task do not delay critical tasks.

#### Pillar 3: Fault Recovery
The system is restored to an acceptable state.
- **Rollback / Checkpointing** — Revert to the last known good state.
- **Reconfiguration** — Switch to a redundant component.
- **Voting** — Use majority logic to mask the faulty output (used in TMR).
- **Graceful Degradation** — Shed non-critical functionality to preserve essential services.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Redundancy

Redundancy is classified along **four** primary axes, all of which are high-yield KTU topics.

| Type | Description | Real-Time Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Hardware Redundancy** | Physical duplication of components | Dual-redundant flight control CPUs |
| **Software Redundancy** | Multiple diverse software versions for the same function (N-version programming) | 3 independently written compilers for voting |
| **Information Redundancy** | Extra bits added to data for error detection/correction | Hamming codes, CRC, Reed-Solomon in CAN bus |
| **Time Redundancy** | Re-execution of the same task within its deadline | Re-trying a sensor read if the first fails parity check |

### 2.3 Hardware Redundancy Sub-Forms (The Most-Tested Sub-Topic)

This is the **single most important** classification for KTU 2024 questions.

#### (a) Passive Redundancy (Masking Redundancy)
The faulty component's effect is **hidden** from the system output. Voting occurs continuously.
- **TMR (Triple Modular Redundancy)** — 3 identical modules, 1 majority voter.
- **NMR (N-Modular Redundancy)** — $N$ odd, $N \geq 3$ modules.
- The system produces correct output as long as **strict majority** modules are healthy.

#### (b) Active Redundancy (Standby Sparing)
A **primary** module operates; a **standby (backup)** module is idle and is switched in *only upon* failure detection.
- **Cold Standby** — Backup is unpowered; activation delay is highest.
- **Hot Standby** — Backup runs in lockstep with primary; near-zero switchover delay.
- **Warm Standby** — Backup is partially initialized; intermediate delay.

#### (c) Hybrid Redundancy
Combines passive and active forms. Example: TMR with one spare module that is switched in to replace a detected faulty module — preserves TMR configuration after a single failure.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes **every** high-yield formula you will need for a KTU 2024 question on this topic. These are mathematically verified against the standard reliability theory textbook (Birolini, *Reliability Engineering*, Springer).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below assume a **constant failure rate $\lambda$** (exponential distribution), which is the standard KTU assumption. Units: $\lambda$ is in **failures/hour**, $R(t)$ is **dimensionless**, $A$ is **dimensionless**, MTBF/MTTF/MTTR are in **hours**.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Exponential Reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Most fundamental equation |
| 2 | Unreliability / CDF | $F(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failure by time $t$ |
| 3 | Failure Rate – Reliability Link | $\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = \text{const}$ | $f(t) = \frac{dF(t)}{dt}$ is the PDF |
| 4 | Mean Time To Failure | $\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | Non-repairable systems |
| 5 | Mean Time Between Failures | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Repairable systems |
| 6 | Steady-State Availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Long-run fraction uptime |
| 7 | Series System Reliability | $R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | **All** must work |
| 8 | Parallel System Reliability | $R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - R_i)$ | **At least one** must work |
| 9 | Identical Parallel Subsystem | $R_{\text{parallel},n} = 1 - (1 - R)^n$ | $n$ identical components |
| 10 | TMR Reliability | $R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$ | $3R^2$ for exactly-one failure |
| 11 | NMR Reliability (k-out-of-n) | $R_{k/n} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | At least $k$ of $n$ must work |
| 12 | M-out-of-N special: 2-out-of-3 | $R_{2/3} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$ | Same as TMR |
| 13 | M-out-of-N special: 2-out-of-4 | $R_{2/4} = R^4 + 4R^3(1-R) + 6R^2(1-R)^2$ | 6 ways exactly 2 survive |
| 14 | Hazard / Failure Rate | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}}$ | Constant-rate assumption |
| 15 | Mission Time for Target R | $t = -\frac{\ln(R_{\text{target}})}{\lambda}$ | Time to reach reliability $R$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse the $k$-out-of-$n$ formula with the *general* parallel formula. TMR is **2-out-of-3** (need 2 good to vote), NOT **1-out-of-3** (which would be simple parallel). The $k$ value matters.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Redundancy Technique | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace (Boeing 787) | Quadruplex flight control computers with majority voting | Zero-tolerance for erroneous control surface commands |
| Automotive (Brake-by-wire) | Dual ECUs with watchdog mutual monitoring | ISO 26262 ASIL-D requires single-point failure coverage |
| Railway Signaling (CBTC) | Hot standby ATP + ATS subsystems | SIL-4 certification per IEC 61508 |
| Spacecraft (Mars Rover) | Cold standby redundant computers + AFT (Autonomous Fault Tree) | No possibility of human repair |
| Medical (Implantable Pacemaker) | Dual processors with cross-checked outputs | Patient safety; battery replacement requires surgery |
| Datacenter (Google, AWS) | N+1 or 2N power and cooling + multi-AZ replication | 99.999% ("five nines") availability SLA |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Series System Reliability

A **series** system functions correctly if and only if **every** component functions correctly. The components fail independently.

**Step 1 — State the independence assumption.** The survival of component $i$ up to time $t$ is the event $S_i$. For an RTOS subsystem (CPU, memory, sensor, bus), we assume:

$$
P(S_1 \cap S_2 \cap \dots \cap S_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(S_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the definition of reliability.** Since $R_i(t) = P(S_i)$, we obtain:

$$
R_{\text{series}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Specialize to identical components and exponential model.** If all $n$ components have the same constant failure rate $\lambda$, then $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ for all $i$. Substituting:

$$
R_{\text{series}}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda t} = \left(e^{-\lambda t}\right)^{n} = e^{-n \lambda t}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret.** The equivalent failure rate of the series system is the **sum** of individual failure rates:

$$
\lambda_{\text{series}} = n \lambda
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Adding more components in series *decreases* overall system reliability. This is a counter-intuitive but crucial result — the more parts you chain, the more ways the system can fail. This is why complex RTOS pipelines must be carefully partitioned.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Parallel System Reliability

A **parallel** system functions correctly as long as **at least one** component functions correctly. The system fails only when **all** components have failed.

**Step 1 — Compute the unreliability of a single component.** By definition:

$$
P(\text{component } i \text{ fails}) = 1 - R_i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the probability that ALL components fail.** Using independence:

$$
P(\text{all fail}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the probability that the system DOES NOT fail (i.e., at least one works).** Use the complement rule:

$$
R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - P(\text{all fail}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Specialize to $n$ identical components with reliability $R$ each.**

$$
R_{\text{parallel},n} = 1 - (1 - R)^{n}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the exponential model** $R = e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$
R_{\text{parallel},n}(t) = 1 - \left(1 - e^{-\lambda t}\right)^{n}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the binomial expansion** to obtain an explicit polynomial:

$$
R_{\text{parallel},n}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \binom{n}{k} (-1)^{k+1} e^{-k \lambda t} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (-1)^{k+1} \binom{n}{k} e^{-k\lambda t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Adding components in parallel *increases* reliability. Doubling a parallel subsystem (going from $n=1$ to $n=2$) gives the largest *relative* improvement; subsequent additions yield diminishing returns. This is the mathematical basis for choosing $n=2$ (DPR — Dual Modular Redundancy) or $n=3$ (TMR) in practice.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) with Voter

TMR is the most-tested parallel-redundancy case in KTU. The system has **3 identical modules** feeding a **majority voter**. The voter outputs the value held by at least **2 of 3** modules.

**Step 1 — Enumerate the four mutually exclusive outcomes for the three modules.** Let each module be either **Good (G)** with probability $R$ or **Bad (B)** with probability $1-R$.

| Outcome | Count | Probability | Voter Output | System Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| G G G | 0 failures | $R^3$ | Correct | **Success** |
| G G B | 1 failure | $3R^2(1-R)$ | Correct (2 of 3 good) | **Success** |
| G B B | 2 failures | $3R(1-R)^2$ | **Faulty** (1 of 3 good) | **Failure** |
| B B B | 3 failures | $(1-R)^3$ | **Faulty** | **Failure** |

The combinatorial coefficient $3$ in row 2 comes from $\binom{3}{1} = 3$ (choosing which *one* of the 3 modules fails). The coefficient $3$ in row 3 comes from $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ (choosing which *two* of the 3 modules fail).

**Step 2 — Sum the success probabilities.** The voter is correct whenever at least 2 of 3 modules are good:

$$
R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify algebraically.**

$$
R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2 - 3R^3 = 3R^2 - 2R^3
$$

**Step 4 — Factor.**

$$
R_{\text{TMR}} = R^2(3 - 2R)
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check.** Plug in $R = 0.9$:

$$
R_{\text{TMR}} = 3(0.9)^2 - 2(0.9)^3 = 3(0.81) - 2(0.729) = 2.43 - 1.458 = 0.972
$$

A single module has $R = 0.900$. TMR boosts this to $R_{\text{TMR}} = 0.972$. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Observation:** TMR improves reliability *only when* $R > 0.5$. For low individual reliability, TMR can be **worse** than a single module! Verify: at $R = 0.4$, $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3(0.16) - 2(0.064) = 0.48 - 0.128 = 0.352 < 0.4$. TMR is **not always** an improvement.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — 2-out-of-4 Redundancy (Series-Parallel Hybrid)

A common KTU problem involves a system where **at least 2 of 4** processors must work. This is a generalization of TMR.

**Step 1 — Apply the binomial sum formula.** The probability of exactly $k$ successes out of $n$ is $\binom{n}{k}R^k(1-R)^{n-k}$. The system needs $k \geq 2$:

$$
R_{2/4} = \sum_{k=2}^{4} \binom{4}{k} R^k (1-R)^{4-k}
$$

**Step 2 — Expand term by term.**

$$
R_{2/4} = \binom{4}{2} R^2 (1-R)^2 + \binom{4}{3} R^3 (1-R) + \binom{4}{4} R^4
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the binomial coefficients** $\binom{4}{2}=6$, $\binom{4}{3}=4$, $\binom{4}{4}=1$:

$$
R_{2/4} = 6R^2(1-R)^2 + 4R^3(1-R) + R^4
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** For $R = 0.95$:

- $6(0.95)^2(0.05)^2 = 6 \cdot 0.9025 \cdot 0.0025 = 0.01354$
- $4(0.95)^3(0.05) = 4 \cdot 0.8574 \cdot 0.05 = 0.17148$
- $(0.95)^4 = 0.81451$
- $R_{2/4} = 0.01354 + 0.17148 + 0.81451 = 0.99953$

Remarkable — using four moderately reliable modules ($R=0.95$ each) and tolerating up to 2 failures gives $R \approx 0.9995$, a **"three-nines"** system.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Series-Parallel Combined Subsystem (Analytical Matrix)

The KTU 2024 scheme places high weight on **mixed** reliability block diagrams. Consider a system with two **parallel** sub-blocks A and B connected in **series**, where A contains 3 components in parallel and B contains 2 components in series.

**Step 1 — Compute the reliability of sub-block A** (3 components in parallel, identical $R_a$):

$$
R_A = 1 - (1 - R_a)^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the reliability of sub-block B** (2 components in series, identical $R_b$):

$$
R_B = R_b^2
$$

**Step 3 — Combine A and B in series** (the full system requires both blocks working):

$$
R_{\text{system}} = R_A \cdot R_B = \left[1 - (1 - R_a)^3\right] \cdot R_b^2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute exponential model** $R_a = e^{-\lambda_a t}$, $R_b = e^{-\lambda_b t}$:

$$
R_{\text{system}}(t) = \left[1 - \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_a t}\right)^3\right] \cdot e^{-2\lambda_b t}
$$

**Step 5 — General series-parallel evaluation strategy** (memorize this for KTU):

$$
\boxed{\; \text{Work from the } \textbf{innermost} \text{ block outward, applying series rule at each series junction and parallel rule at each parallel junction.} \;}
$$

### 3.6 Analytical Reliability Comparison Matrix

The following comparison table is a **high-yield KTU 14-mark question template**. Memorize it.

| Configuration | Reliability Expression | $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$ (approx) | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single Module | $R$ | $\lambda$ | Non-critical consumer devices |
| 2-out-of-2 (Series) | $R^2$ | $2\lambda$ | Unwanted in critical systems |
| 2 Parallel (1-out-of-2) | $1-(1-R)^2 = 2R - R^2$ | $\approx \lambda/2$ | Non-critical redundancy |
| 2-out-of-3 (TMR) | $3R^2 - 2R^3$ | $\approx \lambda/3$ | Flight control, railway |
| 2-out-of-4 | $R^4 + 4R^3(1-R) + 6R^2(1-R)^2$ | $\approx \lambda/4$ | Spacecraft control |
| Cold Standby (2 modules) | $2R - R^2$ | Same as parallel | Slower switchover tolerance |
| Hot Standby (2 modules) | $2R - R^2$ | Same as parallel | Zero switchover tolerance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TMR (Triple Modular Redundancy) with Voter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Signal] --> mod1[Module 1 CPU]
    inputA --> mod2[Module 2 CPU]
    inputA --> mod3[Module 3 CPU]
    mod1 --> vot[Majority Voter]
    mod2 --> vot
    mod3 --> vot
    vot --> outA[Corrected Output]
    
    style mod1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style mod2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style mod3 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style vot fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style outA fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

**Reading the diagram:** All three CPU modules receive the *same* input and execute the *same* control algorithm. Their outputs feed a majority voter. As long as at most one module is faulty, the voter outputs the correct majority result. If two or more modules are faulty, the voter itself produces a wrong result — this is the **fundamental limit of TMR**.

### 4.2 Active Redundancy with Hot Standby

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inp1[Input] --> primA[Primary Module]
    inp1 --> sbA[Standby Module Lockstep]
    primA --> detA[Health Monitor]
    sbA --> detA
    detA --> swA{Module OK?}
    swA -- Yes --> selA[Output Selector selects Primary]
    swA -- No --> selB[Output Selector switches to Standby]
    selA --> outp1[System Output]
    selB --> outp1
    
    style primA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style sbA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style detA fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style outp1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
```

**Reading the diagram:** The primary and standby modules execute the same task synchronously (lockstep). A health monitor compares outputs and flags a fault. If a discrepancy is detected beyond an acceptable window, the output selector switches to the standby. The **switchover latency** is the time between fault occurrence and standby engagement — this is a critical design parameter in real-time systems with hard deadlines.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Fault-Tolerant RTOS Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Detection
        wdA[Watchdog Timer]
        parA[Parity Checker ECC Memory]
        sanA[Software Sanity Assertions]
    end
    
    subgraph Isolation
        mpuA[Memory Protection Unit MPU]
        ttsA[Time Triggered Scheduler]
        spA[Separate Power Domain]
    end
    
    subgraph Recovery
        vtrA[Majority Voter TMR]
        rebA[Rollback to Checkpoint]
        recA[Reconfigure to Spare]
        gdeA[Graceful Degradation]
    end
    
    appA[Application Tasks] --> Detection
    Detection --> Isolation
    Isolation --> Recovery
    Recovery --> appA
    
    style Detection fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style Isolation fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404
    style Recovery fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style appA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical **3-stage pipeline** of fault tolerance inside an RTOS. Every task is wrapped by these three stages. Detection raises an exception; isolation prevents fault propagation; recovery restores the system to a correct state. The arrows form a closed loop because recovery re-enables the application to continue execution.

### 4.4 Series-Parallel Reliability Block Diagram (Worked-Example Template)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Source] --> sA[Sub Block A 3 parallel]
    sA --> sB[Sub Block B 2 series]
    sB --> snkA[Sink]
    
    sA --> p1A[Comp A1]
    sA --> p2A[Comp A2]
    sA --> p3A[Comp A3]
    
    sB --> q1A[Comp B1]
    sB --> q2A[Comp B2]
    
    style sA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style sB fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
```

**Reading the diagram:** A reliability block diagram (RBD) is *not* a circuit diagram. Every path from `Source` to `Sink` represents a *minimal* combination of working components that keeps the system alive. Compute $R_{\text{system}} = R_A \cdot R_B = [1-(1-R_a)^3] \cdot R_b^2$ using the derivation from §3.5.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Model Question Format Note:** KTU 2024 Part A carries 3 marks each, expects a *definition* plus *one worked numeric or one supporting statement*. No internal choice in Part A.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**

Define the following terms with respect to fault-tolerant real-time systems: (i) Reliability, (ii) MTTF, and (iii) Fault Masking. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Reliability $R(t)$** is the conditional probability that a system performs its intended function over the interval $[0, t]$, given it was operational at $t=0$. Mathematically, $R(t) = P(\text{system survives up to time } t \mid \text{operational at } t=0)$.

(ii) **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)** is the expected time elapsed before a non-repairable system fails. For a constant failure rate $\lambda$, $\text{MTTF} = \frac{1}{\lambda}$.

(iii) **Fault Masking** is a property of a redundant system where the effect of a faulty component is *hidden* from the system output without explicit detection or reconfiguration. Example: A 2-out-of-3 majority voter masks the output of one faulty processor.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**

Differentiate between **Active Redundancy** and **Passive Redundancy**. Give one real-time example of each. **[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Active Redundancy | Passive Redundancy |
|---|---|---|
| Fault handling | Detects fault, then switches to spare | Fault is hidden by voting; no switch needed |
| Detection required? | **Yes** — explicit fault detection | **No** — voter masks automatically |
| Standby state | Spare is idle (or running in standby) | All modules run continuously |
| Switchover delay | **Non-zero** — must detect + switch | **Zero** — output is always the majority |
| Failure mode | If switchover fails, system fails | System fails only if > N/2 modules fail |
| Example | Hot-standby secondary flight computer | Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) with majority voter |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Internal Choice Rule:** Each 14-mark question in the End Semester Examination has an internal choice — students answer *either* the main question **OR** the alternative. The total marks are split as **Part (a) — 7 marks** + **Part (b) — 7 marks**.

---

#### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model QP]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** technique. Derive an expression for the reliability of a TMR system and discuss its limitations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition and Block Diagram.** Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) is a passive (masking) hardware redundancy technique in which three identical modules process the same input in parallel, and a majority voter selects the output held by at least two of the three modules. (Block diagram: see §4.1 above — 3 modules feeding a voter.)

**Step 2 — Reliability Derivation.** Let $R$ be the reliability of each identical module, and assume module failures are independent. Enumerate the four mutually exclusive outcomes:

$$
R_{\text{TMR}} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R) + 0 \cdot 3R(1-R)^2 + 0 \cdot (1-R)^3
$$
