Consider a two-state Markov chain: **UP** and **DOWN**. Transitions:
- UP → DOWN at failure rate $\lambda$ (per hour)
- DOWN → UP at repair rate $\mu$ (per hour)

**Step 1: Solve the steady-state balance equation.**

Let $\pi_U$ = steady-state probability of being UP = $A$, $\pi_D$ = steady-state probability of being DOWN = $1-A$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_U \cdot \lambda &= \pi_D \cdot \mu \quad \text{(flow balance)} \\
A \cdot \lambda &= (1-A) \cdot \mu \\
A\lambda + A\mu &= \mu \\
A(\lambda + \mu) &= \mu \\
A &= \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual explanation:**
- Line 1: In steady state, the rate of leaving UP equals the rate of leaving DOWN (otherwise probability would accumulate).
- Line 2: Substitute the values.
- Line 3: Expand the right-hand side.
- Line 4: Collect $A$ terms.
- Line 5: Solve for $A$.

**Step 2: Convert to MTTF/MTTR form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\mu}{\lambda + \mu} = \frac{1/\text{MTTR}}{1/\text{MTTF} + 1/\text{MTTR}} \\
&= \frac{1/\text{MTTR}}{(\text{MTTR} + \text{MTTF})/(\text{MTTF} \cdot \text{MTTR})} \\
&= \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} \\
&= \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the classic KTU formula. **For high availability, you need either large MTTF (reliable components) or small MTTR (fast recovery).**

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Recovery Time Bound for Checkpointing

A task executes for time $T_{\text{exec}}$ with checkpoints every $T_{\text{cp}}$ seconds. A fault at uniform random time requires rolling back to the **last** checkpoint. Expected re-execution time = $T_{\text{cp}} / 2$.

**Step 1: Expected total execution time with checkpointing.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[T_{\text{total}}] &= T_{\text{exec}} + P_{\text{fault per cp}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{cp}}}{2} \cdot N_{\text{cp}} \\
&= T_{\text{exec}} + \lambda T_{\text{cp}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{cp}}}{2} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{exec}}}{T_{\text{cp}}} \\
&= T_{\text{exec}} \left(1 + \frac{\lambda T_{\text{cp}}}{2}\right) + C_{\text{cp}} \cdot \frac{T_{\text{exec}}}{T_{\text{cp}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C_{\text{cp}}$ is the per-checkpoint cost (memory write, context save).

**Step 2: Minimize the overhead with respect to $T_{\text{cp}}$.**

Set $\dfrac{dE[T_{\text{total}}]}{dT_{\text{cp}}} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d}{dT_{\text{cp}}}\left[\frac{\lambda T_{\text{cp}}}{2} + \frac{C_{\text{cp}}}{T_{\text{cp}}}\right] &= 0 \\
\frac{\lambda}{2} - \frac{C_{\text{cp}}}{T_{\text{cp}}^{2}} &= 0 \\
T_{\text{cp}}^{\text{opt}} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 C_{\text{cp}}}{\lambda}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Young's formula** — a famous result often appearing in KTU elective papers.

### 3.4 Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A controller uses TMR with each module having $R = 0.95$ per mission. The voter has $R_v = 0.99$. Compute (a) ideal TMR reliability, (b) practical TMR reliability, (c) compare to simplex.

**Solution (a):**
$$
R_{\text{TMR, ideal}} = 3(0.95)^{2} - 2(0.95)^{3} = 3(0.9025) - 2(0.857375) = 2.7075 - 1.71475 = 0.99275
$$

**Solution (b):**
$$
R_{\text{TMR, practical}} = 0.99 \times 0.99275 = 0.98282
$$

**Solution (c):**
Simplex $R = 0.95$. TMR provides a $0.99275 - 0.95 = 0.04275$ gain in the ideal case, but only $0.98282 - 0.95 = 0.03282$ when voter failure is included. **The voter is a single point of failure.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input[Input Signal] --> m1[Module A]
    input --> m2[Module B]
    input --> m3[Module C]
    m1 --> v[Majority Voter]
    m2 --> v
    m3 --> v
    v --> out[Corrected Output]
    v -.-> ed[Error Detector]
    ed -.-> alarm[Fault Flag]

    classDef primary fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef redundant fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef voter fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class input primary
    class m1,m2 redundant
    class v,ed voter
    class out,alarm output
```

**Reading the diagram:** All three modules receive the *same* input in lockstep. Their outputs feed the voter, which selects the value that appears at least twice. A discrepancy triggers an error detector that flags the faulty module for replacement.

### 4.2 Hot Standby Redundancy with Watchdog

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p[Primary Module] --> s1{Health Check via WDT}
    s1 -- healthy --> out1[Active Output]
    s1 -- unhealthy --> sw[Failover Switch]
    b[Backup Module] --> sw
    sw --> out1
    wdt[Hardware Watchdog Timer] --> s1
    wdt -.timeout.-> sw

    classDef active fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef backup fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000

    class p,out1 active
    class b backup
    class s1,sw,wdt control
```

**Reading the diagram:** The primary runs normally. A hardware watchdog continuously verifies the primary's "heartbeat." If the WDT expires (primary hangs), the failover switchover routes the backup module's output to the active line.

### 4.3 Checkpoint-Based Rollback Recovery Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([System Boot]) --> chk0[Take Initial Checkpoint C0]
    chk0 --> exec1[Execute Task Segment 1]
    exec1 --> chk1[Save Checkpoint C1]
    chk1 --> exec2[Execute Task Segment 2]
    exec2 --> chk2[Save Checkpoint C2]
    chk2 --> exec3[Execute Task Segment 3]
    exec3 --> fault{Fault Detected?}
    fault -- No --> done([Task Complete])
    fault -- Yes --> sel[Select Last Good Checkpoint]
    sel --> restore[Restore State from Ck]
    restore --> reexec[Re-execute from Ck+1]
    reexec --> exec3

    classDef safe fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef fault fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef data fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000

    class start,done,exec1,exec2,exec3,chk0,chk1,chk2,restore,reexec safe
    class fault,sel fault
    class chk0,chk1,chk2 data
```

**Reading the diagram:** The system runs in segments, saving state at boundaries. On fault detection, the most recent valid checkpoint is selected, state is restored, and execution resumes from the next segment.

### 4.4 N-Version Programming Software Redundancy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    spec[Common Specification] --> t1[Team 1 Implementation]
    spec --> t2[Team 2 Implementation]
    spec --> t3[Team 3 Implementation]
    t1 --> vote[Decision Voter]
    t2 --> vote
    t3 --> vote
    vote --> out[Agreed Output]
    vote -. disagreement .-> log[Discrepancy Log]

    classDef spec fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef team fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef votefill fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef outbox fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class spec spec
    class t1,t2,t3 team
    class vote votefill
    class out,log outbox
```

**Reading the diagram:** Multiple *independent* teams produce different implementations from the same specification, defeating common-mode software bugs. A decision voter selects the consensus output and logs disagreements for offline analysis.

### 4.5 Layered Fault Tolerance Stack (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer - Domain Specific Checks] --> L2[Middleware Layer - Health Monitors, IPC Guards]
    L2 --> L3[RTOS Kernel Layer - Task Supervision, Memory Protection]
    L3 --> L4[Hardware Abstraction Layer - ECC, Parity, MPU]
    L4 --> L5[Physical Layer - Sensors, Actuators, Power]

    L2 -.reports to.-> LOG[Central Fault Log]
    L3 -.schedules.-> REC[Recovery Task]
    L4 -.asserts.-> WDT[Watchdog Timer]

    classDef layer fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef sidecar fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000

    class L1,L2,L3,L4,L5 layer
    class LOG,REC,WDT sidecar
```

**Reading the diagram:** Fault detection and recovery is *layered* — hardware detects physical faults, the kernel detects timing and memory faults, middleware detects communication faults, and the application detects semantic faults. Each layer feeds upward into a central fault log and triggers the recovery task.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a fault, an error, and a failure in a real-time system. Give one example of each from an automotive domain.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Term | Definition (1 m) | Automotive Example (0.5 m) |
|---|---|---|
| **Fault** | The *root cause* of an incorrect system state, often dormant. | A cracked solder joint on the ECU's CAN bus transceiver. |
| **Error** | The *manifestation* of a fault — an incorrect value in the system state. | The received throttle position byte becomes `0xFF` instead of `0x7F`. |
| **Failure** | The *external deviation* from specified service that the user observes. | The Electronic Stability Control (ESC) applies incorrect brake force, causing the vehicle to skid. |

*Marking tip (1 m):* Award 1 mark for the correct one-line definition of each, 0.5 m per example, and 0.5 m for the explanation that they are causally linked (fault → error → failure).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is a watchdog timer? Explain how a windowed watchdog is more powerful than a basic watchdog.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **watchdog timer (WDT)** is a hardware countdown timer that the application software must periodically reset ("pet") to prove it is alive. If the software hangs, it stops petting the WDT, the counter reaches zero, and a system reset is triggered (1 mark).

**Basic WDT (1 mark):** Only checks the *upper bound* — pet must arrive before timeout. Detects "too slow" execution / hang.

**Windowed WDT (1 mark):** Pet must arrive within a defined time window $[T_{\min}, T_{\max}]$. Detects both *too slow* (timeout) AND *too fast* execution (pet arrived too early — indicates an out-of-bounds loop or erroneous early restart). This catches a wider class of software bugs in safety-critical code, which is why ASIL-D systems prefer windowed WDTs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** scheme in detail. Derive the expression for the reliability of a TMR system with a perfect voter, and show with a numerical example how TMR improves reliability over a simplex system.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Discuss the **four-stage fault handling pipeline** (Detection → Isolation → Masking → Recovery). For each stage, give one concrete hardware or software mechanism used in RTOS and explain its operation.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks:**

**TMR Concept (2 marks):**
- Three identical hardware modules receive the same input simultaneously.
- A majority voter (2-out-of-3 logic) selects the output that appears at least twice.
- TMR can tolerate any **single** module failure (masking the fault).
- Standard TMR has reliability:
  $$R_{\text{TMR}} = 3R^{2} - 2R^{3}$$
  where $R$ is the per-module reliability.

**Derivation (3 marks):**
System succeeds if at least 2 of 3 modules work (binomial distribution):

$$
P(\text{success}) = \binom{3}{2}R^{2}(1-R) + \binom{3}{3}R^{3} = 3R^{2} - 2R^{3}
$$

**Numerical example (2 marks):** Let $R = 0.9$ per module.
- Simplex: $R_{\text{simplex}} = 0.900$
- TMR (ideal voter):
  $$R_{\text{TMR}} = 3(0.9)^{2} - 2(0.9)^{3} = 3(0.81) - 2(0.729) = 2.43 - 1.458 = 0.972$$
- Improvement: $\Delta R = 0.072$ (7.2 percentage points).

**TMR is effective only when $R > 0.5$**; below that threshold, the voter becomes the dominant failure source.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks:**

| Stage (1.5 m each) | Mechanism | Operation (0.25 m) |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Detection** | Hardware Watchdog Timer (WDT) | External counter; application must reset it periodically. If reset is missed (task hangs), WDT expires and forces a system reset. |
| **2. Isolation** | Memory Protection Unit (MPU) | Configures per-task memory regions; any out-of-bound access triggers a memory fault exception, isolating the rogue task. |
| **3. Masking** | ECC (Error Correcting Code) Memory | Memory controller appends check bits; single-bit errors are automatically corrected in hardware, masking transient bit-flips from the application. |
| **4. Recovery** | Checkpoint-Restart in RTOS | Periodic state snapshots in non-volatile memory; on failure, the supervisor task reloads the last checkpoint and resumes execution. |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **N-Version Programming (NVP)** and **Recovery Blocks (RB)** as software fault-tolerance techniques. Use a labeled diagram for each and discuss the role of the acceptance test in RB.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A safety-critical RTOS measures MTTF = 5000 hours and MTTR = 2 hours. Compute (i) the failure rate, (ii) MTBF, (iii) steady-state availability, and (iv) the probability of survival (reliability) at $t = 1000$ hours. Comment on whether this system is suitable for a 24×7 industrial control application.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks:**

**N-Version Programming (3.5 marks):**
- *Concept:* N independent teams implement the *same* specification in N different ways.
- *Execution:* All N versions run concurrently on separate inputs.
- *Voting:* A **decision voter** compares all N outputs and selects the consensus (typically majority).
- *Goal:* Defeat common-mode software faults (assumption: independent teams will make different mistakes).
- *Diagram label:* `Spec → [V1, V2, V3] → Voter → Output`

**Recovery Blocks (3.5 marks):**
- *Concept:* A *primary* algorithm executes first, then an **acceptance test (AT)** validates the result.
- *On AT failure:* An alternate algorithm is tried; if it also fails the AT, the next alternate is tried, and so on.
- *Acceptance Test:* A *cheap* but *rigorous* post-condition check (e.g., range check, checksum, reasonableness bound). It is the heart of RB — too loose and faults slip through; too tight and good results are rejected.
- *Diagram label:* `Primary → AT → [Pass: Output | Fail: Alt1 → AT → ... → AltN → AT]`

**Comparison (roll-up to 7):** NVP is *forward* (all run, vote) — high resource cost, single decision. RB is *sequential* (try then verify) — lower resource cost, depends heavily on AT quality.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** MTTF = 5000 h, MTTR = 2 h.

**(i) Failure rate (1.5 m):**
$$
\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{5000} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ failures/hour}
$$

**(ii) MTBF (1.5 m):**
$$
\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR} = 5000 + 2 = 5002 \text{ hours}
$$

**(iii) Steady-state availability (2 m):**
$$
A = \frac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}} = \frac{5000}{5002} \approx 0.9996 = 99.96\%
$$

**(iv) Reliability at $t = 1000$ h (2 m):**
$$
R(1000) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(2 \times 10^{-4})(1000)} = e^{-0.2} \approx 0.8187
$$

**Comment (collect remaining marks):**
- Availability of 99.96% corresponds to roughly **210 minutes of downtime per year**, which is **below** the "five-nines" (99.999%, ~5 min/yr) target often required for industrial control.
- Survival probability of ~0.82 over 1000 hours means ~18% chance of a failure over ~42 days of continuous operation — significant for a safety-critical system.
- **Verdict:** The system is *acceptable* for non-safety supervisory control but should be **augmented with TMR or dual-redundancy** for safety-critical loops (e.g., emergency shut-down).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Fault Tolerance Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing fault vs. error vs. failure (1–2 marks lost).** Write the exact chain: *fault → error → failure* on the answer sheet, even if the question only asks for one term.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the voter's own reliability in TMR (2–3 marks lost).** If a question gives $R_v < 1$, the correct formula is $R_{\text{TMR, practical}} = R_v \cdot (3R^{2} - 2R^{3})$, NOT just $3R^{2} - 2R^{3}$. The voter is a **single point of failure** — examiners test this.
>
> 3. **Writing TMR formula as $3R^2$ or $R^3$ alone.** The full expansion $3R^2 - 2R^3$ must be derived or stated explicitly.
>
> 4. **Mixing MTTF with MTBF.** MTTF applies to **non-repairable** components (e.g., a sensor that is replaced on failure). MTBF applies to **repairable** systems. Many students write MTTF when MTBF is asked and lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Omitting units in numerical answers.** Always write "hours," "failures/hour," etc.
>
> 6. **In checkpointing questions, ignoring the per-checkpoint cost $C_{\text{cp}}$.** The Young's formula optimum includes both the rollback penalty and the checkpoint save cost. If you only minimize the rollback side, you are incomplete.
>
> 7. **In drawing diagrams, using "end" as a node name in Mermaid/text diagrams** — Mermaid treats it as a reserved keyword. Use "END_NODE" or "finish" instead.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fault → Error → Failure** is the *causal chain* — memorize this trio in order.
- **Redundancy types:** Hardware, Information (ECC, parity, CRC), Time, Software (NVP, RB). KTU expects you to name all four.
- **TMR reliability formula:** $R_{\text{TMR}} = 3R^{2} - 2R^{3}$ (perfect voter). TMR helps only when $R > 0.5$.
- **Series vs Parallel reliability:** Series → multiply $R_i$. Parallel (no voter) → $1 - \prod(1 - R_i)$. TMR is a *voted* parallel system.
- **Availability formula:** $A = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}} = \dfrac{\text{MTTF}}{\text{MTBF}}$.
- **Five-nines availability** ($99.999\%$) ≈ 5.26 minutes downtime/year. Required for telecom-grade systems.
- **Watchdog timer:** Simplest fault detector; *windowed* WDT detects both slow and fast faults.
- **Recovery paradigms:** Rollback (backward, deterministic, simpler) vs Roll-forward (forward, faster, fault-specific).
- **Young's formula** for optimal checkpoint interval: $T_{\text{cp}}^{\text{opt}} = \sqrt{2C_{\text{cp}}/\lambda}$.
- **Total recovery time bound:** $T_{\text{recovery}} = T_{\text{detect}} + T_{\text{isolate}} + T_{\text{switch}} + T_{\text{re-exec}} \le \text{deadline slack}$.
- **N-Version Programming** uses *parallel* execution + *voting*; **Recovery Blocks** uses *sequential* execution + *acceptance test*.
- **The voter is a single point of failure** — always mention voter reliability in TMR derivations.
- **Common-mode failures** defeat redundancy — use **diversity** (different vendors, different algorithms) to mitigate.
- **Hard RTOS preference:** Rollback recovery (bounded) over roll-forward (unpredictable).
- **Memory Protection Unit (MPU):** Primary RTOS mechanism for fault *isolation*.
- **ECC memory:** Primary hardware mechanism for fault *masking* of bit-flips.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Memory Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Memory Management** in a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) refers to the systematic, deterministic, and bounded control of primary (main) memory allocation, deallocation, address translation, and protection among multiple concurrent tasks/processes, while strictly guaranteeing that the **worst-case memory access time** is predictable and falls within the system's hard deadline constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PERAT757, Module 4):**
> Memory Management in RTOS involves techniques for static and dynamic allocation, partitioning schemes, memory protection, and addressing mechanisms specifically optimized to minimize latency, eliminate fragmentation, and ensure deterministic access time — a stark contrast to General-Purpose Operating Systems (GPOS) where fairness and throughput dominate over timing guarantees.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Hospital ICU Analogy

Imagine a busy hospital **Intensive Care Unit (ICU)** where every patient (real-time task) needs a critical-care bed (memory block) and life-saving equipment (memory-mapped I/O).

- A **GPOS** is like a *general hospital ward* — beds are reused, wait-listed patients queue up, and the admission desk may take unpredictable time to find a free bed. Acceptable for non-critical patients.
- An **RTOS Memory Manager** is like a *pre-designated ICU scheduler* — each critical patient has a **guaranteed bed reserved in advance** (static allocation), instruments are pre-wired to that bed (memory-mapped I/O), and the time to reach the bed is bounded by a hard contract (WCET — Worst Case Execution Time).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RTOS Memory Management is Special:**
> In hard real-time systems (missile guidance, anti-lock brakes, pacemakers), a memory access that takes **20 microseconds today and 200 microseconds tomorrow** is unacceptable. The memory manager must guarantee *constant or bounded latency* — the most fundamental difference from desktop OS memory management.

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU Mandatory Definitions)

| Term | Definition | KTU Exam Importance |
|------|------------|---------------------|
| **Determinism** | The property that the time taken for any memory operation is bounded and predictable. | ★★★★★ |
| **Fragmentation** | Wasted memory that cannot serve a new allocation request despite free space existing. | ★★★★★ |
| **Internal Fragmentation** | Wasted space *inside* an allocated block (allocated > requested). | ★★★★ |
| **External Fragmentation** | Wasted space *between* allocated blocks (free memory is non-contiguous). | ★★★★★ |
| **Partition** | A contiguous block of memory reserved for a task or pool. | ★★★★ |
| **Memory Protection** | Hardware-enforced isolation preventing one task from corrupting another's memory. | ★★★★★ |
| **TLB (Translation Lookaside Buffer)** | A specialized, fully-associative cache that stores recent virtual-to-physical address translations. | ★★★★ |
| **Paging** | Fixed-size memory mapping using **pages** (logical) and **frames** (physical). | ★★★★★ |
| **Segmentation** | Variable-size memory mapping based on logical program units (code, data, stack). | ★★★★ |
| **Buddy System** | A binary-tree based allocation algorithm that splits/merges blocks in powers of two. | ★★★★★ |

### 1.4 RTOS Memory Management vs GPOS Memory Management

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Table — Frequently Asked 3-Mark Question in KTU:**

| Parameter | RTOS Memory Manager | GPOS Memory Manager |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Determinism, bounded latency | Maximum throughput, fairness |
| **Allocation Strategy** | Mostly static / block-based pre-allocation | Dynamic, demand-paged, swap-based |
| **Fragmentation Handling** | Avoided by design (fixed partitions, pools) | Tolerated (compaction, paging) |
| **Memory Protection** | Mandatory in safety-critical RTOS | Standard via MMU |
| **Overhead** | Minimal, predictable | Variable, complex |
| **Typical Algorithms** | Fixed partition, Buddy, Memory Pools | Slab, Page replacement (LRU, Clock) |
| **Examples** | VxWorks, μC/OS-II, FreeRTOS, QNX | Linux, Windows, macOS |

### 1.5 Memory Architecture Hierarchy in Embedded RTOS

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        CPU Registers (fastest)          │  ← ~1 ns
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   L1 Cache (SRAM, ~32-128 KB)           │  ← ~2-5 ns
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   L2/L3 Cache (SRAM, ~256KB-8MB)        │  ← ~10-20 ns
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Main Memory (DRAM, MB-GB)             │  ← ~50-100 ns
├─────────────────────────────────────────┤
│   Secondary Storage (Flash/SSD/HDD)     │  ← μs-ms (Avoided in RTOS!)
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU:** In a hard real-time system, the memory hierarchy access times must be **statically analyzable**. Swapping to disk is *almost always forbidden* in hard RTOS because disk access time is unbounded.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Memory Allocation Strategies — Detailed Classification

Memory allocation in an RTOS is broadly classified into **two paradigms**:

#### A. Static Memory Allocation

Memory is reserved for variables, buffers, and task stacks **at compile time**. The linker assigns fixed addresses; no allocation happens at runtime.

**Advantages:**
- Zero allocation overhead (O(1) — no searching).
- Bounded, deterministic memory footprint.
- No fragmentation possible.

**Disadvantages:**
- Wastes memory if worst-case usage is overestimated.
- Cannot adapt to dynamic workload changes.

#### B. Dynamic Memory Allocation

Memory is allocated at runtime from a heap. Common schemes used in RTOS are:

1. **Fixed-Size Partition (Memory Pool / Block Buffer)**
2. **Variable-Size Partition (First-Fit, Best-Fit, Worst-Fit)**
3. **Buddy System (Binary / Fibonacci)**
4. **Slab Allocation** (used in VxWorks)

### 2.2 Fixed-Size Partition Scheme (Memory Pools)

The heap is divided into **N equal blocks of size B** at system initialization. A free-list keeps track of available blocks. Allocation = remove first block from free-list (O(1)). Deallocation = return block to free-list (O(1)).

- **Internal Fragmentation** = B − requested_size (up to B−1 bytes per block).
- **No external fragmentation** (all blocks identical size).
- **Highly deterministic** — ideal for RTOS.

> [!NOTE]
> **μC/OS-II uses fixed-size memory partitions** called "Memory Partitions" created via `OSMemCreate()`. Each partition contains fixed-size blocks.

### 2.3 Variable-Size Partition Schemes

#### (i) First-Fit
Scan free-list from beginning; allocate the **first block large enough**.
- **Pros:** Fast, low search overhead.
- **Cons:** Suffers from external fragmentation; small "splinters" accumulate at the head of the free-list.

#### (ii) Best-Fit
Scan entire free-list; allocate the **smallest block that fits**.
- **Pros:** Produces smallest leftover fragment, good large-block preservation.
- **Cons:** Slower (must search entire list); creates many tiny unusable fragments.

#### (iii) Worst-Fit
Scan entire free-list; allocate the **largest available block**.
- **Pros:** Leaves large leftover blocks reusable.
- **Cons:** Slowest; degrades large-block pool quickly. **Rarely used in RTOS.**

### 2.4 Buddy System (Binary Buddy Allocator)

The buddy system manages memory in **blocks whose sizes are powers of two** (1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ... KB). When a request arrives:
- The smallest 2^k block ≥ requested size is found.
- If a larger block is available, it is **split recursively** into two "buddies".
- When a block is freed, it checks if its **buddy is also free**; if so, they are **coalesced (merged)**.

**Buddy Address Formula (Critical for KTU):**

Given a block of size 2^k at address A, its **buddy address** is:

$$
B(A) = A \oplus 2^{k}
$$

where ⊕ is bitwise XOR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This XOR property ensures that the buddy's address differs from A by exactly one bit — the bit corresponding to the block size. This is what makes the buddy system an **O(log N)** algorithm (compared to O(N) for variable partitions) and a **favorite KTU exam topic**.

### 2.5 Buddy System Worked Example (Step-by-Step)

Suppose we have a **64 KB** initial free block and the following requests arrive in order:

| Step | Request | Action | Resulting Blocks |
|------|---------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | 18 KB | Round up to 32 KB. Split 64→32+32. Allocate one 32. | 32(used), 32(free) |
| 2 | 15 KB | Round up to 16 KB. Split 32→16+16. Allocate one 16. | 32(used), 16(used), 16(free) |
| 3 | 16 KB | Exact fit. Allocate 16. | 32(used), 16(used), 16(used) |
| 4 | Free the 16 KB from Step 2 | Coalesce with buddy → 32 KB free. | 32(used), 32(free) |
| 5 | Free the 32 KB from Step 1 | Coalesce with buddy → 64 KB free. | 64(free) |

### 2.6 Memory Protection & MPU/MMU

- **MMU (Memory Management Unit):** Full hardware support for virtual memory, paging, protection. Used in systems like QNX, VxWorks (MMU mode), embedded Linux.
- **MPU (Memory Protection Unit):** Lightweight hardware that defines a small number (typically 8–16) of memory regions with permissions (Read/Write/Execute). Used in Cortex-M series microcontrollers (e.g., ARMv7-M).
- **No MMU/MPU systems:** e.g., FreeRTOS in default mode — relies on programmer discipline. Memory corruption is a constant risk.

### 2.7 Paging (Most Tested RTOS Memory Concept)

Logical memory is divided into fixed-size **pages**; physical memory into same-size **frames**. A **page table** maps each page to a frame.

**Effective Access Time (EAT) — Critical KTU Formula:**

$$
\text{EAT} = (1 - p) \cdot t_m + p \cdot (t_m + t_{TLB})
$$

where:
- $p$ = TLB hit rate (probability TLB has the translation)
- $1 - p$ = TLB miss rate
- $t_m$ = Main memory access time
- $t_{TLB}$ = TLB lookup time (added only on TLB miss because page table must be accessed)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If the page table itself is two-level (typical for large address spaces), the miss penalty includes **two memory accesses**, and the formula extends accordingly. **Always read the question carefully in KTU numericals.**

### 2.8 Segmentation

Memory is divided into variable-size **segments** based on logical program units (Code, Data, Stack, Heap). A **segment table** stores base address and limit for each segment.

**Logical Address = (Segment Number, Offset)**

Physical Address = Segment Base + Offset (after limit check).

| Feature | Segmentation | Paging |
|---------|--------------|--------|
| **Block size** | Variable (logical) | Fixed |
| **Programmer visibility** | Yes | No |
| **External fragmentation** | Yes | No |
| **Internal fragmentation** | No | Yes (last page) |
| **Used in** | x86 (legacy), some RTOS hybrid | Most modern OS including RTOS |

### 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

| # | Concept | Formula / Key Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Buddy address | $B(A) = A \oplus 2^{k}$ | Block size = $2^k$ |
| 2 | Internal fragmentation (fixed partition) | $W = B - R$ (where B=block, R=request) | Bytes |
| 3 | Memory utilization | $U = \dfrac{\text{Useful bytes}}{\text{Total allocated}}$ | Fraction (0–1) |
| 4 | EAT (single-level paging) | $(1-p)t_m + p(t_m + t_{TLB}) = t_m + p \cdot t_{TLB}$ | Seconds |
| 5 | EAT (two-level paging on TLB miss) | $t_m + 2t_{TLB} + 2t_m \cdot (1-p)$ variations | Seconds |
| 6 | Page table size | $\text{PTE} \times 2^{\text{VA bits} - \text{page offset bits}}$ | Bytes |
| 7 | Number of pages | $2^{\text{virtual address bits} - \text{offset bits}}$ | Count |
| 8 | Number of frames | $2^{\text{physical address bits} - \text{offset bits}}$ | Count |
| 9 | Effective access time (associative TLB) | $t_m \cdot (1 + p \cdot t_{TLB}/t_m)$ simplification | — |
| 10 | WCET (memory) | Sum of worst-case access times across all instructions | ns/μs |

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR RTOS):** Memory partitions are statically configured; an MPU enforces isolation between Safety Integrity Levels (ASIL-A to ASIL-D).
- **Avionics (ARINC 653, VxWorks 653):** Each avionics partition gets a fixed time slot AND a fixed memory region — temporal AND spatial partitioning.
- **Medical Implants (FreeRTOS in pacemaker):** No MMU, no swap; all memory is statically allocated to avoid any pointer-related non-determinism.
- **Mars Rovers (VxWorks):** Used buddy system variants with extensive static analysis. Memory errors were strictly forbidden due to 20-minute round-trip to Earth.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Effective Access Time (EAT) with TLB

Let us derive the **Effective Access Time (EAT)** formula from first principles — a frequent 7-mark question in KTU Module 4.

**Given:**
- TLB lookup time = $t_{TLB}$
- Main memory access time = $t_m$
- TLB hit ratio = $p$ (probability that translation is in TLB)
- TLB miss ratio = $(1 - p)$

**Step 1: Identify the two cases for memory access.**

Every memory access either hits the TLB (case A) or misses it (case B).

**Case A: TLB Hit (probability p)**
The CPU first consults the TLB, finds the physical frame number, and then performs **one** main memory access to fetch the data.

$$
T_{\text{hit}} = t_{TLB} + t_m
$$

**Case B: TLB Miss (probability 1 − p)**
The CPU consults the TLB, fails to find the entry, must then access the **page table in main memory** (one extra memory access), and finally accesses the **actual data** in main memory (second memory access). TLB is updated on the side.

$$
T_{\text{miss}} = t_{TLB} + t_m + t_m = t_{TLB} + 2t_m
$$

**Step 3: Compute weighted average (EAT).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= p \cdot T_{\text{hit}} + (1-p) \cdot T_{\text{miss}} \\
&= p(t_{TLB} + t_m) + (1-p)(t_{TLB} + 2t_m) \\
&= p \cdot t_{TLB} + p \cdot t_m + t_{TLB} + 2t_m - p \cdot t_{TLB} - 2p \cdot t_m \\
&= t_{TLB} + p \cdot t_m + 2t_m - 2p \cdot t_m \\
&= t_{TLB} + t_m (p + 2 - 2p) \\
&= t_{TLB} + t_m (2 - p)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final KTU Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\text{EAT} = t_{TLB} + (2 - p) \cdot t_m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:**
> - If $p = 1$ (TLB always hits) → EAT = $t_{TLB} + t_m$ (one TLB + one memory access). ✓
> - If $p = 0$ (TLB never hits) → EAT = $t_{TLB} + 2t_m$ (one TLB + two memory accesses). ✓
> - For $p = 0.5$ → EAT = $t_{TLB} + 1.5 t_m$. ✓

---

### 3.2 Numerical Problem 1: EAT Calculation (Classic 7-Mark KTU Style)

> **Question:** A paging system uses a TLB with 80% hit ratio. TLB lookup time is 20 ns, and main memory access time is 100 ns. Calculate the effective memory access time. What hit ratio is needed to keep EAT below 150 ns?

**Step 1: Plug values into EAT formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= t_{TLB} + (2 - p) \cdot t_m \\
&= 20 + (2 - 0.8) \times 100 \\
&= 20 + (1.2) \times 100 \\
&= 20 + 120 \\
&= 140 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for hit ratio p such that EAT ≤ 150 ns.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 + (2 - p)(100) &\leq 150 \\
(2 - p)(100) &\leq 130 \\
2 - p &\leq 1.3 \\
p &\geq 0.7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** EAT = **140 ns**. A hit ratio of at least **70%** is needed to keep EAT ≤ 150 ns.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Problem 2: Buddy System Address Calculation

> **Question:** In a Buddy system with initial 1 MB (20-bit address) free block, calculate the buddy address of a 64 KB block starting at address 0x0000 and a 16 KB block starting at 0x40000.

**Solution:**

Block size = 64 KB = $2^{16}$ bytes.
Buddy address = $A \oplus 2^{16} = 0\text{x}00000 \oplus 0\text{x}10000 = 0\text{x}10000$.

So the buddy is at **0x10000**, exactly 64 KB ahead. ✓

Block size = 16 KB = $2^{14}$ bytes.
Buddy address = $0\text{x}40000 \oplus 0\text{x}4000 = 0\text{x}44000$.

So the buddy is at **0x44000**. ✓

---

### 3.4 Numerical Problem 3: Page Table Size and Number of Pages

> **Question:** A system has 32-bit virtual address, 28-bit physical address, and a page size of 4 KB. Each page table entry (PTE) is 4 bytes. Calculate (a) number of pages, (b) number of frames, (c) page table size.

**Solution:**

Page offset bits = $\log_2(4\text{ KB}) = \log_2(2^{12}) = 12$ bits.

**Step (a) — Number of pages (virtual):**
Virtual address bits = 32 → page number bits = $32 - 12 = 20$.
$$
\text{Number of pages} = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 = 1 \text{ M pages}
$$

**Step (b) — Number of frames (physical):**
Physical address bits = 28 → frame number bits = $28 - 12 = 16$.
$$
\text{Number of frames} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ K frames}
$$

**Step (c) — Page table size:**
$$
\text{Page table size} = \text{Number of pages} \times \text{PTE size} = 2^{20} \times 4 \text{ B} = 4 \text{ MB}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Allocation Scheme Selection in RTOS

| Scenario | Recommended Scheme | Justification |
|----------|--------------------|--------------|
| Hard real-time, safety-critical (e.g., pacemaker) | Static allocation / Fixed memory pool | Zero allocation jitter |
| Soft real-time, mixed workload (e.g., router) | Buddy system | Good balance, low fragmentation, log-time ops |
| Multimedia streaming | Slab allocator (object caches) | Frequent fixed-size object creation |
| Deeply embedded (8/16-bit MCU, no MMU) | No heap, all static | Memory safety, predictability |
| Multi-process RTOS with protection | Paging + MMU | Hardware isolation, virtual address spaces |
| Legacy x86 real-time systems | Segmentation | Native hardware support |

---

### 3.6 Memory Layout of an RTOS Task (Typical Stack Diagram)

```
Higher Address (e.g., 0x7FFFFFF0)
┌────────────────────────────┐
│  Command-line args (argc)  │  ← Top of stack
├────────────────────────────┤
│  Environment variables     │
├────────────────────────────┤
│  Stack (grows ↓)           │
│       ...                  │
│       ...                  │
├────────────────────────────┤
│  ↓ Free Space ↓            │
├────────────────────────────┤
│  Heap (grows ↑)            │
├────────────────────────────┤
│  BSS (uninitialized data)  │
├────────────────────────────┤
│  Data (initialized)        │
├────────────────────────────┤
│  Text / Code (Read-Only)   │
└────────────────────────────┘
Lower Address (e.g., 0x00000000)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "draw the memory layout", include arrows showing **stack growing down** and **heap growing up** — this is a frequently missed detail worth 1 mark.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: RTOS Memory Management Module Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Application Tasks T1 T2 T3] --> B[RTOS Memory Manager API]
    B --> C[Static Allocator]
    B --> D[Dynamic Allocator]
    D --> D1[Fixed Partition Pool]
    D --> D2[Variable Partition First-Fit Best-Fit]
    D --> D3[Binary Buddy System]
    D --> D4[Slab Allocator]
    B --> E[Memory Protection Layer]
    E --> E1[MMU Virtual Memory]
    E --> E2[MPU Region Protection]
    E --> E3[No Protection Programmer Discipline]
    C --> F[Physical Memory DRAM SRAM]
    D1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    D3 --> F
    D4 --> F
    E1 --> F
    E2 --> F
    F --> G[Address Translation TLB]
    G --> H[CPU Core]
    H --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Paging System with TLB (Address Translation Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[CPU Issues Virtual Address VA] --> B{TLB Lookup}
    B -->|Hit p percent| C[Get Physical Frame from TLB]
    B -->|Miss 1-p percent| D[Access Page Table in Main Memory]
    D --> E[Update TLB with new mapping]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Form Physical Address PA]
    F --> G[Access Main Memory at PA]
    G --> H[Return Data to CPU]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Buddy System Splitting and Coalescing

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[64KB Free Block] -->|Request 18KB Need 32KB| B[Split 64KB into 32KB and 32KB]
    B --> C[Allocate 32KB Block to Task]
    B --> D[Remaining 32KB Free]
    D -->|Request 15KB Need 16KB| E[Split 32KB into 16KB and 16KB]
    E --> F[Allocate 16KB to Task]
    E --> G[Remaining 16KB Free]
    G -->|Request 16KB| H[Allocate 16KB exactly]
    F -->|Task frees memory| I[Return 16KB to free list]
    I -->|Check buddy also free| J[Coalesce 16KB and 16KB into 32KB]
    C -->|Task frees memory| K[Return 32KB to free list]
    K -->|Check buddy also free| L[Coalesce 32KB and 32KB into 64KB]
    J --> K
    L --> M[System back to 64KB Free]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Memory Hierarchy and Access Path

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CPU] --> B[L1 Cache SRAM]
    B -->|Miss| C[L2 Cache SRAM]
    C -->|Miss| D[L3 Cache SRAM if present]
    D -->|Miss| E[Main Memory DRAM]
    E -->|Page Fault| F[Secondary Storage Flash SSD - Forbidden in Hard RTOS]
    E --> G[TLB Virtual to Physical Translation]
    G --> E
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Segmentation vs Paging Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SEG[Segmentation Model]
        S1[Logical Address: Seg Num + Offset] --> S2[Segment Table Lookup]
        S2 --> S3[Base + Limit Check]
        S3 --> S4[Physical Address]
    end
    subgraph PAG[Paging Model]
        P1[Logical Address: Page Num + Offset] --> P2[TLB or Page Table Lookup]
        P2 --> P3[Frame Number + Offset]
        P3 --> P4[Physical Address]
    end
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Explain the three main differences between memory management in an RTOS and a General-Purpose Operating System (GPOS).** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

1. **Determinism vs Throughput:** RTOS memory management prioritizes **bounded, predictable access time** (determinism), while a GPOS prioritizes **maximum throughput and fair sharing**, even if individual allocation times vary.

2. **Allocation Strategy:** RTOS typically uses **static allocation or fixed-size memory pools** at compile time, ensuring zero runtime allocation overhead. GPOS uses **dynamic, demand-paged allocation with swapping** to maximize memory utilization, which is **forbidden in hard RTOS** due to unbounded disk access.

3. **Memory Protection:** RTOS uses **lightweight MPU or no protection** to keep overhead minimal but enforce isolation between safety-critical tasks. GPOS relies on full **MMU-based virtual memory** with rich protection but higher overhead.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "RTOS uses less memory" or "RTOS is faster" — these are vague and not specifically about *memory management*. Always frame the answer in terms of *allocation strategy*, *timing predictability*, and *protection mechanism*.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define fragmentation. Differentiate between internal and external fragmentation with an example.** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Fragmentation** is the phenomenon where memory becomes unusable for allocation requests despite free space existing in the system.

- **Internal Fragmentation:** Occurs when the allocated block is **larger than what the process actually requested**, leaving wasted space *inside* the block. *Example:* In a fixed-size partition scheme with block size 16 KB, if a task requests 12 KB, the remaining 4 KB is wasted inside the block (internal fragmentation = 4 KB).

- **External Fragmentation:** Occurs when free memory is divided into **small, non-contiguous chunks** scattered between allocated blocks, none of which is large enough to satisfy a new request. *Example:* In a variable-size partition scheme, after several allocations and deallocations, you may have free blocks of 5 KB, 8 KB, and 3 KB, but a new request of 20 KB cannot be satisfied even though the total free memory is 16 KB.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Do not confuse the two. A common student error is to say "internal fragmentation happens between blocks" — it is **inside** allocated blocks; **external** is **between** allocated blocks.

---

### 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (Choose ONE: 14 Marks)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)** [Full KTU Model]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the Buddy System memory allocation algorithm in detail. How is the buddy of a block computed? Show with a suitable example how splitting and coalescing work.** (7 Marks)

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A paging system uses a TLB with hit ratio 75%. TLB access time is 30 ns, and main memory access time is 120 ns. Calculate the effective memory access time. If we need EAT ≤ 200 ns, what minimum hit ratio is required?** (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):** The Buddy System is a memory allocation algorithm that manages memory in blocks whose sizes are always **powers of two** (1 KB, 2 KB, 4 KB, ..., 1 MB). It maintains a free-list for each block size.

**2. Buddy Computation (2 Marks):**

Given a block of size $2^k$ starting at address A, its **buddy address** is:

$$
B = A \oplus 2^k
$$

This works because blocks of size $2^k$ are aligned on $2^k$ boundaries, so XORing the bit corresponding to the size flips it to give the buddy.

**3. Allocation Procedure (2 Marks):**
- Round up the request R to the next power of two, say $2^k$.
- Search the free-list for size $2^k$. If a free block exists, allocate it.
- If not, look for a larger block ($2^{k+1}$, $2^{k+2}$, ...) and **split** it recursively into two buddies until the required size is obtained.
- Place the unused buddy on the appropriate free-list.

**4. Deallocation and Coalescing (2 Marks):**
- Return the block to its size's free-list.
- Check if the **buddy is also free**. If yes, merge them into a block of size $2^{k+1}$ and recursively check the merged block's buddy.

**Example (Embedded within):** With 64 KB initial, request 18 KB → rounds to 32 KB → split 64 KB into two 32 KB buddies → allocate one 32 KB. Subsequent free operations coalesce buddies back to 64 KB.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Step 1: Apply EAT formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EAT} &= t_{TLB} + (2 - p) \cdot t_m \\
&= 30 + (2 - 0.75) \times 120 \\
&= 30 + (1.25) \times 120 \\
&= 30 + 150 \\
&= 180 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for hit ratio p such that EAT ≤ 200 ns.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
30 + (2 - p)(120) &\leq 200 \\
(2 - p)(120) &\leq 170 \\
2 - p &\leq 1.4167 \\
p &\geq 0.5833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** EAT = **180 ns** at 75% hit ratio. Minimum required hit ratio ≈ **58.34%** to maintain EAT ≤ 200 ns.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — Equally Weighted]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain paging with a neat diagram. Derive the effective access time formula when a TLB is used.** (7 Marks)

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**A system has 32-bit virtual addresses, 24-bit physical addresses, and page size of 8 KB. Each PTE is 4 bytes. Calculate: (i) number of pages, (ii) number of frames, (iii) page table size, (iv) internal fragmentation if a process uses 1,234,567 bytes of memory.** (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Paging Explanation (3 Marks):** Paging is a memory management scheme that divides logical memory into fixed-size **pages** and physical memory into same-size **frames**. A **page table** maintained by the OS (and cached in TLB) maps each page to a frame. The logical address is split into **(page number, offset)**, and the physical address is **(frame number, offset)**.

**Diagram (2 Marks):** See Section 4.2 Mermaid Paging diagram for equivalent representation. The address translation flow is: VA → TLB → (on miss) Page Table → Frame Number → PA → Main Memory.

**EAT Derivation (2 Marks):** As derived in Section 3.1:

$$
\text{EAT} = t_{TLB} + (2 - p) \cdot t_m
$$

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Page offset bits** = $\log_2(8 \text{ KB}) = \log_2(2^{13}) = 13$ bits.

**(i) Number of pages:**
$$
\text{Pages} = 2^{(32 - 13)} = 2^{19} = 524{,}288 \text{ pages}
$$

**(ii) Number of frames:**
$$
\text{Frames} = 2^{(24 - 13)} = 2^{11} = 2048 \text{ frames}
$$

**(iii) Page table size:**
$$
\text{Size} = 2^{19} \times 4 \text{ B} = 2^{21} \text{ B} = 2 \text{ MB}
$$

**(iv) Internal fragmentation:**
Number of pages actually used = $\lceil 1{,}234{,}567 / 8192 \rceil = \lceil 150.7 \rceil = 151$ pages.
Allocated memory = $151 \times 8192 = 1{,}236{,}992$ bytes.
Internal fragmentation = $1{,}236{,}992 - 1{,}234{,}567 = 2{,}425$ bytes (≈ 2.37 KB).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 
> 1. **Common Mistake — Page Table Size Unit Confusion:** Students often compute the number of pages correctly but forget to **multiply by PTE size**. Always end with explicit units (bytes/KB/MB).
> 2. **Common Mistake — Internal Fragmentation Computation:** Many students simply compute `1,234,567 mod 8192`. While numerically correct for the *current* last page, the *correct* approach is: total allocated − total requested. Both yield the same number, but the framing matters.
> 3. **Common Mistake — EAT Formula Sign:** A surprisingly common error is writing EAT = $t_m + p \cdot t_{TLB}$ without the $(2-p)$ factor. Always include **two memory accesses on a TLB miss** (one for page table, one for data).
> 4. **Common Mistake — Buddy Address XOR:** Some students use A + $2^k$ instead of A ⊕ $2^k$. While these give the *same result* when addresses are aligned on $2^k$ boundaries, the **formal definition is XOR**, and it is what KTU expects.
> 5. **Diagram Hygiene:** In memory layout diagrams, *forgetting arrows on the stack/heap* is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. Always show direction of growth.

---

### 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **RTOS memory management is defined by determinism**, not throughput. The WCET of every memory operation must be bounded.
- ✅ **Static allocation > Dynamic allocation** in hard real-time systems. If dynamic is necessary, use **fixed-size pools** for O(1) deterministic allocation.
- ✅ **Buddy system formula:** $B(A) = A \oplus 2^k$. Memory is always partitioned in **powers of two**.
- ✅ **Paging EAT formula (single-level with TLB):** $\text{EAT} = t_{TLB} + (2 - p) \cdot t_m$. Memorize the derivation steps, not just the result.
- ✅ **Page table size** = Number of pages × PTE size = $2^{(\text{VA bits} - \text{offset bits})} \times \text{PTE size}$.
- ✅ **Internal fragmentation** is inside allocated blocks; **External fragmentation** is between allocated blocks.
- ✅ **MMU** provides full virtual memory; **MPU** is a lightweight region-based protection for small embedded cores. Some RTOS (FreeRTOS in default mode) use **neither** and rely on programmer discipline.
- ✅ **No swapping to disk in hard RTOS** — disk latency is unbounded and violates the hard deadline.
- ✅ **TLB hit** = 1 memory access; **TLB miss** = 2 memory accesses (one for page table, one for data).
- ✅ **Segmentation causes external fragmentation** (variable block sizes); **Paging causes internal fragmentation** (last page partially used).
- ✅ **Common RTOS memory API patterns:** μC/OS-II uses `OSMemCreate/Get/Put` (block pools); FreeRTOS uses `pvPortMalloc/vPortFree` (heap_1 to heap_5 schemes).
- ✅ **Memory protection in safety-critical RTOS (AUTOSAR, ARINC 653)** is mandatory — it is enforced by hardware (MPU/MMU), not by software convention.
- ✅ **WCET analysis** of memory access paths must consider: cache miss, TLB miss, page fault — all of which are ideally bounded in a properly designed hard RTOS.

> **End of Module 4 — Memory Management Notes (PERAT757, KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** I/O Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **I/O Management in Real-Time Systems** refers to the coordinated set of hardware mechanisms, software abstractions, device drivers, buffering schemes, and scheduling policies that govern the transfer of data between the CPU/memory subsystem and the external peripheral devices (sensors, actuators, disks, network interfaces, etc.) while **preserving deterministic timing guarantees** required by hard and soft real-time applications.

In the context of a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS), I/O Management is **not merely data transport** — it is a time-bounded contract. The system must guarantee that an I/O request issued by a task will complete within a **bounded Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** and a **bounded Worst-Case I/O Latency (WCIOL)**.

The standard metrics used to evaluate I/O management quality in KTU-style problems are:

| Metric | Symbol | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Throughput | $T_p$ | Requests/second |
| Latency | $L$ | milliseconds (ms) |
| Jitter | $J$ | $\mu$s |
| Mean Time Between Failures | MTBF | hours |
| Device Utilization | $U_d$ | percentage (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Jitter ($J$)** is the deviation in latency between successive I/O operations. In hard real-time systems (e.g., anti-lock braking, flight control), **$J$ must be provably bounded** — this is the central differentiator between RTOS I/O and general-purpose OS I/O.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Waiter-Chef-Dishwasher Model"**

Imagine a restaurant kitchen:

- **The CPU** is the **head chef** — can only cook one dish at a time, must work fast.
- **I/O devices** (disk, sensor, network) are the **refrigerator, oven, dishwasher** — slow, mechanical, asynchronous.
- **The Device Driver** is the **waiter** — takes the chef's order, goes to the device, waits for it to be ready, brings the result back.
- **The I/O Scheduler** is the **kitchen manager** — decides *which* order to serve first when many come in.
- **The Buffer** is the **pass-through window** — temporarily holds dishes so the chef isn't blocked.
- **The Interrupt** is the **buzzer** — the dishwasher rings when done, telling the chef "your plates are ready!"

**Without the manager (scheduler)**, the chef wastes time. **Without the buzzer (interrupt)**, the chef must keep peeking (**polling**). **Without the pass-through window (buffer)**, the chef stops every few seconds. A **real-time kitchen** must guarantee that the soup is plated in **exactly 4 minutes** — not "around 4 minutes."

---

## 1.3 Classification of I/O Devices

I/O devices are classified along three orthogonal axes:

1. **Data Transfer Mode**
   - **Character-stream devices** — one byte at a time (keyboard, serial port, mouse).
   - **Block devices** — fixed-size blocks (disk, SSD, flash).

2. **Access Pattern**
   - **Sequential access** (tape, sensor stream).
   - **Random access** (disk, RAM-disk).

3. **Timing Criticality (RTOS-specific)**
   - **Hard real-time I/O** — missing deadline = system failure (missile seeker).
   - **Soft real-time I/O** — missing deadline degrades quality (video stream).
   - **Non-real-time I/O** — no timing guarantee (log file write).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three I/O Handling Paradigms

### Paradigm A — Programmed I/O (Polled I/O)

The CPU actively loops, repeatedly checking a **status register** until the device signals readiness. The CPU is **100% busy** during I/O — no overlap with computation.

**Operational Steps:**
1. CPU writes command to device control register.
2. CPU enters a **tight loop**: `while (status_reg & BUSY_BIT) ;`
3. When `READY_BIT = 1`, CPU reads/writes the data register.
4. Repeat for every byte/word.

**Trade-off:**
- ✅ Simple, deterministic, no interrupt overhead.
- ❌ Wastes CPU cycles; unscalable for slow devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **hard real-time embedded systems** (e.g., bare-metal 8051 firmware, automotive ECUs reading sensors at fixed rates), **polled I/O is often preferred** because interrupt latency is non-deterministic. The worst-case poll cycle is **provably bounded**, satisfying timing analysis.

---

### Paradigm B — Interrupt-Driven I/O

The CPU issues the I/O command, then continues useful work. When the device is ready, it raises a **hardware interrupt (IRQ)**, causing the CPU to suspend the current task, save context, and run the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**.

**Operational Steps:**
1. CPU writes command → returns to other work.
2. Device raises `IRQ` line when done.
3. CPU completes current instruction, saves PC + PSW to stack.
4. ISR executes, reads/writes data register, clears interrupt flag.
5. `IRET` (Interrupt Return) instruction restores context.

**Interrupt Latency Equation:**

$$
L_{int} = t_{detect} + t_{save} + t_{dispatch} + t_{ISR} + t_{restore}
$$

Where:
- $t_{detect}$ — time to recognize the IRQ line (clock-cycle dependent).
- $t_{save}$ — context save time (register push).
- $t_{dispatch}$ — vector lookup + ISR entry.
- $t_{ISR}$ — actual service routine execution.
- $t_{restore}$ — context restore.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interrupt latency must be bounded** in real-time systems. The **Worst-Case Interrupt Latency (WCIL)** is the maximum value of $L_{int}$ across all possible system states and is a key schedulability input.

---

### Paradigm C — Direct Memory Access (DMA)

For high-throughput block transfers, a dedicated **DMA Controller (DMAC)** moves data between the I/O device and main memory **without CPU intervention**. The CPU is interrupted only when the entire block transfer is complete.

**DMA Transfer Modes:**

| Mode | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Burst Mode** | DMAC owns the bus for entire block; CPU is locked out. | High-speed disk reads. |
| **Cycle Stealing** | DMAC transfers one word per bus cycle, interleaving with CPU. | Slower peripherals, less CPU impact. |
| **Transparent Mode** | DMAC transfers only when CPU is *not* using the bus. | Maximizes CPU performance. |

**Effective Transfer Time:**

$$
t_{DMA} = \frac{N \cdot B}{R_{bus}} + t_{setup} + t_{teardown}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = number of blocks/words.
- $B$ = block size in bytes.
- $R_{bus}$ = bus transfer rate (bytes/second).
- $t_{setup}, t_{teardown}$ = DMAC initialization + final interrupt overhead.

---

## 2.2 I/O Buffering Strategies

Buffers are intermediate memory regions used to decouple producer/consumer speeds.

| Strategy | Description | Real-Time Suitability |
|---|---|---|
| **No Buffer** | Direct hand-off; strict synchronization required. | Hard real-time microcontrollers. |
| **Single Buffer** | One shared buffer; producer/consumer alternate. | Simple pipelines, low data rates. |
| **Double Buffer (Ping-Pong)** | Two buffers; one filled while other is consumed. | Streaming audio/video, radar DSP. |
| **Circular Buffer (Ring Buffer)** | $N$ slots with head/tail pointers, FIFO discipline. | UART streams, sensor sampling, CAN bus. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Circular Buffer Capacity Rule:** To avoid overflow with arrival rate $\lambda$ (items/s) and service rate $\mu$ (items/s), the buffer size $B$ must satisfy:

$$
B \geq \lambda \cdot T_{max\_block}
$$

Where $T_{max\_block}$ is the worst-case duration the consumer is blocked.

---

## 2.3 I/O Scheduling in RTOS

Unlike general-purpose OS schedulers, RTOS I/O schedulers must consider **deadlines**, not just throughput. Common policies include:

- **Priority-based I/O** — Highest-priority task's I/O is dispatched first.
- **Deadline-aware I/O** — Earliest Deadline First (EDF) extended to disk.
- **Anticipatory I/O** — Issue I/O slightly *before* it's logically needed (hides latency).

---

## 2.4 Disk Scheduling Algorithms (Mandatory for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners **frequently** ask for seek-time calculations using these algorithms. Memorize the formula and procedure.

Let initial head position $= H_0$, request queue $= \{r_1, r_2, ..., r_n\}$ on cylinders $0$ to $C_{max}$.

### Algorithm Definitions

| Algorithm | Movement Policy | Real-Time Property |
|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | Serve in arrival order. | Fair but high seek time. |
| **SSTF** | Pick nearest request. | Low seek, but **starvation possible**. |
| **SCAN (Elevator)** | Sweep in one direction, reverse at ends. | Bounded waiting. |
| **C-SCAN** | Sweep one way, jump to other end, repeat. | Uniform wait time. |
| **LOOK / C-LOOK** | Like SCAN/C-SCAN but **reverse at last request**, not at cylinder end. | Fewer wasted moves. |
| **EDF-Disk** | Earliest deadline first. | **Real-time optimal** (theoretical). |

### Total Seek Time

$$
T_{seek}^{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \vert H_{i+1} - H_i \vert \cdot t_{per\_cylinder}
$$

Where $t_{per\_cylinder}$ is the time to move the head across one cylinder (typically **1–3 ms** in classic disks; **~0 for SSDs**).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Interrupt Latency | $L_{int} = t_{detect} + t_{save} + t_{dispatch} + t_{ISR} + t_{restore}$ | All times in $\mu$s. |
| 2 | DMA Block Transfer Time | $t_{DMA} = \dfrac{N \cdot B}{R_{bus}} + t_{setup} + t_{teardown}$ | $N$ blocks, $B$ bytes/block, $R_{bus}$ B/s. |
| 3 | Buffer Capacity Bound | $B \geq \lambda \cdot T_{max\_block}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate. |
| 4 | Disk Total Seek Time | $T_{seek}^{total} = \sum \vert H_{i+1} - H_i \vert \cdot t_{cyl}$ | Sum of head movements. |
| 5 | Throughput | $T_p = \dfrac{N_{IO}}{T_{total}}$ | I/O ops per second. |
| 6 | Rotational Latency (avg) | $L_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2R}$ | $R$ = rotations/second. |
| 7 | Average I/O Access Time | $T_{acc} = T_{seek} + L_{rot} + \dfrac{B}{R_{transfer}}$ | Combined disk access model. |
| 8 | Worst-Case I/O Latency | $WCIOL = T_{seek}^{max} + L_{rot}^{max} + T_{transfer}$ | Used in schedulability analysis. |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **directly testable** in KTU ESE Numerical/Analytical questions.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR)**: Wheel-speed sensors use **interrupt-driven I/O** with bounded WCIL of ~10 µs.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C)**: Flight control disks use **EDF scheduling** to guarantee navigation data freshness.
- **Industrial PLCs**: Use **polled I/O** for deterministic scan cycles (e.g., Siemens S7 at 1 ms cycle).
- **Medical devices**: Patient monitors use **double-buffered DMA** to prevent sample loss at high heart rates.
- **Robotics**: Sensor fusion uses **ring buffers** between ADC and DSP tasks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Worst-Case I/O Service Time for a Periodic Task

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **theoretical foundation** for KTU questions on RTOS I/O schedulability.

### Problem Setup

A real-time task $\tau$ issues a block I/O request every period $P$. The request reads $N$ blocks of $B$ bytes each from a disk. The disk has the following parameters:
- Average seek time: $T_{seek}^{avg} = a$ ms
- Rotational latency: $L_{rot} = b$ ms (½ rotation)
- Transfer rate: $R_{tr}$ bytes/ms

**Step 1 — Total bytes transferred per request.**

$$
D = N \cdot B
$$

This is the payload in bytes. Each I/O request must move exactly $D$ bytes from disk to memory.

**Step 2 — Transfer time for $D$ bytes.**

$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{D}{R_{tr}} = \frac{N \cdot B}{R_{tr}}
$$

We divide total bytes by throughput. Units: $\frac{\text{bytes}}{\text{bytes/ms}} = \text{ms}$ ✓

**Step 3 — Average disk service time (single request).**

$$
T_{svc} = T_{seek}^{avg} + L_{rot} + T_{transfer}
$$

$$
T_{svc} = a + b + \frac{N \cdot B}{R_{tr}}
$$

**Step 4 — Worst-case service time.**

The worst case occurs when:
- $T_{seek}^{max} = 2a$ (full stroke, both directions considered).
- $L_{rot}^{max} = 2b$ (full rotation).
- Transfer time is constant.

$$
T_{svc}^{WC} = 2a + 2b + \frac{N \cdot B}{R_{tr}}
$$

**Step 5 — Schedulability condition.**

For the task to meet its deadline $D_{line}$ with $N_{IO}$ I/O requests per period:

$$
N_{IO} \cdot T_{svc}^{WC} \leq D_{line}
$$

> This single inequality is the **schedulability test for the I/O subsystem**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — SSTF Disk Scheduling

**Given:**
- Request queue: $\{98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67\}$
- Initial head position: $H_0 = 53$
- Cylinder range: $0$ to $199$
- $t_{per\_cylinder} = 1$ ms

**Find:** Total seek time and order of service.

**Step 1 — Sort requests:** $\{14, 37, 65, 67, 98, 122, 124, 183\}$

**Step 2 — SSTF choice rule:** At position 53, find nearest neighbor in request set.

$$
\text{Dist}(53, 65) = 12, \quad \text{Dist}(53, 37) = 16
$$

So next is **65** (smaller distance).

**Step 3 — Continue greedily:**

| Step | From | To | Distance (cyl) | Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 53 | 65 | 12 | 12 |
| 2 | 65 | 67 | 2 | 2 |
| 3 | 67 | 37 | 30 | 30 |
| 4 | 37 | 14 | 23 | 23 |
| 5 | 14 | 98 | 84 | 84 |
| 6 | 98 | 122 | 24 | 24 |
| 7 | 122 | 124 | 2 | 2 |
| 8 | 124 | 183 | 59 | 59 |

**Step 4 — Total seek time:**

$$
T_{seek}^{total} = (12 + 2 + 30 + 23 + 84 + 24 + 2 + 59) \cdot t_{cyl}
$$

$$
T_{seek}^{total} = 236 \cdot 1 \text{ ms} = 236 \text{ ms}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — SCAN (Elevator) Algorithm

**Same queue and initial head, head moving initially toward higher cylinders.**

**Service order (moving up first):**

$53 \to 65 \to 67 \to 98 \to 122 \to 124 \to 183 \to (\text{reverse}) \to 37 \to 14$

| Step | From | To | Distance (cyl) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 53 | 65 | 12 |
| 2 | 65 | 67 | 2 |
| 3 | 67 | 98 | 31 |
| 4 | 98 | 122 | 24 |
| 5 | 122 | 124 | 2 |
| 6 | 124 | 183 | 59 |
| 7 | 183 | 37 | 146 |
| 8 | 37 | 14 | 23 |

**Total movement:**

$$
T_{seek}^{total} = (12 + 2 + 31 + 24 + 2 + 59 + 146 + 23) = 299 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** SSTF (236 ms) < SCAN (299 ms) in this case. However, SCAN **guarantees bounded starvation** — every request is served within $2 \cdot (C_{max})$ cylinders, a hard real-time-friendly property.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of I/O Techniques

| Criterion | Polled I/O | Interrupt I/O | DMA |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU overhead | **High** (busy-wait) | **Low** (only on IRQ) | **Very Low** (one IRQ/block) |
| Latency determinism | **High** (provable bound) | Medium (IRQ latency varies) | Medium (bus arbitration delay) |
| Implementation complexity | Trivial | Moderate | High (DMAC setup) |
| Suitability for block transfer | Poor | Acceptable | **Excellent** |
| Suitability for byte stream | Acceptable | **Excellent** | Overkill |
| Typical use in RTOS | Sensor polling @ fixed rate | UART, GPIO events | Disk, ADC multi-channel, Ethernet |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: RTOS I/O Subsystem

---

## 4.2 State Diagram — Interrupt-Driven I/O Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> IssueCommand: App calls read write
    IssueCommand --> WaitIRQ: CPU returns to scheduler
    WaitIRQ --> ISREntry: Device asserts IRQ line
    ISREntry --> DataTransfer: Read or write data register
    DataTransfer --> AckIRQ: Clear interrupt flag
    AckIRQ --> RestoreContext: IRET instruction
    RestoreContext --> Idle: Resume preempted task
    ISREntry --> NestedISR: Higher priority IRQ asserted
    NestedISR --> ISREntry: After nested handler completes
    DataTransfer --> ErrorHandler: Device error or timeout
    ErrorHandler --> Idle: Error code returned to app
```

---

## 4.3 Decision Flow — I/O Strategy Selection in RTOS Design

```mermaid

    Q1{"Data rate?"}:::q
    P1["POLLED I/O"]:::io

    P2["INTERRUPT I/O"]:::io
    Q3{"Latency tolerance?"}:::q
    P3["DMA TRANSFER"]:::io
    Q4{"Hard or soft RT?"}:::q
    P4["BUFFERED INTERRUPT"]:::io
    End["INTEGRATE IN DRIVER"]:::e

    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -- "Low and periodic" --> P1
    Q1 -- "Moderate" --> Q2
    Q1 -- "High burst" --> Q3
    Q2 -- "Byte or short stream" --> P2
    Q2 -- "Block and bounded" --> P4
    Q3 -- "Strict deadline" --> P3
    Q3 -- "Soft deadline" --> P4
    Q4 -- "Hard" --> P3
    Q4 -- "Soft" --> P4
    P1 --> End
    P2 --> End
    P3 --> End
    P4 --> End

```

---

## 4.4 Timing Diagram — Polled vs Interrupt vs DMA

```mermaid
gantt

    dateFormat X

    section Polled I/O

    ISR executes             :b2, 4, 6
    CPU resumes             :b3, 6, 10
    section DMA
```
