```mermaid
    DMAC transfers block    :c2, 1, 9
    Final DMA ISR           :c3, 9, 10
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define interrupt latency. List the components of interrupt latency in a typical RTOS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Interrupt latency is the time interval between the instant an interrupt request (IRQ) is raised by a peripheral device and the instant the first instruction of the corresponding Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) begins execution.

**Components (2 marks):**
1. **Detection time** ($t_{detect}$) — time for the CPU to recognize the asserted IRQ line, typically one clock cycle for edge-triggered interrupts, more for level-triggered with masking.
2. **Context save time** ($t_{save}$) — time to push the current task's registers, program counter (PC), and program status word (PSW) onto the stack.
3. **Dispatch time** ($t_{dispatch}$) — time for the interrupt controller to vector to the correct ISR (lookup in vector table).
4. **ISR execution time** ($t_{ISR}$) — actual time to service the device and clear the interrupt flag.
5. **Restore time** ($t_{restore}$) — time to pop saved context and return via `IRET`.

**Equation:** $L_{int} = t_{detect} + t_{save} + t_{dispatch} + t_{ISR} + t_{restore}$

---

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between programmed I/O and interrupt-driven I/O. State one real-time application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Programmed I/O (Polled) | Interrupt-Driven I/O |
|---|---|---|
| CPU activity | Busy-waits in loop checking status. | Performs other work, serviced on IRQ. |
| Latency | Predictable, bounded by loop time. | Depends on IRQ controller + context save. |
| Power | High (CPU always active). | Low (CPU can idle). |
| Determinism | **High** — easy to bound. | Medium — IRQ nesting complicates bounds. |
| Preferred when | Hard RT, fixed periodic sampling. | Asynchronous event-driven I/O. |

**Applications (1 mark):**
- **Polled I/O preferred:** Automotive ECU reading crankshaft position sensor at every 1 ms crank-angle tick — the loop is provably bounded.
- **Interrupt-driven I/O preferred:** UART reception in a wireless sensor node — CPU sleeps, wakes on byte arrival, no wasted cycles.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** Module 4 carries either Q4 or Q5 in the ESE. Internal choice is **mandatory**. Both alternatives must be solved in your **practice notebook** for the model solutions below.

---

### Question 4A — 14 Marks: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the three I/O handling techniques — Programmed I/O, Interrupt-Driven I/O, and DMA — with neat block diagrams. Compare them on the basis of CPU utilization, latency, and suitability for real-time systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A real-time disk-based data logger issues block I/O requests of $N = 16$ blocks, each $B = 4096$ bytes, every $P = 100$ ms. Disk parameters: avg seek $= 8$ ms, rotational latency $= 4$ ms (½ rotation), transfer rate $= 80$ MB/s. The task deadline is $D = 50$ ms. Determine if the task is schedulable. If not, suggest one improvement.

---

#### Model Solution to 4A(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Programmed I/O (2 marks):**
- CPU executes a tight loop reading device status register.
- Diagram: `CPU → Data Bus → Status Reg [poll] → Device → Ready bit set → CPU reads Data Reg`.
- **CPU utilization:** Low (wasted cycles); **Latency:** Bounded and deterministic; **Suitability:** Hard RT periodic tasks.

**2. Interrupt-Driven I/O (2 marks):**
- CPU initiates I/O, returns to other work; device asserts IRQ on completion.
- Diagram: `CPU → Command Reg → Device; Device → IRQ line → Interrupt Controller → CPU ISR`.
- **CPU utilization:** High (free during I/O); **Latency:** Bounded by WCIL; **Suitability:** Asynchronous event-driven RT tasks.

**3. DMA (2 marks):**
- DMAC handles block transfer autonomously; CPU interrupted only at end.
- Diagram: `CPU → DMAC [setup] → Bus Arbiter → Device/Memory; Final IRQ → CPU`.
- **CPU utilization:** Very high; **Latency:** Bounded by bus arbitration + transfer; **Suitability:** High-throughput block transfers (disk, network).

**Comparison Table (1 mark):** as presented in Section 3.4.

---

#### Model Solution to 4A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute average I/O service time (2 marks):**

$$
T_{svc} = T_{seek}^{avg} + L_{rot} + T_{transfer}
$$

**Transfer time:**

$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{N \cdot B}{R_{tr}} = \frac{16 \cdot 4096}{80 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
T_{transfer} = \frac{65536}{80 \times 10^6} = 0.8192 \text{ ms} \approx 0.82 \text{ ms}
$$

**Total:**

$$
T_{svc} = 8 + 4 + 0.82 = 12.82 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Compare with deadline (2 marks):**

Since $T_{svc} = 12.82 \text{ ms} < D = 50 \text{ ms}$, the **single I/O request is schedulable** within one period.

**Step 3 — Account for worst case (1 mark):**

$$
T_{svc}^{WC} = 2 \cdot T_{seek}^{avg} + 2 \cdot L_{rot} + T_{transfer} = 16 + 8 + 0.82 = 24.82 \text{ ms}
$$

Even in worst case, $T_{svc}^{WC} = 24.82 \text{ ms} < 50 \text{ ms}$. **Task is schedulable ✓**

**Step 4 — If multiple I/O bursts per period (1 mark):**

Suppose 2 I/O requests per period $P = 100$ ms:

$$
2 \cdot T_{svc}^{WC} = 49.64 \text{ ms} < 50 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(marginal)}
$$

**Improvement suggestion (1 mark):** Use **DMA** to free CPU for other tasks and **elevator (SCAN) disk scheduling** to reduce average seek time by ~30–40%, increasing headroom for additional I/O bursts.

---

### Question 4B — 14 Marks: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]` — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain circular (ring) buffering. Why is it the most common buffering strategy in real-time I/O? Derive the buffer-overflow avoidance condition.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Consider a disk with $C_{max} = 199$ cylinders. Request queue: $\{176, 79, 34, 60, 92, 11, 41, 114\}$. Initial head $= 50$, moving **towards higher cylinder numbers**. Compute the total head movement for:
- (i) FCFS
- (ii) SSTF
- (iii) C-SCAN
- (iv) C-LOOK

Assume $t_{cyl} = 1$ ms. State which algorithm is most suitable for a **hard real-time database query** and justify.

---

#### Model Solution to 4B(a) — 7 Marks

**1. Definition (2 marks):**
A circular (ring) buffer is a fixed-size $N$-slot FIFO queue with two pointers:
- **Head (read pointer)** $H$ — location of next item to be consumed.
- **Tail (write pointer)** $T$ — location of next free slot for the producer.

When $H$ or $T$ reaches slot $N-1$, it **wraps around** to slot $0$ using modulo arithmetic: $T \leftarrow (T+1) \bmod N$. This eliminates memory copying and supports lock-free single-producer/single-consumer (SPSC) operation.

**2. Why preferred in RTOS (3 marks):**

| Property | Benefit in RTOS |
|---|---|
| $O(1)$ enqueue / dequeue | Bounded WCET — critical for schedulability. |
| No dynamic allocation | Avoids heap fragmentation; deterministic memory. |
| Cache-friendly contiguous memory | Predictable access time. |
| Lock-free SPSC variant | Avoids priority inversion in mutex-protected buffers. |
| Fixed capacity | Worst-case memory usage provable at compile time. |

**3. Overflow condition derivation (2 marks):**

Let:
- $\lambda$ = producer arrival rate (items/s)
- $\mu$ = consumer service rate (items/s)
- $T_{max\_block}$ = max time consumer is blocked (s)

In time $T_{max\_block}$, producer can deposit up to $\lambda \cdot T_{max\_block}$ items. To prevent overflow:

$$
B \geq \lambda \cdot T_{max\_block}
$$

This is the **static buffer sizing rule** for hard real-time ring buffers.

---

#### Model Solution to 4B(b) — 7 Marks

**Sorted request set:** $\{11, 34, 41, 60, 79, 92, 114, 176\}$

**(i) FCFS (1.5 marks):** Serve in given order starting from 50.

| From → To | Movement |
|---|---|
| 50 → 176 | 126 |
| 176 → 79 | 97 |
| 79 → 34 | 45 |
| 34 → 60 | 26 |
| 60 → 92 | 32 |
| 92 → 11 | 81 |
| 11 → 41 | 30 |
| 41 → 114 | 73 |

**Total FCFS = 510 cylinders = 510 ms**

**(ii) SSTF (1.5 marks):** Always pick nearest.

Path: $50 \to 41 \to 34 \to 11 \to 60 \to 79 \to 92 \to 114 \to 176$

| From → To | Movement |
|---|---|
| 50 → 41 | 9 |
| 41 → 34 | 7 |
| 34 → 11 | 23 |
| 11 → 60 | 49 |
| 60 → 79 | 19 |
| 79 → 92 | 13 |
| 92 → 114 | 22 |
| 114 → 176 | 62 |

**Total SSTF = 204 cylinders = 204 ms**

**(iii) C-SCAN (1.5 marks):** Move up to end (199), jump to 0, move up to highest request.

Path: $50 \to 60 \to 79 \to 92 \to 114 \to 176 \to 199 \to (\text{jump to } 0) \to 11 \to 34 \to 41$

| From → To | Movement |
|---|---|
| 50 → 60 | 10 |
| 60 → 79 | 19 |
| 79 → 92 | 13 |
| 92 → 114 | 22 |
| 114 → 176 | 62 |
| 176 → 199 | 23 |
| 199 → 0 | 199 (jump) |
| 0 → 11 | 11 |
| 11 → 34 | 23 |
| 34 → 41 | 7 |

**Total C-SCAN = 389 cylinders = 389 ms**

**(iv) C-LOOK (1.5 marks):** Move up to 176, jump to 11, then up to 41.

Path: $50 \to 60 \to 79 \to 92 \to 114 \to 176 \to (\text{jump to } 11) \to 34 \to 41$

| From → To | Movement |
|---|---|
| 50 → 60 | 10 |
| 60 → 79 | 19 |
| 79 → 92 | 13 |
| 92 → 114 | 22 |
| 114 → 176 | 62 |
| 176 → 11 | 165 (jump) |
| 11 → 34 | 23 |
| 34 → 41 | 7 |

**Total C-LOOK = 321 cylinders = 321 ms**

**(v) Best for hard real-time (1 mark):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **C-SCAN is most suitable for hard real-time database queries.** Justification: C-SCAN provides **uniform wait time** for all requests — every request is guaranteed service within a single sweep cycle. SSTF, while faster, can cause **starvation** of far-away cylinders, violating the bounded-latency contract required for hard RT.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing SCAN and C-SCAN** — SCAN reverses at the end and serves requests on the return trip. C-SCAN jumps back to the start and only serves requests while moving **in one direction**. Many students lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting the "jump" in C-SCAN/C-LOOK** — the return-to-zero movement must be **counted in the total**, not omitted. The disk head physically moves; time accumulates.
> 3. **Initial direction matters** — if the problem says "moving towards higher cylinders," you must start serving requests with cylinder number > 50 first. Reversing this order loses full marks.
> 4. **Unit consistency** — if $t_{cyl}$ is in ms, total movement in cylinders × ms = ms. Do not write seconds unless converted.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Three I/O paradigms:** Polled (deterministic, wasteful), Interrupt (event-driven, WCIL-bound), DMA (high-throughput, low CPU load).
- ✅ **Interrupt latency equation:** $L_{int} = t_{detect} + t_{save} + t_{dispatch} + t_{ISR} + t_{restore}$. **Worst-case** value is schedulability input.
- ✅ **DMA modes:** Burst, Cycle Stealing, Transparent. Burst has highest throughput; Transparent has lowest CPU impact.
- ✅ **Buffer strategies:** None → Single → Double (Ping-Pong) → Circular (Ring). Real-time systems overwhelmingly use **ring buffers** for SPSC streams.
- ✅ **Buffer overflow rule:** $B \geq \lambda \cdot T_{max\_block}$.
- ✅ **Disk scheduling algorithms — memorise order of preference for RT:**
  1. **EDF-Disk** (theoretical optimal for deadlines).
  2. **C-SCAN** (uniform wait, hard RT).
  3. **C-LOOK** (practical C-SCAN variant, no wasted end-to-end travel).
  4. **SCAN/LOOK** (bounded wait, possible end-cluster unfairness).
  5. **SSTF** (fastest average, but starvation risk — **avoid in hard RT**).
  6. **FCFS** (fairest, slowest — never optimal).
- ✅ **Total seek time formula:** $T_{seek}^{total} = \sum \vert H_{i+1} - H_i \vert \cdot t_{cyl}$. Use **vertical bars carefully** in markdown — use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid table-breaking.
- ✅ **Average disk access time:** $T_{acc} = T_{seek} + L_{rot} + \dfrac{B}{R_{transfer}}$.
- ✅ **Worst-case access time** uses $2 \cdot T_{seek}^{avg}$ and $2 \cdot L_{rot}$ bounds.
- ✅ **Schedulability test:** $N_{IO} \cdot T_{svc}^{WC} \leq D_{line}$ — single most-tested inequality in RTOS I/O.
- ✅ **RTOS design mantra:** *Predictable > Fast.* A 200 ms bounded latency is preferable to a 50 ms average latency with 10 s tail.
- ✅ **Common devices:**
  - Polled → sensors, knobs, switches.
  - Interrupt → UART, USB, GPIO events.
  - DMA → disk, SSD, network packets, multi-channel ADC.
- ✅ **Jitter ($J$):** The single most important metric distinguishing RTOS I/O from general-purpose OS I/O. Must be **bounded**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Middleware: Middleware Services for Real-Time Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Middleware: Middleware Services for Real-Time Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Middleware** is a reusable, distributed software layer that abstracts the heterogeneity of underlying networks, hardware, and operating systems, providing standardized, location-transparent services and APIs to distributed real-time applications. In the context of **Real-Time Systems (RTS)**, *Real-Time Middleware (RT-MW)* extends conventional middleware with **deterministic, time-bounded** semantics for scheduling, communication, and resource management.

According to the **OMG (Object Management Group)** specifications that drive KTU-aligned RTOS curricula, Real-Time Middleware is a *Distributed Object Computing (DOC)* substrate that enforces **Quality of Service (QoS)** contracts — such as **end-to-end deadline**, **jitter bound**, **priority inversion control**, and **schedulability** — between cooperating tasks/jobs across a network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Hook:** A Real-Time Middleware is *"a software infrastructure positioned between the RTOS/Hardware and the application layer, that provides standardized services (naming, event, time, resource, QoS) with deterministic, time-predictable behavior."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a busy **international airport**. Airplanes (your distributed applications) speak different "languages" — they are made by Boeing, Airbus, Embraer (different OS/hardware). Without a common translator, they cannot coordinate take-offs, landings, or gates. The **Air Traffic Control (ATC) tower** is your **Middleware**:

- It translates between different aircraft systems (network protocols).
- It enforces **strict timing** — every plane has a slot; delays are catastrophic.
- It provides shared services: weather updates, runway allocation, gate assignment.
- It guarantees **predictability** — no plane is left waiting indefinitely.

Similarly, RT-Middleware translates between heterogeneous nodes, enforces **timing guarantees**, and provides shared services like **publish/subscribe, naming, time synchronization, and resource reservation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction — Conventional vs Real-Time Middleware:**
>
> | Property | Conventional Middleware (e.g., CORBA) | Real-Time Middleware (e.g., TAO) |
> |---|---|---|
> | Goal | Functional correctness | **Temporal + functional** correctness |
> | Metric | Throughput, latency (avg) | **Worst-case latency, jitter** |
> | Scheduling | Best-effort | **Priority-based, preemptive** |
> | Example | Standard CORBA | Real-Time CORBA, RT-Java, MQTT-SN |

### 1.3 Why Middleware in Real-Time Systems?

Modern embedded/cyber-physical systems (e.g., **AUTOSAR** in automotive, **ARINC 653** in avionics, **DDS** in robotics) require:

1. **Heterogeneity**: Different ECUs, microcontrollers, and network protocols.
2. **Modularity**: Reusable components (e.g., brake-by-wire ↔ steering-by-wire).
3. **Predictability**: Hard deadlines that *cannot* be missed.
4. **Distribution**: Sensors and actuators scattered physically.

The RT-MW sits between the **application layer** and the **RTOS/HW layer**, providing a clean abstraction while preserving timing guarantees.

### 1.4 Core Properties of RT-Middleware (KTU High-Yield Points)

- **Transparency**: Hides network, OS, and hardware heterogeneity.
- **Determinism**: Bounded execution/communication times.
- **Composability**: Subsystems can be analyzed and integrated independently.
- **QoS Awareness**: Supports explicit, enforceable QoS contracts.
- **Reusability**: Plug-and-play deployment of components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standards Mapped to KTU Syllabus:**
> - **OMG Real-Time CORBA 1.2** — priority propagation, bounded latency.
> - **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** — real-time publish-subscribe.
> - **POSIX 1003.21** — middleware over real-time OS interfaces.
> - **RTSJ (Real-Time Specification for Java)** — managed/unmanaged memory.
> - **AUTOSAR RTE** — automotive RT-MW standard.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Position of RT-Middleware

The RT-MW resides in the **middleware tier** of the classic distributed real-time stack:

$$
\text{Application} \;\rightarrow\; \text{RT-Middleware} \;\rightarrow\; \text{RTOS / Network / Drivers} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Hardware}
$$

It encapsulates four orthogonal concerns:
1. **Communication** (RPC, event, message-passing).
2. **Coordination** (synchronization, transactions).
3. **Service** (naming, time, persistence).
4. **Management** (QoS, fault-tolerance, security).

### 2.2 Classification of Real-Time Middleware Services

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups middleware services into the following families:

#### 2.2.1 Communication Services
- **Remote Procedure Call (RPC)**: Synchronous, request-reply. Used in **hard real-time** if bounded.
- **Real-Time RPC (RT-RPC)**: Adds priority, deadline, and bounded execution time.
- **Message-Oriented Middleware (MOM)**: Asynchronous queues (e.g., DDS, MQTT).
- **Publish/Subscribe**: Decouples producers and consumers via *topics* (e.g., ROS 2, RTI Connext DDS).
- **Event Service**: Push/pull of event notifications with QoS filters.

#### 2.2.2 Naming & Trading Services
- **Naming Service**: Maps logical names → physical references (analogous to DNS).
- **Trading Service**: Object lookup with **attribute-based matching** (e.g., "find the camera with < 5 ms latency").
- **Location Service**: Tracks mobile nodes (robots, drones).

#### 2.2.3 Time & Clock Services
- **Global Time Service**: Synchronizes clocks across nodes (e.g., **PTP — IEEE 1588**).
- **Timer Service**: Schedules callbacks at future instants.
- **Deadline Service**: Computes remaining time before a deadline violation.

#### 2.2.4 Resource Management Services
- **Scheduling Service**: Distributes scheduling policies across the cluster.
- **Processor Set Service**: Reserves CPU capacity on a node.
- **Memory Pool Service**: Pre-allocated, bounded memory regions.
- **Network Bandwidth Reservation**: E.g., **CAN-FD arbitration**, **TTEthernet**.

#### 2.2.5 Quality of Service (QoS) Services
- **Priority Propagation**: End-to-end priority inheritance (avoid *priority inversion*).
- **Bounded Latency**: CORBA `CLIENT_PROPAGATED`, `SERVER_DECLARED` models.
- **Jitter Control**: Periodic execution with bounded jitter.
- **Reliability**: BEST_EFFORT vs RELIABLE delivery (DDS QoS).

#### 2.2.6 Fault Tolerance & Security
- **Replication Service**: Active/passive replicas for high availability.
- **Fault Detection**: Watchdog timers, heartbeat monitoring.
- **Security**: Authentication, encryption (TLS/DTLS in real-time).

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready reference** — every entry is a direct hit from past KTU question papers.

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning / Formula** | **Units** | **KTU Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R_i$ | Release time of task $i$ | ms | Earliest start instant |
| $D_i$ | Absolute deadline | ms | Hard real-time constraint |
| $T_i$ | Period (periodic task) | ms | $f_i = 1/T_i$ is frequency |
| $C_i$ | Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET) | ms | Critical for schedulability |
| $J_i$ | Release jitter | ms | Affects schedulability analysis |
| $U_i$ | Utilization of task $i$ = $C_i / T_i$ | dimensionless | Sum $\sum U_i$ for load |
| $U_{bound}^{RM}$ | Rate Monotonic upper bound: $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | dimensionless | $\le \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ for large $n$ |
| $E2E_{lat}$ | End-to-end latency | ms | Sums of local + network |
| $W_k$ | Worst-case response time iteration | ms | $W_k^{n+1} = C_k + \sum_{i \in hp(k)} \lceil W_k^n / T_i \rceil C_i$ |
| $B_{net}$ | Network blocking delay | ms | Lowest-priority message blocking time |
| $\Delta$ | Clock skew bound | µs | For time-sync services |
| QoS-D | Deadline QoS property | ms | Hard/soft/firm |
| QoS-P | Priority property | int | 0 (realtime) – 32767 (best-effort) |
| QoS-B | Bandwidth property | Kbps | Network reservation |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Each Service — Operational Logic

1. **Why Naming Service?**
   Hard-coded IP addresses are fragile; in dynamic fleets (UAV swarms), nodes join/leave. A **Trader/Naming** service decouples *what* a node does from *where* it is — location transparency.

2. **Why Time Service?**
   Distributed real-time control (e.g., coordinated robot arms) requires **clock synchronization** within microseconds. Without it, sensor fusion is impossible. **IEEE 1588 PTP** can achieve sub-microsecond precision on switched Ethernet.

3. **Why Resource Reservation?**
   Best-effort scheduling leads to **deadline misses** under load. Reservation-based MW (e.g., **RT-CORBA's Processor Set Service**) pre-allocates CPU and memory *offline*, ensuring each task gets its contracted capacity.

4. **Why Priority Propagation?**
   In distributed systems, a high-priority client may block on a low-priority server (the *distributed priority inversion* problem). RT-MW propagates client priority to the server, allowing *priority inheritance* across the wire.

5. **Why QoS Contracts?**
   Application-level contracts (deadline, throughput, reliability) must be **enforced** by middleware, not just *requested*. The MW translates them into kernel-level scheduling, network buffers, and memory pools.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR RTE)**: Middleware between OEM application software and ECU hardware. Manages ~100+ ECUs with deterministic CAN/FlexRay/Ethernet communication.
- **Avionics (ARINC 653 + FACE)**: Spatial/temporal partitioning enforced by middleware.
- **Industrial IoT (OPC UA + DDS)**: Latency-critical control loops over TSN.
- **Robotics (ROS 2)**: DDS-based pub/sub for sensor-actuator loops with QoS.
- **Medical Devices (FDA IEC 62304)**: Middleware ensures deterministic alarm propagation in patient monitors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Hook:** A typical exam question will ask *"Explain the four categories of RT-MW services with one example each."* The four categories are: **Communication, Naming/Trading, Time, Resource Management**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Framework: Building a Real-Time Middleware Service

We now derive — step-by-step — the **end-to-end latency** $L_{e2e}$ for a real-time RPC call passing through an RT-Middleware. This is a **favourite KTU derivation**.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of End-to-End Latency

Consider a client task $T_c$ on **Node A** calling a server task $T_s$ on **Node B** through an RT-Middleware (e.g., RT-CORBA).

**Step 1 — Identify the time components.**

The total time from the *moment the client issues the call* to the *moment the result is received* consists of:

$$
L_{e2e} = L_{client} + L_{marshalling} + L_{net,transmit} + L_{net,prop} + L_{server,queue} + L_{demarshalling} + L_{server,exec} + L_{return}
$$

**Step 2 — Define each term precisely.**

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| $L_{client}$ | Time from call invocation to MW entry |
| $L_{marshalling}$ | Converting in-memory data to wire format |
| $L_{net,transmit}$ | Time on the wire (packet size / bandwidth) |
| $L_{net,prop}$ | Physical propagation delay (length / speed_of_signal) |
| $L_{server,queue}$ | Time in the MW server-side priority queue |
| $L_{demarshalling}$ | Reconstructing data on the server side |
| $L_{server,exec}$ | Actual WCET of the server operation |
| $L_{return}$ | Symmetric path for the reply |

**Step 3 — Express the network component.**

The transmission time for a packet of size $S$ bits over a link of bandwidth $B$ bits/s is:

$$
L_{net,transmit} = \frac{S}{B}
$$

The propagation time for a link of length $d$ meters through a medium with signal velocity $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (copper/fiber):

$$
L_{net,prop} = \frac{d}{v}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad L_{net} = \frac{S}{B} + \frac{d}{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Bound the queuing component.**

The server-side queue is priority-scheduled. Under a **fixed-priority** policy, the worst-case queueing delay is bounded by the **schedulability analysis**:

$$
L_{server,queue} = R_s^{BCCT} - C_s
$$

where $R_s^{BCCT}$ is the worst-case **busy-centre completion time** of server task $s$, computed by the iterative equation:

$$
W_{s}^{n+1} = C_s + \sum_{i \in hp(s)} \left\lceil \frac{W_{s}^{n}}{T_i} \right\rceil C_i
$$

The iteration starts with $W_s^0 = C_s$ and terminates when $W_s^{n+1} = W_s^n$ or $W_s^n > D_s$ (deadline miss).

**Step 5 — Add bounded blocking.**

If **priority inheritance** is used, the queueing delay may also include a single blocking term:

$$
L_{server,queue}^{PI} = R_s^{BCCT} + B_s
$$

where $B_s$ is the worst-case blocking from lower-priority tasks holding a shared resource.

**Step 6 — Final assembled formula.**

$$
\boxed{
L_{e2e} = L_{client} + L_{marsh} + \frac{S}{B} + \frac{d}{v} + R_s^{BCCT} + B_s + L_{demarsh} + C_s + L_{return}
}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical evaluation (worked example).**

A client invokes a remote read on a CAN bus:
- $C_s = 50 \,\mu\text{s}$ (server WCET)
- $S = 128$ bits (CAN frame payload)
- $B = 1\,\text{Mbps}$ (CAN bit rate)
- $d = 10$ m, $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $hp(s)$ set contributes $\sum \lceil W_s/T_i \rceil C_i = 30 \,\mu\text{s}$
- $B_s = 0$ (no shared resource)
- $L_{marsh} = L_{demarsh} = 5 \,\mu\text{s}$ each
- $L_{client} = L_{return} = 2 \,\mu\text{s}$ each

Compute each term:

$$
L_{net,transmit} = \frac{128}{10^6} = 128 \,\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
L_{net,prop} = \frac{10}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.05 \,\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
R_s^{BCCT} = 50 + 30 = 80 \,\mu\text{s}
$$

$$
L_{e2e} = 2 + 5 + 128 + 0.05 + 80 + 0 + 5 + 50 + 2 = 272.05 \,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Interpretation:** The end-to-end latency is dominated by the **network transmission** (128 µs) — a typical result for short-range real-time control loops.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Response-Time Analysis for a Distributed Real-Time Task Chain

**Problem setup.** A pipeline of three real-time tasks executes across three nodes:

$$
T_1 \;\xrightarrow{\text{Net}_{1,2}}\; T_2 \;\xrightarrow{\text{Net}_{2,3}}\; T_3
$$

Each task has its own period $T_i$, WCET $C_i$, and uses a network message with transmission time $L_{net,i}$.

**Step 1.** The end-to-end deadline $D_{e2e}$ is partitioned:

$$
D_{e2e} = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 + D_{net,1,2} + D_{net,2,3}
$$

**Step 2.** For each local task, the worst-case response time is computed independently:

$$
R_i = J_i + W_i, \quad W_i^{n+1} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{W_i^n}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Step 3.** The task chain is *schedulable* if:

$$
R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + L_{net,1,2} + L_{net,2,3} \le D_{e2e}
$$

**Step 4.** Apply **Holf & Tindell's transformation** to include release jitter from upstream tasks:

$$
W_i^{n+1} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{W_i^n + J_j}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Step 5.** The jitter of $T_{i+1}$ is bounded by the response time of $T_i$ plus the network delay:

$$
J_{i+1} = R_i + L_{net,i,i+1}
$$

**Step 6.** Iterate Step 2 → Step 5 until all $R_i$ converge. If any $R_i > D_i$, the chain is **unschedulable** and the design must be revised (e.g., shorten periods, raise priorities, or offload to faster hardware).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of RT-Middleware Service

| **Application Need** | **Recommended RT-MW Service** | **Rationale** |
|---|---|---|
| Hard-deadline control loop | Real-Time RPC + Processor Set | Bounded latency, reserved CPU |
| High-frequency sensor fan-out | DDS / Publish-Subscribe | Decouples N producers from M consumers |
| Mobile node tracking | Naming + Trading Service | Location-transparent lookup |
| Multi-node clock coherence | IEEE 1588 PTP Time Service | Sub-µs synchronization |
| Fault-tolerant actuator | Replication Service | Active replicas, voting |
| Bandwidth-critical stream | Resource Reservation + QoS | Guaranteed link capacity |

### 3.5 Worked Proof: Rate Monotonic Bound for Distributed Tasks

A KTU favourite. Given $n$ independent periodic distributed tasks scheduled by **Rate Monotonic (RM)**, each with utilization $U_i = C_i / T_i$, the system is schedulable if:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i \le n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

For large $n$, the bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. This **sufficient** (not necessary) condition is the foundation of many RT-MW schedulability claims.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Real-Time Middleware Stack — Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    APP2["Application Layer\nHard Real-Time Logic"]:::app

    MWRES["Resource Management Services\nProcessor Set, Memory Pool"]:::mw
    MWQOS["Quality of Service Layer\nPriority, Deadline, Jitter"]:::mw
    RTOS1["RTOS Kernel A\nVxWorks, FreeRTOS"]:::rtos
    RTOS2["RTOS Kernel B\nRTLinux, QNX"]:::rtos
    HW1["ECU 1\n32-bit MCU"]:::hw
    HW2["ECU 2\n64-bit SoC"]:::hw
    NET["Real-Time Network\nCAN, TTE, FlexRay"]:::net

    APP1 --> MWCOM
    APP2 --> MWCOM
    APP1 --> MWNAM
    APP2 --> MWTIM
    APP1 --> MWRES
    APP2 --> MWQOS
    MWCOM --> RTOS1
    MWNAM --> RTOS1
    MWTIM --> RTOS2
    MWRES --> RTOS2
    MWQOS --> RTOS1
    RTOS1 --> HW1
    RTOS2 --> HW2
    HW1 <--> NET
    HW2 <--> NET
```

### 4.5 Distributed Priority Inheritance — Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant HC as High-Priority Client
    participant MW as RT Middleware
    participant LS as Low-Priority Server
    participant HS as High-Priority Server
    HC->>MW: RT-RPC call (priority P_HIGH)
    MW->>LS: Propagate P_HIGH
    LS-->>MW: Holds shared resource
    Note over MW,LS: LS temporarily inherits P_HIGH
    LS->>HS: Releases resource
    HS->>MW: Server task resumes
    MW-->>HC: Returns result on time
```
