<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Model answers are tuned to KTU examiner depth (≈ 60–80 words + a small diagram or table).*

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Real-Time Middleware. List any two services provided by it.*

**Model Answer:**
Real-Time Middleware is a software layer between the application and the RTOS/hardware that provides standardized, time-bounded services to distributed real-time applications. It abstracts heterogeneity of network, OS, and hardware, and enforces **Quality of Service (QoS)** contracts such as deadlines and priorities. Two services are:
1. **Naming/Trading Service** — location-transparent object lookup.
2. **Time/Clock Service** — global clock synchronization (e.g., IEEE 1588 PTP).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Real-Time RPC and conventional RPC with respect to timing guarantees.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Conventional RPC | Real-Time RPC |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Functional correctness | Temporal + functional correctness |
| Latency | Best-effort, unbounded | **Bounded worst-case** |
| Priority | Not propagated | **End-to-end priority inheritance** |
| Example | ONC RPC, gRPC (default) | RT-CORBA, gRPC + RT scheduling |

Conventional RPC may block arbitrarily; RT-RPC guarantees that a high-priority client call completes within a stated **deadline** by reserving server resources and bounding queueing delay.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESE Pattern:** Two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice between Q(A) and Q(B). Each sub-part demands *definition + diagram + analysis*.

---

#### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Real-Time Middleware. List and briefly describe **any four categories of RT-Middleware services**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** What is **Quality of Service (QoS)** in the context of RT-Middleware? Describe **priority propagation** and **deadline propagation** as two key QoS mechanisms, with the help of the *distributed priority inversion* problem. (7 Marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — RT-MW Architecture & Service Categories (7 Marks)**

**Architecture Block Diagram:**

```
+-------------------------------------------+
|        APPLICATION LAYER                  |
+-------------------------------------------+
|  Communication | Naming | Time | Resource |
|  ----------- Real-Time Middleware ---------|
|   QoS Management & Enforcement Layer       |
+-------------------------------------------+
|   RTOS (Scheduler, IPC) | Drivers         |
+-------------------------------------------+
|   Network / Heterogeneous Hardware        |
+-------------------------------------------+
```

**Four Service Categories:**

1. **Communication Services** — RT-RPC, publish/subscribe (DDS), message queues. Provide deterministic data exchange.

2. **Naming and Trading Services** — Map logical object names to physical references; allow attribute-based lookup ("find a camera with < 2 ms latency").

3. **Time and Clock Services** — Provide globally synchronized clocks using protocols like **IEEE 1588 PTP** and offer **timer/deadline** services.

4. **Resource Management Services** — Reserve CPU (Processor Set Service), memory (Memory Pool Service), and network bandwidth for tasks, ensuring their QoS contracts are met.

5. *(Optional 5th)* **Fault Tolerance Services** — Replication, voting, and watchdog monitoring for high availability.

**Real-world example:** **AUTOSAR RTE** (Run-Time Environment) in automotive ECUs implements all four categories, supporting hard real-time control over CAN and FlexRay.

---

**Part (b) — QoS, Priority & Deadline Propagation (7 Marks)**

**QoS Definition:**
Quality of Service (QoS) is a *set of measurable, enforceable performance parameters* — such as **deadline, jitter, priority, throughput, reliability** — that an application specifies and the RT-MW guarantees through **resource reservation and scheduling**.

**Distributed Priority Inversion (DPI):**
Occurs when a **high-priority** client task on Node A calls a server on Node B, but the server is **blocked** by a low-priority task holding a shared resource. The client effectively waits for the low-priority task — its priority is *inverted* across the network.

**Priority Propagation Solution:**
The middleware tags each request with the **client's priority**. The server-side scheduler *inherits* the highest client priority currently waiting. This is **end-to-end priority inheritance**, the same algorithm used inside an RTOS but extended across nodes.

**Deadline Propagation:**
Each request carries a **deadline** (absolute or relative). The server computes a *latest start time* and rejects the call (or escalates priority) if its local queueing would cause a miss. In **RT-CORBA**, the `PriorityModelPolicy` and `TimingPolicy` enforce this.

**Mechanism in code (sketch):**
```c
// Pseudo-code (middleware internals)
request_t req;
req.client_priority = client_task->priority;  // propagated
req.deadline        = current_time + client_task->deadline;
enqueue_server_priority_queue(req);
```

**Benefit:** Hard deadlines are *enforced* rather than *hoped for*, satisfying hard real-time constraints in distributed systems.

---

#### ❓ Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Discuss the **Naming and Trading Services** of RT-Middleware with a suitable example scenario. Compare **client-propagated** vs **server-declared** priority models. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Derive the **end-to-end latency** $L_{e2e}$ for a real-time RPC passing through an RT-Middleware. Compute $L_{e2e}$ for the following data: $C_s = 100 \,\mu\text{s}$, $S = 256$ bits, $B = 10\,\text{Mbps}$, $d = 5$ m, $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s, $L_{marsh} = L_{demarsh} = 5 \,\mu\text{s}$, $L_{client} = L_{return} = 3 \,\mu\text{s}$, and $R_s^{BCCT} = 120 \,\mu\text{s}$. (7 Marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Naming, Trading, and Priority Models (7 Marks)**

**Naming Service:**
Maps **logical names** → **IORs (Interoperable Object References)**. Example: a robot's controller task refers to the lidar by the name `"LIDAR_FRONT"`, not its IP address. The naming service resolves it at runtime, allowing re-binding when the IP changes.

**Trading Service:**
Extends naming with **attribute-based lookup**. Example: a navigation subsystem queries:
> *"Find a camera with resolution > 2 MP, latency < 5 ms, located in the front bumper."*

The trader matches attributes and returns the best object reference. This is a **Quality of Service (QoS)**-aware service — it is rare in conventional middleware but essential in RT-MW.

**Priority Models (RT-CORBA):**

| Feature | Client-Propagated | Server-Declared |
|---|---|---|
| Source of priority | From the calling client task | Set by server-side configuration |
| Adaptability | Dynamic, follows call site | Static, fixed per object |
| Use case | Distributed control loops | Dedicated real-time services |
| Pros | Honors client's urgency | Predictable server load |
| Cons | Susceptible to priority inversion | Cannot adapt to client urgency |

In safety-critical systems, **client-propagated** is preferred (e.g., vehicle brake-by-wire calling the ABS controller with the highest priority).

**Example Scenario — UAV Swarm:**
- A **UAV leader** issues a "re-form swarm" command with priority 250 (high).
- The MW propagates priority 250 to every wingman's flight controller.
- A low-priority telemetry task on the wingman is preempted.
- The maneuver completes within 10 ms — within deadline.

---

**Part (b) — Derivation & Numerical Computation (7 Marks)**

**Derivation (recap from Section 3.2):**
$$
L_{e2e} = L_{client} + L_{marsh} + \frac{S}{B} + \frac{d}{v} + R_s^{BCCT} + B_s + L_{demarsh} + C_s + L_{return}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute network transmission time.**

$$
L_{net,transmit} = \frac{S}{B} = \frac{256 \text{ bits}}{10 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 25.6 \,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute propagation time.**

$$
L_{net,prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{5}{2 \times 10^8} = 0.025 \,\mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all components.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{e2e} &= 3 + 5 + 25.6 + 0.025 + 120 + 0 + 5 + 100 + 3 \\
&= 261.625 \,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $L_{e2e} \approx 261.6 \,\mu\text{s}$.

**Interpretation:** The dominant terms are $R_s^{BCCT}$ (120 µs — queuing + scheduling) and $C_s$ (100 µs — execution). The network contributes only ~25 µs. To meet a tight deadline, the designer should optimize **server-side scheduling** (e.g., dedicated Processor Set) rather than upgrading the link.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Loss Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing Naming vs Trading** — Naming maps *name → reference*; Trading uses *attribute-based matching*. Examiners deduct **1 mark** if the distinction is missing.
> 2. **Forgetting $B_s$ (blocking term)** in latency derivations — losing **2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion** in latency: $S/B$ must yield **seconds**, then convert to ms/µs. Examiner strictly checks units.
> 4. **Mixing RM bound with EDF** — RM bound is $n(2^{1/n}-1)$; EDF is $1.0$. Examiners consider this a **2-mark** error.
> 5. **Skipping diagram in Part A(a)** — KTU mandates at least one labeled block diagram; absent = **1.5 marks** lost.
> 6. **Not stating "worst-case"** for execution time / latency — always write **WCET**, not just "execution time".

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — pin this before the exam.**

- **Middleware** = software layer between application and OS/hardware providing standardized, transparent services.
- **Real-Time Middleware** = middleware with **deterministic, time-bounded** semantics; enforced via **QoS contracts**.
- **Four core service categories:** Communication, Naming/Trading, Time, Resource Management (plus QoS & Fault Tolerance as cross-cutting).
- **Real-Time RPC** adds priority, deadline, and bounded latency to conventional RPC.
- **Publish/Subscribe (DDS)** is preferred for fan-out, asynchronous sensor streams.
- **Naming Service** = name → reference mapping (DNS-like).
- **Trading Service** = attribute-based object lookup (QoS-aware).
- **Time Service** = global clock synchronization (IEEE 1588 PTP, sub-µs).
- **Resource Management** = Processor Set, Memory Pool, Network Bandwidth reservation.
- **Distributed Priority Inversion** solved by **end-to-end priority inheritance** propagated by the MW.
- **End-to-end Latency formula:**
$$
L_{e2e} = L_{client} + L_{marsh} + \tfrac{S}{B} + \tfrac{d}{v} + R_s^{BCCT} + B_s + L_{demarsh} + C_s + L_{return}
$$
- **Response-Time Analysis:** $W^{n+1} = C + \sum \lceil W^n/T \rceil C$, terminates if $W > D$.
- **RM schedulability bound:** $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1) \le \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- **Jitter $J$** is the variability in release/response time; must be bounded for periodic tasks.
- **Standards to remember:** **OMG RT-CORBA, DDS, AUTOSAR RTE, ARINC 653, RTSJ, POSIX 1003.21, IEEE 1588 PTP**.
- **Use client-propagated priority** for dynamic distributed control; **server-declared** for fixed services.
- **Always quote WCET**, not average or best-case execution time.
- **Draw a labeled block diagram** in every Part-A answer (at least 1.5 marks reserved).
- **Final tip:** KTU loves the *distributed priority inversion* problem; expect it as a 7-marker.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies and Applications: Automotive Systems
Aerospace and Defense
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Aerospace and Defense # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aerospace & Defense RTOS (Real-Time Operating System)** refers to a class of safety-critical, deterministic, hard real-time operating systems engineered to host flight control, navigation, mission management, avionics, missile guidance, and unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) workloads where the **correctness of the system depends not only on logical results but also on the time at which those results are produced**. These RTOS implementations are bound by certification standards such as **DO-178C** (software), **ARINC 653** (avionics partitioning), and **MIL-STD-1553** (defense databus). In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an **Aerospace & Defense RTOS** is a **Hard Real-Time System** in which missing a deadline is treated as a **catastrophic system failure** because it can directly result in loss of life, mission failure, or asset destruction. Examples include the flight control computer of a fighter aircraft (e.g., **F-35 Lightning II**), the inertial navigation unit of a cruise missile, and the autopilot of a satellite launch vehicle. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Fighter Pilot's Co-Pilot > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The "Iron Co-Pilot"** > Imagine a fighter pilot flying a combat mission at **Mach 1.5** through enemy territory. The pilot cannot manually toggle 600 switches per second while dodging missiles. The **Aerospace RTOS acts like an invisible Iron Co-Pilot** — an absolutely reliable assistant that: > - Reads the radar **every 10 milliseconds** without ever being late. > - Fires the counter-measure flare **exactly when** the heat-seeking missile is 1.2 km away (no sooner, no later). > - Never "hangs" or "blue-screens" — even if a software bug exists, the system must degrade gracefully. > - If one processor dies, another identical processor takes over in **microseconds**. Just as a human co-pilot must respond within strict timing windows (or the plane crashes), an Aerospace RTOS must guarantee **bounded response times** to every stimulus — this is the essence of **determinism**. ## 1.3 Why RTOS, Not GPOS (General Purpose OS)? | Property | GPOS (Windows/Linux) | Aerospace & Defense RTOS (VxWorks 653, INTEGRITY-178B, LynxOS-178) | |---|---|---| | Primary Goal | Throughput & User Experience | **Determinism & Predictability** | | Latency | Variable (μs to seconds) | **Bounded (μs)** | | Deadline Miss | Application bug | **System-level catastrophic failure** | | Certification | None | **DO-178C Level A**, **EAL 6+** | | Scheduler | Fair-share / CFS | **Fixed-priority pre-emptive / Time-partitioned** | | Memory | Virtual paging allowed | **Spatial & Temporal partitioning** mandatory | ## 1.4 Mandatory Design Pillars (KTU 2024 Highlight) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Five Non-Negotiable Pillars of an Aerospace & Defense RTOS:** > 1. **Hard Real-Time Guarantee** — Worst-case execution time (WCET) is provable. > 2. **Fault Tolerance** — Continued operation despite hardware/software faults (Triple Modular Redundancy). > 3. **Spatial Isolation** — One partition cannot corrupt another's memory (ARINC 653). > 4. **Temporal Isolation** — One partition cannot starve another's CPU time. > 5. **Certifiability** — Code, design, and testing traceable from requirements to test cases (DO-178C). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hard Real-Time Constraint A task $\tau_i$ in an aerospace system is characterized by the **4-tuple**: $$\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i, J_i)$$ where: - $C_i$ = Worst-Case Execution Time (**WCET**) - $T_i$ = Period of arrival - $D_i$ = Relative deadline - $J_i$ = Release jitter (e.g., from sensor sampling delay) The **absolute deadline** must be respected: $r_i + D_i$, where $r_i$ is the release time. ## 2.2 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — The Industry Standard In aerospace flight control, **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is the most widely deployed fixed-priority algorithm (proved optimal by **Liu & Layland, 1973**). > **RMS Rule:** *Shorter period → Higher priority.* If $T_i < T_j$, then $P_i > P_j$. ### 2.2.1 RMS Utilization Bound (Liu-Layland Theorem) For $n$ independent, periodic tasks scheduled under RMS on a single processor: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ As $n \to \infty$, the bound asymptotically approaches $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$. | n | Bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | |---|---| | 1 | **1.000** | | 2 | **0.828** | | 3 | **0.780** | | 4 | **0.757** | | 5 | **0.743** | | $\infty$ | **0.693** | > [!NOTE] > If a flight control computer has **3 critical tasks** (radar tracking, control surface actuation, navigation), the total CPU utilization must be $\leq 78\%$. In aerospace practice, designers target **$\leq 70\%$** to leave headroom for interrupts and error handlers. ## 2.3 Response Time Analysis (RTA) — The Board-Exam Favourite The **worst-case response time** $R_i$ of task $\tau_i$ is the smallest fixed point of: $$R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ where $hp(i)$ denotes the set of tasks with **higher priority** than $\tau_i$. **Termination Rule:** Iterate until $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ (converged) OR $R_i^{(k+1)} > D_i$ (deadline missed → unschedulable). ## 2.4 Fault Tolerance via Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) > [!IMPORTANT] > Aerospace flight control computers (e.g., **F-22 Raptor** FADEC, **SpaceX Falcon 9** avionics) use **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** with majority voting. The **system reliability** of TMR with identical channels each having reliability $R$: $$R_{TMR} = R^3 + 3R^2(1-R) = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$ For a single channel $R = 0.999$ (typical MIL-spec component): $$R_{TMR} = 3(0.999)^2 - 2(0.999)^3 = 0.999998$$ ### 2.4.1 Reliability Block Diagram Formulae | Configuration | Reliability Equation | |---|---| | **Series (all must work)** | $R_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | | **Parallel (k-out-of-n)** | $R_{sys} = \sum_{i=k}^{n} \binom{n}{i} R^i (1-R)^{n-i}$ | | **TMR (1-out-of-3)** | $R_{sys} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ | | **Standby Sparing** | $R_{sys} = R_{primary} + (1-R_{primary}) \cdot R_{switch} \cdot R_{backup}$ | ## 2.5 ARINC 653 — The Avionics Partitioning Standard > [!IMPORTANT] > **ARINC 653** (Avionics Application Standard Software Interface) defines a **time and space partitioned** architecture used in Boeing 787, Airbus A350, and most modern military aircraft. ### 2.5.1 Spatial Partitioning Each partition $P_k$ is allocated a fixed, non-overlapping memory region. Hardware **Memory Management Units (MMUs)** enforce isolation — a fault in the In-Flight Entertainment partition **cannot crash** the autopilot partition. ### 2.5.2 Temporal Partitioning A **Major Time Frame (MTF)** of duration $T_{MTF}$ is divided into fixed **Minor Frames (MFs)**. Each partition $P_k$ receives a pre-determined **scheduling window** within each MF. $$\sum_{k=1}^{N_{part}} W_k \leq T_{MTF}$$ where $W_k$ is the execution window granted to partition $k$. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Aerospace & Defense RTOS | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Task model | $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i, J_i)$ | ms typically | | 2 | CPU Utilization | $U = \sum C_i / T_i$ | Dimensionless, $\leq 1$ | | 3 | RMS Util. Bound | $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | Optimal for fixed-priority | | 4 | Response Time (RTA) | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$ | Iterative fixed-point | | 5 | TMR Reliability | $R_{TMR} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ | Majority voting | | 6 | Series Reliability | $R_s = \prod R_i$ | All channels must work | | 7 | Parallel Reliability | $R_p = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$ | Any one must work | | 8 | MTBF | $MTBF = 1/\lambda$ | $\lambda$ = failure rate (failures/hr) | | 9 | Availability | $A = MTBF/(MTBF + MTTR)$ | Dimensionless, $\to 1$ | | 10 | Minor Frame Budget | $\sum W_k \leq T_{MTF}$ | ARINC 653 constraint | | 11 | Deadline Test | $R_i \leq D_i$ | Schedulability criterion | | 12 | Jitter Bound | $J_i \leq D_i - C_i$ | For periodic sensors | ## 2.7 Real-World Aerospace & Defense Applications > [!NOTE] > **Three Canonical Deployment Scenarios:** > 1. **Flight Control Computer (FCC)** — Runs at **80 Hz** (12.5 ms loop), uses TMR + RMS, certified to DO-178C Level A. > 2. **Missile Guidance Seeker** — Hard deadline of **1 ms** for target tracking update; uses lock-step dual-redundant processors. > 3. **UAV Autopilot (e.g., MQ-9 Reaper)** — Mix of hard real-time (control loop @ 100 Hz) and soft real-time (telemetry link @ 1 Hz) tasks. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Liu-Layland RMS Utilization Bound We shall prove that for $n$ periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling, the **least upper bound** on achievable utilization is: $$U_n = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ ### Step 1: Define the Critical Instant A **critical instant** for task $\tau_i$ is a release time where all higher-priority tasks $\tau_j$ are released simultaneously with $\tau_i$. This produces the **maximum response time** for $\tau_i$. ### Step 2: Demand Function Let $W_i(t)$ be the worst-case processor demand on task $\tau_i$ over interval $[0, t]$: $$W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j$$ ### Step 3: Schedulability Condition Task $\tau_i$ meets its deadline if and only if there exists some $t \in [0, D_i]$ such that: $$W_i(t) \leq t$$ The minimum $t$ satisfying this is $R_i$ (the response time). ### Step 4: Worst-Case Task Set Construction Liu & Layland construct the worst-case task set achieving the bound. Consider $n$ tasks where the ratio $C_i/T_i$ is chosen as: $$\frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{T_{i-1}}{2 T_i} \quad \text{for } i = 2, 3, \ldots, n$$ with $C_1 = T_1$ (task 1 saturates the CPU completely at $t = T_1$). ### Step 5: Recurrence Setup For this critical instant, task $\tau_i$ completes at: $$R_i = T_i = \sum_{k=1}^{i} C_k \quad \text{... (worst case)}$$ $$T_i = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$ ### Step 6: Simplification For the critical scenario where $T_j = 2 T_{j+1}$ (each task has double the period of the next): $$\left\lceil \frac{T_i}{T_j} \right\rceil = 2^{i-j}$$ Substituting: $$T_i = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} 2^{i-j} C_j = C_i + 2^{i-1} C_1 + 2^{i-2} C_2 + \cdots + 2 C_{i-1}$$ ### Step 7: Telescoping Sum Let $u_k = C_k / T_k$. From Step 4, $T_k = 2 T_{k+1}$, hence: $$\frac{C_k}{T_k} = \frac{C_k}{2 T_{k+1}} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{C_k}{T_{k+1}}$$ This gives the recurrence $u_k = u_1 / 2^{k-1}$. ### Step 8: Sum of Utilizations Total utilization of the $n$ tasks: $$U_n = \sum_{k=1}^{n} u_k = u_1 \left( 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \cdots + \frac{1}{2^{n-1}} \right)$$ $$U_n = u_1 \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^n}{1 - 1/2} = 2 u_1 \left( 1 - 2^{-n} \right)$$ ### Step 9: Solve for $u_1$ From Step 4, $u_1 = C_1/T_1 = 1$ (saturated case). But for the **minimum** $u_1$ that still allows the task set to be schedulable: $$u_1 = \frac{T_1}{T_1 + T_2 + \cdots}$$ Substituting back: $$\boxed{U_n = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)}$$ ### Step 10: Limiting Behaviour Taking the limit: $$\lim_{n \to \infty} U_n = \lim_{n \to \infty} n \left( e^{(\ln 2)/n} - 1 \right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\ln 2}{1} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Response Time Analysis (RTA) for Flight Control Tasks A military UAV has **3 hard real-time tasks** with parameters: | Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) | Purpose | |---|---|---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ (Stabilizer) | 1 | 5 | 5 | Roll/Pitch control | | $\tau_2$ (Navigator) | 2 | 10 | 10 | GPS update | | $\tau_3$ (Telemetry) | 3 | 20 | 20 | Ground link | **Verify schedulability** using RTA. Priority: $\tau_1 > \tau_2 > \tau_3$. ### Step 1: Response Time of $\tau_1$ (highest priority) $$R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 1 \text{ ms}$$ Converged in 1 iteration: $R_1 = 1$ ms $\leq D_1 = 5$ ms ✓ ### Step 2: Response Time of $\tau_2$ $$R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 2 \text{ ms}$$ $$R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{R_2^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ ms}$$ $$R_2^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ ms}$$ **Converged:** $R_2 = 3$ ms $\leq D_2 = 10$ ms ✓ ### Step 3: Response Time of $\tau_3$ $$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3 \text{ ms}$$ $$R_3^{(1)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}$$ $$R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$ $$R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 2 + 2 = 7 \text{ ms}$$ **Converged:** $R_3 = 7$ ms $\leq D_3 = 20$ ms ✓ ### Step 4: Final Utilization Check $$U = \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{20} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 0.55$$ $$U_{bound}(n=3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7798$$ Since $0.55 \leq 0.7798$, the task set is **schedulable under RMS**. ✓ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: TMR Reliability for Satellite On-Board Computer A satellite's attitude control computer uses **Triple Modular Redundancy**. Each channel has a constant failure rate $\lambda = 10^{-5}$ per hour. Compute reliability over a **1000-hour mission**. ### Step 1: Per-Channel Reliability (Exponential Model) $$R = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-10^{-5} \times 1000} = e^{-0.01} \approx 0.99005$$ ### Step 2: TMR System Reliability $$R_{TMR} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$ $$R_{TMR} = 3(0.99005)^2 - 2(0.99005)^3$$ $$= 3(0.98020) - 2(0.97045)$$ $$= 2.94060 - 1.94090 = 0.99970$$ ### Step 3: Interpretation Probability of survival over 1000 hours ≈ **99.97%**, versus **99.00%** for a single channel. The TMR delivers a **30× improvement** in unreliability probability — critical for an unmanned satellite that cannot be serviced. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Problem Type | Governing Equation | Convergence Criterion | Aerospace Interpretation | |---|---|---|---| | Schedulability Test | $R_i \leq D_i$ | RTA fixed-point iteration | "Will the autopilot miss its deadline?" | | Utilization Bound | $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Bound exists for all $n$ | "Does CPU have headroom?" | | TMR Reliability | $R_{TMR} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$ | Always $< 1$ for $R < 1$ | "Can we survive one channel failure?" | | ARINC 653 Partitioning | $\sum W_k \leq T_{MTF}$ | Static schedule table | "Are all partitions getting their slot?" | | MTBF / Availability | $A = MTBF/(MTBF+MTTR)$ | $0 \leq A \leq 1$ | "What fraction of mission time is system operational?" | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Aerospace RTOS Layered Architecture (ARINC 653 Compliant) ```mermaid flowchart TB HWLAYER["HARDWARE LAYER
Triple Modular Redundant CPUs
Memory Management Unit MMU
MIL STD 1553 Databus"] BSP["Board Support Package BSP
Drivers and Interrupt Controllers"] HYP["ARINC 653 Hypervisor
Partition Scheduler Time and Space"] P1["Partition 1 Flight Control
Hard Real Time 50 Hz"] P2["Partition 2 Navigation
Hard Real Time 20 Hz"] P3["Partition 3 Display
Soft Real Time 10 Hz"] P4["Partition 4 Maintenance
Non Critical Background"] APPS["Avionics Applications
APEX API Interface"] HWLAYER --> BSP BSP --> HYP HYP --> P1 HYP --> P2 HYP --> P3 HYP --> P4 P1 --> APPS P2 --> APPS P3 --> APPS P4 --> APPS ``` ## 4.2 Triple Modular Redundancy with Majority Voter ```mermaid flowchart LR SENSOR["Flight Sensor Input
Inertial Measurement Unit"] CPUA["Processor Channel A
Channel 1"] CPUB["Processor Channel B
Channel 2"] CPUC["Processor Channel C
Channel 3"] VOTER["Majority Voter
2 out of 3 Logic"] ACTUATOR["Control Surface Actuator
Elevator and Aileron"] SENSOR --> CPUA SENSOR --> CPUB SENSOR --> CPUC CPUA --> VOTER CPUB --> VOTER CPUC --> VOTER VOTER --> ACTUATOR ``` ## 4.3 ARINC 653 Major Time Frame Schedule (Gantt-like Logical Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR MTF["Major Time Frame MTF Duration 80 ms"] MF1["Minor Frame 1
0 to 20 ms
Partition Flight"] MF2["Minor Frame 2
20 to 40 ms
Partition Nav"] MF3["Minor Frame 3
40 to 60 ms
Partition Flight"] MF4["Minor Frame 4
60 to 80 ms
Partition Display"] MTF --> MF1 MTF --> MF2 MTF --> MF3 MTF --> MF4 ``` ## 4.4 Fault-Tolerant Task Scheduling Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Task Released by Interrupt or Timer Tick"] PRIOCHK["Priority Lookup in Active Partition Table"] PREEMPT["Higher Priority Task Running"] QUEUE["Place Task in Ready Queue"] SCHED["Fixed Priority Preemptive Scheduler"] EXEC["Execute Task on Allocated CPU Core"] WATCH["Watchdog Timer Started"] COMPLETE["Task Completion or Deadline"] WDCHK["Watchdog Triggered"] RESET["Activate Backup Partition or Switch Processor"] START --> PRIOCHK PREEMPT --> QUEUE PRIOCHK --> SCHED QUEUE --> SCHED SCHED --> EXEC EXEC --> WATCH WATCH --> COMPLETE WATCH --> WDCHK WDCHK --> RESET ``` ## 4.5 Defense System Multilevel Security Architecture (MILS) ```mermaid flowchart TB TOP["TOP SECRET Level
Mission Command and Targeting"] SEC["SECRET Level
Tactical Communications"] UNCLAS["UNCLASSIFIED Level
Telemetry and Health"] SEP["Separation Kernel
MILS Foundation"] HWSEP["Hardware Enforced MMU Isolation"] TOP --> SEP SEC --> SEP UNCLAS --> SEP SEP --> HWSEP ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between Hard Real-Time Systems and Soft Real-Time Systems. Why is an Aerospace Flight Control System classified as a Hard Real-Time System?** **Model Answer:** A **Hard Real-Time System** is one in which missing a deadline constitutes a **total system failure** (possibly catastrophic). A **Soft Real-Time System** allows occasional deadline misses with only degraded quality of service. | Property | Hard Real-Time (Aerospace FCC) | Soft Real-Time (Multimedia) | |---|---|---| | Deadline Miss | Catastrophic | Acceptable (quality drops) | | Example | F-35 Autopilot, Missile Seeker | Video streaming, Audio playback | | Certification | DO-178C Level A | None | | Response Bound | **Provable upper bound** | Statistical / best-effort | A flight control computer running at 80 Hz (12.5 ms loop) must respond deterministically — a single missed deadline could destabilize the aircraft. Therefore, it is classified as **hard real-time**. ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Q: Explain the concept of Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR) used in aerospace RTOS. State the formula for its reliability.** **Model Answer:** **TMR** is a fault-tolerance technique in which **three identical processor channels** execute the same task in lock-step. Their outputs are fed to a **majority voter** that selects the 2-out-of-3 agreement, masking any single-channel fault. $$R_{TMR} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$ where $R$ is the per-channel reliability. For $R = 0.999$, $R_{TMR} \approx 0.999998$, demonstrating the dramatic reliability improvement needed in aerospace applications such as the Space Shuttle's primary avionics and modern fly-by-wire systems. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE of Question A or Question B.** --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]` **Q: Aerospace & Defense RTOS demand strict determinism and fault tolerance.** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **ARINC 653 time and space partitioning architecture**. Discuss how spatial and temporal isolation guarantee fault containment. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Liu-Layland utilization bound** for Rate Monotonic Scheduling. For an aircraft attitude control system with 4 tasks having parameters shown below, verify schedulability using RMS. **(7 Marks)** | Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | |---|---|---| | $\tau_1$ | 2 | 10 | | $\tau_2$ | 3 | 20 | | $\tau_3$ | 5 | 40 | | $\tau_4$ | 6 | 80 | #### Model Solution to Part (a) — ARINC 653 Architecture **Definition:** ARINC 653 is an industry standard for avionics software that defines a **partitioned operating system architecture** where multiple independent avionics functions share one processor with strict isolation. **Spatial Isolation:** - The processor's **Memory Management Unit (MMU)** is configured to give each partition $P_k$ a unique, non-overlapping virtual address space. - Any attempt by partition $P_k$ to access memory outside its range triggers a **memory fault** and is contained — the partition is restarted without affecting siblings. **Temporal Isolation:** - The **Major Time Frame (MTF)** is divided into fixed Minor Frames. - Each partition receives a **deterministic time window** in each MTF cycle. - A partition is **strictly forbidden** from executing beyond its window, ensuring no partition can starve another. **Benefit:** This allows **Mixed Criticality** — a Level A (catastrophic) flight control partition can co-exist on the same hardware as a Level C (minor failure) in-flight entertainment partition without cross-contamination. *(Diagram: refer to SECTION 4.1 — Layered Architecture)* #### Model Solution to Part (b) — Liu-Layland Bound & RMS Verification **Liu-Layland Theorem:** For $n$ independent periodic tasks under RMS, a sufficient condition for schedulability is: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$ **Derivation Sketch:** The proof constructs the *worst-case* task set using the recurrence $T_i = 2 T_{i+1}$ (each task's period is double the next), where the critical instant aligns all task releases. The telescoping sum of utilizations yields $U_n = 2u_1(1 - 2^{-n})$. Setting $u_1 = 1$ (saturated highest-priority task) and taking the ratio of geometric sums gives the bound. As $n \to \infty$, $U_n \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$. **Application to Aircraft Attitude Control (4 tasks):** $$U = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{20} + \frac{5}{40} + \frac{6}{80}$$ $$U = 0.200 + 0.150 + 0.125 + 0.075 = 0.550$$ **Liu-Layland Bound for $n = 4$:** $$U_{bound} = 4 \left( 2^{1/4} - 1 \right) = 4 \left( 1.1892 - 1 \right) = 4 \times 0.1892 = 0.7569$$ **Comparison:** $U = 0.550 < U_{bound} = 0.7569$ ✓ **Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling**. There is approximately **20.7%** CPU headroom available for non-critical tasks, error handlers, and interrupts. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]` **Q:** **(a)** Describe the **Triple Modular Redundancy (TMR)** fault-tolerance architecture used in defense avionics. Compute the system reliability for 1000 hours if each channel has failure rate $\lambda = 10^{-4}$ per hour. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **Reliability Block Diagram (RBD)** of a defense guidance system having a primary processor (R=0.99), a backup processor (R=0.98), and a switch (R=0.999). Compare with a purely series configuration. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Part (a) — TMR Reliability Computation **Architecture:** Three identical processor channels (Channel A, B, C) execute the same instructions in **lock-step** (synchronized by a global clock). Their outputs are compared in a **majority voter** that selects the 2-out-of-3 result. **Per-Channel Reliability over $t = 1000$ hours:** $$R = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-10^{-4} \times 1000} = e^{-0.1} = 0.9048$$ **System Reliability (TMR):** $$R_{TMR} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$$ $$= 3(0.9048)^2 - 2(0.9048)^3$$ $$= 3(0.8187) - 2(0.7408)$$ $$= 2.4560 - 1.4816 = 0.9744$$ **Comparison:** - Single channel: $R = 0.9048$ (90.48%) - TMR system: $R_{TMR} = 0.9744$ (97.44%) - **Unreliability reduction:** From $9.52 \times 10^{-2}$ to $2.56 \times 10^{-2}$, a **3.72× improvement**. This is the architectural pattern used in the **F-22 Raptor** Flight Control System and **SpaceX Crew Dragon** abort computers. #### Model Solution to Part (b) — Reliability Block Diagram **Series Configuration (All components must work):** $$R_{series} = R_p \times R_s = 0.99 \times 0.999 = 0.98901$$ But this includes no redundancy — any single failure kills the mission. **Standby Sparing (Primary + Switch + Backup):** $$R_{standby} = R_p + (1 - R_p) \cdot R_{switch} \cdot R_b$$ $$= 0.99 + (1 - 0.99)(0.999)(0.98)$$ $$= 0.99 + (0.01)(0.999)(0.98)$$ $$= 0.99 + 0.00979 = 0.99979$$ **Comparison Table:** | Configuration | Reliability | Improvement | |---|---|---| | Series (no redundancy) | 0.98901 | Baseline | | Standby Sparing | 0.99979 | **1.09% absolute / 10.4× unreliability reduction** | **Block Diagram (described):** ``` Primary Processor [R=0.99] ---> Output | | (failure sensed) v Switch [R=0.999] ---> Backup Processor [R=0.98] ---> Output ``` **Conclusion:** Even with imperfect switch (0.999), standby sparing outperforms the series configuration by an order of magnitude, justifying its use in defense guidance systems where mission success is paramount. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ with $0.693$:** The bound is a *function of $n$*. Students who write $U \leq 0.693$ for any task set lose 1 mark. Always compute the bound for the *given* $n$. > 2. **Forgetting the iteration in RTA:** Response time $R_i$ requires *iteration* until convergence. Students who compute $R_i$ in a single step lose 2 marks. > 3. **Confusing RMS with EDF:** EDF (Earliest Deadline First) can achieve 100% utilization, but RMS is bounded by $n(2^{1/n}-1)$. They are **not** equivalent. > 4. **TMR formula: $R^3 + 3R^2(1-R)$ vs $3R^2 - 2R^3$** — Both are correct but the latter is algebraically simplified; either form is accepted. > 5. **Drawing ARINC 653 without showing partitioning** — merely listing features without a *labelled* spatial/temporal diagram loses up to 3 marks. > 6. **Wrong units:** Period and WCET must be in *consistent* units (ms or μs, not mixed). --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Aerospace & Defense RTOS"** > > ✅ **1. Definition** — Aerospace RTOS is a **hard real-time, safety-critical, fault-tolerant** OS certified to **DO-178C** and conforming to **ARINC 653**. > > ✅ **2. Hard vs Soft Real-Time** — Hard = deadline miss = catastrophic failure; Soft = degraded quality tolerated. > > ✅ **3. Task Model** — $\tau_i = (C_i, T_i, D_i, J_i)$. WCET $C_i$ is the **most critical parameter**. > > ✅ **4. RMS Priority Rule** — Shorter period → Higher priority (proven optimal for fixed-priority). > > ✅ **5. Liu-Layland Bound** — $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$. Limits: $U_1=1.0$, $U_2=0.828$, $U_\infty = 0.693$. > > ✅ **6. RTA Iteration** — Solve $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i/T_j \rceil C_j$ iteratively until convergence. > > ✅ **7. TMR Formula** — $R_{TMR} = 3R^2 - 2R^3$. Requires majority voting of 3 channels. > > ✅ **8. Series Reliability** — $R_s = \prod R_i$; **Parallel Reliability** — $R_p = 1 - \prod (1-R_i)$. > > ✅ **9. ARINC 653** — Time + Space partitioning. MTF divided into Minor Frames; MMU enforces memory isolation. > > ✅ **10. Standards Trifecta** — DO-178C (software), ARINC 653 (interface), MIL-STD-1553 (databus). > > ✅ **11. Mixed Criticality** — A single FCC can host Level A (catastrophic) and Level D (minor) partitions safely. > > ✅ **12. Aerospace Examples** — F-35 FCC, MQ-9 Reaper autopilot, PSLV/GSLV launch vehicle avionics, Tejas Mk2 flight controls. > > ✅ **13. Common Mistake** — DO-178C has **Levels A-E**; flight control is Level A (most stringent). > > ✅ **14. Board Exam Heuristic** — If a numerical problem gives $n$ tasks, ALWAYS compute the bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ AND verify via RTA; show both for full marks.

topics.title: Memory Management in RTOS: Implementing Dynamic Memory Allocation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Memory Management in RTOS: Implementing Dynamic Memory Allocation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Memory Management in an RTOS** is the deterministic and bounded-time process of controlling and coordinating computer memory resources to satisfy the strict temporal constraints of real-time tasks. Unlike General-Purpose Operating Systems (GPOS), an RTOS must guarantee that every memory operation — allocation, deallocation, and access — completes within a **predictable, worst-case bounded execution time (WCET)**, ensuring that no task ever exceeds its deadline due to memory-related delays.

**Dynamic Memory Allocation (DMA)** specifically refers to the mechanism by which memory blocks of variable or fixed size are assigned to tasks at *runtime*, from a pre-reserved heap region, as opposed to being statically bound at compile time.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The core challenge in RTOS memory management is achieving *determinism* and *bounded latency* while simultaneously minimizing **fragmentation** (both internal and external). Traditional `malloc()`/`free()` from GPOS are considered **non-deterministic** and are therefore unsafe for hard real-time systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a large **parking lot at a busy airport**:
- The parking lot represents the **heap memory**.
- Cars (tasks) arrive needing varying-sized parking spots (memory blocks).
- A **traffic marshal** (the memory manager) directs each car to a space.
- In a *non-real-time* mall parking, the marshal may take ages to find the perfect spot (this is fine — no one has a strict deadline).
- In an **airport** (RTOS), the marshal MUST park a VIP's plane within an *absolute guaranteed time* (deadline). The marshal cannot wander around looking for a "best" spot — he needs a **fixed, fast, pre-planned strategy** (e.g., slots reserved by size, buddy system, memory pools).

This is precisely why RTOS Dynamic Memory Allocation is engineered with predictability as the primary design goal, often at the cost of peak utilization efficiency.

## 1.3 Core Real-Time Metrics

The following physical and logical constants govern RTOS memory design:

- **Fragmentation Ratio** (target: as close to **0** as possible)
- **Allocation Latency (WCET)**: Must be bounded — **O(1)** is the gold standard.
- **Heap Size (H)**: Total bytes reserved for dynamic allocation.
- **Block Size (B)**: Power of 2 in buddy systems, variable in segregated lists.
- **Determinism Bound (D)**: A formal upper limit on allocation time.
- **Memory Utilization (U)**: The fraction of heap currently in productive use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between *predictability* (RTOS requirement) versus *throughput* (GPOS requirement). Always emphasize that in RTOS, **"worst-case"** behavior matters more than **"average-case"** behavior.

## 1.4 Why Dynamic Allocation in RTOS at All?

Although static allocation is the safest choice in hard real-time, dynamic allocation is necessary for:
- Variable-length data structures (e.g., dynamically sized messages in inter-task communication).
- Systems where the maximum memory requirement cannot be determined at design time.
- Adaptive/evolving firmware where features are added after deployment.
- Object-oriented embedded designs (C++/embedded C++ with `new`/`delete`).

The implementation must therefore be **deterministic**, **fragmentation-resistant**, and **overhead-aware**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architecture of an RTOS Memory Manager

A real-time dynamic memory allocator consists of four interacting subsystems:

1. **Heap Initialization Subsystem** — Carves the heap into a known initial state at boot.
2. **Allocation Engine** — Implements the chosen search strategy (First-Fit, Best-Fit, Buddy, TLSF, Pool).
3. **Free-List Maintenance Subsystem** — Tracks available blocks, performs splitting and coalescing.
4. **Determinism Guard / WCET Profiler** — Enforces upper bounds via algorithms with known complexity.

## 2.2 Fundamental Allocation Strategies (Operational Walkthrough)

### Strategy A: First-Fit
- **Logic**: Scan the free list from the beginning; return the *first* block large enough.
- **Pros**: Fast (early exit), simple.
- **Cons**: Suffers from **external fragmentation** at the low-address end of the heap ("splintering").
- **Determinism**: O(N) worst-case where N = number of free blocks.

### Strategy B: Best-Fit
- **Logic**: Scan *entire* free list; return the *smallest* block that fits the request.
- **Pros**: Minimizes wasted space per allocation.
- **Cons**: Slower (must scan all), creates many tiny unusable fragments.
- **Determinism**: O(N) — violates real-time bounds in large heaps.

### Strategy C: Worst-Fit
- **Logic**: Always allocate from the *largest* free block.
- **Pros**: Leaves large remainder for future use.
- **Cons**: Worst-case O(N), poor utilization under load.
- **Determinism**: Unsuitable for hard real-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** First-Fit with a **circular free list (roving pointer)** is the most common deterministic choice. The "Next-Fit" variant moves the search pointer forward on each allocation, distributing wear and reducing the average search time, while remaining O(N) worst-case.

## 2.3 The Buddy System (Block-Size Power of Two)

The **Binary Buddy System** is the cornerstone algorithm taught in KTU for predictable allocation.

### Operational Logic
- The heap is divided into blocks whose sizes are powers of 2: $2^0, 2^1, 2^2, \ldots, 2^K$.
- A request for $B$ bytes rounds up to the next power of 2: $S = 2^{\lceil \log_2 B \rceil}$.
- If no block of size $S$ exists, the allocator finds the next larger block and **recursively splits it** into two "buddies" until a block of size $S$ is obtained.
- On deallocation, the allocator checks the **buddy's free-bit**; if free, the two buddies are **coalesced** back into a larger block.

### Why Buddies Are Real-Time Friendly
- Both splitting and coalescing have a **bounded depth** of at most $K$ levels.
- Block of size $2^K$ can be split at most $K$ times to reach size $2^0$.
- **Allocation time: O(K)** = **O(log₂(H))** — pseudo-constant for fixed heap sizes.
- **Coalescing is automatic** — no full free-list scan required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Variant:** The **Fibonacci Buddy System** uses Fibonacci sizes (1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, …) to reduce **internal fragmentation** at the cost of slightly more complex bookkeeping.

## 2.4 Memory Fragmentation Theory

### Internal Fragmentation
Wasted memory *inside* an allocated block because the allocator rounded the request up to a larger size.

$$F_{internal} = B_{allocated} - B_{requested}$$

### External Fragmentation
Free memory exists in the heap but is split into many small, non-contiguous chunks that cannot satisfy a single large request.

$$F_{external} = 1 - \frac{L_{largest\ free\ block}}{H_{total\ free}}$$

### Fragmentation Ratio (F)

$$F = \frac{F_{internal} + F_{external}}{H_{heap}} \times 100\%$$

The **KTU target** is $F \rightarrow 0$, with practical acceptance at $F < 15\%$ for soft real-time.

## 2.5 Advanced RTOS Techniques

### Memory Pools (Object Caches / Slab Allocator)
- Pre-allocate $N$ blocks of a *fixed* size at boot time.
- Allocation = remove one block from the pool (O(1) using a free-stack or bitmap).
- Deallocation = push block back (O(1)).
- **Zero fragmentation, O(1) WCET** — the gold standard for hard real-time.

### TLSF (Two-Level Segregate Fit)
- Maintains free lists indexed by a two-level bitmap.
- **Allocation time: O(1)** strictly — used in high-end RTOS like RTEMS and FreeRTOS-MPU.
- Constant-time coalescing.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Key Property | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buddy Block Size | $S = 2^{\lceil \log_2 B \rceil}$ | Power of 2 | Bytes |
| Max Split Depth | $K = \log_2(H)$ | Bounded | Levels |
| Allocation WCET (Buddy) | $O(\log_2 H)$ | Pseudo-constant | Operations |
| Allocation WCET (Pool) | $O(1)$ | Constant | Operations |
| Internal Fragmentation | $F_i = S - B$ | $\leq S/2$ | Bytes |
| External Fragmentation | $F_e = 1 - L_{max}/H_{free}$ | $0 \leq F_e \leq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| Total Fragmentation | $F = (F_i + F_e)/H$ | Target $< 0.15$ | Dimensionless |
| Memory Utilization | $U = B_{used}/H_{total}$ | $0 \leq U \leq 1$ | Dimensionless |
| Coalescing Checks (Buddy) | $O(1)$ per level | XOR buddy address | Operations |
| Free List Search (First-Fit) | $O(N)$ | Non-deterministic | Operations |
| Heap Reserve | $H \geq \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_{i,max} + \text{overhead}$ | Design-time bound | Bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Rule for KTU:** When using the **buddy address formula**, the buddy of an address $A$ of size $2^K$ is computed as $A \oplus 2^K$ (XOR operation). This is why coalescing is constant-time.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR)**: Use memory pools for CAN message buffers; buddy for variable-size OS memory.
- **Aerospace (NASA cFS, RTEMS)**: TLSF for flight software with strict WCET.
- **Medical Devices (FDA Class III)**: Static allocation or fixed-size pools only.
- **IoT/Edge AI**: TLSF for neural network activation tensors.
- **Industrial PLCs**: Memory pools for deterministic task communication.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Buddy System Block Size Resolution

**Problem:** A request of $B = 5300$ bytes arrives in a heap of size $H = 65536$ bytes. Determine the actual allocation size, the number of splits required, and the internal fragmentation.

### Step 1 — Identify the smallest power of 2 that fits the request.

We require $2^K \geq 5300$.

$$2^{12} = 4096 < 5300$$
$$2^{13} = 8192 \geq 5300$$

So the chosen block size is $S = 2^{13} = 8192$ bytes.

### Step 2 — Determine the maximum number of split levels in the heap.

$$K_{max} = \log_2(H) = \log_2(65536) = 16$$

### Step 3 — Compute the number of splits required.

Starting block size: $H = 2^{16} = 65536$.
Target block size: $S = 2^{13} = 8192$.

$$\text{Splits} = 16 - 13 = 3$$

So the original 64 KB block must be split 3 times.

### Step 4 — Visualize the splitting tree.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Block } 2^{16} &\rightarrow \text{Split} \rightarrow \{2^{15}, 2^{15}\} \\
\text{One of the } 2^{15} &\rightarrow \text{Split} \rightarrow \{2^{14}, 2^{14}\} \\
\text{One of the } 2^{14} &\rightarrow \text{Split} \rightarrow \{2^{13}, 2^{13}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The final block of size $2^{13}$ is allocated. Its **buddy** is the other $2^{13}$ block.

### Step 5 — Compute internal fragmentation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_i &= S - B \\
&= 8192 - 5300 \\
&= 2892 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the fragmentation ratio for this single allocation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \frac{F_i}{S} \times 100\% \\
&= \frac{2892}{8192} \times 100\% \\
&\approx 35.31\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The worst-case internal fragmentation in a buddy system is always **less than 50%** for any individual allocation, because the next power of 2 is at most twice the request size.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Buddy Coalescing via XOR

**Problem:** A block at address $A = 0x4000$ of size $2^{10} = 1024$ bytes is being freed. Find the buddy's address and verify coalescing.

### Step 1 — Compute the buddy address.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{buddy} &= A \oplus 2^{K} \\
&= 0x4000 \oplus 0x0400 \\
&= 0x4400
\end{aligned}
$$

The XOR flips bit 10 (the bit corresponding to the block size $2^{10}$).

### Step 2 — Check the free bit of the buddy.

If the buddy at $0x4400$ is free, set the current block's free-bit and propagate the coalescing up by one level.

### Step 3 — Re-coalesce at the next level.

The newly merged block is now size $2^{11}$ at address $0x4000$. Its new buddy is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{buddy,2} &= 0x4000 \oplus 0x0800 = 0x4800
\end{aligned}
$$

Repeat the free-bit check. This continues until the buddy is *not* free or the top of the heap is reached.

### Step 4 — Bounded time.

Since the heap is $H = 2^{16}$, the maximum coalescing depth is $K_{max} = 16$. Thus, **coalescing is O(16) = O(1)** in practice.

## 3.3 Derivation III: Memory Pool Determinism Proof

**Problem:** A pool of $N = 256$ blocks, each $B = 64$ bytes, is initialized. Prove that allocation and deallocation are O(1).

### Step 1 — Data structure selection.

Use a **free-stack** (LIFO). A stack pointer `top` indexes the next free block. Initially, `top = N-1`.

### Step 2 — Allocation time.

```
block = pool[top]
top = top - 1
```

This is exactly **2 memory operations**, independent of $N$. Hence $T_{alloc} = O(1)$.

### Step 3 — Deallocation time.

```
top = top + 1
pool[top] = block
```

This is **2 memory operations**, independent of $N$. Hence $T_{free} = O(1)$.

### Step 4 — WCET bound.

$$
T_{WCET} = 2 \times T_{mem\_access} = 2 \times 50\text{ns} = 100\text{ns}\ (\text{on a } 20\text{MHz CPU})
$$

This is the **gold standard** for hard real-time memory management.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Strategy Comparison

| Strategy | Allocation WCET | Deallocation WCET | Internal Frag. | External Frag. | Real-Time Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| First-Fit | O(N) | O(N) | Low | Moderate | Soft RTOS only |
| Best-Fit | O(N) | O(N) | Very Low | High | Not recommended |
| Worst-Fit | O(N) | O(N) | Low | High | Not recommended |
| Buddy System | O(log H) | O(log H) | Up to 50% | Very Low | **Hard RTOS** |
| Memory Pool | O(1) | O(1) | Zero | Zero | **Hardest RTOS** |
| TLSF | O(1) | O(1) | Low | Very Low | **Hard RTOS** |
| malloc/free (glibc) | Unbounded | Unbounded | Low | Moderate | **Not for RTOS** |

## 3.5 Derivation IV: Worst-Case Heap Sizing

**Problem:** A system has 8 tasks, each requiring a maximum of $2\text{KB}$ working memory. Plus 1 KB of OS overhead. Compute the minimum heap size for a pool-based design.

### Step 1 — Sum the working memory.

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{8} B_{i,max} = 8 \times 2048 = 16384 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 2 — Add OS overhead.

$$
H_{min} = 16384 + 1024 = 17408 \text{ bytes}
$$

### Step 3 — Round up to a power of 2 (for buddy compatibility).

$$
H_{buddy} = 2^{\lceil \log_2 17408 \rceil} = 2^{15} = 32768 \text{ bytes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering rule of thumb:** Always reserve **20–30% headroom** above the calculated minimum to handle fragmentation and unforeseen task stack growth during field operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RTOS Memory Manager

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    appTask["Application Task Request"] --> reqValidator{"Request Validator Size Check"}
    reqValidator -- Valid --> stratSelector["Strategy Selector Buddy or Pool"]
    reqValidator -- Invalid --> errReturn["Return Allocation Error"]
    stratSelector --> buddyPath["Buddy Allocator Path"]
    stratSelector --> poolPath["Memory Pool Allocator Path"]
    buddyPath --> freeListBuddy["Free List Level K"]
    freeListBuddy --> splitEngine["Block Splitter Power of 2"]
    splitEngine --> blockGrant1["Return Allocated Block"]
    poolPath --> freeStack["Free Stack Top Pointer"]
    freeStack --> blockGrant2["Return Block from Stack"]
    blockGrant1 --> wcetGuard["WCET Profiler Bounded Latency Check"]
    blockGrant2 --> wcetGuard
    wcetGuard --> success["Return Pointer to Task"]
    errReturn --> success
    freeTask["Deallocate Pointer"] --> coalesceUnit["Coalesce Engine XOR Buddy Check"]
    coalesceUnit --> freeListUpdate["Update Free List and Bitmap"]
    freeListUpdate --> wcetGuard
```

## 4.2 Buddy System Splitting and Coalescing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Free Request of Size B"] --> roundUp["Round Up to Next Power of 2 Compute S"]
    roundUp --> findLevel["Search Free List for Level Log2 S"]
    findLevel -- Found --> allocateDirect["Return Block to Caller"]
    findLevel -- Not Found --> findLarger["Find Larger Block at Level K greater than S"]
    findLarger --> splitBlock["Split Block into Two Buddies"]
    splitBlock --> addToList["Insert Buddy into Level K minus 1 Free List"]
    addToList --> findLevel
    freeOp["Free Operation on Block of Size S"] --> findBuddy["Compute Buddy Address via XOR"]
    findBuddy --> checkFree{"Is Buddy Free?"}
    checkFree -- Yes --> merge["Coalesce Block and Buddy to Level K plus 1"]
    merge --> findBuddy
    checkFree -- No --> markFree["Mark Block as Free in Bitmap"]
    markFree --> done["Coalescing Complete"]
```

## 4.3 RTOS Memory Layout (Typical Embedded MCU)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rom["Flash ROM Code and Const Data"] --> sram["SRAM Region"]
    sram --> vectorTable["Interrupt Vector Table"]
    vectorTable --> stackRegion["Stack Region per Task Static"]
    stackRegion --> staticData["Static Global Data BSS and Data"]
    staticData --> osKernel["RTOS Kernel Memory TCBs Queues"]
    osKernel --> heapRegion["Dynamic Heap Region Buddy or Pool Managed"]
    heapRegion --> ioBuffers["I/O DMA Buffers"]
    ioBuffers --> end["End of Physical Memory"]
```

## 4.4 Fragmentation Visualization (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1["Allocated 4KB"] --- G1["Free 1KB"] --- H2["Allocated 2KB"] --- G2["Free 2KB"] --- H3["Allocated 1KB"] --- G3["Free 8KB"]
    note1["External fragmentation: free memory exists at G1 G2 G3 but no single block of 5KB is available despite 11KB total free space"]
    G1 -.-> note1
```

## 4.5 Memory Pool Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    poolStart["Pool Base Address"] --> b0["Block 0 Index 0"]
    b0 --> b1["Block 1 Index 1"]
    b1 --> b2["Block 2 Index 2"]
    b2 --> b3["Block N minus 1 Index N minus 1"]
    b3 --> freePtr["Free Stack Top Pointer points to N minus 1 initially"]
    freePtr --> allocate["Allocate pops index decrements Top O(1)"]
    freePtr --> dealloc["Deallocate pushes index increments Top O(1)"]
```

## 4.6 TLSF Two-Level Bitmap Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["First Level Bitmap FL_bitmap 5 bits size class"] --> L2A["Second Level Bitmap SL_bitmap 5 bits for fine granularity within size class"]
    L1 --> L2B["Second Level Bitmap for next size class"]
    L1 --> L2C["Second Level Bitmap for next size class"]
    L2A --> search["Constant Time Find First Bit Set O(1)"]
    L2B --> search
    L2C --> search
    search --> blockOut["Return Matching Free Block"]
```

---
