# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"Differentiate between internal and external fragmentation in the context of RTOS memory management. Why is external fragmentation critical in hard real-time systems?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Internal Fragmentation (1 mark):** Occurs when an allocated block is *larger* than the requested size. The unused portion inside the block is wasted. Example: Buddy system allocating $8192$ bytes for a $5300$-byte request wastes $2892$ bytes internally.

- **External Fragmentation (1 mark):** Occurs when free memory is scattered into many non-contiguous chunks. The total free memory may be sufficient, but no single contiguous block is large enough for a new request. Example: Heap has $11$ KB free, but the largest contiguous free block is only $8$ KB, so a $10$ KB request fails.

- **Criticality in Hard RT (1 mark):** External fragmentation is **non-deterministic** — it can cause allocation failures unpredictably. In a hard real-time system, a missed memory allocation is equivalent to a missed deadline, which can be catastrophic (e.g., a vehicle's airbag deployment task failing to allocate its control buffer).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**"Explain why the standard `malloc()`/`free()` implementation of glibc is unsuitable for hard real-time systems. Name two deterministic alternatives."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Why malloc/free fails (2 marks):**
  1. **Unbounded Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET):** glibc's `malloc` scans a complex tree of arenas, bins, and cached chunks. There is no upper bound on allocation time — it may take microseconds in the average case but milliseconds under fragmentation or contention.
  2. **Non-deterministic coalescing:** Background coalescing, thread-arena contention, and unbounded free-list growth mean that even `free()` is not bounded.
  3. **Induces priority inversion** when the heap lock is held by a low-priority task.

- **Deterministic Alternatives (1 mark):**
  1. **Memory Pools / Object Caches** — O(1) bounded allocation.
  2. **Buddy System** — O(log H) bounded allocation, automatic coalescing.
  3. (Bonus acceptable) **TLSF** — Strict O(1) bounded allocation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [Module 4 Internal Choice Option 1]

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Binary Buddy System** algorithm for dynamic memory allocation. Show how a request of **7000 bytes** is satisfied in a heap of size **16 KB**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss **internal fragmentation** in the buddy system and derive the **worst-case fragmentation ratio**. Compare the buddy system with **memory pools** in terms of determinism and fragmentation. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Diagram (1 mark):**
*(Draw the splitting tree as shown in Section 4.2.)*

**Algorithm Steps (4 marks):**

1. The heap of size $16 \text{ KB} = 2^{14}$ bytes is the initial free block.
2. Request $B = 7000$ bytes is rounded up to the next power of 2: 
   - $2^{12} = 4096 < 7000$
   - $2^{13} = 8192 \geq 7000$
   - So allocate a block of size $S = 2^{13} = 8192$ bytes.
3. Splits required: $14 - 13 = 1$ split.

**Splitting Sequence (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial block: } & 2^{14} = 16384 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{After 1 split: } & \{8192, 8192\} \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Allocation: } & \text{Return one 8192-byte block to the caller.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Buddy address calculation:** If the allocated block starts at $0x0000$, its buddy starts at $0x0000 \oplus 0x2000 = 0x2000$.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Internal Fragmentation Discussion (3 marks):**

- Internal fragmentation occurs because the allocator rounds the request up to a power of 2.
- The worst case is when the request is just *one byte* larger than a power of 2. Example: $B = 2^{K} + 1$ is allocated as $2^{K+1}$.
- Wasted space = $2^{K+1} - (2^K + 1) = 2^K - 1$.

**Worst-Case Fragmentation Ratio Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{max} &= \frac{2^{K+1} - B}{2^{K+1}} \bigg|_{B = 2^K + 1} \\
&= \frac{2^{K+1} - 2^K - 1}{2^{K+1}} \\
&= \frac{2^K - 1}{2^{K+1}} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{2^{K+1}} \\
&\approx \frac{1}{2} = 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

As $K \to \infty$, $F_{max} \to 50\%$. Thus, **worst-case internal fragmentation is strictly less than 50%.**

**Comparison with Memory Pools (2 marks):**

| Property | Buddy System | Memory Pool |
|---|---|---|
| Allocation WCET | O(log H) | O(1) |
| Internal Fragmentation | Up to 50% | Zero |
| External Fragmentation | Very Low | Zero (fixed size) |
| Block Size Flexibility | Variable (any power of 2) | Fixed per pool |
| Implementation Complexity | Moderate | Low |
| Best Use Case | Variable-sized data | Fixed-size objects (e.g., packets) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [Module 4 Internal Choice Option 2]

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Define **determinism** in the context of RTOS memory allocation. Explain why **Memory Pools** are considered the most deterministic allocation mechanism. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a **memory pool** of 128 blocks, each 256 bytes, for an embedded RTOS. Show the data structures, allocation, and deallocation routines. Compute the WCET for each operation. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Definition of Determinism (2 marks):**

Determinism in RTOS memory allocation means that the **worst-case execution time (WCET)** of every memory operation — `alloc()` and `free()` — is **bounded by a known, finite, constant upper limit** that does not depend on:
- The number of allocated blocks
- The fragmentation state of the heap
- The history of past allocations
- The interleaving with other tasks

A memory allocator is "deterministic" if and only if $T_{WCET}(alloc)$ and $T_{WCET}(free)$ are both $O(1)$.

**Why Memory Pools are the Most Deterministic (5 marks):**

1. **Fixed-size blocks (1 mark):** No size search is required — every block is the same size. The allocator simply picks the next free block.
2. **O(1) allocation (2 marks):** Allocation reduces to a stack pop: read the free-stack top, decrement the pointer, return the block. The number of operations is **constant** (2 memory accesses), independent of $N$.
3. **O(1) deallocation (1 mark):** Deallocation is a stack push — a constant-time operation.
4. **Zero fragmentation (1 mark):** Because all blocks are identical, neither internal nor external fragmentation can occur. Thus, the failure mode "no suitable block" is impossible once the pool is sized correctly.

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU-defining property:** Memory pools achieve the **strongest possible determinism** — both allocation and deallocation are strictly constant-time — making them the gold standard for hard real-time systems where deadline misses are unacceptable.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Memory Pool Specifications (1 mark):**

- $N = 128$ blocks
- $B = 256$ bytes per block
- Total heap requirement: $128 \times 256 = 32,768$ bytes $= 32$ KB

**Data Structures (2 marks):**

```
pool_buffer[128][256]   // Static 2D array, total 32 KB
free_stack[128]          // Indices of free blocks
top = 127                // Initially points to the last block
```

**Initialization Routine (1 mark):**

```
for i = 0 to 127:
    free_stack[i] = i    // Block i is free
top = 127                // Stack is full of free indices
```

**Allocation Routine (1 mark):**

```
int allocate_block():
    if (top < 0):
        return -1        // Pool exhausted (deterministic failure)
    block_index = free_stack[top]
    top = top - 1
    return block_index
```

**Deallocation Routine (1 mark):**

```
void free_block(int block_index):
    top = top + 1
    free_stack[top] = block_index
```

**WCET Computation (1 mark):**

Assuming each memory access takes $T_{mem} = 50$ ns (at 20 MHz CPU clock):

- **Allocation WCET:** 2 memory accesses (read free_stack, write top) = $2 \times 50 = 100$ ns.
- **Deallocation WCET:** 2 memory accesses (write top, write free_stack) = $2 \times 50 = 100$ ns.

Both are strictly $O(1)$ and **deterministically bounded at 100 ns**, satisfying the hardest real-time requirements.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing fragmentation types** (1–2 marks lost): Many students mix up internal and external fragmentation. Remember: *internal* = wasted space *inside* a block; *external* = scattered free space between blocks.
> 2. **Forgetting the worst-case 50% bound** (2 marks lost): When asked for "maximum" buddy fragmentation, derive $F_{max} \to 50\%$, not "very high" or "arbitrary".
> 3. **Writing `malloc` pseudocode for hard RT** (3 marks lost): Examiners deduct heavily when students propose unbounded algorithms. Always state the **WCET** explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring coalescing** (1 mark lost): Any buddy-system solution without explaining **automatic coalescing via XOR** is incomplete.
> 5. **Missing the determinism argument** (2 marks lost): Always conclude with the WCET statement — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests *bounded latency* as a learning outcome.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Memory management in RTOS is about determinism, not throughput** — always justify any choice by its **WCET bound**.
- [x] **Two main strategies:** *Block-based* (Buddy, TLSF) for variable sizes; *Pool-based* for fixed-size objects.
- [x] **Buddy system block size:** $S = 2^{\lceil \log_2 B \rceil}$ — the next power of 2 ≥ request.
- [x] **Buddy coalescing:** XOR the block address with its size to locate its partner; merges are automatic and bounded.
- [x] **Worst-case internal fragmentation** of buddy system: **< 50%**, asymptotically approaching 50%.
- [x] **External fragmentation ratio:** $F_e = 1 - L_{max}/H_{free}$.
- [x] **Memory pools** achieve **O(1) allocation and deallocation** — the strongest determinism guarantee.
- [x] **TLSF** is the practical O(1) choice when variable block sizes are needed.
- [x] **Avoid `glibc malloc/free`** in hard real-time — unbounded WCET and lock contention.
- [x] **Heap sizing rule:** Always round up to a power of 2 and add 20–30% headroom.
- [x] **RTOS memory layout order:** Vector Table → Task Stacks → Static Data → Kernel TCBs → Dynamic Heap → DMA Buffers.
- [x] **Design rule:** If you know the object size at design time, **use a pool**. Use buddy/TLSF only when sizes are truly variable.
- [x] **K-Book keyword checklist:** *determinism, WCET, fragmentation, buddy, coalesce, pool, TLSF, free-list, block splitting, heap.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Real-Time Middleware Implementation: Developing Middleware Components for a Real-Time Application
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Real-Time Middleware Implementation: Developing Middleware Components for a Real-Time Application

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Real-Time Middleware** is a software abstraction layer that resides between the underlying Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) / hardware platform and the application logic, providing standardized, reusable, and QoS-aware services (such as transparent communication, scheduling coordination, fault tolerance, and resource reservation) so that distributed, time-critical applications can be constructed without re-implementing low-level infrastructure for every new system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):** *Real-Time middleware is a configurable software infrastructure that integrates the RTOS kernel services with distributed application components, providing a component-based execution environment with bounded, predictable, and analyzable end-to-end timing behavior.*

**Middleware Component** is an independently deployable, well-defined software unit (specified via an Interface Definition Language or contract) that exposes real-time properties (priority, deadline, period, QoS parameters) to the middleware container, which then maps these properties onto underlying RTOS primitives.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Restaurant Kitchen" Analogy**
> Imagine you (the chef = application) need to deliver 50 dishes to customers within strict time deadlines. Without help, you would have to:
> - Buy raw materials directly from farms (hardware drivers)
> - Manage the fire, gas, and knives yourself (RTOS scheduling)
> - Handle each customer's order, billing, and serving (networking, I/O)
>
> A **Real-Time Middleware** is like a **Head Waiter + Kitchen Manager** sitting between you and the customers. He:
> - Takes orders, prioritizes VIP customers (QoS / priority mapping)
> - Reserves stoves and pans so no dish is starved (resource reservation)
> - Guarantees "Soup in 5 min, Steak in 20 min" (deadline guarantees)
> - Speaks the customer's language (interface translation / IDL)
>
> You, the chef, just **cook** — the middleware handles all real-time coordination.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **End-to-End Latency Bound:** typically **< 100 µs to 10 ms** for hard real-time middleware
- **Jitter Bound:** must be **< 10% of the deadline** for predictable behavior
- **CPU Reservation Granularity:** typically **1 ms** for typical RTOS (VxWorks, RTEMS, QNX)
- **Standardized Interfaces:** **CORBA (OMG), DDS (OMG), RTSJ (JSR-1), POSIX 1003.13**
- **Memory Overhead per Component:** **< 5 KB** for container-managed components
- **Default Priority Levels:** **0–255** (8-bit) for typical RTOS priority space

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU expects students to clearly distinguish **real-time middleware** from **general-purpose middleware** (e.g., standard Java RMI, .NET remoting). The distinguishing feature is **temporal predictability** and **deterministic resource access**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Layered Architecture of Real-Time Middleware

The middleware sits as **Layer 3** in the typical real-time system stack:

| Layer | Component | Responsibility |
|-------|-----------|----------------|
| 5 | Application Tasks | Domain-specific logic |
| 4 | Component Framework / Container | Lifecycle, binding, deployment |
| **3** | **Real-Time Middleware** | **QoS, scheduling, comms abstraction** |
| 2 | RTOS Kernel | Task scheduling, ISR, synchronization |
| 1 | Hardware Platform | CPU, memory, I/O, network |

### 2.2 Core Middleware Services for Real-Time Systems

1. **Transparent Communication Service** — hides network protocol details; provides synchronous/invoke, deferred, and one-way invocations.
2. **Scheduling & Dispatching Service** — maps abstract real-time priorities (from component contracts) onto native RTOS priorities.
3. **Resource Reservation Service** — allocates CPU time, memory pools, network bandwidth.
4. **Event Service** — real-time publish/subscribe with deadline propagation.
5. **Naming & Trading Service** — locates components by name and required QoS.
6. **Fault Tolerance Service** — replication with bounded recovery time.

### 2.3 Why Real-Time Middleware?

- **Why it exists:** Embedded systems have hundreds of tasks; hand-coding communication is error-prone and not portable.
- **How it works:** A **container** wraps each component, intercepting all operations, attaching deadlines, and forwarding to the RTOS.

### 2.4 Real-Time CORBA (RT-CORBA) — Key Concepts

- **Priority Models:**
  - *Client Propagated:* Client's priority is sent to server.
  - *Server Declared:* Server sets priority per operation.
  - *Priority Banded:* Priorities grouped into bands.
- **CORBA Messaging:** Adds asynchronous invocations.
- **Real-Time Scheduling Service:** Server-side thread pool with deadline-aware dispatching.

### 2.5 Real-Time Java (RTSJ / JSR-1)

| Element | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `RealtimeThread` | Hard real-time thread, no GC interference |
| `NoHeapRealtimeThread` | Runs outside managed heap |
| `AsyncEventHandler` | Deadline-driven, fire-and-forget |
| `Memory Areas` | `ScopedMemory`, `ImmortalMemory` |
| `PriorityScheduler` | 28+ priority levels, with `getMinimumInterarrival()` |

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $R_i = C_i + T_{comm,i} + T_{m/w,i}$ | End-to-end response time = computation + communication + middleware overhead | ms |
| 2 | $U_{cpu} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le U_{max}$ | CPU utilization bound (Liu & Layland) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $D_{end} = D_{component} + D_{network} + D_{marshalling}$ | End-to-end deadline decomposition | ms |
| 4 | $J_{bounded} = \frac{T_{max\_path} - T_{min\_path}}{D_{deadline}} \le 0.10$ | Jitter as fraction of deadline | ratio |
| 5 | $T_{marshalling} = \frac{N_{bytes}}{B_{network}} \cdot 8$ | Marshalling time vs. network bandwidth | ms |
| 6 | $P_{effective} = f(P_{abstract}, P_{OS\_max})$ | Priority mapping function | priority level |
| 7 | $M_{overhead} = k \cdot N_{components}$ | Middleware memory overhead | bytes |
| 8 | $D_{remaining} = D_{end} - (T_{exec} + T_{comm})$ | Remaining deadline budget | ms |
| 9 | $\Delta_{lateness} = T_{actual} - T_{deadline}$ | Lateness measure (negative = early) | ms |
| 10 | $N_{threads} = \left\lceil \frac{U_{cpu}}{U_{per\_thread}} \right\rceil$ | Thread pool sizing | count |

> [!NOTE]
> Always write the boundary condition: e.g., $U_{cpu} \le 0.693$ for Rate Monotonic (Liu & Layland bound for $n \to \infty$) or $U_{cpu} \le 1.0$ for EDF.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** ARINC-653 compliant middleware in avionics (IMA — Integrated Modular Avionics).
- **Automotive:** AUTOSAR Adaptive Platform uses middleware to abstract POSIX + multicore.
- **Robotics:** ROS 2 (Robot Operating System 2) uses DDS middleware with QoS policies.
- **Telecommunications:** IMS (IP Multimedia Subsystem) uses real-time middleware for call control.
- **Industrial Control:** OPC UA Pub/Sub with TSN for deterministic Ethernet.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Priority Mapping for Hard Real-Time Middleware

**Problem Setup:** A component contract specifies an abstract priority $P_{abstract} \in [1, 10]$. The underlying RTOS uses a 32-level priority space $[0, 31]$ where $0$ is highest. We must derive a deterministic mapping $P_{effective} = f(P_{abstract})$.

**Step 1:** Identify the boundary conditions.
- Minimum abstract priority must map to *minimum* OS priority.
- Maximum abstract priority must map to *maximum* OS priority (with 0 reserved for kernel critical sections).

**Step 2:** Reserve levels.
- Reserve 0–1 for kernel ISR / critical tasks.
- Available range: $[2, 31]$ → span = $31 - 2 = 29$ levels.

**Step 3:** Linear interpolation.

$$
P_{effective} = P_{reserved\_low} + \left\lfloor \frac{(P_{abstract} - 1) \cdot (P_{reserved\_high} - P_{reserved\_low})}{P_{abstract,max} - 1} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values $P_{reserved\_low} = 2$, $P_{reserved\_high} = 31$, $P_{abstract,max} = 10$.

$$
P_{effective} = 2 + \left\lfloor \frac{(P_{abstract} - 1) \cdot 29}{9} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 5:** Verify for boundary values.

- $P_{abstract} = 1$ (lowest): $P_{effective} = 2 + \lfloor 0 \rfloor = 2$ ✓
- $P_{abstract} = 10$ (highest): $P_{effective} = 2 + \lfloor 29 \rfloor = 31$ ✓
- $P_{abstract} = 5.5$ (mid): $P_{effective} = 2 + \lfloor 16.1 \rfloor = 18$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This monotonic mapping preserves ordering, ensuring that a higher abstract priority always yields a higher (numerically greater) OS priority, which in most RTOS (where higher number = higher priority) is the desired behavior.

### 3.2 Derivation: Deadline Propagation Across Middleware Stack

**Problem:** A client task sets an end-to-end deadline of $D_{end} = 20$ ms. The middleware incurs 4 distinct delays: marshalling $T_{marsh}$, network transmission $T_{net}$, demarshalling $T_{demarsh}$, and server processing $T_{server}$. Derive the budget each phase must respect.

**Step 1:** Total budget equation.

$$
D_{end} = T_{marsh} + T_{net} + T_{demarsh} + T_{server} + 2 \cdot T_{m/w\_overhead}
$$

The factor $2$ accounts for middleware overhead at client and server ends.

**Step 2:** Assume equal allocation for a balanced design.

$$
T_i = \frac{D_{end} - 2 \cdot T_{m/w\_overhead}}{4}
$$

**Step 3:** Insert measured values: $D_{end} = 20$ ms, $T_{m/w\_overhead} = 1$ ms.

$$
T_i = \frac{20 - 2 \cdot 1}{4} = \frac{18}{4} = 4.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4:** So each of marshalling, network, demarshalling, and server must complete within **4.5 ms**.

**Step 5:** For marshalling, given 8 KB payload and 100 Mbps network:

$$
T_{net} = \frac{N_{bytes} \cdot 8}{B} = \frac{8192 \cdot 8}{100 \times 10^6} = 0.655 \text{ ms}
$$

This is well within the 4.5 ms budget; the saved 3.84 ms can be reallocated to $T_{server}$ to handle more complex processing.

**Step 6:** Reallocated budget:

$$
T_{server,new} = T_{server,old} + (T_{net,old} - T_{net,new}) = 4.5 + (4.5 - 0.655) = 8.345 \text{ ms}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Middleware Design Decisions

| Decision Axis | Option A | Option B | Trade-off |
|---------------|----------|----------|-----------|
| Communication | Synchronous RPC | Asynchronous (one-way) | Predictability vs. throughput |
| Scheduling | Fixed priority (RM) | Dynamic (EDF) | Simplicity vs. CPU utilization |
| Memory | Static allocation | Dynamic pools | Determinism vs. flexibility |
| Component Binding | Compile-time (static) | Run-time (dynamic) | Performance vs. reconfigurability |
| Marshalling | Custom binary | CDR (CORBA) | Speed vs. portability |
| Threading Model | Thread-per-call | Thread pool | Latency vs. resource usage |
| QoS Specification | Implicit (priority only) | Explicit (deadline, jitter) | Simplicity vs. expressiveness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** For **hard real-time**, always prefer *synchronous RPC + static binding + fixed priority + static memory*. Soft real-time tolerates *async + dynamic + pools*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Real-Time Middleware Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    app1[Application Task A]
    app2[Application Task B]
    app3[Application Task C]
    cont1[Component Container]
    cont2[Component Container]
    sched[Middleware Scheduler]
    qos[QoS Manager]
    resv[Resource Reservation]
    comm[Communication Layer]
    rtapi[RTOS API Layer]
    rtos[RTOS Kernel]
    hw[Hardware Platform]
    
    app1 --> cont1
    app2 --> cont1
    app3 --> cont2
    cont1 --> sched
    cont2 --> sched
    cont1 --> qos
    cont2 --> qos
    sched --> resv
    qos --> resv
    resv --> comm
    comm --> rtapi
    rtapi --> rtos
    rtos --> hw
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Component Lifecycle in Real-Time Middleware

```mermaid
graph LR
    spec[IDL Specification] --> gen[Code Generation]
    gen --> impl[Component Implementation]
    impl --> pack[Packaging]
    pack --> deploy[Deployment]
    deploy --> inst[Instantiation by Container]
    inst --> bind[Binding to Ports]
    bind --> run[Active State]
    run -->|fault| err[Error Handler]
    err -->|recover| run
    run -->|shutdown| stop[Stop State]
    stop --> dest[Destroy]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Real-Time Middleware Interaction Flow (RPC)

```mermaid
graph TB
    client[Client Task] -->|invoke op| marsh1[Marshal Arguments]
    marsh1 -->|add deadline| prop[Deadline Propagation]
    prop -->|send| transp[Transport]
    transp -->|recv| demarsh[Demarshal]
    demarsh -->|enqueue| qos[QoS Admission]
    qos -->|admit| sched[Server Scheduler]
    qos -->|reject| rej[Reject with Reason]
    sched -->|dispatch| server[Server Task]
    server -->|execute| srvcode[Service Code]
    srvcode -->|marshal result| resp[Response]
    resp -->|send back| client
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: QoS Negotiation Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Client Component] -->|Offer: priority=7, deadline=10ms| B[Middleware]
    B -->|Check resources| C{Resources available?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Reserve CPU + Memory]
    C -->|No| E[Negotiate Lower QoS]
    D -->|Accept| F[Contract Signed]
    E -->|Counter-offer| A
    F --> G[Service Activated]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Adaptation for Physical Layout)

When Mermaid is insufficient for depicting physical deployments, the following **block-level functional flow** represents the middleware as a *virtual machine* overlaying the RTOS:

| Block Position | Block Name | Input | Output |
|----------------|------------|-------|--------|
| 1 | Application Logic | Domain data | Service calls |
| 2 | IDL Stub/Skeleton | Service calls | Type-safe invocations |
| 3 | ORB / DDS Core | Invocations | Marshalled bytes + QoS tags |
| 4 | Priority Propagator | QoS tags | RTOS priority value |
| 5 | Transport Adapter | Marshalled bytes | Network frames |
| 6 | RTOS System Calls | Frames / syscalls | Hardware events |
| 7 | Hardware Platform | Events | Sensor data / actuators |

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram is commonly used in KTU board answer sheets when students are asked to "draw the middleware architecture."

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> *Define Real-Time Middleware. List any four services it provides.*

**Model Answer:**
Real-Time Middleware is a software layer that lies between the RTOS and the application, providing a standardized infrastructure to build distributed, time-critical systems with predictable behavior.

Four services:
1. **Transparent communication** (synchronous/asynchronous invocations)
2. **Priority propagation and mapping** to RTOS priorities
3. **Resource reservation** (CPU, memory, network bandwidth)
4. **Event/Notification service** with deadline propagation

*(Expected length: 8–10 lines; marks awarded for *definition* + *correct 4 services*.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**
> *Differentiate between synchronous and asynchronous invocations in real-time middleware with one example each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Synchronous Invocation | Asynchronous Invocation |
|--------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Blocking | Client blocks until response | Client does not block |
| Predictability | High (bounded latency) | Lower (no response timing) |
| Use case | Critical control loop | Logging, telemetry |
| Example | Remote function call in RT-CORBA | One-way `send()` in CORBA Messaging |

*(Expected length: 6–8 lines.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the layered architecture of a real-time middleware system with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each layer in ensuring predictable behavior.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the concept of **Real-Time CORBA (RT-CORBA)**. Explain its priority models, the Real-Time Scheduling Service, and the importance of end-to-end predictability.

#### Model Solution for Q3(a):

A real-time middleware system is organized into **five layers**, each providing abstraction over the layer below:

1. **Application Layer** — Contains the domain logic; issues high-level invocations.
2. **Component Container Layer** — Manages component lifecycle: instantiation, binding, activation, destruction.
3. **Middleware Layer** — Provides services: ORB (Object Request Broker), event service, naming, trading.
4. **RTOS Abstraction Layer** — Wraps OS-specific calls (POSIX, VxWorks API) into portable calls.
5. **Hardware/RTOS Kernel Layer** — The actual real-time operating system and the physical hardware.

**Role in Predictability:**
- Layer 1 stays unaware of timing constraints; the *contract* specifies deadlines.
- Layer 2 enforces the contract at invocation.
- Layer 3 marshals, adds deadline/priority tags, and routes the call.
- Layer 4 maps abstract priority to OS priority deterministically.
- Layer 5 guarantees bounded interrupt latency and execution time.

*Diagram should show 5 horizontal stacked layers with arrows indicating data/control flow.*

#### Model Solution for Q3(b):

**Real-Time CORBA (RT-CORBA)** is an OMG specification (formal/04-09-01) extending standard CORBA with real-time guarantees.

**Priority Models:**
- **Client-Propagated Priority:** The client task's priority is sent with the request. The server thread inherits this priority dynamically.
- **Server-Declared Priority:** The server assigns a fixed priority per operation in its IDL.
- **Priority-Banded:** Priorities are grouped into bands; intra-band FIFO, inter-band strict priority.

**Real-Time Scheduling Service:**
- Server uses a **thread pool** with **priority-aware dispatching**.
- The pool can be configured per-CORBA object.
- Uses `TOTAL_ORDER` and `LATE_BIND` policies for deadline-aware admission.

**End-to-End Predictability:**
- Combines:
  - **Priority propagation** ensures server executes at appropriate priority.
  - **Thread pool sizing** prevents unbounded queuing delay.
  - **Bound protocol** (RT-CORBA over GIOP/IIOP) bounds transmission delay.
  - **Scheduling Service** enforces per-method deadlines.

*Conclusion: RT-CORBA achieves deterministic end-to-end behavior by integrating priority mapping, bounded resource pools, and deadline-aware dispatching.*

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 — Alternative Choice]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **Real-Time Specification for Java (RTSJ / JSR-1)**. Discuss `RealtimeThread`, `NoHeapRealtimeThread`, `AsyncEventHandler`, and the memory model (Scoped, Immortal).
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Develop a step-by-step methodology to implement a middleware component for a real-time control application. Show the priority mapping, deadline propagation, and QoS negotiation phases.

#### Model Solution for Q4(a):

**RTSJ (JSR-1)** is the Java Community Process specification for real-time Java, defining memory, threads, scheduling, and asynchronous events that operate without the interference of garbage collection.

**Key Thread Types:**

- **`RealtimeThread`** — A `Thread` subclass with **no-heap-real-time** semantics; runs at a high fixed priority with bounded execution. *Not allowed* to allocate from the heap during critical execution.

- **`NoHeapRealtimeThread`** — A special thread that runs **outside the garbage-collected heap**; highest priority class; cannot reference Java objects on the heap. Used for **hard real-time** tasks.

- **`AsyncEventHandler`** — A deadline-driven handler fired by an `AsyncEvent`. Has methods:
  - `handleAsyncEvent()` — invoked when event fires.
  - `setDeadline(HighResolutionTime)` — binds a deadline.
  - `setReleaseParameters()` — sets period, cost, deadline.
  - Used as **soft real-time** units (priority 0–9) typically.

**Memory Model:**
- **`ImmortalMemory`** — Never garbage-collected; objects live forever; thread-safe.
- **`ScopedMemory`** — Pre-allocated memory region that is entered and exited; objects allocated in it are collected when the scope exits (deterministic GC).
- **Per-thread `LTMemory`** — Large scoped memory, optimized for LIFO.

**Scheduling:** `PriorityScheduler` provides 28 distinct priority levels (10 real-time + 18 normal) with `getMinimumInterarrival()` and `getDeadlinesMissed()` for monitoring.

#### Model Solution for Q4(b):

**Step-by-Step Implementation of a Real-Time Middleware Component:**

**Step 1: Define the IDL Contract**
Specify the component's operations, parameters, and **QoS annotations** (priority, deadline, period).

**Step 2: Generate Stubs and Skeletons**
Use an IDL compiler to generate language-specific stubs (client-side) and skeletons (server-side). This provides type-safe invocation.

**Step 3: Implement the Component**
Write the service code. Avoid dynamic memory allocation inside critical sections.

**Step 4: Specify QoS in Deployment Descriptor**
Declare priority (1–10), deadline (e.g., 10 ms), and periodicity (e.g., 50 ms) in a deployment XML.

**Step 5: Priority Mapping Phase**
Map abstract priority to OS priority using the deterministic function:

$$
P_{effective} = P_{reserved\_low} + \left\lfloor \frac{(P_{abstract} - 1) \cdot (P_{OS,max} - P_{OS,min} - 1)}{P_{abstract,max} - 1} \right\rfloor
$$

This ensures monotonic ordering.

**Step 6: Deadline Propagation Phase**
On each invocation, the middleware:
- Computes $T_{remaining} = D_{end} - T_{elapsed}$
- Sets a watchdog timer.
- Aborts execution if budget exceeded.

**Step 7: QoS Negotiation Phase**
- Client offers: $\{P=7, D=10\text{ms}\}$.
- Middleware checks CPU and memory resources.
- If feasible → contract signed; if not → counter-offer with relaxed parameters.

**Step 8: Activation and Monitoring**
- Container instantiates the component.
- Schedules it under the agreed QoS.
- Periodically reports `deadlines_missed` counter.

**Step 9: Verification via Response-Time Analysis**
Apply Rate Monotonic Analysis to prove:

$$
R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil \cdot C_j \le D_i
$$

**Step 10: Deployment**
Package as a deployable unit (e.g., `.car` for CORBA Component Model) and install on the target RTOS.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse "real-time" with "high-performance" — real-time means *bounded, predictable* behavior, not "fast."
> 2. **Do NOT** claim Java is unsuitable for real-time — RTSJ makes it possible; but standard JVM is **not** real-time safe.
> 3. **Do NOT** forget to mention **garbage collection interference** as the main reason for RTSJ.
> 4. **Do NOT** use `dynamic memory allocation` inside `NoHeapRealtimeThread` — this is a hard violation.
> 5. In diagrams, **always label all blocks** and **use arrows**; a diagram without labels fetches **0 of the 2 marks** allotted for "neat diagram" in 14-mark questions.
> 6. When asked to "develop a middleware component," KTU expects a **stepwise methodology** (≥ 5 steps), not just a definition.
> 7. **Always state boundary conditions** for formulas — e.g., for Liu & Layland, the bound is **0.693** for RM with $n \to \infty$ tasks, not 1.0.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Definition (must remember):** Real-Time Middleware = *Software abstraction layer between RTOS and applications, providing predictable, QoS-aware services.*
> - **Three properties that distinguish RT-middleware from general middleware:**
>   1. **Temporal predictability** (bounded latency & jitter)
>   2. **Priority transparency** (priority flows from client to server)
>   3. **Resource reservation** (CPU, memory, network pre-allocated)
> - **Layered architecture:** Application → Container → Middleware → RTOS Abstraction → RTOS → Hardware.
> - **RT-CORBA priority models:** *Client-Propagated, Server-Declared, Priority-Banded.*
> - **RT-CORBA key services:** *Real-Time Scheduling Service, Messaging Service (async).*
> - **RTSJ key elements:** *RealtimeThread, NoHeapRealtimeThread, AsyncEventHandler, ScopedMemory, ImmortalMemory, PriorityScheduler.*
> - **NoHeapRealtimeThread cannot reference heap objects** — most critical RTSJ rule.
> - **Standardized specs:** *CORBA (OMG), DDS (OMG), RTSJ (JSR-1), POSIX 1003.13, AUTOSAR.*
> - **Formula — End-to-end Response Time:** $R_i = C_i + T_{comm,i} + T_{m/w,i}$
> - **Formula — Jitter bound:** $J \le 0.10 \cdot D_{deadline}$
> - **Formula — CPU utilization bound (RM):** $U_{cpu} \le n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$; for $n \to \infty$, $U_{cpu} \le 0.693$.
> - **Lifecycle phases:** *Specification → Generation → Implementation → Packaging → Deployment → Instantiation → Binding → Active → Destruction.*
> - **Real-world examples:** *ARINC-653 (avionics), AUTOSAR (automotive), ROS 2 / DDS (robotics), OPC UA + TSN (industry).*
> - **Design rule for hard real-time:** *Synchronous RPC + Static binding + Fixed priority + Static memory + Custom binary marshalling.*
> - **Q&A Triggers:** If the question asks for "QoS-aware" or "predictable," immediately mention **deadline propagation + priority mapping + resource reservation**.
> - **Avoid saying "fast" — say "bounded" or "predictable."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Study Implementation: Implementing a Real-Time System for a Specific Application (e.g., Real-Time Data Acquisition)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT757 - REAL TIME OPERATING SYSTEM > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Case Study Implementation: Implementing a Real-Time System for a Specific Application (e.g., Real-Time Data Acquisition) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-Time Data Acquisition System (RTDAS):** A specialized computing system that captures analog or digital signals from physical sensors, converts them into processable digital form, performs deterministic real-time analysis, and stores or displays the results within strictly bounded time constraints — governed by a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) that guarantees worst-case response latencies. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PERAT757)**, a *Case Study Implementation* requires the student to: 1. Identify a real-world problem with **hard/soft timing constraints**. 2. Decompose the system into **concurrent tasks** with defined priorities, periods, and deadlines. 3. Map hardware I/O (sensors, ADC, actuators) onto **RTOS primitives** (tasks, ISRs, semaphores, queues, mailboxes). 4. Validate the schedule using **Rate Monotonic Analysis (RMA)** and deadline analysis. 5. Justify the choice of RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RT-Linux) based on the application's jitter, latency, and reliability requirements. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital ICU" Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuition (Plain English):** > Think of a Real-Time Data Acquisition System like a **Hospital ICU (Intensive Care Unit)**. > > - **Patients = Sensor Inputs** (ECG, temperature, blood pressure probes) continuously generating data. > - **Nurses = ISR (Interrupt Service Routines)** that immediately attend to alarms and life-critical events. > - **Doctors = High-priority RTOS Tasks** that analyze data and make decisions (e.g., administer drug). > - **Reception Desk = Message Queues** that buffer patient data for orderly processing. > - **Shift Schedule = RTOS Scheduler** ensuring the right "task-doctor" handles the right "event-patient" at the right time, **every time, without delay**. > > Just as a delayed ICU response can be fatal, a delayed RTOS response in a hard real-time system can cause system failure. The RTOS guarantees deterministic behavior — the response is not just *fast*, it is *bounded and predictable*. --- ## 1.3 Canonical Case Study — Multi-Channel Temperature Monitoring RTDAS > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selected Case Study:** *Industrial Boiler Temperature Monitoring System with Alarm, Logging & Display* > > This is a **textbook-grade** example (referenced in standard RTOS literature such as *Pavol Balogh's "Real-Time Concepts for Embedded Systems"* and *Qing Li's "Real-Time Concepts for Embedded Systems"*) and is highly aligned with KTU 2024 evaluation patterns. ### System Specification (As per Case Study) | Parameter | Value | Justification | |-----------|-------|---------------| | Number of sensors | 8 (K-type thermocouples) | 8 boiler zones | | Sampling rate per channel | **10 Hz** | Thermal inertia, Nyquist compliance | | Total sampling frequency | **80 Hz** (aggregate) | 8 × 10 Hz | | Display refresh | **2 Hz** | Human-perceivable update rate | | Alarm latency (hard deadline) | **≤ 50 ms** | Safety-critical overheat trip | | Data log write rate | **1 Hz** | Non-volatile storage archiving | | Operating environment | Industrial (EMI, vibration) | Robust RTOS needed | > [!NOTE] > **Hard vs. Soft Real-Time Boundary in this case study:** > - **Hard Real-Time:** Over-temperature alarm (≤ 50 ms deadline, missing it = hardware damage). > - **Soft Real-Time:** Display refresh and data logging (deadline miss = visual lag, no catastrophe). --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Metrics (Bolded for emphasis):** > - **Nyquist Rate:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ — sampling rate must be at least twice the highest signal frequency to avoid aliasing. > - **CPU Utilization Bound (Liu & Layland):** $U_{bound} = n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$ > - **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET):** $C_i$ — upper bound on task $i$'s CPU time. > - **Jitter:** $J = T_{max} - T_{min}$ — variability in task release/response times. > - **Interrupt Latency:** Time from interrupt assertion to first instruction of ISR. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Phases of an RTDAS The implementation of a Real-Time Data Acquisition System is decomposed into **five logical phases**, each with deterministic timing requirements: ### Phase 1 — Sensor Signal Acquisition - **What:** Physical phenomena (temperature, pressure, vibration) are converted by transducers (thermocouples, RTDs, strain gauges) into low-level analog voltages (typically **mV range**). - **Why:** Real-world signals are continuous-time, continuous-amplitude. A computer processes discrete-time, discrete-amplitude data. - **How:** Transducer → Signal Conditioning (amplification, filtering) → ADC. ### Phase 2 — Analog-to-Digital Conversion - **What:** ADC samples the conditioned analog signal at a fixed rate $f_s$ and quantizes it into an $N$-bit digital word. - **Resolution:** $\Delta = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^N}$ (volts per LSB). - **Trigger modes:** Polling (software-initiated), Timer-triggered (deterministic), or Interrupt-driven (event-driven). ### Phase 3 — RTOS Task Processing - **What:** Digital samples are routed to tasks via ISR → Queue → Task notification mechanism. - **Key RTOS primitives used:** - `xQueueCreate()` — buffer between ISR and processing task. - `xTaskCreate()` — registers periodic tasks with the scheduler. - `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR()` — signals high-priority alarm task. - `vTaskDelayUntil()` — ensures **periodic**, not purely *relative*, timing. ### Phase 4 — Storage and Communication - **What:** Processed data is written to non-volatile storage (SD card, Flash, EEPROM) and/or transmitted over industrial buses (CAN, Modbus, RS-485, Ethernet). - **Constraint:** Write operations are slow and **blocking**; must be deferred to a low-priority background task to avoid jitter in time-critical paths. ### Phase 5 — Display and HMI - **What:** Visual output on LCD/TFT/OLED with refresh independent of sampling rate. - **Why decouple?** Human vision cannot perceive changes faster than ~24 Hz; over-refreshing wastes CPU and increases I/O contention. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are *examinable* under PERAT757 Module 4. Memorize the table.** | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---------|-------------|---------------| | 1 | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | **Nyquist–Shannon sampling criterion** | Hz; $f_s$ = sample rate, $f_{max}$ = signal bandwidth | | 2 | $\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ | **ADC quantization step (LSB)** | Volts; $N$ = ADC resolution, $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage | | 3 | $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$ | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio of ideal ADC** | dB | | 4 | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | **Total CPU Utilization** | Fraction (0 ≤ U ≤ 1) | | 5 | $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | **Liu–Layland schedulability bound (RM)** | Valid for $D_i = T_i$ | | 6 | $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | **Worst-Case Response Time (RMA)** | Solved by fixed-point iteration | | 7 | $B = f_s \times N_{ch} \times N_{bits} \times t_{proc}$ | **Buffer memory requirement** | Bytes; $t_{proc}$ = processing window | | 8 | $J = R_{max} - R_{min}$ | **Jitter (response time variability)** | Time units | | 9 | $T_{latency} = T_{ISR\_entry} + T_{ISR\_exec}$ | **End-to-end interrupt latency** | Time units | | 10 | $P_{miss} = 1 - \frac{N_{deadlines\_met}}{N_{total}}$ | **Deadline miss probability** | Fraction | --- ## 2.3 Task Decomposition Strategy (Used in Case Study) > [!NOTE] > **Why decompose into multiple tasks?** A monolithic loop cannot guarantee deterministic response. **RTOS task separation** enables priority-based preemption, isolating hard real-time (alarm) from soft real-time (logging) paths. For the boiler temperature RTDAS, the **recommended task set** is: | Task | Priority (RM) | Period $T_i$ | WCET $C_i$ | Type | Justification | |------|---------------|--------------|------------|------|---------------| | **T_Alarm** | 5 (highest) | 50 ms | 2 ms | Hard | Over-temperature trip; lives at 20 Hz | | **T_Sample** | 4 | 12.5 ms | 1 ms | Hard | Read 8 ADCs at 80 Hz aggregate | | **T_Process** | 3 | 12.5 ms | 3 ms | Hard | Apply calibration, filter, threshold check | | **T_Display** | 2 | 500 ms | 5 ms | Soft | 2 Hz LCD update | | **T_Logger** | 1 (lowest) | 1000 ms | 10 ms | Soft | Write 1 record/sec to SD card | ### Engineering Justification of the Decomposition - **T_Alarm** must preempt everything else because it is the *safety interlocks* of the system. - **T_Sample** runs at 80 Hz to satisfy the aggregate sampling rate ($8 \text{ channels} \times 10 \text{ Hz}$). - **T_Process** performs the digital signal processing (averaging, threshold comparison). - **T_Display** and **T_Logger** are *best-effort* — they tolerate occasional deadline misses without functional compromise. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industrial Relevance:** > - **Process Control:** Oil refineries, chemical plants (DCS systems). > - **Medical Devices:** Patient monitors, infusion pumps, ventilators. > - **Aerospace:** Flight data recorders ("black boxes"), engine telemetry. > - **Automotive:** ECU sensor fusion (airbag crash detection within 2 ms). > - **Smart Grid:** Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) sampling at 30–120 Hz. > - **Scientific Instruments:** Particle accelerators, telescopes, seismographs. > > The case study methodology you learn here is **directly transferrable** to all of these domains. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Nyquist Sampling Rate for Temperature Channels **Given:** A boiler's temperature signal has a maximum significant frequency component of $f_{max} = 4 \text{ Hz}$ (thermal process dynamics). **To find:** Minimum sampling rate per channel. **Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist criterion.** $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values.** $$f_s \geq 2 \times 4 \text{ Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_s = 8 \text{ Hz (minimum per channel)}}$$ **Step 3 — Apply engineering safety margin (oversampling factor of 1.25×).** > [!NOTE] > **Why oversample?** Practical signals have noise and high-frequency transients. A 25% safety margin is standard in industrial DAQ design (per IEC 61131). $$f_{s,design} = 1.25 \times 8 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the aggregate sampling rate for 8 channels.** $$f_{agg} = 8 \times 10 \text{ Hz} = 80 \text{ Hz}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to task period.** $$T_{sample} = \frac{1}{f_{agg}} = \frac{1}{80} = 12.5 \text{ ms}$$ This matches the **$T_{Sample} = 12.5$ ms** value used in Section 2.3. ✓ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ADC Resolution Trade-off **Given:** Thermocouple output range = **0 to 50 mV**; required temperature resolution = **0.5 °C**; thermocouple sensitivity ≈ **40 µV/°C**. **To find:** Required ADC resolution $N$. **Step 1 — Compute voltage corresponding to 0.5 °C.** $$\Delta V = 40 \, \mu V/°C \times 0.5 \, °C = 20 \, \mu V$$ **Step 2 — Apply LSB relation.** $$\Delta V \leq \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$$ **Step 3 — With on-chip PGA gain of 64×, the amplified full-scale becomes.** $$V_{FS,amp} = 50 \, mV \times 64 = 3200 \, mV = 3.2 \, V$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $N$.** $$2^N \geq \frac{V_{FS,amp}}{\Delta V} = \frac{3.2}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 160{,}000$$ $$N \geq \log_2(160{,}000) \approx 17.29$$ **Step 5 — Round up (use next available ADC resolution).** $$\boxed{N = 18 \text{ bits (or 16-bit with oversampling)}}$$ **Step 6 — Compute theoretical SNR.** $$SNR = 6.02 \times 18 + 1.76 = 110.12 \text{ dB}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Practical note:** 24-bit sigma-delta ADCs (e.g., ADS1256) are common in industrial thermocouple DAQ. Oversampling and averaging in software further reduce effective noise. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Liu–Layland Schedulability Test **Given task set:** | Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | Priority (RM) | |------|------------|------------|---------------| | T_Alarm | 2 | 50 | 5 | | T_Sample | 1 | 12.5 | 4 | | T_Process | 3 | 12.5 | 3 | | T_Display | 5 | 500 | 2 | | T_Logger | 10 | 1000 | 1 | **Step 1 — Compute individual utilizations.** $$u_{alarm} = \frac{C_{alarm}}{T_{alarm}} = \frac{2}{50} = 0.0400$$ $$u_{sample} = \frac{1}{12.5} = 0.0800$$ $$u_{process} = \frac{3}{12.5} = 0.2400$$ $$u_{display} = \frac{5}{500} = 0.0100$$ $$u_{logger} = \frac{10}{1000} = 0.0100$$ **Step 2 — Sum total CPU utilization.** $$U_{total} = 0.0400 + 0.0800 + 0.2400 + 0.0100 + 0.0100 = 0.3800$$ So $U_{total} = 38\%$. **Step 3 — Compute Liu–Layland bound for $n = 5$ tasks.** $$U_{bound} = 5 \cdot (2^{1/5} - 1)$$ $$U_{bound} = 5 \cdot (1.1487 - 1) = 5 \times 0.1487 = 0.7435$$ **Step 4 — Apply sufficient condition.** $$U_{total} = 0.3800 \leq 0.7435 = U_{bound}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Task set is SCHEDULABLE under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. ✓}}$$ **Step 5 — Resource headroom available.** $$U_{slack} = U_{bound} - U_{total} = 0.7435 - 0.3800 = 0.3635 \;(36.35\%)$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The system has ~36% CPU idle capacity, which is healthy. This headroom can absorb ISR execution, context-switch overhead, and allow safe addition of up to 1–2 more low-priority tasks. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Worst-Case Response Time (RTA) For the **highest-priority task (T_Alarm)**: **Step 1 — Initialize.** $$R_0 = C_{alarm} = 2 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2 — Account for preemption by all higher-priority tasks.** (None above T_Alarm in our set, so $R_1$ is trivial.) $$R_1 = 2 \text{ ms}$$ For **T_Process** (which has higher-priority tasks T_Alarm and T_Sample): **Step 1 — Initialize.** $$R_0 = C_{process} = 3 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2 — First iteration.** $$R_1 = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{12.5} \right\rceil \cdot 1$$ $$R_1 = 3 + 1 \times 2 + 1 \times 1 = 6 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Second iteration.** $$R_2 = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{12.5} \right\rceil \cdot 1$$ $$R_2 = 3 + 1 \times 2 + 1 \times 1 = 6 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 4 — Fixed-point convergence: $R_n = R_{n-1}$ → solution found.** $$\boxed{R_{process} = 6 \text{ ms} \leq D_{process} = T_{process} = 12.5 \text{ ms ✓}}$$ **Step 5 — Compute T_Display response time** (preempted by T_Alarm, T_Sample, T_Process, T_Logger is lower so excluded): Wait — T_Logger is *lower* priority, so it cannot preempt. Only higher-priority tasks preempt. **Initialize:** $R_0 = C_{display} = 5$ ms $$R_1 = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{12.5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{12.5} \right\rceil \cdot 3$$ $$R_1 = 5 + 1 \times 2 + 1 \times 1 + 1 \times 3 = 11 \text{ ms}$$ **Next iteration:** $$R_2 = 5 + 1 \times 2 + 1 \times 1 + 1 \times 3 = 11 \text{ ms}$$ $$\boxed{R_{display} = 11 \text{ ms} \leq 500 \text{ ms ✓}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **All tasks meet their deadlines under the Rate Monotonic schedule.** The case study design is **provably deterministic**. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Decisions & Trade-offs | Design Choice | Alternative A | Alternative B | Justification for Selection | |---------------|---------------|---------------|------------------------------| | **Scheduling Policy** | Rate Monotonic (RM) | Earliest Deadline First (EDF) | RM is static and simpler; no dynamic overhead. Suitable for fixed-priority industrial RTOS. | | **ADC Triggering** | Timer-triggered DMA | Software-polled ADC | DMA = zero CPU intervention = deterministic latency. Polling is non-deterministic under load. | | **Communication: ISR→Task** | Direct queue post | Semaphore + shared flag | Queue post is atomic, race-free, and supports multiple buffered samples. | | **Storage strategy** | Synchronous write | Buffered async write | Async buffer decouples slow SD write from real-time path; eliminates jitter. | | **Display updates** | On every sample | Decoupled 2 Hz task | Prevents 80 Hz display thrashing; reduces SPI contention. | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End System Block Diagram (RTDAS Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S1[Physical Layer Sensors] TH1[Thermocouple Zone 1] TH2[Thermocouple Zone 2] TH3[Thermocouple Zone N] end subgraph S2[Signal Conditioning Front End] AM1[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 64x] LP1[Anti Alias Filter fc 40 Hz] MX1[Analog Multiplexer 8 to 1] end subgraph S3[Hardware Acquisition] ADC1[24 bit Sigma Delta ADC] DMA1[DMA Controller Auto Transfer] TMR1[Hardware Timer Trigger 80 Hz] end subgraph S4[RTOS Kernel] ISR1[ADC ISR Handler] Q1[Sample Queue xQueue 64] TSK1[Task T_Sample Priority 4] TSK2[Task T_Process Priority 3] TSK3[Task T_Alarm Priority 5] TSK4[Task T_Display Priority 2] TSK5[Task T_Logger Priority 1] SEM1[Alarm Binary Semaphore] end subgraph S5[Outputs and HMI] LCD1[LCD TFT Display 2 Hz] SD1[SD Card Logger 1 Hz] ACT1[Relay Cutoff Hard Real Time] NET1[Ethernet Modbus TCP] end TH1 --> AM1 TH2 --> AM1 TH3 --> MX1 AM1 --> LP1 LP1 --> MX1 MX1 --> ADC1 TMR1 --> ADC1 ADC1 --> DMA1 DMA1 --> ISR1 ISR1 --> Q1 Q1 --> TSK1 TSK1 --> TSK2 TSK2 --> TSK4 TSK2 --> TSK5 TSK2 --> SEM1 SEM1 --> TSK3 TSK3 --> ACT1 TSK4 --> LCD1 TSK5 --> SD1 TSK5 --> NET1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The flow proceeds *left to right*, from the physical phenomenon (heat) through analog front-end, hardware acquisition, RTOS task graph, and finally to outputs (display, storage, actuator cut-off). **Solid arrows** denote data flow; **DMA** is shown explicitly because it is the *deterministic zero-CPU-overhead* bridge between hardware and software. --- ## 4.2 RTOS Task State Machine — Lifecycle of a Data Sample ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Blocked Blocked --> Ready : Timer ISR fires

highest priority Running --> Ready : Preempted by

xTaskDelete note right of Running Worst case execution time Ci
must be bounded
and measured offline end note ``` --- ## 4.3 Data Flow Sequence Diagram (Time-Ordered) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant TMR as Hardware Timer participant ADC as ADC Hardware participant ISR as ADC ISR participant Q as Sample Queue participant SP as Task T_Sample participant PR as Task T_Process participant AL as Task T_Alarm participant RL as Relay Actuator TMR->>ADC: Trigger conversion at 80 Hz ADC->>ISR: Conversion complete interrupt ISR->>Q: xQueueSendFromISR sample Q->>SP: Wake on queue receive SP->>PR: xQueueSend processed ready PR->>PR: Filter compare threshold alt Temperature exceeds safe limit PR->>AL: xSemaphoreGiveFromISR AL->>RL: Trip relay cutoff end Note over PR,AL: Hard real time path 50 ms deadline ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Path Analysis:** The vertical arrows from `TMR` → `ISR` → `AL` → `RL` form the **hard real-time safety path**. Answer each in **3–4 lines maximum**. Below are model Q&A pairs. --- ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper Style] **Define a Real-Time Data Acquisition System. List any four typical industrial applications.** **Model Answer:** > A Real-Time Data Acquisition System (RTDAS) is a computer-based system that captures analog/digital signals from physical sensors, converts them to digital form using an ADC, processes them under bounded timing constraints using an RTOS, and stores or displays the results — all within deterministic deadlines. > > **Four industrial applications:** > 1. Industrial process control (temperature/pressure monitoring in refineries). > 2. Medical patient monitoring (ECG, SpO₂, blood pressure). > 3. Automotive sensor fusion and airbag crash detection. > 4. Smart grid Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) for power-quality monitoring. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for applications — ½ mark each.)* --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper Style] **What is the Nyquist sampling criterion? If a vibration sensor's maximum frequency is 250 Hz, what is the minimum sampling rate required?** **Model Answer:** > The Nyquist sampling criterion states that a bandlimited signal must be sampled at a rate **at least twice its highest frequency component** to allow perfect reconstruction without aliasing. > > Given $f_{max} = 250 \text{ Hz}$: $$f_s \geq 2 \times 250 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$ $$\boxed{f_{s,min} = 500 \text{ Hz}}$$ *(3 marks: 1 for criterion, 1 for formula, 1 for numerical answer.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module-level question with **internal choice between Q-A and Q-B**. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. Sub-questions are typically: > - (a) → Theory/Design/Architecture (7 marks) > - (b) → Numerical/Analysis/Comparison (7 marks) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [Choice Option A] **Q-A(a)** Design the task architecture for a real-time boiler temperature monitoring system with 8 sensors sampled at 10 Hz/channel, an alarm task with 50 ms deadline, a 2 Hz display, and a 1 Hz data logger. Use Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Draw the task diagram. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Identify tasks from system requirements:** | Task | Function | Period (ms) | Priority (RM) | |------|----------|-------------|---------------| | T_Alarm | Over-temperature trip | 50 | 5 (highest) | | T_Sample | Read 8 ADCs | 12.5 | 4 | | T_Process | Filter, threshold check | 12.5 | 3 | | T_Display | LCD refresh | 500 | 2 | | T_Logger | SD card write | 1000 | 1 (lowest) | **Step 2 — Period derivation:** - Aggregate sampling rate = 8 × 10 Hz = 80 Hz → $T_{sample} = 12.5$ ms. - Alarm period chosen to satisfy 50 ms deadline with 50% safety margin: $T_{alarm} = 50$ ms. - Display at 2 Hz → $T_{display} = 500$ ms. - Logger at 1 Hz → $T_{logger} = 1000$ ms. **Step 3 — Communication primitives:** - **ISR (ADC) → T_Sample:** `xQueueSendFromISR()`. - **T_Sample → T_Process:** `xQueueSend()`. - **T_Process → T_Alarm:** `xSemaphoreGive()` (only on threshold violation). - **T_Process → T_Display:** Shared memory (read-only snapshot) or `xQueuePeek()`. - **T_Process → T_Logger:** Ring buffer of last 80 samples. **Step 4 — Task Diagram (Mermaid-style description for paper):** ``` [Hardware Timer 80Hz] | v [ADC + DMA] --IRQ--> [T_Sample] --Queue--> [T_Process] --Semaphore--> [T_Alarm] | v [Relay Cutoff] [Display] [Logger] ``` **Step 5 — Justify priority assignment:** Highest rate → highest priority (RM rule). T_Sample and T_Process have the same period; assign T_Sample higher (it must run first to unblock the pipeline). *(Mark split: 2 for table, 1 for derivation, 2 for communication design, 2 for diagram & priority justification.)* --- **Q-A(b)** For the task set designed in (a), the WCET values are: T_Alarm = 2 ms, T_Sample = 1 ms, T_Process = 3 ms, T_Display = 5 ms, T_Logger = 10 ms. Verify schedulability using (i) Liu–Layland bound, and (ii) Worst-Case Response Time analysis. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(i) Liu–Layland Sufficient Test:** $$U = \frac{2}{50} + \frac{1}{12.5} + \frac{3}{12.5} + \frac{5}{500} + \frac{10}{1000}$$ $$U = 0.040 + 0.080 + 0.240 + 0.010 + 0.010 = 0.380$$ $$U_{bound}(n=5) = 5(2^{1/5} - 1) = 5 \times 0.1487 = 0.7435$$ Since $U = 0.380 \leq 0.7435 = U_{bound}$, **the task set is schedulable under RM**. ✓ **(ii) Worst-Case Response Time (Fixed-Point Iteration):** For **T_Process** (preempted by T_Alarm and T_Sample): $$R_0 = C_{process} = 3 \text{ ms}$$ $$R_1 = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{12.5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 \text{ ms}$$ $$R_2 = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{12.5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark$$ Convergence → $R_{process} = 6$ ms $\leq D_{process} = 12.5$ ms. ✓ For **T_Display** (preempted by all four higher-priority tasks): $$R_0 = 5 \text{ ms}$$ $$R_1 = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{50} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{12.5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{12.5} \right\rceil \cdot 3$$ $$R_1 = 5 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 3 = 11 \text{ ms}$$ Fixed point reached → $R_{display} = 11$ ms $\leq 500$ ms. ✓ **Conclusion:** All tasks meet their deadlines. The system is **provably deterministic** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. *(Mark split: 2 marks for U computation, 1 for Liu–Layland, 2 for RTA iteration, 1 for final conclusion, 1 for T_Display analysis.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [Choice Option B — Alternative Design] **Q-B(a)** Compare polled I/O, interrupt-driven I/O, and DMA-based acquisition for a real-time data acquisition system. Recommend the most suitable for a hard real-time multi-channel DAQ with 8 sensors. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (Tabular Comparison):** | Feature | Polled I/O | Interrupt-Driven I/O | DMA-Based I/O | |---------|-----------|----------------------|---------------| | **CPU involvement** | Continuous polling (100% in tight loops) | Per-sample interrupt (high overhead) | Zero CPU during transfer | | **Latency determinism** | Poor (depends on loop frequency) | Good (deterministic per-trigger) | **Excellent (hardware-paced)** | | **CPU utilization** | 60–80% wasted on I/O wait | 15–25% spent in ISR overhead | **< 5%** | | **Sampling jitter** | High (varying loop time) | Medium (depends on ISR latency) | **Minimal** (timer + DMA trigger) | | **Code complexity** | Simple | Moderate (ISR design) | Higher (DMA buffer mgmt) | | **Best use case** | Single-channel slow sensors | Event-driven, sparse data | **Multi-channel, high-rate DAQ** | | **Scalability to 8 channels** | Poor | Moderate | **Excellent** | **Recommendation:** **DMA-based, timer-triggered acquisition** is the optimal choice because: 1. It supports **deterministic sampling** at exactly 80 Hz (set by hardware timer). 2. CPU remains free for processing tasks (filtering, alarm logic, display). 3. Combined with double-buffering, it eliminates ISR-induced jitter. 4. The 50 ms alarm deadline is easily met because the ISR (on DMA-complete) is invoked only **once per 8-channel frame**, not per channel. **Code-level primitives (FreeRTOS context):** - `HAL_ADC_Start_DMA()` — initiates burst acquisition. - `HAL_TIM_Base_Start_IT()` — generates 80 Hz trigger via Timer. - `xQueueSendFromISR()` — posts completed frame to T_Sample. - **Critical:** Use `portYIELD_FROM_ISR()` to force context switch if higher-priority task unblocked. *(Mark split: 4 for table, 1 for recommendation justification, 2 for design rationale.)* --- **Q-B(b)** For a 12-bit ADC with a 0–5 V reference, what is the LSB voltage? If the sensor output range is 0–100 °C mapped linearly to 0–5 V, what is the digital code at 73.5 °C? Also compute the SNR. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — LSB voltage calculation.** $$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^N} = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} \approx 1.2207 \text{ mV}$$ **Step 2 — Voltage at 73.5 °C.** $$V_{in} = \frac{73.5}{100} \times 5 = 3.675 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3 — Digital code.** $$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{\Delta} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{3.675}{0.0012207} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3010.5 \right\rfloor = 3010$$ In hex: $3010_{10} = \text{0BC2}_{16}$ → binary: `101111000010`. **Step 4 — Theoretical SNR (assuming ideal 12-bit ADC).** $$SNR = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 73.0 + 1.76 = 74.76 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 5 — Quantization error bounds.** $$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2} = \pm 0.6104 \text{ mV}$$ This corresponds to a temperature error of: $$\pm \frac{0.6104 \text{ mV}}{5 \text{ V}} \times 100 °C = \pm 0.0122 \text{ °C}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** A 12-bit ADC gives ~0.012 °C resolution — more than sufficient for boiler control (typical industrial requirement: ±0.5 °C). For higher precision (e.g., ±0.01 °C), use 16-bit or 24-bit ADC. *(Mark split: 1.5 for LSB, 1.5 for digital code, 2 for SNR, 1 for quantization error analysis, 1 for engineering interpretation.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in RTDAS Case Study Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing Sampling Rate vs. Task Period** — Students often write "10 Hz" as the task period (giving 100 ms) instead of computing $T = 1/f_{agg} = 12.5$ ms for the 8-channel aggregate. **Examiner penalty: −1 to −2 marks.** > > 2. **Forgetting Nyquist Safety Margin** — Writing $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ without a practical oversampling factor loses 1 mark. Always state and justify the oversampling factor. > > 3. **Wrong Liu–Layland Application** — Some students apply the bound with $n = 3$ or $n = 4$ instead of the *actual number of tasks* $n = 5$. Recount carefully. > > 4. **RTA Convergence Mistake** — Stopping the fixed-point iteration after one round (e.g., reporting $R_1 = 11$ ms without verifying $R_2 = R_1$). **Examiner penalty: −1 mark.** Always iterate until $R_n = R_{n-1}$. > > 5. **Missed Communication Primitives** — The case study explicitly requires RTOS primitive selection (queue, semaphore, mailbox). A design that uses only global flags will lose 2–3 marks due to race-condition concerns. > > 6. **Ignoring DMA** — Pure interrupt-based acquisition (without DMA) wastes marks. Modern KTU papers expect **DMA + Timer-trigger** as the canonical hard real-time acquisition scheme. > > 7. **Priority Inversion** — Students frequently forget to mention **priority inheritance protocols** (e.g., `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT)`) when tasks share resources. A case study answer that uses shared memory without mentioning this **will lose 2 marks** in 2024-scheme evaluations. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:** - ✅ **RTDAS = Sensor + Signal Conditioning + ADC + RTOS Tasks + Storage/HMI**, all under **bounded latency**. - ✅ **Hard real-time** paths (alarm, actuator trip) require **deterministic** scheduling — never use `vTaskDelay()` (use `vTaskDelayUntil()`). - ✅ **Nyquist rate:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ → in practice, **oversample by 1.25× to 4×** to handle transients and noise. - ✅ **ADC resolution formula:** $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^N$ ; **SNR formula:** $6.02N + 1.76$ dB (ideal). - ✅ **Liu–Layland bound:** $U_{bound} = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ — sufficient but *not necessary* condition for RM schedulability. - ✅ **RTA equation:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$ — solved by **fixed-point iteration** until convergence. - ✅ **DMA + Hardware Timer** is the gold standard for high-rate multi-channel acquisition (zero CPU, deterministic). - ✅ **Task priority assignment (RM rule):** Highest frequency = highest priority. - ✅ **Communication primitives:** ISR→Task = `xQueueSendFromISR()`; Task→Task = `xQueueSend()`; Event signalling = `xSemaphoreGive()`. - ✅ **Resource sharing:** Use **priority inheritance mutexes** to prevent unbounded priority inversion (this is **exam-favorite** content). - ✅ **Memory budgets:** Always size stack per task (`configMINIMAL_STACK_SIZE × multiplier`); use **memory pools** for predictable allocation. - ✅ **Watchdog timers** must be present in any safety-critical DAQ — never let a deadlock go undetected. - ✅ **Jitter reduction techniques:** (i) Timer-triggered sampling, (ii) DMA, (iii) avoid dynamic memory in real-time path, (iv) lock critical sections to short durations. - ✅ **Verification:** Schedulability is *necessary but not sufficient* — also perform **runtime profiling**, **stress testing**, and **fault injection** in the validation phase. - ✅ **Soft vs. Hard boundary:** Display & logging → soft; alarm & actuator → hard. Never let a soft task block a hard task. - ✅ **Common industrial buses:** CAN (automotive), Modbus (industrial), RS-485 (long-distance), Ethernet/IP (modern plants), I²C/SPI (board-level). --- ### **End of Module 4 Case Study Notes — PERAT757** *All formulas, derivations, diagrams, and questions aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) B.Tech evaluation pattern.*
